selected_rules.activation_condition: Clinically N-0 neck
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET as a better alternative to conventional imaging for nodal staging
selected_rules.source_statement: In the clinically N-0 neck, PET does not appear to be better than conventional imaging, because of an unacceptably high false-negative rate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Clinically accessible site of suspected recurrence or surveillance after treatment
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on PET for assessment because accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: With respect to recurrence and tumour surveillance after treatment, the evidence suggests that sites of disease that are clinically accessible for assessment did not benefit from PET imaging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has head and neck squamous cell carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal, or treatment may be significantly modified
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for M staging and bilateral nodal staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended in the M and bilateral nodal staging of all patients with head and neck squamous cell carcinoma where conventional imaging is equivocal, or where treatment may be significantly modified.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Primary site is unknown after conventional imaging
selected_rules.execute: Use PET after conventional imaging and before or in addition to diagnostic panendoscopy
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended in all patients after conventional imaging and in addition to, or prior to, diagnostic panendoscopy where the primary site is unknown.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has cervical lymph node metastases and the primary site is unknown after conventional imaging or clinical examination
selected_rules.execute: Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor
selected_rules.source_statement: One primary study indicated that patients with cervical metastasis and an unknown primary site after undergoing conventional imaging or clinical examination benefit from PET/CT prior to panendoscopy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has nasopharyngeal carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging and assessment of recurrence of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma if conventional imaging is equivocal.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has nasopharyngeal carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal for recurrence assessment
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for assessment of recurrence
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging and assessment of recurrence of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma if conventional imaging is equivocal.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Need to detect distant metastatic disease in head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use PET because it is better than conventional imaging for distant metastasis detection
selected_rules.source_statement: There is strong evidence that PET imaging is valuable in detecting distant metastatic disease and is better than conventional imaging.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The neck mass is easily palpable and accessible for physical examination and biopsy. The clinician is considering PET/CT to look for nodal disease and to help localize a primary site before panendoscopy.
recommended_actions: Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor in a patient with cervical lymph node metastases and an unknown primary after conventional imaging or clinical examination.
Do not rely on PET for assessment of the accessible neck mass because clinically accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the recommendation to use PET/CT before panendoscopy because the patient has cervical nodal metastasis with an unknown primary after conventional imaging and exam. It also triggers the caution against relying on PET for assessment of a clinically accessible site, since the palpable neck mass is directly examinable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The node is easily palpable but a prior core biopsy 10 days ago confirmed squamous cell carcinoma, and the workup is now being planned for restaging after a recent excisional biopsy. The clinician is considering PET/CT before panendoscopy to localize a primary site.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like an unknown-primary neck metastasis with negative exam and CT, but the nodal diagnosis has already been established and the workflow is now restaging after tissue confirmation rather than initial unknown-primary evaluation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Obtain PET/CT before panendoscopy to localize the occult primary tumor
Use PET/CT to guide the next diagnostic step in this unknown-primary neck mass
recommended_actions: Use PET for M staging and bilateral nodal staging if conventional imaging is equivocal or treatment may be significantly modified
Proceed with standard head and neck cancer staging and endoscopic evaluation based on the confirmed diagnosis
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a clean unknown-primary presentation for PET/CT prior to panendoscopy, because the node has already been biopsied and the current clinical question is staging/restaging after confirmed squamous cell carcinoma. The safer interpretation is to manage it as confirmed head and neck cancer staging, not as first-line occult primary localization.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation was for cervical metastasis with an unknown primary after conventional imaging or clinical examination. Here the mass is no longer an unworked-up unknown primary because biopsy has already confirmed SCC and the evaluation has shifted to staging/restaging, so the prior recommendation is no longer guideline-concordant.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The neck mass is easily palpable and accessible for examination, but the patient has a documented severe iodinated contrast allergy and has declined further invasive procedures for now. The clinician is considering PET/CT to look for nodal disease and to help localize a primary site before panendoscopy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still sounds like an occult head and neck primary with a palpable cervical node and negative conventional workup, but the intended workflow is no longer appropriate because the patient is declining invasive evaluation and the lesion is being followed conservatively rather than proceeding directly to panendoscopy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Obtain PET/CT before panendoscopy to localize the occult primary tumor
Use PET/CT to assess the palpable neck mass and decide on the next step
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the PET/CT-before-panendoscopy recommendation because the patient is not proceeding with diagnostic panendoscopy and invasive workup is deferred by preference. The safer interpretation is to document the limitation, reassess goals of care, and pursue shared decision-making about alternative diagnostic steps.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule assumes a patient with cervical metastasis and unknown primary after conventional imaging or clinical examination who is being worked up toward panendoscopy. Here, refusal of invasive procedures changes the workflow enough that the specific recommendation does not apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast cancer is HER2-positive
selected_rules.execute: Do not use multigene profiling assays because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is currently insufficient evidence to use multigene profiling assays among patients with either HER2-positive ... breast cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast cancer is triple negative
selected_rules.execute: Do not use multigene profiling assays because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is currently insufficient evidence to use multigene profiling assays among patients with either HER2-positive or triple negative breast cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering multiple multigene profiling assays on the same tumour specimen for one treatment decision
selected_rules.execute: Do not order multiple different multigene profiling assays on the same tumour specimen for a single decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: Requesting multiple tests with different multigene profiling assays on an individual tumour specimen to guide a single treatment decision is discouraged.

selected_rules.activation_condition: node-positive ER-positive HER2-negative breast cancer with higher risk features and considering withholding adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on Prosigna, EndoPredict/EPclin, or Breast Cancer Index to justify withholding adjuvant chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The Prosigna, EndoPredict/EPclin, and Breast Cancer Index assays can identify low-risk node-positive patients whose prognostic outcomes are favourable; however, these assays have not demonstrated predictive evidence to support withholding adjuvant chemotherapy among higher risk, node-positive, ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering MammaPrint, Oncotype DX, Prosigna, or EndoPredict to extend adjuvant endocrine therapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use these assays to guide extension of adjuvant endocrine therapy because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: MammaPrint, Oncotype DX, Prosigna, and EndoPredict currently have insufficient evidence to guide extension of adjuvant endocrine therapy;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has lymph node-negative disease or 1-3 positive lymph nodes and is being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy, with supporting clinical/pathological/patient factors
selected_rules.execute: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: Multigene profiling assays are recommended for use in patients with lymph node-negative or lymph node-positive (1-3 lymph nodes) disease who are under consideration for adjuvant chemotherapy if the use is supported by other clinical, pathological, or patient-related factors.

selected_rules.activation_condition: one multigene profiling assay is being ordered for a patient
selected_rules.execute: Request only one assay per patient for a specific treatment decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: One multigene profiling assay should be requested per patient to guide a specific treatment decision.

selected_rules.activation_condition: early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer and systemic therapy decision is needed
selected_rules.execute: Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with early-stage estrogen receptor (ER)-positive/human epidermal growth factor 2 (HER2)-negative breast cancer, clinicians should consider using multigene profiling assays ... to help guide the use of systemic therapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman is seen after lumpectomy for a 1.8 cm invasive ductal carcinoma. Pathology shows ER-positive, HER2-negative disease with 2 of 12 axillary lymph nodes involved, no distant metastases, and intermediate grade. She is otherwise healthy and is anxious to avoid chemotherapy if it would not add meaningful benefit. Her oncologist is deciding on adjuvant systemic therapy and is considering whether a genomic assay could help refine the chemotherapy discussion. The team plans to use the result for this single treatment decision and does not intend to send multiple different assays on the same specimen.
recommended_actions: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision, since she has node-positive disease with 1-3 positive nodes and relevant clinical/pathological factors.
Request only one multigene profiling assay for this specific treatment decision.
Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy in this early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer case.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer with 1-3 positive nodes and a real adjuvant chemotherapy decision, which supports use of a multigene profiling assay. The rules also specify that only one assay should be ordered for a single treatment decision. The case does not trigger any rule against assay use, because it is not HER2-positive, triple-negative, or a scenario of withholding chemotherapy based on a low-risk result.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman is seen after lumpectomy for a 1.8 cm invasive ductal carcinoma. Pathology shows ER-positive, HER2-negative disease with 4 of 12 axillary lymph nodes involved, no distant metastases, and intermediate grade. She is otherwise healthy and is anxious to avoid chemotherapy if it would not add meaningful benefit. Her oncologist is deciding on adjuvant systemic therapy and is considering whether a genomic assay could help refine the chemotherapy discussion. The team plans to use the result for this single treatment decision and does not intend to send multiple different assays on the same specimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still appears to be early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer after lumpectomy with a chemotherapy decision pending, but the nodal burden is just beyond the 1-3 node range.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a multigene profiling assay to help decide whether adjuvant chemotherapy can be omitted.
Use a single genomic assay result to support the adjuvant systemic therapy discussion.
Proceed with multigene profiling because the patient has limited nodal involvement and wants to avoid chemotherapy.
recommended_actions: Request only one multigene profiling assay per patient for this specific treatment decision.
Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy only if it is otherwise appropriate for the clinical context.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer guideline-concordant for the main recommendation because she has 4 positive axillary nodes, which falls outside the node-negative or 1-3 node group where the assay is recommended to support adjuvant chemotherapy decisions. The safer interpretation is that nodal disease is more extensive, so management should not rely on a multigene assay to decide on withholding chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation applies to lymph node-negative or 1-3 node-positive disease under consideration for adjuvant chemotherapy. With 4 involved nodes, that criterion is not met. The instruction to request only one assay remains relevant in general, but the key assay-support recommendation is invalidated.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman returns after mastectomy for a 2.1 cm invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast. The tumor is ER-positive and HER2-negative, with 1 of 14 axillary nodes involved and no distant metastases. Grade is intermediate, and she has mild osteopenia but is otherwise well. Her oncologist is weighing adjuvant chemotherapy versus endocrine therapy alone and is considering a multigene profiling assay. Because tissue is limited, the team plans to send a single assay on the primary tumor to help with that decision.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation closely matches node-positive ER-positive, HER2-negative early breast cancer after definitive surgery, with a chemotherapy decision still pending, but one subtle feature changes the clinical use case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a multigene profiling assay to help decide on adjuvant chemotherapy.
Send one genomic assay on the specimen for the chemotherapy discussion.
Use the assay result to avoid chemotherapy if the recurrence risk appears low.
recommended_actions: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
Request only one multigene profiling assay for this specific treatment decision.
Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy in this early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer case.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is still guideline-concordant for multigene assay use because it remains early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer with 1 positive node and a genuine adjuvant chemotherapy decision. The subtle difference is that the patient is receiving initial adjuvant treatment rather than being in a setting where the assay would be inappropriate.
challenging_examples.rationale: This vignette preserves the core scenario that triggers the recommendation: node-positive (1-3 nodes) ER-positive, HER2-negative disease being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy, with only one assay planned. No invalidating detail was introduced that removes the indication.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed biopsy-confirmed prostate cancer; intermediate- or high-risk; being considered for radical therapy; knowledge of extraprostatic extension would alter management
selected_rules.execute: Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).
selected_rules.source_statement: Multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) use for pre-treatment local staging of prostate cancer is a reasonable option for assessment of extraprostatic extension (EPE) in intermediate-and highrisk patients being considered for radical therapy if knowledge of EPE will alter management.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents after a transrectal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 4+3=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 6 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing radical prostatectomy versus radiation-based treatment, and the team notes that knowing whether there is extraprostatic extension would affect the surgical plan and whether nerve-sparing could be attempted. He is otherwise fit and wants definitive treatment.
recommended_actions: Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).
source_case.rationale: This is a newly diagnosed, biopsy-confirmed intermediate-risk prostate cancer case being considered for radical therapy, and knowledge of possible extraprostatic extension would change management. That matches the rule’s activation condition, supporting pre-treatment mpMRI for local staging.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents after a transperineal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 4+3=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 6 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing radical prostatectomy versus radiation-based treatment, and the team notes that local staging would help with counseling, although the patient is leaning strongly toward definitive radiotherapy regardless of nerve-sparing considerations. He is otherwise fit and wants treatment, but the surgeon does not expect imaging to change the operative plan.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The cancer risk profile and treatment discussion are nearly identical to the source case, but the management decision is no longer dependent on knowing extraprostatic extension.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Obtain a pre-treatment multiparametric prostate MRI to assess for extraprostatic extension.
Use MRI findings to help decide whether nerve-sparing can be attempted.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains newly diagnosed intermediate-risk prostate cancer, but the key condition for the imaging recommendation is missing: knowing EPE would not alter management because the patient is proceeding with definitive radiotherapy and the surgical plan is not being used to guide current decisions. Safer interpretation is that mpMRI may be optional for local characterization, but it is not guideline-concordant on this specific indication.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule applies only when knowledge of EPE will affect radical-treatment planning. Here, the management intent is effectively unchanged by local staging, so the activation condition is not met.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents after a transrectal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 3+4=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 2 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing active surveillance versus focal therapy, and the team is considering whether local staging might help define the extent of disease. He is otherwise fit and wants to avoid immediate radical treatment if possible. A pelvic MRI from 3 months ago was done for unrelated hip pain and showed only mild degenerative changes, with no dedicated prostate sequences.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has biopsy-proven prostate cancer and PSA in the same range, but the risk category and treatment pathway are different from the source vignette.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a multiparametric prostate MRI for pre-treatment staging to assess extraprostatic extension.
Use MRI staging to guide a radical prostatectomy plan.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although the clinical context resembles the source case, this is favorable intermediate-risk disease being discussed for surveillance or focal treatment rather than intermediate/high-risk disease being considered for radical therapy. The prior non-prostate MRI also does not satisfy dedicated local staging. The safer interpretation is that the cited pre-treatment mpMRI recommendation is not triggered here.
challenging_examples.rationale: The activation condition requires newly diagnosed intermediate- or high-risk prostate cancer being considered for radical therapy, with EPE knowledge affecting management. This case does not meet that threshold.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with cervical cancer for whom radiotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with cervical cancer for whom treatment with radiotherapy is being considered (described above) should be offered concurrent cisplatin with their course of radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with cervical cancer receiving cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy compared with radiotherapy alone or hydroxyurea
selected_rules.execute: Use concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy because pooled survival favored this approach.
selected_rules.source_statement: Pooled survival rates detected a statistically significant effect in favour of cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy compared with radiotherapy alone or with hydroxyurea (relative risk of death, 0.74; 95% confidence interval, 0.64 to 0.86).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with locally advanced cervical cancer treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The pooled relative risk of death among the six trials that enrolled only women with locally advanced cervical cancer was 0.78 (95% confidence interval, 0.67 to 0.90) in favour of cisplatin-based chemotherapy and radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The pooled relative risk for the two trials in high-risk early-stage disease also demonstrated a significant benefit for the addition of cisplatin-based chemotherapy to radiotherapy (relative risk, 0.56; 95% confidence interval, 0.41 to 0.77).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with bulky clinical stage IB cervical cancer (>4 cm) who are treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with bulky clinical stage IB (>4 cm) cervical cancer, who are treated with radiotherapy,

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer after hysterectomy who will be treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer (node-positive or margin-positive), who will be treated with radiotherapy following hysterectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with locally advanced cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with locally advanced cervical cancer,

selected_rules.activation_condition: When choosing a cisplatin regimen for concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 based on available toxicity data.
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the review of the available toxicity data from the randomized controlled trials, the Disease Site Group felt that cisplatinum should be given weekly (40 mg/m2).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 47-year-old woman presents with postcoital bleeding and pelvic pain. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix. MRI shows a 5.5 cm cervical mass with parametrial extension but no distant metastases, consistent with locally advanced disease. Her oncology team is planning definitive pelvic radiotherapy with brachytherapy. She has adequate renal function and performance status to receive chemotherapy. The patient asks whether anything should be added to radiation to improve outcomes.
recommended_actions: Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy for locally advanced cervical cancer.
Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 when selecting a concurrent cisplatin regimen.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a woman with cervical cancer in whom radiotherapy is being considered, which triggers the general recommendation to add concurrent cisplatin. Because her disease is locally advanced and primary treatment is radiotherapy, the locally advanced cervical cancer rule also applies. The weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 option is included as a conditional regimen consideration based on available toxicity data.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 47-year-old woman presents with postcoital bleeding and pelvic pain. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix. MRI shows a 4.8 cm cervical mass with parametrial extension but no distant metastases, and she is being prepared for definitive surgery rather than primary pelvic radiotherapy. Her renal function and performance status are adequate, and she asks whether anything should be added to improve outcomes if radiation is eventually needed later.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case keeps the same age, histology, symptoms, and locally advanced-appearing MRI findings, but the immediate treatment plan is surgery rather than radiotherapy, so the chemoradiation recommendation no longer applies.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer concurrent weekly cisplatin with pelvic radiotherapy.
Start cisplatin-based chemoradiation because this is locally advanced cervical cancer.
Plan weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 with definitive radiation.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although the tumor appears locally advanced, she is not currently being considered for radiotherapy. The cisplatin-with-radiation recommendation is tied to a planned radiotherapy course, so the safer interpretation is to manage according to the surgical plan and only revisit chemoradiation if adjuvant radiation becomes indicated later.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule requires cervical cancer for whom radiotherapy is being considered. Here, the treatment intent has shifted to primary surgery, so the trigger is absent despite similar imaging and symptoms.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old woman with biopsy-proven squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix has completed hysterectomy for a 3.2 cm tumor. Final pathology shows a negative vaginal cuff margin, no parametrial invasion, and no pelvic lymph node involvement. She recovered well and is now being referred to radiation oncology because of persistent pelvic discomfort, but the team is still discussing whether observation alone is appropriate. Her creatinine clearance is normal, and she wonders if chemotherapy should be added to any radiation plan.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This preserves the post-hysterectomy cervical cancer context and the question about adding chemotherapy, but removes the high-risk features that would justify concurrent cisplatin with adjuvant radiation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add concurrent cisplatin to postoperative pelvic radiotherapy.
Use cisplatin-based chemoradiation because she has cervical cancer after hysterectomy.
Plan weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 with adjuvant radiation.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not high-risk early-stage disease after hysterectomy: the margins and nodes are negative and there is no parametrial involvement. Without a guideline trigger for adjuvant radiotherapy plus cisplatin, observation or standard postoperative follow-up is the safer interpretation unless another indication for radiation emerges.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation applies to women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer after hysterectomy who will be treated with radiotherapy. Negative margins and node-negative pathology remove that condition, so concurrent cisplatin is not guideline-concordant on the information given.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with resected stage II colon cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of adjuvant chemotherapy for all patients with stage II colon cancer is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II patient without high-risk features who does not require adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: In stage II (in the absence of high-risk features) where a patient does not require adjuvant chemotherapy, MSI/dMMR testing is not recommended as it will not influence that decision.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II colon cancer with MSI/dMMR after surgery
selected_rules.execute: Do not give fluoropyrimidine monotherapy as adjuvant treatment.
selected_rules.source_statement: Adjuvant chemotherapy with a fluoropyrimidine monotherapy regimen following surgery in patients who have MSI/dMMR is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk stage II colon cancer treated with adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine.
selected_rules.source_statement: When treated with adjuvant therapy, high-risk stage II patients should receive a fluoropyrimidine.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II patient being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Perform MSI/dMMR testing.
selected_rules.source_statement: MSI/dMMR testing should be performed for all stage II patients for whom adjuvant chemotherapy is being considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with resected stage III colon cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use an oxaliplatin-containing regimen such as FOLFOX, FLOX, or XELOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: These oxaliplatin-containing regimens have demonstrated superior overall survival when compared with 5-FU plus LV and are the recommended regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: low-risk stage III colon cancer (T1-3 N1)
selected_rules.execute: Prefer 3 months of CAPOX over FOLFOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: Low-risk stage III (T1-3 N1): 3 months of CAPOX is preferred over FOLFOX.

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk stage III colon cancer (T4 +/- N2)
selected_rules.execute: Give 6 months of oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy with CAPOX or FOLFOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: High-risk stage III (T4 +/-N2): 6 months of oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy (CAPOX or FOLFOX).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 stage II colon cancer with clear margins, 18 lymph nodes examined, no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. He has recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and discussing postoperative management.
recommended_actions: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision because the patient has stage II disease without high-risk features and the result would not influence the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
source_case.rationale: This is a resected stage II colon cancer case without high-risk features, so routine adjuvant chemotherapy is not recommended. Because he does not require adjuvant chemotherapy based on the available features, MSI/dMMR testing is not recommended for this decision.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 stage II colon cancer with clear margins and 18 lymph nodes examined, with no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. At follow-up, he reports that the surgeon noted the preoperative CT had already shown two small indeterminate liver lesions, one of which is now being re-reviewed by radiology. He has recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and discussing postoperative management.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a straightforward resected stage II colon cancer after surgery, but the indeterminate liver lesions introduce concern for possible metastatic disease and change the adjuvant decision context.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Reassure him that no adjuvant chemotherapy is needed for this stage II colon cancer
Skip MSI/dMMR testing because the pathology is low risk
Proceed with routine postoperative observation only
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer a clean stage II adjuvant decision because the indeterminate liver lesions raise concern for occult metastatic disease. The safer interpretation is to clarify staging with appropriate imaging review/workup before applying stage II postoperative recommendations.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendations apply to resected stage II colon cancer without additional findings that change staging or treatment intent. Possible liver metastases make the case non-concordant with the source rule context, so routine stage II adjuvant guidance should not be applied yet.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 stage II colon cancer with clear margins, 18 lymph nodes examined, no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. He has recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and discussing postoperative management. The pathology report also notes that the tumor is mismatch repair proficient, and this result was obtained before the postoperative visit.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the vignette is unchanged, but the presence of an MSI/dMMR result means the patient is already being considered in a biomarker-guided adjuvant pathway rather than the low-risk stage II scenario where testing would be unnecessary.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients
Avoid any MSI/dMMR testing because the patient has stage II disease without high-risk features
Dismiss the molecular result as irrelevant and observe only
recommended_actions: Perform MSI/dMMR testing for stage II patients for whom adjuvant chemotherapy is being considered.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not concordant with the source recommendation because MSI/dMMR status is already available and adjuvant chemotherapy is actively being considered. In that setting, biomarker testing is relevant to decision-making rather than unnecessary.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation against MSI/dMMR testing applies only when a stage II patient without high-risk features does not require adjuvant chemotherapy. Here, chemotherapy is being considered and the tumor has already been profiled, so the testing-related rule no longer matches.

selected_rules.activation_condition: No clinical recommendations are present in the provided text
selected_rules.execute: Do not extract any treatment, staging, or diagnostic action from this text
selected_rules.source_statement: Recommendations Section 2: Recommendations and Key Evidence Section 3: Guideline Methods Overview Section 4: Systematic Review Section 5: Internal and External Review

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document contains only administrative and copyright/contact information
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action should be derived from the document content
selected_rules.source_statement: For information about this document, please contact Alex Sun, the lead author, through the PEBC via: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

selected_rules.activation_condition: Text provides report citation and website/contact details only
selected_rules.execute: Do not apply any guideline-based clinical management rule
selected_rules.source_statement: For information about the PEBC and the most current version of all reports, please visit the CCO website at http://www.cancercare.on.ca or contact the PEBC office at: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

selected_rules.activation_condition: Text states copyright and reproduction restrictions only
selected_rules.execute: No clinical recommendation can be extracted from this statement
selected_rules.source_statement: Copyright This report is copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario; the report and the illustrations herein may not be reproduced without the express written permission of Cancer Care Ontario.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is seen in clinic for follow-up after a recent oncology visit. He asks whether he should start any new treatment based on the document he was given, but the handout contains only report citation information, website details, and contact information for the guideline office. There are no clinical findings, diagnoses, staging details, or treatment recommendations included in the text he brought. He has no new symptoms and is mainly seeking clarification about what the document means for his care.
recommended_actions: Do not apply any guideline-based clinical management rule from this document.
Do not derive any clinical action from the report citation, website, or contact details alone.
source_case.rationale: The provided text contains only report citation and website/contact details, with no clinical recommendations or management guidance. This triggers the no-recommendation rule for citation/contact-only content, so no treatment, staging, or diagnostic action should be extracted.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man returns for follow-up after a recent oncology visit. He brings a printed document and asks whether it changes his treatment plan. The handout includes a short recommendations section that says his disease is metastatic colorectal cancer with an ECOG performance status of 0, and it lists a specific systemic therapy option along with monitoring advice. However, the page also clearly states that the recommendations apply only to patients with RAS wild-type tumors. This patient’s prior molecular testing showed a KRAS exon 2 mutation. He has no new symptoms and is otherwise clinically stable.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette still centers on a follow-up oncology handout with guideline-like language, but a single biomarker detail makes the named treatment recommendation inapplicable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start the systemic therapy listed in the handout
Apply the guideline recommendation directly to his current treatment plan
Proceed with management based on the document’s recommendation section
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not guideline-concordant for the cited treatment recommendation because the document’s therapy applies to RAS wild-type disease, while this patient has a KRAS mutation. The safer interpretation is to avoid applying that specific recommendation and instead use biomarker-appropriate metastatic colorectal cancer management.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although the vignette resembles a guideline-based oncology follow-up, the key eligibility condition is not met. The source rule set only contains no-recommendation/document-content rules, and this modified case includes genuine clinical content plus a biomarker mismatch, so the citation/contact-only rule no longer applies.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is seen in clinic after receiving a cancer care handout at a prior visit. He asks whether he should begin the treatment described in the document. The page contains a concise recommendation for first-line therapy in non-squamous metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, and it mentions maintenance treatment after induction. On closer review, the handout specifies that the recommendation is for patients with stage IV disease and no prior systemic therapy. This patient’s imaging instead shows a localized right upper lobe mass with mediastinal nodes confined to the chest, and he is being staged for potentially curative chemoradiation. He has not yet received any cancer-directed treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting still looks like a routine oncology handout review, but the disease stage has shifted from metastatic to potentially curable locoregional disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Initiate the first-line metastatic regimen named in the handout
Follow the maintenance strategy described in the document
Apply the cited lung cancer recommendation to his current staging workup
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The recommendation is not guideline-concordant here because the cited therapy is intended for stage IV metastatic disease, whereas this patient has localized thoracic disease being evaluated for curative-intent treatment. The safer interpretation is to complete staging and manage according to locoregional lung cancer pathways rather than apply the metastatic recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The modified vignette preserves the guideline-follow-up context but changes a decisive eligibility factor: stage. Because the document’s treatment recommendation is restricted to metastatic disease, it should not be applied to a patient with potentially resectable/locoregional disease. No other supplied no-recommendation rule fits this case.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has erectile dysfunction alone
selected_rules.execute: Do not use erectile dysfunction alone as a symptom prompting DRE and PSA testing for metastatic prostate cancer
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group did not think it necessary for a man with erectile dysfunction to undergo a DRE and PSA test and therefore excluded it as a symptom of metastatic prostate cancer

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has unexplained hematuria
selected_rules.execute: Do not treat unexplained hematuria as part of the prostate cancer care algorithm; arrange urologic assessment as appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group also excluded unexplained hematuria as a symptom of metastatic prostate cancer... hematuria requires urologic assessment but is not part of a prostate cancer care algorithm

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has symptoms of metastatic prostate cancer and is age 40 or older
selected_rules.execute: Perform digital rectal examination (DRE) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) testing
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with symptoms of metastatic prostate cancer should have a DRE and PSA was endorsed

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS)
selected_rules.execute: Perform DRE
selected_rules.source_statement: a man with LUTS should have a DRE

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has LUTS and PSA testing is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Exclude urinary infection before PSA testing
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group also endorsed the recommendations to exclude urinary infection before PSA testing

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has LUTS and PSA testing is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Postpone PSA testing for at least one month after treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group also endorsed the recommendations to... postpone PSA testing for at least one month after treatment

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has an abnormal incidental PSA result
selected_rules.execute: Perform DRE
selected_rules.source_statement: if an incidental PSA test was abnormal, then standard practise would be to perform a DRE

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has LUTS
selected_rules.execute: Discuss PSA testing and consider PSA based on individual risk assessment
selected_rules.source_statement: a man with LUTS should have a DRE and a discussion about PSA testing

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He has no known urinary infection symptoms and has not had any recent treatment for urinary issues. On review, his main concern is whether he might have prostate disease because of the persistent urinary symptoms. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. His exam is unremarkable aside from mild suprapubic fullness; no prior PSA testing has been done.
recommended_actions: Perform a digital rectal examination (DRE).
Discuss PSA testing and consider PSA based on individual risk assessment.
source_case.rationale: The vignette includes lower urinary tract symptoms in a man aged over 40, which triggers the recommendation for DRE. LUTS also supports a discussion about PSA testing with consideration of individual risk. No features of metastatic prostate cancer, abnormal incidental PSA, erectile dysfunction alone, or unexplained hematuria were included, so those rules were not triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He reports a brief episode of urinary frequency and suprapubic discomfort 2 weeks ago that resolved after a short course of antibiotics from urgent care. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. On review, his main concern is whether he might have prostate disease because of the persistent urinary symptoms. His exam is unremarkable aside from mild suprapubic fullness; no prior PSA testing has been done.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on a man over 40 with persistent LUTS and no prior PSA testing, but a recent treated urinary infection makes immediate PSA-based evaluation less straightforward.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Perform a digital rectal examination and discuss PSA testing now because of the lower urinary tract symptoms.
Order PSA testing at this visit without considering the recent urinary infection.
recommended_actions: Perform a digital rectal examination (DRE).
Exclude urinary infection before PSA testing.
Postpone PSA testing for at least one month after treatment.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a LUTS presentation, so DRE is appropriate, but PSA testing should not be rushed because he recently had a urinary infection treated only 2 weeks ago. The safer interpretation is to defer PSA until at least a month after treatment and ensure infection has been excluded.
challenging_examples.rationale: LUTS still triggers DRE, but the recent treated urinary infection activates the PSA-related precautions. Because PSA is being considered, infection should be excluded and testing postponed after treatment rather than done immediately.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. He had a PSA measured at a workplace health fair last week, and the result was mildly elevated at 5.8 ng/mL. On review, his main concern is whether he might have prostate disease because of the persistent urinary symptoms. His exam is unremarkable aside from mild suprapubic fullness.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like a routine LUTS evaluation in a man over 40, but the key change is that PSA has already been obtained and is abnormal.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Perform a digital rectal examination and discuss PSA testing based on his urinary symptoms.
Repeat or initiate PSA testing as part of the initial LUTS workup.
recommended_actions: Perform a digital rectal examination (DRE).
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This no longer fits the recommendation to discuss or order PSA based on LUTS alone, because PSA has already been measured and is abnormal. The appropriate next step is DRE for the incidental abnormal PSA, while the prior PSA discussion rule is no longer the relevant trigger.
challenging_examples.rationale: An abnormal incidental PSA triggers DRE. The original LUTS-based PSA discussion is no longer the key action because PSA has already been done and is not merely being considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer requiring or choosing active treatment and eligible for treatment
selected_rules.execute: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and/or radical prostatectomy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with low-risk prostate cancer who require or choose active treatment, LDR alone, EBRT alone, or RP should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy and eligible for brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer brachytherapy boost using low-dose rate or high-dose rate brachytherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT), brachytherapy boost (LDR or high-dose rate should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed high-risk prostate cancer receiving EBRT and androgen-deprivation therapy and eligible for brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer brachytherapy boost using low-dose rate or high-dose rate brachytherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with high-risk prostate cancer receiving EBRT and ADT, brachytherapy boost (LDR or HDR) should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving low-dose rate brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use iodine-125 or palladium-103 as reasonable isotope options.
selected_rules.source_statement: 125 I and 103 Pd are each reasonable isotope options for patients receiving LDR brachytherapy;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with prostate cancer is being managed under this guideline
selected_rules.execute: Encourage participation in clinical trials testing novel or targeted approaches.
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients should be encouraged to participate in clinical trials to test novel or targeted approaches to this disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being counseled about management options
selected_rules.execute: Counsel about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients should be counseled about all their management options (surgery, EBRT, active surveillance, as applicable) in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.

selected_rules.activation_condition: brachytherapy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: Perform brachytherapy at a center following strict quality-assurance standards.
selected_rules.source_statement: Brachytherapy should be performed at a center following strict quality-assurance standards.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has intermediate-risk prostate cancer and is choosing EBRT with or without ADT
selected_rules.execute: Offer brachytherapy boost with LDR or HDR to eligible patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT), brachytherapy boost (LDR or high-dose rate should be offered to eligible patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma presents after biopsy and staging show low-risk disease: PSA 5.2 ng/mL, Gleason 3+3=6, and cT1c exam. He has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years and, after discussing his options, says he prefers active treatment rather than surveillance because of significant anxiety about living with untreated cancer. He is otherwise healthy and eligible for local therapy. In clinic, he asks about surgery versus radiation and wants a clear explanation of the available approaches before deciding. The team reviews his case in a multidisciplinary setting and discusses the main treatment choices with him in a balanced way.
recommended_actions: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and radical prostatectomy as treatment options for this eligible patient with low-risk prostate cancer.
Counsel the patient about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
source_case.rationale: This vignette fits newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer requiring or choosing active treatment in an eligible patient, triggering the recommendation to offer LDR brachytherapy alone, EBRT alone, and/or radical prostatectomy. It also involves counseling about management options, supporting a balanced multidisciplinary discussion.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma returns after biopsy and staging. His PSA is 5.8 ng/mL and Gleason score is 3+3=6, but MRI and exam now show a palpable right-apical lesion consistent with cT2a disease. He remains otherwise healthy with a life expectancy well beyond 10 years and wants active treatment because he is anxious about surveillance. He asks for a clear comparison of surgery and radiation before deciding. The case is reviewed in clinic, and the team discusses treatment choices in a balanced way. He has no urinary obstructive symptoms and is eligible for local therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like newly diagnosed localized prostate cancer in a healthy man considering definitive treatment, but the exam/MRI finding upgrades him from low-risk cT1c to a palpable cT2a lesion.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and radical prostatectomy as standard options for low-risk disease.
Present the management choices in a balanced multidisciplinary discussion and frame active surveillance as the main alternative.
recommended_actions: Encourage participation in clinical trials testing novel or targeted approaches.
Counsel the patient about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer low-risk prostate cancer because the palpable/MRI-visible lesion makes the clinical stage cT2a, so the specific low-risk treatment recommendation is not triggered. The safer interpretation is localized prostate cancer requiring standard risk-appropriate counseling; the general counseling and clinical-trial encouragement recommendations still apply.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer. Changing cT1c to cT2a removes low-risk status. R005 and R006 still apply for a patient with prostate cancer being managed under the guideline and receiving counseling.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man is diagnosed with prostate adenocarcinoma after biopsy for rising PSA. He has Gleason 3+4=7 disease, PSA 11.2 ng/mL, and cT2b exam findings, placing him in intermediate-risk disease. He is otherwise fit and wants definitive therapy. After discussion with radiation oncology, he is leaning toward external beam radiation therapy and asks whether a brachytherapy boost would improve outcomes. On review, his prior transurethral resection of the prostate was only 3 weeks ago, and the radiation oncologist notes the anatomy is not yet suitable for implant planning. He is frustrated but agrees to continue discussing options. The team reviews the case in clinic and explains the available treatments carefully.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The disease appears to be intermediate-risk prostate cancer with a patient choosing EBRT, but a very recent TURP makes brachytherapy boost inappropriate at this time.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer an LDR or HDR brachytherapy boost with EBRT because he is intermediate-risk and wants definitive treatment.
Proceed with brachytherapy eligibility planning and recommend boost treatment during the current radiation course.
recommended_actions: Encourage participation in clinical trials testing novel or targeted approaches.
Counsel the patient about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case resembles intermediate-risk disease choosing EBRT, but recent TURP means he is not currently eligible for brachytherapy boost, so the brachytherapy recommendation is not guideline-concordant. The safer interpretation is to continue risk-appropriate counseling and consider non-brachytherapy definitive options while reassessing local suitability later if needed.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 and R008 require intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with eligibility for brachytherapy. The recent TURP creates ineligibility for implant-based boost right now. R005 and R006 remain applicable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering Doppler technology alone for adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Do not use Doppler technology alone to evaluate the mass.
selected_rules.source_statement: Evaluation of an adnexal mass by Doppler technology alone is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering serum CA-125 as a stand-alone test
selected_rules.execute: Do not use CA-125 alone to distinguish benign from malignant adnexal masses.
selected_rules.source_statement: As a stand-alone modality, serum CA-125 is not recommended for distinguishing between benign and malignant adnexal masses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult woman with a suspicious isolated ovarian mass and ultrasound is technically feasible
selected_rules.execute: Use transvaginal sonography as the first imaging modality.
selected_rules.source_statement: Transvaginal sonography should be the first modality of choice, where technically feasible, in patients with a suspicious, isolated ovarian mass.

selected_rules.activation_condition: need to differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses
selected_rules.execute: Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging for differentiation.
selected_rules.source_statement: Sonography, particularly three-dimensional (3D) sonography, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and computerized tomography (CT) imaging are each recommended for differentiating malignant from benign ovarian masses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: ultrasound may be unreliable
selected_rules.execute: Use MRI to help clarify malignant potential.
selected_rules.source_statement: MRI is the most appropriate test to help clarify the malignant potential in patients where ultrasound may be unreliable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: extra-ovarian disease is suspected or needs to be ruled out
selected_rules.execute: Use CT imaging to assess for extra-ovarian disease.
selected_rules.source_statement: CT is most useful in cases where extra ovarian disease is suspected or needs to be ruled out.

selected_rules.activation_condition: using Doppler technology in adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Combine Doppler technology with morphological assessment.
selected_rules.source_statement: Doppler technology should be combined with a morphological assessment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering morphological scoring systems for adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Use a morphological scoring system to differentiate benign from malignant masses.
selected_rules.source_statement: Morphological scoring systems can be used to differentiate benign from malignant adnexal masses.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests a fullness in the left adnexa, and a recent office ultrasound was limited by overlying bowel gas, so the mass is not well characterized. She has no known prior ovarian disease. The clinician is considering the next imaging step to clarify whether this isolated ovarian mass is benign or malignant. Because the initial ultrasound is technically unreliable, additional imaging is needed to better define malignant potential. There is no clear evidence yet of extra-ovarian spread, but the adnexal lesion remains suspicious and incompletely assessed.
recommended_actions: Use MRI to help clarify the malignant potential because ultrasound may be unreliable.
Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging to help differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a suspicious isolated ovarian/adnexal mass with technically unreliable ultrasound, which triggers MRI as the most appropriate clarifying test. It also involves differentiating malignant from benign ovarian masses, supporting use of sonography, MRI, or CT for characterization.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests a fullness in the left adnexa, and transvaginal ultrasound clearly shows a 4.8 cm complex left ovarian mass with papillary projections and internal vascularity, but no ascites or other pelvic abnormalities. The sonographer notes that image quality is excellent and the lesion is well characterized. The clinician is considering the next imaging step to clarify whether this isolated ovarian mass is benign or malignant. Because the ultrasound is technically reliable and the mass has already been characterized, the team debates whether additional cross-sectional imaging would add value before referral planning.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has a suspicious isolated ovarian mass and the same symptom pattern, but the ultrasound is now technically adequate and fully characterizes the lesion.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order MRI to clarify the malignant potential of the adnexal mass.
Obtain CT imaging to further differentiate benign from malignant ovarian disease.
Escalate imaging because the mass remains suspicious on ultrasound.
recommended_actions: Use transvaginal sonography as the first imaging modality, where technically feasible.
Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging for differentiation only if further characterization is needed.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not concordant with the MRI-after-unreliable-ultrasound recommendation because the ultrasound was technically reliable and already characterized the mass. The safer interpretation is that MRI is not the next guideline-driven step solely to clarify malignant potential in this setting.
challenging_examples.rationale: R005 requires unreliable ultrasound. Here the ultrasound is high quality and diagnostic, so that trigger is absent. The case still fits general initial characterization guidance for ovarian masses, but not the specific recommendation to use MRI because ultrasound may be unreliable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests a fullness in the left adnexa, and a recent office ultrasound was limited by overlying bowel gas, so the mass is not well characterized. She has no known prior ovarian disease. However, a contrast CT abdomen/pelvis obtained two days earlier for unrelated abdominal pain already shows a 3.2 cm simple left ovarian cyst with no solid components, no peritoneal nodularity, and no suspicious lymphadenopathy. The clinician is considering the next imaging step to clarify whether this isolated ovarian mass is benign or malignant. The question is whether the poorly seen ultrasound still justifies more imaging despite the reassuring cross-sectional study.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The ultrasound is still limited and the presenting symptoms are nearly identical, but a recent CT has already characterized the adnexal finding as benign-appearing.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order MRI to clarify malignant potential because the ultrasound was unreliable.
Proceed with additional cross-sectional imaging for the adnexal mass.
Treat the lesion as incompletely assessed despite the recent CT.
recommended_actions: Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging for differentiation of malignant from benign ovarian masses.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not concordant with the MRI recommendation for unreliable ultrasound because the lesion has already been characterized on CT as a simple cyst without suspicious features. The safer interpretation is that MRI is not indicated just to clarify malignant potential when a recent CT has already lowered concern.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although ultrasound was limited, the key modification is that CT has already demonstrated a benign-appearing simple cyst. That removes the practical need for MRI as the most appropriate clarifying test based on unreliable ultrasound alone. General differentiation guidance still applies in principle, but the specific MRI trigger does not.

selected_rules.activation_condition: individual uses indoor tanning devices
selected_rules.execute: avoid use of indoor tanning devices to reduce melanoma risk
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of indoor tanning devices should be avoided to reduce risk of melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: individual is considering or using indoor tanning devices
selected_rules.execute: avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially at younger age
selected_rules.source_statement: All individuals should avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially those at a younger age.

selected_rules.activation_condition: individual is exposed to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices
selected_rules.execute: recognize that there is no safe lower limit of exposure
selected_rules.source_statement: There is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 19-year-old woman presents for a routine visit and mentions that she has been using a tanning bed at a salon twice weekly for the past 3 months to “get a base tan” before spring break. She says several friends started tanning in high school, and she has been considering increasing her sessions because she likes the color it gives her skin. She has no current skin complaints and no personal history of skin cancer, but she is unaware of any specific risks associated with indoor tanning. She asks whether occasional tanning bed use is safe if she does not burn.
recommended_actions: Avoid use of indoor tanning devices to reduce melanoma risk.
Avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially at a younger age.
Recognize that there is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a young individual who is currently using and considering increased use of indoor tanning devices, which triggers the rules advising avoidance. Because she is exposed to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices, the no-safe-lower-limit statement also applies. The recommendations are limited to avoidance and counseling about risk, as specified by the triggered rules.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 19-year-old woman presents for a routine visit and mentions that she used a tanning bed at a salon twice weekly during the past 3 months to “get a base tan” before spring break. She says several friends started tanning in high school, and she has been thinking about restarting a few sessions later in the season because she likes the color it gives her skin. She has no current skin complaints and no personal history of skin cancer, but she adds that she now plans to avoid indoor tanning because she recently learned about melanoma risk. She asks whether her past occasional tanning bed use was safe since she never burned.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting, age, and recent tanning history closely mirror the source, but the patient is no longer currently using or considering indoor tanning.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Counsel her to avoid tanning beds to reduce melanoma risk.
Tell her there is no safe lower limit of exposure to indoor tanning devices.
Provide standard indoor tanning risk counseling for an active user.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the indoor tanning avoidance recommendations because the patient is describing prior use and is currently planning to stop, not current use or active consideration of continued tanning. The safer interpretation is brief preventive counseling about past exposure and general skin protection rather than applying the active-use recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rules require current use, consideration of use, or exposure from indoor tanning devices in the present vignette. Here, the exposure is historical and the patient states she plans to avoid future tanning, so the avoidance recommendation does not actively trigger.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 23-year-old woman comes for a routine visit and asks about tanning beds because she has been using one at a salon twice weekly for the past month to “build a base tan” before an upcoming beach trip. She has no skin complaints and no personal history of skin cancer. She says her sister uses the same salon and has never burned, so she assumes occasional tanning is harmless. On review, she also reports a few recent episodes of sunburn from outdoor beach volleyball on weekends and wonders if the tanning bed helps her avoid burning outside.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The core indoor tanning scenario is nearly unchanged, but she is no longer a younger teen and the age-related emphasis is less central.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Advise her to stop using tanning beds to reduce melanoma risk.
Emphasize that indoor tanning is especially harmful at younger ages.
Explain that a few tanning sessions are safe if she does not burn.
recommended_actions: Recognize that there is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case still involves active indoor tanning exposure, so the no-safe-lower-limit counseling applies. However, the age-focused recommendation is less directly triggered because she is an adult, not a younger adolescent. The safest interpretation is to counsel that any artificial UVR exposure from tanning devices carries risk and should not be considered safe.
challenging_examples.rationale: Active indoor tanning triggers the no-safe-lower-limit statement. The special emphasis on younger age is not well matched here because the patient is 23, so that part is not the best fit for this modified vignette.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has cytogenetically normal AML
selected_rules.execute: Include DNMT3A mutation testing as a biomarker test
selected_rules.source_statement: DNMT3A mutation testing should be included as a biomarker test in cytogenetically normal AML patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis shows a normal karyotype, and there is no prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome or chemotherapy exposure. He is being evaluated at initial diagnosis for prognostic and treatment-planning biomarkers.
recommended_actions: Include DNMT3A mutation testing as a biomarker test in this patient with cytogenetically normal AML.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes newly diagnosed AML with cytogenetically normal disease, which directly activates the rule recommending DNMT3A mutation testing as part of biomarker evaluation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis shows an isolated t(8;21) translocation with otherwise no prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome or chemotherapy exposure. He is being evaluated at initial diagnosis for prognostic and treatment-planning biomarkers.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation is nearly identical to newly diagnosed AML at baseline biomarker workup, but the karyotype is not normal.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order DNMT3A mutation testing as part of the initial biomarker panel for this AML diagnosis.
Proceed with the standard cytogenetically normal AML biomarker pathway.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is newly diagnosed AML, but the cytogenetics are not normal because an isolated t(8;21) is present. The specific recommendation for DNMT3A testing in cytogenetically normal AML is therefore not triggered. Biomarker evaluation should follow the appropriate workup for this non-normal cytogenetic subtype.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies only to cytogenetically normal AML. A defining cytogenetic abnormality removes the case from that indication.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis is pending, but the marrow report already notes a complex karyotype and TP53 immunostaining is strongly abnormal. He is being evaluated after receiving one cycle of induction chemotherapy at an outside hospital for biomarker clarification and treatment planning.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The leukemia context and biomarker-planning language remain the same, but this is no longer an initial-diagnosis cytogenetically normal AML scenario.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add DNMT3A mutation testing now as part of the initial AML biomarker assessment.
Treat this like a newly diagnosed cytogenetically normal AML workup.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although the patient has AML, the case no longer fits the target scenario: the disease is not cytogenetically normal, and he is no longer at the initial diagnosis point. The guideline statement for DNMT3A testing in cytogenetically normal AML is not applicable here; evaluation should be guided by the abnormal cytogenetic/molecular profile and post-induction context.
