challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires initial-diagnosis cytogenetically normal AML. Both conditions are altered here, so the recommendation does not apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with newly diagnosed multiple myeloma being considered for initial transplant-based treatment
selected_rules.execute: Offer autologous stem cell transplantation as part of the initial treatment plan.
selected_rules.source_statement: Autologous SCT is the recommended treatment option for patients with newly diagnosed MM, as part of the initial treatment plan.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed MM patient is free from severe comorbidities and younger than 65 years after initial high-dose chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer a single autologous stem cell transplant.
selected_rules.source_statement: Evidence included in four Clinical Practice Guidelines (CPGs) (1)(2)(3)(4) suggests that a single transplant with autologous SCT should be offered to all MM patients who are free from severe co-morbidities and who are younger than 65 years of age, following the initial treatment with high-dose chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient did not achieve complete remission after initial autologous SCT
selected_rules.execute: Offer a second autologous stem cell transplant.
selected_rules.source_statement: Evidence included in two CPGs (1,2) suggests that double autologous SCT should be offered to patients who did not achieve a complete remission after their initial autologous SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient relapses after a long remission following a single transplant
selected_rules.execute: Offer a second autologous transplant.
selected_rules.source_statement: ...the SCT Steering Committee's consensus opinion is that patients who relapse after a long remission following a single transplant should be offered a second transplant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed MM patient is age 65 or older but has good performance status and no comorbidities that contraindicate transplantation
selected_rules.execute: Consider offering autologous stem cell transplantation.
selected_rules.source_statement: The SCT Steering Committee acknowledged that, while the evidence upon which the CPGs were based almost uniformly excluded patients older than age 65, there was no reason not to offer SCT to patients 65 or older who have good performance status and no co-morbidities that would be a contraindication to transplantation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with MM responds to first autologous transplant with less than a very good partial response and has no progressive disease
selected_rules.execute: Consider tandem (double) autologous stem cell transplantation.
selected_rules.source_statement: Tandem (double) autologous SCT is an option for patients with MM who respond to the first autologous transplant with less than a very good partial response, but not progressive disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has high-risk MM
selected_rules.execute: Consider allogeneic transplantation, preferably within an investigative study.
selected_rules.source_statement: Allogeneic transplantation is an option for patients with high-risk MM preferably within the context of an investigative study.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with high-risk MM is younger than 50 years and is not expected to benefit from autologous SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider allogeneic stem cell transplantation only in an investigative setting.
selected_rules.source_statement: ...it may be offered to patients <50 years of age who are not expected to benefit from autologous SCT (e.g., chromosome 13 deletion) within the investigative setting only (1,2,4).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is newly diagnosed with symptomatic multiple myeloma after presenting with anemia, lytic bone lesions, and elevated serum protein. He has good functional status, walks daily, and has no major comorbidities. Baseline renal function and cardiac evaluation are acceptable, and he is being evaluated after induction therapy with high-dose chemotherapy for transplant-based treatment. He asks whether transplant should be part of his initial plan. He has not yet undergone any stem cell transplant and has no history of relapse or refractory disease.
recommended_actions: Offer autologous stem cell transplantation as part of the initial treatment plan.
Offer a single autologous stem cell transplant after initial high-dose chemotherapy, given that he is under 65 and free of severe comorbidities.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches a newly diagnosed adult with multiple myeloma being considered for initial transplant-based treatment, and he is also younger than 65 with no severe comorbidities after high-dose chemotherapy. That triggers the recommendations to include autologous stem cell transplantation in the initial plan and to offer a single autologous transplant.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with newly diagnosed symptomatic multiple myeloma presents with anemia, lytic bone lesions, and elevated serum protein. He has good functional status, walks daily, and has no major comorbidities. Baseline renal function and cardiac evaluation are acceptable. He completed induction therapy and high-dose chemotherapy, and is now being evaluated for transplant-based treatment. He asks whether transplant should be part of his initial plan. He has not yet undergone any stem cell transplant and has no history of relapse or refractory disease. Cytogenetics now show del(17p) with otherwise standard performance status.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a straightforward transplant-eligible newly diagnosed myeloma patient, but the added high-risk cytogenetics change the guideline pathway.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer autologous stem cell transplantation as part of the initial treatment plan.
Offer a single autologous stem cell transplant after high-dose chemotherapy.
recommended_actions: Consider allogeneic transplantation, preferably within an investigative study.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer a routine initial autologous transplant scenario because the presence of high-risk myeloma shifts the applicable recommendation. The safer interpretation is to consider allogeneic transplantation only in the investigative context rather than defaulting to standard autologous SCT.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 and R002 are invalidated because the vignette no longer cleanly supports the standard autologous recommendation; the added high-risk disease feature activates the separate high-risk myeloma pathway. R007 applies because high-risk MM is present.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man was diagnosed with symptomatic multiple myeloma 10 months ago after presenting with anemia, lytic bone lesions, and elevated serum protein. He completed induction therapy followed by a single autologous stem cell transplant. He recovered well and has good functional status with no major comorbidities. At follow-up, his marrow and labs show a very good partial response, and there is no evidence of progressive disease. He asks whether he should receive another transplant now to improve long-term control.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case retains the transplant-focused workflow and the same disease context, but the patient is now post-transplant with a VGPR rather than being pre-transplant or inadequately responding.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer a second autologous stem cell transplant.
Proceed with tandem autologous transplantation because he is fit and under 65.
Treat the current status as an indication to intensify transplant therapy further.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette does not meet the criteria for the initial-transplant recommendations, and it also does not meet the criterion for tandem autologous transplant because the response is very good partial response rather than less than VGPR. With no progressive disease and no relapse after a long remission, there is no triggered transplant recommendation here.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 and R002 are invalidated because the patient is no longer in the initial-treatment decision point. R003 is invalidated because tandem autologous transplant is only suggested when response is less than VGPR, which is not the case here. No other supplied rule applies.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Screening is being offered outside a centre with experience and expertise in high-risk identification, interpretation, counselling, and diagnostic techniques
selected_rules.execute: Do not provide screening in that setting.
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group believes strongly that screening outside a centre with experience and expertise in identifying the high-risk population, interpreting results and counselling patients, and performing the appropriate diagnostic techniques is ill advised.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Screening is being offered in a setting without comprehensive care
selected_rules.execute: Do not offer screening in that setting.
selected_rules.source_statement: The ASCO guideline also supports screening of high-risk individuals, but only in the settings that can deliver comprehensive care such as that provided to NLST participants.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Person is being screened based on other risk factors alone
selected_rules.execute: Do not expand eligibility solely on the basis of those other risk factors.
selected_rules.source_statement: there is currently insufficient evidence to include participants based on other risk factors such as family history, passive smoking, occupational exposure, radon exposure, previous cancer, and other diseases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Person is high risk for lung cancer and eligible for screening
selected_rules.execute: Screen with low-dose CT (LDCT).
selected_rules.source_statement: Screening for lung cancer with LDCT is recommended in high-risk populations defined as persons 55 to 74 years of age with a minimum smoking history of ≥30 pack-years who currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years and are disease free at the time of screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Person is 55 to 74 years old, has at least 30 pack-years smoking history, currently smokes or quit within past 15 years, and is disease free
selected_rules.execute: Offer LDCT screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: Screening for lung cancer with LDCT is recommended in high-risk populations defined as persons 55 to 74 years of age with a minimum smoking history of ≥30 pack-years who currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years and are disease free at the time of screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Screening program is being designed or delivered
selected_rules.execute: Use an organized, population-based screening program rather than ad hoc opportunistic screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: it is the opinion of the Working Group that screening should be conducted in a manner similar to the NLST trial: that is, in an organized fashion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Screening program is implemented
selected_rules.execute: Include smoking-cessation counseling in the program.
selected_rules.source_statement: The inclusion of smoking-cessation counseling within the screening program is crucial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Screening program is implemented
selected_rules.execute: Build in data collection, monitoring, quality assurance, and evaluation from the start.
selected_rules.source_statement: If elements of data collection and monitoring, quality assurance, and evaluation are built into the screening program from the start, it can be modified while in operation.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man presents to a community clinic asking for “a quick lung cancer scan” because his brother recently died of lung cancer. He has a 22 pack-year smoking history and quit 18 years ago. He feels well, has no respiratory symptoms, and has no known cancer diagnosis. The clinic is a small rural practice without a formal lung cancer screening pathway, specialist counseling support, or access to structured follow-up and diagnostic services. The patient asks whether he should be screened based on his family history and past smoking. The clinician is considering offering low-dose CT screening during the visit.
recommended_actions: Do not provide lung cancer screening in this setting because it lacks the experience and expertise needed for high-risk identification, interpretation, counselling, and diagnostic techniques.
Do not expand screening eligibility solely on the basis of family history or other non-smoking risk factors.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers R001 because screening is being offered outside a centre with the required expertise and diagnostic support. It also triggers R003 because the patient is requesting screening based on family history and past smoking, but he does not meet the evidence-based high-risk criteria; the rules state there is insufficient evidence to include people based on other risk factors alone.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man comes to a suburban primary care clinic asking about “the lung scan my brother had” after his brother died of lung cancer. He has a 34 pack-year smoking history and quit 10 years ago. He feels well, has no cough, dyspnea, hemoptysis, or weight loss, and has no known cancer diagnosis. The clinic recently started a small lung cancer screening initiative, but it is still ad hoc: there is no organized registry, no formal smoking-cessation counseling pathway, and no standardized follow-up plan for indeterminate findings or specialty referral. The clinician is considering ordering a low-dose CT today because the patient appears to meet typical age and smoking thresholds.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The age, smoking exposure, quit interval, and disease-free status closely resemble a standard screening candidate, but the screening workflow lacks the required organized infrastructure and comprehensive follow-up.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order an immediate low-dose CT lung cancer screening study in clinic
Enroll him in the new screening initiative and arrange routine annual screening
Proceed with screening because he meets the usual age and smoking thresholds
recommended_actions: Do not provide lung cancer screening in this ad hoc setting without the required organized program elements and follow-up infrastructure.

If screening is pursued, it should be done only within an organized, population-based program that includes smoking-cessation counseling, data collection, monitoring, quality assurance, and structured diagnostic follow-up.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for immediate opportunistic screening because the setting lacks the organized program features and comprehensive care required for safe delivery. Although the patient meets high-risk smoking criteria, the safer interpretation is that screening should be deferred until he can be enrolled in an appropriate structured program.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient satisfies the age, pack-year, and quit-time criteria for high-risk screening, so R004/R005 are present. However, the vignette removes the organized-program requirement and the supporting elements emphasized in R006-R008, making bedside ad hoc screening discordant.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman asks her family physician for a lung cancer screening CT after her sister was recently diagnosed with lung cancer. She has never smoked, but she reports years of secondhand smoke exposure from her spouse and a brief period working in a nail salon with chemical fumes. She has no respiratory symptoms and no prior cancer history. The clinic is a well-resourced academic center with a formal screening program, structured follow-up, and smoking-cessation counseling available. Because of her family history and exposure history, she wants to know whether she qualifies for annual low-dose CT screening.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The family history and non-smoking exposures make the request seem risk-based, and the screening program itself is appropriately organized and comprehensive.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer annual low-dose CT screening because she has a strong family history and passive smoke exposure
Place her into the lung cancer screening registry based on environmental risk factors
Proceed with screening now since the center has a formal program
recommended_actions: Do not expand lung cancer screening eligibility solely on the basis of family history, passive smoking, or occupational exposure in this patient.
Provide counseling about lung cancer risk reduction and document that she does not meet evidence-based high-risk screening criteria.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette remains discordant because, despite being in an appropriate screening center, the patient is not eligible for LDCT on the basis of non-smoking risk factors alone. The safer interpretation is risk counseling without screening enrollment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The organized-setting requirements are satisfied, so R002 and program-related supportive rules apply. However, she lacks the high-risk smoking profile required for LDCT, and the guideline explicitly says not to include people based on family history or other non-smoking risk factors alone, so R003 is invalidated and no screening recommendation is triggered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Primary diagnosis of pancreatic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for primary diagnosis.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for primary diagnosis of pancreatic cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Evaluating treatment response outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET to evaluate treatment response outside a clinical trial.
selected_rules.source_statement: The above results are based on two small nonrandomized studies and therefore are not strong enough to make a recommendation for using PET in evaluating treatment response outside of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Suspected recurrence of pancreatic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for clinical management of suspected recurrence.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for clinical management of suspected recurrence, nor for restaging at the time of recurrence, due to insufficient evidence and lack of effective therapeutic options.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Restaging at the time of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for restaging at recurrence.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for clinical management of suspected recurrence, nor for restaging at the time of recurrence, due to insufficient evidence and lack of effective therapeutic options.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Recurrent disease after surgical resection
selected_rules.execute: Restrict PET imaging to clinical trials.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET imaging in recurrent disease should be restricted to clinical trials.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is a candidate for potentially curative surgical resection based on conventional staging
selected_rules.execute: Perform PET for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging if a patient is a candidate for potentially curative surgical resection as determined by conventional staging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Suspicious pancreatic mass and standard investigations are not confirmatory
selected_rules.execute: Consider PET/CT to help establish the diagnosis.
selected_rules.source_statement: These pooled LRs suggest that PET and PET/CT offer small benefit in ruling in and ruling out pancreatic cancer when investigating a suspicious pancreatic mass; therefore, they may be useful in establishing a diagnosis when standard investigations are not confirmatory.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy is inconclusive or not possible and diagnosis remains in doubt
selected_rules.execute: Use PET/CT if a positive result would lead to surgical resection for diagnosis and treatment.
selected_rules.source_statement: When biopsy is inconclusive or not possible and the diagnosis remains in doubt, the above evidence supports the use of PET/CT where a positive result would lead to surgical resection for purposes of both diagnosis and treatment.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with 3 months of epigastric pain, 8 kg unintentional weight loss, and new jaundice. Contrast CT shows a 2.8 cm mass in the pancreatic head with mild biliary dilation, but the lesion is still indeterminate on imaging. Endoscopic ultrasound-guided biopsy is attempted twice and is nondiagnostic due to scant tissue. He has no known metastatic disease, and the surgical team is considering whether he could be a candidate for potentially curative resection if the diagnosis is confirmed. The oncology team asks whether PET/CT should be used next to help clarify the diagnosis and guide management.
recommended_actions: Use PET/CT if a positive result would lead to surgical resection for diagnosis and treatment.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches suspected pancreatic cancer with inconclusive biopsy and persistent diagnostic uncertainty. Under these circumstances, PET/CT may be used if a positive result would change management by supporting surgical resection. No other rule is triggered because the case is not a confirmed primary diagnosis, not a recurrence, and not explicitly a preoperative staging scenario after conventional staging has already established resectability.

selected_rules.activation_condition: low rectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use TRUS to predict CRM involvement.
selected_rules.source_statement: TRUS should not be used to predict CRM involvement.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering changing preoperative therapy selection criteria
selected_rules.execute: Do not change selection criteria from T and N categories to distance to the mesorectal fascia, extramural depth of invasion, or extramural vascular invasion based on current evidence.
selected_rules.source_statement: To date, there is insufficient evidence to change the current selection criteria from T and N categories to distance to the MRF (i.e., potential CRM), extramural depth of invasion (EMD) and/or extramural vascular invasion (EMVI).

selected_rules.activation_condition: all rectal cancer patients undergoing elective treatment
selected_rules.execute: Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis.
selected_rules.source_statement: Staging for all rectal cancer patients should include: CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all rectal cancer patients undergoing elective treatment
selected_rules.execute: Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: Staging for all rectal cancer patients should include: CT of the chest or chest X-ray.

selected_rules.activation_condition: CT chest is used for postoperative surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Do CT of the chest at the same time as staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis.
selected_rules.source_statement: If CT of the chest is used for postoperative surveillance, then CT of the chest should be done at the same time as staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: chest X-ray is used for postoperative surveillance and chest X-ray shows abnormalities needing further workup
selected_rules.execute: Do CT of the chest.
selected_rules.source_statement: If chest X-ray is used for postoperative surveillance, then CT of the chest is recommended only if abnormalities requiring further investigation were found on chest X-ray.

selected_rules.activation_condition: CT chest is performed together with CT abdomen and pelvis
selected_rules.execute: Use intravenous contrast.
selected_rules.source_statement: When CT of the chest is performed in combination with CT of the abdomen and pelvis, intravenous contrast is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: preoperative complete colonic examination is possible
selected_rules.execute: Perform colonoscopy before surgery.
selected_rules.source_statement: Complete colonic examination by colonoscopy should be performed preoperatively, if possible.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man presents with intermittent rectal bleeding and tenesmus. Colonoscopy shows a biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma in the low rectum, 5 cm from the anal verge. He is being evaluated for elective curative treatment and has no bowel obstruction, so a complete preoperative colonic examination is feasible. The surgical team is planning staging imaging before deciding on neoadjuvant therapy and resection strategy. The tumor is discussed as potentially close to the mesorectal fascia, but no prior imaging has yet been obtained. The patient asks whether transrectal ultrasound can determine whether the circumferential resection margin is involved.
recommended_actions: Do not use TRUS to predict CRM involvement.
Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis for staging.
Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
Perform colonoscopy before surgery, if complete colonic examination is possible.
source_case.rationale: The vignette includes low rectal cancer, which triggers the recommendation against using TRUS to predict CRM involvement. Because he is undergoing elective treatment for rectal cancer, staging should include CT of the abdomen and pelvis and chest staging with CT of the chest or chest X-ray. Since a complete colonic examination is possible preoperatively, colonoscopy before surgery is recommended.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man presents with intermittent rectal bleeding and tenesmus. Colonoscopy shows a biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma in the low rectum, 5 cm from the anal verge. He is being evaluated for elective curative treatment, and there is no bowel obstruction, so a complete preoperative colonic examination is feasible. The surgical team is planning staging imaging before deciding on neoadjuvant therapy and resection strategy. On pelvic MRI obtained yesterday, the tumor is described as abutting but not clearly breaching the mesorectal fascia, with no definite threatened circumferential resection margin. The patient asks whether transrectal ultrasound can determine whether the circumferential resection margin is involved.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case remains a low rectal cancer undergoing elective staging with the same question about CRM assessment, but pelvic MRI has already been obtained and does not describe definite CRM involvement.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use transrectal ultrasound to predict circumferential resection margin involvement.
Rely on TRUS to refine local staging for CRM status before treatment planning.
recommended_actions: Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis for staging.
Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
Perform colonoscopy before surgery.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains low rectal cancer, but the CRM question is no longer the key actionable issue because pelvic MRI has already provided local staging and does not show definite threatened CRM. TRUS should not be used to predict CRM involvement. The safer approach is standard elective rectal cancer staging with abdominal/pelvic and chest imaging, plus preoperative colonoscopy if the colon can be completely examined.
challenging_examples.rationale: The modification preserves low rectal cancer and elective management but changes the local staging context so the CRM prediction request is no longer guideline-concordant for TRUS. Other rectal cancer staging recommendations still apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man presents with intermittent rectal bleeding and tenesmus. Colonoscopy shows a biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma in the low rectum, 5 cm from the anal verge. He is being evaluated for elective curative treatment, but he has a near-obstructing sigmoid stricture from a separate benign diverticular disease that prevents passage of the scope beyond the descending colon. The surgical team is planning staging imaging before deciding on neoadjuvant therapy and resection strategy. The tumor is discussed as potentially close to the mesorectal fascia, and the patient asks whether transrectal ultrasound can determine whether the circumferential resection margin is involved.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation and rectal cancer workup are almost unchanged, but the colon cannot be completely examined preoperatively because the scope cannot traverse a separate distal colonic stricture.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use transrectal ultrasound to predict circumferential resection margin involvement.
Proceed with preoperative colonoscopy despite the incomplete examination being technically impossible.
Skip additional staging because the rectal lesion is already biopsy-proven.
recommended_actions: Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis for staging.
Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case still represents low rectal cancer, so TRUS should not be used to predict CRM involvement. However, the recommendation for preoperative colonoscopy does not apply because a complete colonic examination is not feasible due to the separate obstructing stricture. Standard staging imaging for rectal cancer remains appropriate.
challenging_examples.rationale: Low rectal cancer continues to trigger the recommendation against TRUS for CRM prediction and the need for staging CT of the abdomen/pelvis and chest staging. The preoperative colonoscopy recommendation is invalidated by the inability to complete the exam.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: Do not use induction chemotherapy as standard care; treat it as investigational only.
selected_rules.source_statement: At this time, induction CT before concurrent CT and RT should be considered an investigational approach.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal after chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: Do not give maintenance chemotherapy after chemoradiation.
selected_rules.source_statement: James et al. 2013 (ACT II), studied maintenance chemotherapy versus none following chemoradiation and found that maintenance chemotherapy does not improve overall survival or colostomy-free survival. Therefore, maintenance chemotherapy following chemoradiation is not recommended in the management of squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal and consideration of radiotherapy alone
selected_rules.execute: Avoid radiotherapy alone when concurrent chemoradiation is feasible.
selected_rules.source_statement: For all stages of localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal, concurrent CT and RT is recommended over RT alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with biopsy-proven localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal
selected_rules.execute: Use concurrent chemotherapy and radiotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.
selected_rules.source_statement: For all stages of localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal, concurrent CT and RT is recommended over RT alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal receiving concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: Use 5-fluorouracil plus mitomycin C as the concurrent chemotherapy regimen with radiation.
selected_rules.source_statement: The optimal CT drug combination for squamous cell cancer of the anal canal is 5fluorouracil (5FU) plus mitomycin C (MMC), given concurrently with radiation treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal who is HIV-positive
selected_rules.execute: Manage the patient the same way as a patient without known HIV, while monitoring for potentially higher toxicity.
selected_rules.source_statement: It is the expert opinion of the Gastrointestinal Cancer Disease Site Group (GI DSG) that HIV-positive patients with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal should be managed in the same way as patients without known HIV. Treating physicians should be aware that a greater than average risk of toxicity is possible.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal and need to choose a mitomycin C schedule
selected_rules.execute: No preferred mitomycin C dosing schedule can be recommended from the available data.
selected_rules.source_statement: In the trials using MMC in the 5FU-MMC combination regimens, MMC schedules include dose of 12 or 15mg/me day 1 only, and a 10mg/m2 Day 1, 29 dosing. There is no comparative data to allow a recommendation of a preferred schedule.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal presents for treatment planning. Staging CT shows no distant metastases, and he is otherwise fit with good renal and marrow function. He asks whether he can start with a short course of chemotherapy before radiation to shrink the tumor first. The oncology team is also discussing whether radiation alone might be simpler, but he is able to tolerate combined treatment. He is HIV-negative and has no major comorbidities. The team plans definitive therapy in the coming weeks and is deciding on the concurrent regimen.
recommended_actions: Use concurrent chemotherapy and radiotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.
Do not use induction chemotherapy as standard care; treat it as investigational only.
source_case.rationale: The patient has localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal and is being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiation, which triggers the recommendation against induction therapy as standard care. Because he is biopsy-proven localized disease and can tolerate combined treatment, concurrent chemoradiation is recommended over radiotherapy alone.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal presents for treatment planning. Staging CT shows no distant metastases, and he is otherwise fit with good renal and marrow function. He asks whether he can start with a short course of chemotherapy before radiation to shrink the tumor first. The oncology team is also discussing whether radiation alone might be simpler, but he is able to tolerate combined treatment. He is HIV-negative and has no major comorbidities. However, endoscopic staging and pelvic MRI now show the lesion is a small T1 tumor confined to the anal margin rather than the anal canal, and the planned approach is local excision with close follow-up rather than definitive chemoradiation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still sounds like localized anal squamous cancer with a discussion of induction chemotherapy versus radiation-only treatment, but the primary site has shifted to the anal margin and the tumor is being managed with local excision instead of definitive chemoradiation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start concurrent chemoradiation with 5-fluorouracil and mitomycin C
Avoid radiotherapy alone and proceed with definitive combined chemoradiation
Do not use induction chemotherapy as standard care
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the anal canal chemoradiation recommendations because the modified case is a small T1 anal margin lesion being planned for local excision, not biopsy-proven localized anal canal cancer requiring definitive concurrent chemoradiation. The safer interpretation is that the anal canal-specific recommendations do not apply here.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule set applies to localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal. Moving the lesion to the anal margin and changing the intent to local excision removes the trigger for concurrent chemoradiation and for the induction-chemotherapy question in this source context.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal presents for treatment planning. Staging CT shows no distant metastases, and he is otherwise fit with good renal and marrow function. He asks whether he can start with a short course of chemotherapy before radiation to shrink the tumor first. The oncology team is also discussing whether radiation alone might be simpler, but he is able to tolerate combined treatment. He is HIV-negative and has no major comorbidities. On review of outside pathology, however, the lesion is not squamous histology but invasive adenocarcinoma arising from the distal rectum extending to the anal verge, and the team is considering rectal-cancer-based staging and treatment rather than anal-canal chemoradiation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still includes a lower anorectal malignancy near the anal verge with a discussion of chemotherapy before radiation, but the histology has changed away from anal squamous cell carcinoma.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use concurrent radiation with 5-fluorouracil and mitomycin C
Avoid radiation alone and proceed with standard anal-cancer chemoradiation
Do not give induction chemotherapy before definitive treatment
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The anal squamous-cell guideline does not apply because the modified diagnosis is distal rectal adenocarcinoma, not squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal. Management should follow rectal/colorectal cancer pathways rather than the anal-cancer chemoradiation recommendations.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation is specific to squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal. Changing the histology to adenocarcinoma invalidates the anal-cancer regimen and the advice against induction chemotherapy in this setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all institutions administering hazardous drugs
selected_rules.execute: form a committee responsible for policies and procedures for hazardous drugs
selected_rules.source_statement: It is strongly recommended that all institutions administering hazardous drugs form such a committee.

selected_rules.activation_condition: committee for hazardous drugs is formed
selected_rules.execute: include representatives from occupational health and safety, joint health and safety committee, pharmacy, nursing, medical oncology, environmental services, risk management, and a patient representative
selected_rules.source_statement: It is also strongly recommended that this committee include, but not be limited to, representatives from various departments and services such as: occupational health and safety, joint health and safety committee, pharmacy, nursing, medical oncology (physician), environmental services, risk management, and a patient representative.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hazardous drug policies and procedures are being developed or revised
selected_rules.execute: use clear processes for developing, reviewing, and revising policies and procedures
selected_rules.source_statement: This committee would be responsible for clear processes of developing, reviewing, and revising policies and procedures related to hazardous drugs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hazardous drug program oversight is in place
selected_rules.execute: routinely conduct risk assessment and gap analysis
selected_rules.source_statement: A risk assessment and gap analysis should be routinely conducted to identify gaps and to inform policies and procedures.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hazardous drug staff orientation and education are needed
selected_rules.execute: ensure a process for orientation and ongoing education for the target population
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, this committee is responsible for ensuring that there is a process in place for orientation and ongoing education for the identified target population.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hazardous drug risk prevention management program is in place
selected_rules.execute: implement and follow up the Risk Prevention Management Program
selected_rules.source_statement: This committee is responsible for implementation and follow-up of the Risk Prevention Management Program related to the use of hazardous drugs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: staff are involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit
selected_rules.execute: provide initial and ongoing hospital-approved education including safe handling and spill or leak management
selected_rules.source_statement: It is legislated that initial and ongoing hospital-approved education be provided to all staff involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit including safe handling and spill or leak management (6).

selected_rules.activation_condition: staff work with hazardous drugs
selected_rules.execute: provide initial and ongoing training on best practice standards
selected_rules.source_statement: It is strongly recommended that all staff have initial and ongoing training related to best practice standards in place at the time.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is admitted to an oncology unit for her next cycle of chemotherapy. The unit recently expanded outpatient infusion services, and the pharmacy director notes that staff from pharmacy, nursing, and environmental services all handle hazardous drugs at different points in the medication circuit. During a quality review, several nurses report that spill response steps are not consistently taught during onboarding, and new float staff are uncertain about safe handling procedures. The oncology service is planning to update its hazardous-drug workflow and wants to reduce variation in practice across shifts. No medication error has occurred, but the team recognizes that the current process depends heavily on informal teaching and individual experience.
recommended_actions: Provide initial and ongoing hospital-approved education for all staff involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit, including safe handling and spill or leak management.
Provide initial and ongoing training for all staff working with hazardous drugs on current best-practice standards.
source_case.rationale: This vignette involves staff handling hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit and identifies gaps in onboarding and spill management teaching, which triggers the requirement for hospital-approved education. It also describes staff working with hazardous drugs and needing standardized preparation, which triggers ongoing training on best-practice standards.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is admitted to an oncology unit for her next cycle of chemotherapy. The unit has recently added a small satellite infusion area, and pharmacy, nursing, and environmental services staff all handle hazardous drugs at different points in the medication circuit. During a quality review, several nurses report that spill response steps are taught on the unit, but only after staff have already started independent shifts, and new float staff are still unsure about safe handling procedures. The oncology service is planning to standardize its hazardous-drug workflow and wants to reduce variation in practice across shifts. No medication error has occurred, but the team recognizes that current teaching is inconsistent and dependent on informal bedside coaching.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting still involves hazardous-drug handling across the medication circuit and mentions inconsistent staff teaching, but the education is described as already occurring in a partial, informal way rather than meeting the specific hospital-approved, initial-and-ongoing requirement.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Update the chemotherapy education package with spill and leak management content for all staff handling hazardous drugs.
Roll out a formal onboarding and annual training program for pharmacy, nursing, and environmental services staff on hazardous-drug best practices.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This version does not clearly establish the guideline trigger for mandatory hospital-approved education because the issue is not absence of a program but inconsistent timing and informal bedside coaching. The safer interpretation is that the unit needs local workflow improvement, but the vignette no longer cleanly supports the specific education recommendation as written.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original case triggered education requirements because staff throughout the medication circuit lacked consistent onboarding and spill training. Here, training is already occurring in a limited form, making the need less clearly a guideline-based mandate rather than a local process gap.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is admitted to an oncology unit for her next cycle of chemotherapy. The unit recently expanded outpatient infusion services, and pharmacy, nursing, and environmental services staff all handle hazardous drugs at different points in the medication circuit. During a quality review, several nurses report that spill response steps are not consistently taught during onboarding, and new float staff are uncertain about safe handling procedures. The oncology service is preparing to update its hazardous-drug workflow and wants to reduce variation in practice across shifts. However, the pharmacy director notes that the current process only covers medications classified as nonhazardous, while hazardous drugs are still being dispensed and administered through a separate external contractor. No medication error has occurred, but the team recognizes that the internal workflow depends heavily on informal teaching and individual experience.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The clinical context and workflow issues are nearly identical, but the hazardous-drug handling described is not actually occurring within the institution’s own medication circuit because those drugs are managed by an external contractor.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Implement hospital-approved hazardous-drug education for all onsite staff involved in the circuit.
Initiate ongoing staff training on safe handling and spill response for the oncology unit team.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Because hazardous drugs are not being handled by the institution’s own staff in the described circuit, the explicit education requirements are not clearly triggered for this unit. The safer interpretation is that the institution should clarify contractor responsibilities and internal oversight, rather than apply the staff-training recommendation directly to this vignette.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case appears similar to the source but changes the operational detail so the hazardous-drug circuit is managed externally. That removes the direct applicability of the staff education and training recommendations for the onsite team.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has glioma and PET is being considered to determine diagnosis or grading
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for diagnosis or grading in gliomas.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for the determination of diagnosis or grading in gliomas.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has glioma and PET is being considered to add diagnostic accuracy beyond MRI and histology
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET to improve diagnostic accuracy or prognostic assessment beyond MRI and histology.
selected_rules.source_statement: none have demonstrated any additional diagnostic accuracy or prognostic influence over and above that provided by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and histology in a multivariate model.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with glioblastoma multiforme has suspected radiation necrosis versus tumour recurrence and PET/CT is being considered
selected_rules.execute: PET/CT may be considered as anecdotal evidence suggests it may differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence, recognizing the lack of a gold standard.
selected_rules.source_statement: Anecdotal evidence exists that PET/CT may differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence, but there is no gold standard for the diagnosis of radiation necrosis in glioblastoma multiforme.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with glioma is being assessed for treatment response using PET
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made for or against PET for treatment response assessment.
selected_rules.source_statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET for the assessment of treatment response in gliomas due to insufficient evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has recurrent glioma and PET or PET/CT is being considered for assessment
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made for or against PET or PET/CT in recurrent gliomas.
selected_rules.source_statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET or PET/CT in the assessment of patients with recurrent gliomas due to insufficient evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has recurrent glioma and FDG PET is being considered
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made for FDG PET in recurrent gliomas because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence was insufficient to generate a recommendation on the use of FDG PET.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with recurrent glioma is being evaluated and PET/CT before and after treatment is proposed to correlate with survival
selected_rules.execute: Do not assume evidence supports prospective PET/CT assessment before and after treatment for survival correlation; evidence is lacking.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET or PET/CT has not been examined in a prospective cohort of gliomas to assess the treatment effect on PET imaging before and after treatment and correlate this with survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with recurrent glioma is being assessed and radiation necrosis is a major diagnostic issue
selected_rules.execute: Recognize radiation necrosis as a major factor in assessing recurrent gliomas; no specific PET recommendation is provided.
selected_rules.source_statement: Radiation necrosis is a major factor in assessing recurrent gliomas.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a prior diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme completed chemoradiation 10 months ago. He now reports worsening headaches and mild word-finding difficulty. MRI shows a new enhancing lesion near the prior resection cavity, but the findings are equivocal for either tumor recurrence or radiation necrosis. His oncology team is considering PET/CT to help clarify the diagnosis before changing therapy. He has no fever, seizures, or other signs of infection, and his neurologic exam is otherwise stable. The main question is whether the new imaging abnormality represents recurrent tumor or treatment-related necrosis.
recommended_actions: PET/CT may be considered because anecdotal evidence suggests it may help differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence in glioblastoma multiforme.
Interpret any PET/CT result cautiously, recognizing that there is no gold standard for diagnosing radiation necrosis in glioblastoma multiforme.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches glioblastoma multiforme with suspected radiation necrosis versus tumour recurrence, which triggers the conditional consideration for PET/CT. The recommendation is limited to cautious consideration because the supporting evidence is anecdotal and there is no gold standard for radiation necrosis.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old woman with an anaplastic astrocytoma underwent gross total resection 18 months ago and completed adjuvant chemoradiation 8 months ago. She returns with gradually worsening short-term memory and a subtle left pronator drift. MRI demonstrates a new 1.8-cm ring-enhancing lesion adjacent to the prior resection bed, along with several smaller nonenhancing T2/FLAIR changes in the surrounding white matter. Her neuro-oncologist is considering PET/CT to help determine whether this represents recurrent tumor or treatment-related change before changing therapy. She has no fever, seizures, or meningismus, and the rest of her exam is stable. The clinical question is whether the new imaging abnormality is recurrent glioma or radiation necrosis.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case keeps the same post-treatment glioma workflow and nearly identical imaging concern, but the tumor is an anaplastic astrocytoma rather than glioblastoma multiforme and the recurrence setting is more general than the specific radiation-necrosis question.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order PET/CT to help distinguish radiation necrosis from recurrent tumor.
Use the PET/CT result to guide whether salvage therapy should be started.
Interpret the scan as a useful discriminator between recurrence and treatment effect.
recommended_actions: No recommendation can be made for or against PET or PET/CT in recurrent gliomas.
Recognize radiation necrosis as a major factor in assessing recurrent gliomas; no specific PET recommendation is provided.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is a recurrent glioma scenario, but it is not the specific glioblastoma multiforme radiation-necrosis versus recurrence situation covered by the conditional PET/CT consideration. The safer interpretation is that PET/CT cannot be endorsed or rejected here on the basis of the supplied guideline, and management should rely on standard neuro-oncologic assessment and follow-up.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves suspected recurrence after chemoradiation, but the histology is anaplastic astrocytoma rather than glioblastoma multiforme. That removes the conditional PET/CT consideration for differentiating radiation necrosis from recurrence. The recurrent-glioma rules still apply, yielding no recommendation or a general recognition of radiation necrosis as a factor.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old man with glioblastoma multiforme completed radiation with concurrent temozolomide 11 months ago and has been clinically stable until this month, when he develops new headaches and mild aphasia. MRI shows a new nodular enhancing focus near the prior cavity, but the radiologist feels the appearance is more consistent with early tumor progression than treatment effect because there is no surrounding pattern typical of radiation necrosis. His oncology team asks whether PET/CT could clarify the diagnosis before switching therapy. He is afebrile, has no seizures, and his neurologic examination is otherwise unchanged. The main question is whether this represents recurrent tumor requiring treatment escalation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has glioblastoma after chemoradiation with a new enhancing lesion near the operative bed, but the imaging impression no longer centers on radiation necrosis versus recurrence.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with PET/CT to sort out recurrence from treatment change.
Use PET/CT to decide whether to change therapy now.
Rely on PET/CT as the next step in evaluating the new enhancement.
recommended_actions: No recommendation can be made for or against PET or PET/CT in recurrent gliomas.
Do not assume evidence supports prospective PET/CT assessment before and after treatment for survival correlation; evidence is lacking.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although this is recurrent glioblastoma, the lesion is being interpreted primarily as probable progression rather than an equivocal radiation-necrosis question. The specific conditional PET/CT consideration is therefore not met. The safer interpretation is that PET/CT remains unsupported for routine recurrent-glioma assessment, and treatment decisions should not depend on it.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule requires suspected radiation necrosis versus tumor recurrence. Here, the lesion is described as more consistent with early progression and not an equivocal necrosis-vs-recurrence problem. That invalidates the conditional PET/CT recommendation, while the recurrent-glioma no-recommendation framework remains applicable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: NSCLC patient has not had mutation testing or is only clinically selected by ethnicity, sex, histology, or smoking status
selected_rules.execute: Do not use first-line EGFR TKI therapy based on clinical selection alone
selected_rules.source_statement: First-line therapy with an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) is not recommended in unselected (patients who have not undergone mutation testing) or clinically selected populations of patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: NSCLC patient is being considered for first-line EGFR TKI based on Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology, or light/never smoking status
selected_rules.execute: Do not use these clinical characteristics to select patients for first-line EGFR TKI therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The use of clinical characteristics such as Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology and light/never smoking status is not recommended to select patients for first-line EGFR TKI therapy, as this strategy does not reliably select patients who have mutations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has progressed after chemotherapy and previously received gefitinib or erlotinib
selected_rules.execute: Do not use a second EGFR TKI such as afatinib because evidence is insufficient and no overall survival improvement is shown
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to recommend the use of a second EGFR TKI, such as afatinib, in patients whose disease has progressed following chemotherapy and gefitinib or erlotinib, as available data does not demonstrate any improvement in overall survival

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is receiving high-dose gefitinib rather than standard-dose gefitinib
selected_rules.execute: Avoid higher-dose gefitinib because grade 3 or 4 toxicity is higher and ILD-type events occurred only with 500 mg/day in one trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Two randomized phase II trials ... identified that grade 3 or 4 toxicity was higher with the higher dose gefitinib. Interstitial lung disease-type events occurred in only one of the two trials, and only with 500 mg/day gefitinib.

selected_rules.activation_condition: NSCLC patient has confirmed EGFR mutation-positive disease
selected_rules.execute: Use first-line EGFR TKI therapy instead of platinum-based chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with EGFR mutation-positive NSCLC, first-line therapy with an EGFR TKI such as gefitinib, erlotinib or afatinib is the preferred treatment compared to platinum-based therapies.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has progressed after platinum-based chemotherapy and is well enough to consider second-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use an EGFR TKI as second- or third-line therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients well enough to consider second-line chemotherapy, an EGFR TKI can be recommended as second-or third-line therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for maintenance therapy after four cycles of platinum-doublet chemotherapy and has not progressed
selected_rules.execute: Use an EGFR TKI as maintenance therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: An EGFR TKI is recommended as an option for maintenance therapy in patients who have not progressed after four cycles of a platinum-doublet chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: NSCLC patient has confirmed EGFR mutation-positive disease and an EGFR TKI is being chosen
selected_rules.execute: Choose among gefitinib, erlotinib, or afatinib based on expected toxicity and cost because no one agent is supported over another
selected_rules.source_statement: There is no evidence to support one EGFR TKI over another, so the decision about which EGFR TKI to use should take into consideration the expected toxicity of the drug as well as the cost.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with newly diagnosed metastatic non-small cell lung cancer is referred after biopsy shows adenocarcinoma. She is a never-smoker and had been presumed to be a good candidate for an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor based on her sex, histology, and smoking history. However, her molecular testing has not yet been performed, and no EGFR mutation status is available. Her oncologist is considering starting gefitinib immediately because of her clinical profile, before mutation results return. She is otherwise stable, with no major organ dysfunction and no prior systemic therapy.
recommended_actions: Do not use first-line EGFR TKI therapy based on clinical selection alone.
Do not use Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology, or light/never smoking status to select the patient for first-line EGFR TKI therapy.
Obtain mutation testing before choosing EGFR TKI therapy.
source_case.rationale: The patient has NSCLC without EGFR mutation testing results, and the proposed EGFR TKI would be chosen solely on clinical characteristics. This matches rules against using unselected or clinically selected populations for first-line EGFR TKI therapy. Because mutation status is unknown, the correct action is to avoid selecting therapy on clinical features alone and to wait for molecular testing.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with newly diagnosed metastatic non-small cell lung cancer is referred after biopsy shows adenocarcinoma. She is a never-smoker and had been presumed to be a good candidate for an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor based on her sex, histology, and smoking history. Molecular testing has now returned, showing no activating EGFR mutation. Her oncologist is considering starting gefitinib because she has no major organ dysfunction and has not received prior systemic therapy. She is clinically stable, with no urgent need for cytotoxic treatment and no evidence of brain metastases.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a classic never-smoker woman with metastatic adenocarcinoma, but the returned molecular result is EGFR wild-type rather than mutation-positive disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start first-line gefitinib based on the patient’s sex, never-smoking status, and adenocarcinoma histology
Choose an EGFR TKI immediately because the clinical profile is highly suggestive of EGFR-driven disease
Use gefitinib as the preferred upfront treatment instead of waiting for further confirmation
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for first-line EGFR TKI therapy because the patient’s EGFR testing is negative. Clinical features alone should not drive EGFR TKI selection, and mutation-negative disease does not meet the indication for first-line EGFR-targeted treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation depended on either absent mutation testing or confirmed EGFR mutation-positive disease. Here, testing is available and negative, so the rule supporting first-line EGFR TKI no longer applies.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer and biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma has progressed after four cycles of platinum-doublet chemotherapy. He remains symptomatic but is still ambulatory and able to consider further systemic therapy. His oncologist is discussing an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor in the second-line setting because the disease is now worsening. However, prior molecular testing identified an activating EGFR exon 19 deletion, and he has never previously received an EGFR TKI. He has mild renal insufficiency but no other major contraindications to treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The workflow still involves metastatic NSCLC after platinum chemotherapy with consideration of an EGFR TKI, but the patient is EGFR mutation-positive and TKI-naive rather than an unselected post-chemotherapy case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use an EGFR TKI as second-line therapy because he has progressed after platinum chemotherapy
Select gefitinib, erlotinib, or afatinib as a later-line option for post-chemotherapy progression
Delay targeted therapy until additional cytotoxic chemotherapy has been tried
recommended_actions: Use first-line EGFR TKI therapy instead of platinum-based chemotherapy.
Choose among gefitinib, erlotinib, or afatinib based on expected toxicity and cost because no one agent is supported over another.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case does not support the post-platinum second-line EGFR TKI recommendation because the patient has confirmed EGFR mutation-positive disease and has not yet received targeted therapy. The safer interpretation is that an EGFR TKI is appropriate as EGFR-directed treatment, with agent selection based on toxicity and cost.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R006 applies to patients considered for second-line chemotherapy after platinum, but the presence of an activating EGFR mutation shifts the case into the mutation-positive upfront targeted-therapy pathway. The post-chemotherapy second-line recommendation is therefore not the best fit.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient phenotype matches the known spectrum for the gene
selected_rules.execute: Do not use phenotype match alone as evidence for pathogenicity.
selected_rules.source_statement: In general, the fact that a patient has a phenotype that matches the known spectrum of clinical features for a gene is not considered evidence for pathogenicity given that nearly all patients undergoing diseasetargeted tests have the phenotype in question.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Applying variant classification rules to a GUS
selected_rules.execute: Do not use variant classification rules developed for recognized genotype-phenotype associations.
selected_rules.source_statement: Special care must be taken when applying the recommended guidelines to a GUS. In such situations, utilizing variant classification rules developed for recognized genotype-phenotype associations is not appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering whether to replicate the ACMG/AMP guideline independently
selected_rules.execute: Do not invest extensive effort to replicate the ACMG/AMP guideline when endorsement is sufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: Further, the Working Group felt that investing extensive effort to replicate the ACMG/AMP guideline would not be justified given the number of experts involved in its creation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using public databases for variant interpretation
selected_rules.execute: Check how often the database is updated, whether curation is supported, and what curation methods were used.
selected_rules.source_statement: When using databases, clinical laboratories should (i) determine how frequently the database is updated, whether data curation is supported, and what methods were used for curation;

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using public databases for variant interpretation
selected_rules.execute: Confirm Human Genome Variation Society nomenclature and identify the genome build and transcript references used for naming variants.
selected_rules.source_statement: (ii) confirm the use of Human Genome Variation Society nomenclature and determine the genome build and transcript references used for naming variants;

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using public databases for variant interpretation
selected_rules.execute: Assess whether the data are validated for analytical accuracy and review any quality metrics, including associated publications if needed.
selected_rules.source_statement: (iii) determine the degree to which data are validated for analytical accuracy (e.g., low-pass next-generation sequencing versus Sanger-validated variants) and evaluate any quality metrics that are provided to assess data accuracy, which may require reading associated publications;

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using public databases for variant interpretation
selected_rules.execute: Determine the source of the observations and whether the observations are independent.
selected_rules.source_statement: and (iv) determine the source and independence of the observations listed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Allele frequency in a control population is greater than expected for a rare Mendelian disorder
selected_rules.execute: Use this as strong support for a benign interpretation (BS1).
selected_rules.source_statement: In general, an allele frequency in a control population that is greater than expected for the disorder is considered strong support for a benign interpretation for a rare Mendelian disorder (BS1)

source_case.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old woman is referred for interpretation of a heterozygous missense variant found on a hereditary cardiomyopathy panel after she developed exertional dyspnea and mild left ventricular hypertrophy on echocardiogram. The laboratory notes that her clinical picture fits the known spectrum for the gene, but the variant is also listed in a public database with several submissions and a relatively high allele frequency in a general population dataset. The report does not clearly state the genome build, transcript used, or whether the database entries were curated or independently observed. The cardiologist asks whether the phenotype alone is enough to support pathogenicity and whether the public database evidence is sufficient to classify the variant.
recommended_actions: Do not use the phenotype match alone as evidence for pathogenicity.
When using public databases, check how often the database is updated, whether curation is supported, and what curation methods were used.
Confirm Human Genome Variation Society nomenclature and identify the genome build and transcript references used for naming the variant.
Determine the source of the observations and whether the observations are independent.
Because the allele frequency in a control population is greater than expected for a rare Mendelian disorder, use this as strong support for a benign interpretation (BS1).
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers rules about phenotype matching, public database evaluation, and allele frequency. The phenotype is compatible with the gene’s spectrum, but that alone should not be treated as evidence for pathogenicity. The database details are incomplete, so the relevant metadata and data quality should be checked before relying on it. The elevated population frequency supports a benign interpretation under BS1.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 36-year-old woman is referred after a hereditary cardiomyopathy panel identified a heterozygous missense variant in a sarcomeric gene. She has exertional dyspnea and mild concentric left ventricular hypertrophy on echocardiogram, and the ordering cardiologist notes that her findings are consistent with the known clinical spectrum for the gene. The variant is listed in a public database with multiple submissions and appears uncommon, but the report includes a genome build and transcript that do not match the laboratory’s current reference sequence. The database page also shows no clear statement about curation methods, and several entries appear to be derived from the same laboratory cohort rather than independent observations. The cardiologist asks whether the phenotype match is sufficient to support pathogenicity and whether the database entries can be relied on for classification.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves a cardiomyopathy panel, a missense variant, phenotype overlap, and a public database, but the allele-frequency clue supporting BS1 has been removed.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat the phenotype match as supporting evidence for pathogenicity.
Accept the public database entries at face value and use them to upgrade the variant.
Classify the variant using the listed database submissions without checking the transcript or whether the observations are independent.
recommended_actions: Do not use phenotype match alone as evidence for pathogenicity.
When using public databases, check how often the database is updated, whether curation is supported, and what curation methods were used.
Confirm Human Genome Variation Society nomenclature and identify the genome build and transcript references used for naming variants.
Determine the source of the observations and whether the observations are independent.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The phenotype is compatible with the gene, but that does not itself count as pathogenic evidence. The public database information is incomplete and internally mismatched, so the safer interpretation is to verify nomenclature, transcript/build, curation, and independence before using the entries for classification.
challenging_examples.rationale: Phenotype overlap alone should not be used to infer pathogenicity. The database is present, but the metadata are insufficient or inconsistent, so the relevant database-quality checks remain appropriate. Because no control-population frequency greater than expected is provided, BS1 is not triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man with exertional dyspnea is evaluated for possible hereditary cardiomyopathy after echocardiography shows mild asymmetric septal hypertrophy. A panel identifies a heterozygous missense variant in a gene known to cause hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and the laboratory notes that his phenotype falls within the recognized spectrum for that gene. The variant is also present in a public database with several submissions, but the report states that the entries come from a manually curated source with clearly described methods, the nomenclature is HGVS-compliant, and the genome build and transcript are specified. On closer review, however, the database frequency is low and not obviously higher than expected for the disorder, and the submitter descriptions do not indicate whether the observations were independent. The cardiologist asks whether the phenotype match and database presence are enough to call the variant pathogenic or benign.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This remains a cardiomyopathy variant-interpretation scenario with a phenotype match and public database entries, but the key frequency evidence is absent and the database details are already well documented.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the phenotype match to support pathogenicity.
Rely on the database presence to downgrade the variant or treat it as benign.
Apply BS1 based on the variant being listed in a public control database.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case does not meet the benign-frequency criterion because the variant is not shown to be more common than expected for the disorder. Although the database metadata are adequate, the phenotype match still should not be used as pathogenic evidence, and the presence of the variant in a database alone does not justify reclassification.
challenging_examples.rationale: The only clearly applicable rule from the source set that would have driven a recommendation is BS1, but the modified case removes the required elevated population frequency. Phenotype match remains non-evidentiary, yet the database-check items are already satisfied rather than needing action, so no supplied rule is actively triggered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has esophageal cancer and is a potential candidate for curative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use PET to improve the accuracy of M staging
selected_rules.source_statement: For the staging workup of patients with esophageal cancer who are potential candidates for curative therapy, PET is recommended to improve the accuracy of M staging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has esophageal cancer and staging workup is being done
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for its incremental value in detecting distant disease in addition to CT +/- endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)
selected_rules.source_statement: There is a significant role for PET for its incremental value in detecting distant disease, in addition to CT +/-endoscopic ultrasound (EUS).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has esophageal cancer and EUS is not available
selected_rules.execute: Use PET because it provides incremental benefit when EUS is absent
selected_rules.source_statement: Especially in the absence of EUS, PET provides an incremental benefit.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has newly diagnosed esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider PET/CT for nodal staging because it may be superior to PET alone
selected_rules.source_statement: There is some evidence that PET/CT is superior to PET alone for nodal staging (Yuan et al, 2006.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has newly diagnosed esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use PET or PET/CT to improve clinical management, prognostic stratification, prediction of regional and locoregional lymph nodes, and pretreatment staging accuracy compared with CT and EUS alone
selected_rules.source_statement: also showed the significant impact of PET and PET/CT on the clinical management, prognostic stratification of patients with newly diagnosed esophageal cancer, prediction of regional and locoregional lymph nodes, and improvement on the accuracy of pretreatment staging compared to CT and EUS alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: PET is used early in treatment or at completion of neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use PET as a possible predictor of complete pathologic response and longer-term survival or event-free survival
selected_rules.source_statement: There is some evidence that PET, either early in treatment or at the completion of neoadjuvant therapy, can predict complete pathologic response, and therefore, predict the longer-term outcome in terms of survival and event-free survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Assessing response after neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider PET as it may be superior to CT and comparable to EUS for assessment of response and prognosis
