Provide psychoeducation about depression in cancer and normalize discussion of symptoms.
Inform her about the impact of depression on quality of life and cancer outcomes.
Destigmatize depression as a serious condition requiring treatment.
Review for medical contributors to her symptoms, including thyroid dysfunction and B12, folate, or iron deficiency if clinically indicated.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a near-miss because her symptoms are described as mild and she is still functioning relatively well, so routine antidepressant initiation is not guideline-concordant. Her pain is controlled and there is no meaningful uncontrolled symptom burden to drive the recommendation for physical symptom optimization. The safer interpretation is to assess, educate, and monitor rather than treat presumptively with an antidepressant.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 is not triggered because the vignette still fits subthreshold/mild depressive symptoms rather than clear major depression. Rule R008 is not triggered because cancer-related physical symptoms are controlled. General screening/assessment and supportive counseling remain appropriate.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with metastatic colorectal cancer on palliative chemotherapy comes for follow-up after reporting low energy and decreased interest for the last 2 weeks. He describes feeling “blue and worn out,” with poor sleep and reduced appetite, but he remains active at home, has not missed appointments, and denies hopelessness or suicidal ideation. He wonders if an antidepressant should be started before his next infusion. His abdominal pain is well controlled, though he has troublesome neuropathy and mild constipation. Recent labs show mild anemia, normal TSH, and a prior workup documented low vitamin D but normal B12 and folate.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves metastatic cancer, fatigue, low mood, and a request for antidepressants, but the mood symptoms are brief and mild, and the main physical symptoms are not the uncontrolled pain/nausea pattern from the source case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Begin an antidepressant for the depressive symptoms now
Treat this as established depression without further assessment
Prioritize mood medication over the current symptom review
recommended_actions: Do not use antidepressants routinely for his subthreshold depressive symptoms or mild depression.
Screen for depression or distress and follow with a diagnostic assessment if positive.
Provide psychoeducation about depression in cancer and discuss its significance.
Explain how depression can worsen quality of life and physical symptom burden.
Destigmatize clinical depression and frame it as treatable.
Review for medical contributors to symptoms, including thyroid disease and nutritional deficiencies when appropriate.
Assess and optimize cancer-related physical symptom control, particularly the neuropathy and constipation.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The vignette is deceptive because it preserves the request for antidepressants, but the symptoms are still mild/subthreshold and not a clear major depressive episode. Unlike the source, pain is controlled and the main ongoing symptom issues are neuropathy and constipation, so the same recommendation against routine antidepressant use still applies while physical symptoms should be addressed.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is still not met because the patient’s mood symptoms remain mild/subthreshold. R008 still applies because there are cancer-related physical symptoms requiring optimization, even though they are different from the source vignette. The other general depression-care measures remain appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering NaF PET or choline PET
selected_rules.execute: Treat NaF PET and choline PET as experimental
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of NaF and choline PET should be considered experimental

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering dietary advice for prostate cancer survivors
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend any specific diet plan or food supplement for improved cancer outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: No diet plan can be recommended because no diet plan or food supplement has been associated with improved cancer outcomes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A man is not being evaluated through regularly scheduled clinical visits and develops symptoms
selected_rules.execute: Perform a PSA test and consider symptom-specific diagnostic imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: In men who are not being evaluated through regularly scheduled clinical visits, a PSA test should be performed if the following symptoms develop. Additionally, diagnostic imaging specific to the patient's symptom(s) may be indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Men after primary therapy with long-lasting treatment effects
selected_rules.execute: Ensure follow-up providers are aware of quality-of-life domains affected and available management options
selected_rules.source_statement: Follow-up healthcare providers should be aware of the domains of quality of life potentially affected by treatment for prostate cancer and the management options available to combat them.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Following prostate cancer treatment when assessing quality of life
selected_rules.execute: Measure individual patient-reported outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: To ensure optimal quality of life in these men, individual patient-reported outcomes should be measured.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Prostate cancer survivor after completing curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide ongoing surveillance
selected_rules.source_statement: For prostate cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required

selected_rules.activation_condition: Any healthcare practitioner provides PSA surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Manage PSA according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway
selected_rules.source_statement: All healthcare practitioners that provide PSA surveillance should manage PSA as per the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Planning survivorship care models for prostate cancer survivors
selected_rules.execute: Include family physicians in survivorship care models
selected_rules.source_statement: Expert opinion supports family physicians being involved in all survivorship care models.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man completed curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer 14 months ago and has been followed in a survivorship clinic. He reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that have affected sleep and daily activities. His PSA surveillance has been done through the clinic, but he is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and which should prompt further review. He asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and also asks if he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits.
recommended_actions: Provide ongoing surveillance after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Manage PSA surveillance according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.
Measure individual patient-reported outcomes to assess quality of life.
Ensure follow-up providers are aware of the quality-of-life domains affected by treatment and the available management options.
Include the family physician in the survivorship care model.
Treat NaF PET and choline PET as experimental if considering them for recurrence/restaging.
Do not recommend any specific diet plan or food supplement for improved cancer outcomes.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a prostate cancer survivor after curative-intent therapy, triggering the need for ongoing surveillance, PSA management per the CCO pathway, patient-reported outcome measurement, survivorship follow-up awareness of quality-of-life domains, and inclusion of the family physician. The patient also asks about recurrence imaging with PET and dietary supplements, which triggers the rules stating NaF and choline PET are experimental and that no specific diet or supplement should be recommended for improved cancer outcomes.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man completed curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer 14 months ago and has been followed in a survivorship clinic. He reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that have affected sleep and daily activities. His PSA surveillance has been done through the clinic, but he is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and which should prompt further review. He asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and also asks if he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Very close to the source survivorship visit, including PSA follow-up, symptoms, and questions about PET imaging and diet, but the original imaging/diet questions remain part of the visit.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a NaF PET or choline PET for early recurrence evaluation
Recommend a prostate cancer-specific diet or supplement to lower recurrence risk
recommended_actions: Provide ongoing surveillance after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Manage PSA surveillance according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.
Measure individual patient-reported outcomes to assess quality of life.
Ensure follow-up providers are aware of the quality-of-life domains affected by treatment and the available management options.
Include the family physician in the survivorship care model.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is still a prostate cancer survivorship follow-up after curative-intent treatment, so surveillance, PSA pathway management, patient-reported outcomes, and shared survivorship care remain appropriate. However, the vignette does not specifically establish that NaF PET or choline PET are being considered for recurrence/restaging in a guideline-triggering way beyond a hypothetical question, and the diet/supplement question is not a separate indication for any active intervention. The safer interpretation is to continue routine survivorship care and avoid endorsing non-evidence-based imaging or supplements.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient remains in post-treatment survivorship with ongoing quality-of-life concerns and PSA monitoring, preserving the surveillance-related recommendations. The subtle change is that the PET and diet/supplement items are framed as general questions during follow-up rather than a definite restaging decision point, making the original not-recommended actions less directly applicable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man completed curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer 14 months ago and is now in a shared-care follow-up program. He reports persistent fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes. His PSA surveillance has been done through the clinic, but at today’s visit he says he is feeling well overall and has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits. He asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and whether a “prostate cancer diet” or supplement would reduce recurrence risk. The clinic note also mentions that his most recent PSA is undetectable and stable compared with prior values.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same survivorship setting, symptoms, and patient questions, but adds a stable undetectable PSA that makes recurrence-focused imaging less appropriate right now.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Escalate to NaF PET or choline PET because of concern for recurrence
Advise a specific dietary program or supplement to prevent prostate cancer recurrence
recommended_actions: Provide ongoing surveillance after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Manage PSA surveillance according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.
Measure individual patient-reported outcomes to assess quality of life.
Ensure follow-up providers are aware of the quality-of-life domains affected by treatment and the available management options.
Include the family physician in the survivorship care model.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although he remains an appropriate prostate cancer survivor for ongoing surveillance and symptom management, the stable undetectable PSA means there is no current guideline-based trigger for recurrence/restaging imaging. The dietary supplement question still should not lead to a specific cancer-reducing diet recommendation. The safest approach is routine survivorship follow-up with PSA monitoring and symptom assessment, not advanced PET imaging at this time.
challenging_examples.rationale: The added stable undetectable PSA makes the recurrence imaging question a near-miss rather than an active indication, while the survivorship and quality-of-life follow-up recommendations still apply. The diet/supplement request remains non-recommended, but the case does not establish a need for PET-based restaging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: routine monitoring or surveillance of lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not use FDG PET/CT for routine surveillance.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is not recommended for the routine monitoring and surveillance of lymphoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: functional imaging is important and anatomical imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal, and/or in potentially curable cases, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: potentially curable case and functional imaging is important
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal, and/or in potentially curable cases, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: functional imaging is important, anatomical imaging is equivocal, and treatment choices may be affected in limited stage indolent lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal and treatment choices may be affected in limited stage indolent lymphomas, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: early stage (I or II) Hodgkin lymphoma after 2 or 3 cycles of chemotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered as definitive single-modality therapy
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to assess early response and inform whether to complete therapy or give more therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is recommended for the assessment of early response in early stage (I or II) HL following two or three cycles of chemotherapy when chemotherapy is being considered as the definitive single-modality therapy, to inform completion of therapy or if more therapy is warranted.

selected_rules.activation_condition: potentially curable case with suspected recurrence and conventional imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to investigate recurrence of Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma.
selected_rules.source_statement: In potentially curable cases, when functional imaging is considered to be important and conventional imaging is equivocal, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended to investigate recurrence of HL or NHL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: residual mass after chemotherapy in Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma and further potentially curative therapy is being considered and biopsy cannot be safely or readily performed
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to evaluate the residual mass.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is recommended for the evaluation of residual mass(es) following chemotherapy in a patient with HL or NHL when further, potentially curative, therapy (such as radiation or stem cell transplantation) is being considered and when biopsy cannot be safely or readily performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: FDG PET is abnormal during initial diagnosis of lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Use clinical follow-up or biopsy to confirm findings because false positives can occur.
selected_rules.source_statement: FDG PET may disclose higher rates of systemic disease; however, due to false-positive results, FDG PET scans should be subject to clinical follow-up or biopsy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with previously treated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma completed six cycles of R-CHOP eight months ago and achieved a complete metabolic response. She now returns for a routine follow-up visit without B symptoms, palpable adenopathy, or abnormal laboratory findings. Her oncologist notes that she feels well and asks whether she should have an FDG PET/CT scan to “make sure nothing is coming back.” There is no new clinical concern, and prior post-treatment imaging was reassuring. The main question is whether to continue scheduled surveillance imaging or use PET/CT as a routine monitoring test.
recommended_actions: Do not use FDG PET/CT for routine surveillance of lymphoma.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes routine monitoring/surveillance after lymphoma treatment without new symptoms or equivocal findings, which matches the rule against routine FDG PET/CT surveillance.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with previously treated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma completed six cycles of R-CHOP eight months ago and has remained in complete metabolic response on her last post-treatment scan. She now comes for a routine follow-up visit and feels well overall, but mentions two weeks of increasing fatigue and a new left neck fullness that she noticed in the mirror. Her oncologist finds a small, firm cervical node on exam, and a contrast CT obtained last week shows a 1.8 cm left supraclavicular node that is difficult to characterize because of adjacent postoperative scarring from a prior biopsy. There are no fevers or night sweats, and laboratory studies are otherwise unremarkable. The main question is whether to keep the visit limited to surveillance imaging or evaluate the suspicious but anatomically equivocal finding further.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The lymphoma history and post-treatment follow-up setting are unchanged, but new symptoms and an equivocal suspicious node shift the scenario from routine surveillance to recurrence evaluation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order FDG PET/CT as routine surveillance for lymphoma remission
Continue scheduled monitoring without additional workup because she lacks B symptoms
recommended_actions: Perform FDG PET/CT to investigate suspected recurrence of lymphoma because conventional imaging is equivocal in a potentially curable case.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer routine surveillance. The new neck fullness and equivocal CT finding create concern for recurrence, so the safer interpretation is suspected relapse requiring further evaluation rather than routine monitoring.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 does not apply because the case is no longer asymptomatic routine surveillance. R006 applies because there is suspected recurrence in a potentially curable patient with equivocal conventional imaging.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with stage II classic Hodgkin lymphoma has completed two cycles of ABVD with curative intent. His interim PET/CT was obtained because the treating team was deciding whether to continue chemotherapy alone or intensify treatment. The scan shows marked reduction in nodal size, but a small residual mediastinal node remains with faint FDG uptake just above background. The radiology report states that the Deauville score is 3 and the appearance is compatible with a favorable response. He feels well, has no B symptoms, and his blood counts are improving. The team asks whether another FDG PET/CT should be arranged now to clarify response before deciding on the next step in treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves early-stage Hodgkin lymphoma after two cycles of chemotherapy, but the interim scan has already been performed and shows a favorable response, so the rule for obtaining early response PET no longer applies.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order another FDG PET/CT to reassess early treatment response
Repeat imaging before deciding whether to continue the current chemotherapy plan
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a setting for another guideline-indicated early response PET/CT. The interim PET has already been done and is favorable; management should proceed based on the current response assessment and treatment plan rather than repeating PET without a new clinical indication.
challenging_examples.rationale: R005 is invalidated because the early response scan has already been obtained after two cycles, and the question is about repeating imaging rather than performing the recommended interim assessment. No other supplied rule is clearly triggered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When providing palliative care across the disease course
selected_rules.execute: Use a team-based approach that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms and supports patients and families.
selected_rules.source_statement: Palliative care focuses on care that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms; it can be delivered throughout the course of the disease; it involves a team of healthcare providers and supports patients as well as their families.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When selecting palliative care guidelines for review
selected_rules.execute: Include only papers that provide recommendations on palliative care and exclude papers that only describe guideline or consensus methods without recommendations.
selected_rules.source_statement: Papers were included if they provided any recommendations on palliative care. Papers were excluded if they only provided guideline or consensus methods and no recommendations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When screening palliative care guidelines for this review
selected_rules.execute: Restrict inclusion to guidelines published from 2011 to 2016.
selected_rules.source_statement: Due to the large number of guidelines, the Working Group decided to restrict the review to include only guidelines published in the past five years (2011 to 2016).

selected_rules.activation_condition: When selecting higher-quality guidelines for this review
selected_rules.execute: Include only guidelines with an AGREE Rigour of Development subsection score of 28 or higher.
selected_rules.source_statement: To identify the best-quality guidelines, the AGREE Instrument Rigour of Development scale was used and only guidelines that scored 28 or above were included in this review.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When using the broad palliative care guideline for adults
selected_rules.execute: Apply the guideline covering the palliative care path from first diagnosis of serious illness to death.
selected_rules.source_statement: The "Palliative Care for Adults" guideline by McCusker et al. ... covers the palliative care path from the patient's first diagnosis of a serious illness to death.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When caring for patients in the last days and hours of life
selected_rules.execute: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, decision support, care and management, education, and organization/policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: This is a Canadian guideline about patient care in the last days and hours of life, and the recommendations in this guideline were specific to nurses. The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment for end of life, Decision support at end of life, Care and management at end of life, Education, and Organization and policy recommendations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When assessing or preventing pressure ulcers in palliative care
selected_rules.execute: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, planning, interventions, discharge or transfer/care arrangements, and education/organization-policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The topic of this guideline was the assessment and prevention of pressure ulcers. This guideline does not focus solely on palliative care, but the treatment and assessment of pressure ulcers in palliative care is a part of the guideline. ... The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment, Planning, Interventions, Discharge or transfer or the care arrangements, and Education and organization/policy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When managing chronic cough in patients receiving palliative care
selected_rules.execute: Use simple linctus, then a therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, then an opioid or opioid derivative such as dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine.
selected_rules.source_statement: The recommendations include use of simple linctus, therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, and then prescription of an opioid or opioid derivative (dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He is not in the final days of life, but his symptoms are worsening and he asks for help managing them at home while continuing cancer-directed therapy. The oncology team notes that his physical symptoms are only part of the problem; he also needs emotional support and help for his wife, who is overwhelmed by caregiving responsibilities.
recommended_actions: Use a team-based palliative care approach that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms.
Provide support for both the patient and his family, including caregiver support and help with coping.
Apply the broad adult palliative care guideline across the disease course, from first diagnosis of serious illness through death.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers R001 because palliative care is being provided across the disease course and should address physical symptoms, emotional needs, and family support. It also triggers R005 because the patient has a serious illness and is being managed along the palliative care path from diagnosis onward. No other rules are directly relevant because he is not in the last days of life, and no pressure ulcer, chronic cough-specific, or guideline-selection scenario is described.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now receiving second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He asks for help managing symptoms at home while continuing cancer-directed therapy. The oncology team notes that his physical symptoms are only part of the problem; he also needs emotional support and help for his wife, who is overwhelmed by caregiving responsibilities. However, he and his family have explicitly declined involvement of any palliative care service, preferring that all supportive issues remain within oncology follow-up.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The cancer type, treatment line, symptom burden, and family stress are nearly identical to the source case, but the patient and family have refused palliative care involvement.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Initiate a team-based palliative care referral to address symptoms and family needs.
Apply the broad adult palliative care guideline across the illness course.
Arrange home-based palliative support for the patient and caregiver.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the palliative care pathway because the key trigger is absent: the patient has declined palliative care involvement. The safer interpretation is to continue oncology-led supportive management, revisit goals of care later, and respect the current refusal while documenting it.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 and R005 depend on providing or using the palliative care pathway. Here, the clinical need is similar, but the patient/family refusal means the vignette does not actually describe guideline-directed palliative care delivery.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He is not in the final days of life, but his symptoms are worsening and he asks for help managing them at home while continuing cancer-directed therapy. The oncology team notes that his physical symptoms are only part of the problem; he also needs emotional support and help for his wife, who is overwhelmed by caregiving responsibilities. His last hospitalization ended with a do-not-resuscitate order, but he and his oncologist still expect active disease-directed treatment and do not consider him to be in the terminal phase.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall palliative-care context, metastatic lung cancer, symptom burden, and family distress remain the same, but he is not in the last days or hours of life.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the end-of-life nursing guideline for last-days care and decision support.
Focus primarily on terminal-phase assessment and comfort measures.
Treat this as a last-days-of-life care pathway because of the DNR order.
recommended_actions: Use a team-based palliative care approach that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms.
Provide support for both the patient and his family, including caregiver support and help with coping.
Apply the broad adult palliative care guideline across the disease course, from first diagnosis of serious illness through death.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case still supports general palliative care, but it does not meet criteria for the last-days-and-hours-of-life guideline because the patient remains on active second-line cancer therapy and is not in the terminal phase. The safer interpretation is broad palliative support rather than end-of-life nursing recommendations.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 and R005 still apply because this is ongoing palliative care for serious illness. R006 is invalidated because the patient is specifically not in the last days of life, so the end-of-life nursing guideline should not be selected.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IIIB cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not apply the bevacizumab recommendation from GOG-0240 eligibility
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, GOG-0240 did not include patients with stage IIIB cancer (local extension to pelvic sidewall) or IVA cancer (invasion into bladder or rectum).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IVA cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not apply the bevacizumab recommendation from GOG-0240 eligibility
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, GOG-0240 did not include patients with stage IIIB cancer (local extension to pelvic sidewall) or IVA cancer (invasion into bladder or rectum).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and randomized clinical trials are available
selected_rules.execute: offer participation in randomized clinical trials evaluating systemic therapy regimens
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that all patients with recurrent, metastatic or persistent cervical cancer be offered the opportunity to participate in randomized clinical trials, if available, that evaluate the efficacy of and adverse effects of systemic therapy regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and systemic therapy is indicated
selected_rules.execute: use cisplatin with paclitaxel
selected_rules.source_statement: Cisplatin with paclitaxel is recommended for this patient population

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and a cisplatin combination is being used
selected_rules.execute: substitute carboplatin for cisplatin when appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: The substitution of carboplatin for cisplatin in these combinations is also recommended for this target population because carboplatin is associated with fewer adverse effects and greater ease of administration.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IVB, recurrent, or persistent cervical cancer not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy, performance status 0 to 1, adequate renal hepatic and bone marrow function, and no prior chemotherapy for recurrence, nonhealing wounds, active bleeding, or inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism
selected_rules.execute: use bevacizumab with cisplatin-paclitaxel
selected_rules.source_statement: Bevacizumab in combination with cisplatin-paclitaxel is recommended for a specific subset of the target population, which includes only patients that match the characteristics of the GOG-0240 study population.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and carboplatin-paclitaxel is feasible to administer
selected_rules.execute: use carboplatin-paclitaxel as a treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on these results, and on the feasibility of administration, carboplatin-paclitaxel is recommended as a treatment option for recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and systemic therapy is indicated
selected_rules.execute: consider cisplatin-vinorelbine
selected_rules.source_statement: cisplatin in other combinations, including cisplatin-vinorelbine, cisplatin-gemcitabine, and cisplatin-topotecan may also be considered.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has progressive pelvic pain, hydronephrosis, and enlarging para-aortic nodes on imaging. Her disease is deemed recurrent and not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate marrow counts. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center. She has no active bleeding, no nonhealing wounds, and no history of inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
recommended_actions: Offer participation in a randomized clinical trial evaluating systemic therapy regimens, if the patient is interested and eligible.
Use cisplatin with paclitaxel as systemic therapy.
Carboplatin-paclitaxel is also a feasible treatment option if administration considerations favor carboplatin.
source_case.rationale: The patient has recurrent cervical cancer with systemic therapy indicated, so she meets criteria for cisplatin-paclitaxel and for consideration of carboplatin-paclitaxel as an alternative option. Because randomized clinical trials are available, she should also be offered trial participation. Bevacizumab is not triggered because the vignette does not establish the specific GOG-0240-eligible subset, and no stage IIIB/IVA exclusion is needed here.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has progressive pelvic pain and enlarging para-aortic nodes on imaging. Her disease is deemed recurrent and not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate marrow counts. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center. She has new gross hematuria from tumor erosion into the bladder, but no nonhealing wounds and no history of inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like recurrent cervical cancer with good performance status and available trial access, but the new bladder invasion-associated hematuria removes the safe bevacizumab eligibility profile.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer bevacizumab with cisplatin-paclitaxel because she otherwise resembles the GOG-0240 population.
Proceed with cisplatin-paclitaxel plus bevacizumab as first-line therapy.
Use carboplatin-paclitaxel only if cisplatin is felt to be difficult, while still adding bevacizumab.
recommended_actions: Offer participation in randomized clinical trials evaluating systemic therapy regimens, if the patient is interested and eligible.
Use cisplatin with paclitaxel as systemic therapy.
Carboplatin-paclitaxel is also a feasible treatment option if administration considerations favor carboplatin.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains recurrent cervical cancer requiring systemic treatment and trial consideration, but the new gross hematuria from bladder erosion means she no longer fits the low-bleeding-risk subset for bevacizumab. The safer interpretation is to avoid bevacizumab and use standard platinum-taxane therapy, with trial enrollment if eligible.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient still meets criteria for R003, R004, and R007. However, the added tumor-related hematuria/bladder erosion invalidates the bevacizumab eligibility subset from R006.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old woman with recurrent cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has enlarging pelvic disease and new lung metastases. The disease is not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1 and adequate renal, hepatic, and bone marrow function. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center, and she is interested in learning about systemic treatment. Her history is notable for a remote pulmonary embolism that is now fully anticoagulated and stable, with no active bleeding or nonhealing wounds. Her oncologist is considering first-line systemic therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette preserves the classic recurrent, fit patient with trial access, but the stable anticoagulated thromboembolism history disqualifies the bevacizumab subset.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add bevacizumab to cisplatin-paclitaxel because her performance status and organ function are adequate.
Start cisplatin-paclitaxel-bevacizumab since she has metastatic recurrence and no active bleeding.
Offer carboplatin-paclitaxel with bevacizumab if cisplatin is less suitable.
recommended_actions: Offer participation in randomized clinical trials evaluating systemic therapy regimens, if the patient is interested and eligible.
Use cisplatin with paclitaxel as systemic therapy.
Carboplatin-paclitaxel is also a feasible treatment option if administration considerations favor carboplatin.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: She still qualifies for systemic therapy and trial discussion, but the prior thromboembolism means the vignette does not meet the stricter bevacizumab-eligible criteria, even though it is anticoagulated and stable. The safer reading is platinum-taxane therapy without bevacizumab.
challenging_examples.rationale: R003, R004, and R007 still apply. R006 is not triggered because the case includes a history of thromboembolism, which removes the specific eligibility profile required for bevacizumab with cisplatin-paclitaxel.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma and sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease is being diagnosed
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for diagnosis
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for the diagnosis of sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging of I, IIa, or IIb melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma stage I, IIa, or IIb
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for the diagnosis of sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging of I, IIa, or IIb melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has suspected brain metastases from melanoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely use PET to diagnose brain metastases
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET or PET/CT is not recommended for the diagnosis of brain metastases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has primary uveal malignant melanoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely use PET to detect primary uveal malignant melanoma
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET is not recommended for the detection of primary uveal malignant melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma and is high-risk with potentially resectable disease
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging of high-risk patients with potentially resectable disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At recurrence, an isolated metastasis is identified
selected_rules.execute: Use PET
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for isolated metastases at time of recurrence or when contemplating metastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At recurrence, metastectomy is being contemplated
selected_rules.execute: Use PET before metastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for isolated metastases at time of recurrence or when contemplating metastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Assessing treatment response in malignant melanoma
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation for or against PET
selected_rules.source_statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET for the assessment of treatment response in malignant melanoma due to insufficient evidence.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology now shows a single sentinel node with a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit. He has no neurologic symptoms, no known distant disease, and his primary tumor was otherwise consistent with stage II disease. His oncology team is discussing whether PET imaging should be ordered to further characterize the nodal finding before deciding on additional management.
recommended_actions: Do not use PET for diagnosis of the sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease.
Do not use PET for staging of this melanoma presentation.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule for melanoma with sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease being diagnosed. The guideline states PET is not recommended for diagnosing sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging stage I, IIa, or IIb melanoma. No other rule is clearly triggered because the case does not describe high-risk potentially resectable disease, recurrence, isolated metastasis, or treatment-response assessment.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology now shows a single sentinel node with a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit, and completion lymph node dissection from a different basin later revealed an additional 2.1 cm nodal metastasis. He has no neurologic symptoms and no known distant disease, but his oncologist is considering whether PET imaging should be obtained before planning further management and possible resection of the additional nodal disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on melanoma with nodal micrometastatic disease and discussion of PET before next steps, but the added recurrent isolated nodal metastasis and contemplated metastectomy change the indication.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order PET to characterize the sentinel node micrometastasis before deciding on additional management.
Use PET for staging the nodal finding in this otherwise low-stage melanoma.
recommended_actions: Use PET before metastectomy for the isolated nodal recurrence being considered for resection.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer the simple sentinel-node micrometastatic scenario. The additional nodal metastasis represents recurrent isolated disease with resection being contemplated, which shifts the case into a setting where PET is guideline-concordant for pre-metastectomy evaluation rather than for diagnosing the original micrometastatic sentinel node finding.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 is invalidated because the case is no longer limited to sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease. Rule R007 is triggered because an isolated metastasis at recurrence is present and metastectomy is being contemplated. PET is therefore appropriate before the planned resection, not for the original sentinel-node diagnosis.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology now shows a single sentinel node with a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit. He has no neurologic symptoms, no known distant disease, and his primary tumor was otherwise consistent with stage II disease. His oncology team is discussing whether PET imaging should be ordered to further characterize the nodal finding before deciding on additional management. A recent brain MRI done for unrelated headaches was normal, and there is no concern for brain metastases.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like a melanoma staging question with a positive sentinel node and consideration of PET, but the added details keep the disease limited to stage II-type localized melanoma without a new indication for PET.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order PET to evaluate the sentinel node micrometastasis.
Use PET for staging before deciding on adjuvant treatment.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The added normal brain MRI does not create a PET indication, and the case remains focused on a stage II melanoma presentation with sentinel-node micrometastatic disease. PET is not guideline-concordant for diagnosing the sentinel node finding or for staging stage I, IIa, or IIb melanoma in this setting.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 applies to sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease, but the recommendation is not to use PET. No other supplied rule is triggered because there is no high-risk potentially resectable disease, recurrence, isolated metastasis, brain metastasis, uveal melanoma, or treatment-response assessment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use surgery plus radiation therapy as the initial local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone and surgery is planned
selected_rules.execute: Take both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
selected_rules.source_statement: Both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans should be taken into consideration for surgical planning.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider surgery alone as a local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy alone as a local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Decide local treatment with a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, considering age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
selected_rules.source_statement: The local treatment for an individual patient should be decided by a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient after consideration of the following: 1) patient characteristics (e.g., age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and existing comorbidities), 2) the potential benefit compared with the potential complications from surgery and/or toxicities associated with RT, and 3) patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: complete tumour resection is impossible
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy alone as the optimal local treatment.
selected_rules.source_statement: If complete tumour resection is impossible, RT alone may be the optimal choice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: postoperative residual tumour or positive margins
selected_rules.execute: Consider postoperative radiation therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: RT may be a treatment option postoperatively in patients who have residual tumours or positive margins.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone, surgery is planned, and there is good chemotherapy response in certain anatomic locations
selected_rules.execute: Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.
selected_rules.source_statement: In certain anatomic locations with good chemotherapy response, the post-chemotherapy MRI may be the appropriate imaging modality to plan surgical resection margins.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 14-year-old boy with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the distal femur completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has had a marked radiographic and symptomatic response. Repeat MRI shows substantial shrinkage of the intramedullary and soft-tissue components, while the pre-chemotherapy MRI demonstrated more extensive marrow involvement. The orthopedic oncologist is planning limb-sparing surgery, but the lesion’s location near the metaphysis raises concern about achieving clear margins without excessive functional loss. The case is reviewed at a multidisciplinary tumor board with the patient and family, who prefer an approach that preserves function if oncologically safe. The team notes that complete resection appears feasible, though the final extent of surgery will depend on careful comparison of the pre- and post-chemotherapy MRI studies.
recommended_actions: Take both the pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to help plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.

Decide local treatment at a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, weighing age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches localized Ewing’s sarcoma of bone with surgery planned after neoadjuvant chemotherapy, triggering preoperative staging and planning rules. The good chemotherapy response and an anatomic location where margin planning is important support using the post-chemotherapy MRI for resection margins. The multidisciplinary decision-making rule is also triggered because local treatment should be individualized with the patient and team.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 14-year-old boy with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the distal femur completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has had a marked radiographic and symptomatic response. Repeat MRI shows substantial shrinkage of the intramedullary and soft-tissue components, while the pre-chemotherapy MRI demonstrated more extensive marrow involvement. The orthopedic oncologist is planning surgery, but because the tumor now abuts a neurovascular bundle and a wide resection would likely require an above-knee amputation, the team is considering definitive local therapy options. The case is reviewed at a multidisciplinary tumor board with the patient and family, who prefer to avoid major functional loss if oncologically safe. The surgeons note that complete resection may not be feasible without unacceptable morbidity, and final local control may depend on radiation rather than resection. The pre- and post-chemotherapy MRI studies are available for comparison.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same age, diagnosis, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, distal femur location, and tumor board workflow, but introduces a neurovascular encasement making complete resection impractical.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Plan limb-sparing surgery followed by radiation therapy to maximize local control.
Use both MRIs to map a resection margin and proceed with surgery plus postoperative RT.
Choose combined surgery and radiation as the initial local treatment because margins are tight.
recommended_actions: Take both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.

Decide local treatment with a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, considering age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
Consider radiation therapy alone as the optimal local treatment.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for recommending surgery-based local control because complete tumor resection is not realistically achievable without major morbidity. The safer interpretation is that RT alone may be the appropriate local treatment, with MRI still useful for planning and tumor board discussion still required.
challenging_examples.rationale: R006 is triggered because complete tumour resection is impossible; R001 is invalidated because surgery plus RT is not the initial standard local option. R002 and R005 still apply, and the comparison MRI remains relevant for planning.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 14-year-old boy with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the distal femur completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has had a marked radiographic and symptomatic response. Repeat MRI shows substantial shrinkage of the intramedullary and soft-tissue components, while the pre-chemotherapy MRI demonstrated more extensive marrow involvement. The orthopedic oncologist is planning limb-sparing surgery, but the post-treatment scan was obtained at an outside facility without the original pre-chemotherapy sequences for side-by-side comparison. The tumor board discusses local control with the patient and family, who want limb preservation if safe. Because the lesion appears smaller and resection seems feasible, the team is ready to finalize margins based on the new MRI alone. The bone lesion remains near the metaphysis, and the surgeons are cautious about preserving function while maintaining clearance.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case keeps the same diagnosis, neoadjuvant response, planned surgery, and anatomic site, but removes access to the pre-chemotherapy MRI needed to interpret extent of disease accurately for margin planning.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the post-chemotherapy MRI alone to define the resection margins.
Proceed with limb-sparing surgery based only on the new scan because the tumor has shrunk.
Finalize the operative plan without retrieving the original baseline imaging.
recommended_actions: Take both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.

Decide local treatment with a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, considering age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for using the post-chemotherapy MRI to plan margins because the baseline MRI is unavailable for comparison. The safer interpretation is to obtain or review the pre-treatment imaging before relying on the response scan for surgical planning.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 and R005 still apply, but R008 does not because the prerequisite of using the post-chemotherapy MRI as the appropriate modality in certain locations with good response is not satisfied without baseline comparison. The operative plan should not rely on the post-treatment MRI alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent
selected_rules.execute: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care
selected_rules.source_statement: Planned sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are both acceptable standards of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Choose treatment case by case based on patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy, and patient preference
selected_rules.source_statement: Therefore, choice of treatment should be made on a caseby-case basis based on considerations that include patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy and patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use sequential chemotherapy as a strategy to provide access to all effective cytotoxic drugs
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the results of this systematic review, patients should have access to all effective cytotoxic drugs using a sequential strategy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening visceral disease
selected_rules.execute: Consider upfront combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy may be more appropriate for patients with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic or bulky life-threatening visceral disease given their higher overall response rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: A meta-analysis of five trials (1-5) demonstrates a survival advantage for combination chemotherapy (HR, 0.92; 95%CI, 0.86-0.99, p=0.02).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that the survival advantage of combination chemotherapy is likely very small and may not be clinically significant
selected_rules.source_statement: Median survival advantage in most trials is 3 to 6 weeks (range <1 week to 12 weeks). Therefore, any survival advantage that exists is likely to be very small and not clinically significant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia
selected_rules.source_statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia
selected_rules.source_statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4), febrile neutropenia (1) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status has newly diagnosed unresectable metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with multiple bilobar liver metastases and small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or severe cancer-related symptoms, but he is anxious to start treatment quickly. His creatinine and liver tests are acceptable, and he has no major comorbidities other than well-controlled hypertension. After discussing goals of care, he states he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team reviews that both sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are reasonable options in this palliative setting, and notes that the survival difference between approaches is likely small. They also discuss that combination therapy may increase the risk of neutropenia and febrile neutropenia.
recommended_actions: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care.
Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy.
Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia.
Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent, triggering R001. The case also supports the general metastatic setting for R005, R007, and R008. No rule about rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening disease was used, since those features were not present.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status is newly diagnosed with metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma involving multiple bilobar liver metastases and several small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction, but over the last 2 weeks he has developed worsening right upper quadrant pain and early satiety from a rapidly enlarging hepatic mass. His creatinine and liver tests remain acceptable, and he has no major comorbidities other than well-controlled hypertension. After discussing goals of care, he says he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team reviews options and notes that treatment choice should be individualized based on disease tempo and toxicity concerns, and they discuss that a more intensive regimen may be reasonable because the disease is progressing quickly and is becoming symptomatic.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still has unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer, good performance status, and palliative intent, but the disease is now rapidly progressing and symptomatic, shifting the guideline emphasis away from a generic sequential-versus-combination equivalence discussion.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer either sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as equivalent standard options.
Frame the survival difference between sequential and combination therapy as the main decision point.
Focus primarily on the higher neutropenia risk of combination treatment.
recommended_actions: Choose treatment case by case based on patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy, and patient preference.
Consider upfront combination chemotherapy.
Use sequential chemotherapy as a strategy to provide access to all effective cytotoxic drugs.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a straightforward palliative-metastatic case for which either sequential or upfront combination therapy is equally emphasized. The rapid progression, symptom burden, and enlarging liver disease make the situation one where upfront combination chemotherapy becomes more appropriate, while general case-by-case selection and sequential access to cytotoxics still apply.
challenging_examples.rationale: R004 is triggered by rapidly progressing, symptomatic visceral disease. R002 and R003 remain applicable for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer. The generic equivalence and toxicity/survival talking points from R001, R005, R006, R007, and R008 are not the key guideline-concordant takeaway for this modified presentation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status has newly diagnosed colorectal adenocarcinoma with multiple bilobar liver metastases and a few indeterminate pulmonary nodules. His creatinine and liver tests are acceptable, and he has no major comorbidities other than well-controlled hypertension. After discussing goals of care, he states he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team notes that because he has a potentially resectable primary lesion and limited extrahepatic disease, they are planning neoadjuvant systemic therapy with the possibility of later metastasectomy if he responds. They discuss chemotherapy options and toxicity, but the intent is explicitly to convert him to resection rather than to manage incurable disease indefinitely.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still looks like a typical metastatic colorectal cancer case with good performance status and liver-dominant spread, but the treatment intent has shifted to potentially curative downstaging rather than purely palliative management.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Present sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as standard palliative options.
Emphasize that combination therapy offers only a small survival advantage over sequential therapy.
Discuss the increased risks of neutropenia and febrile neutropenia from upfront combination treatment.
recommended_actions: Choose treatment case by case based on patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy, and patient preference.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case no longer fits unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent, because the team is aiming for conversion to resection. The palliative sequencing recommendation is therefore not guideline-concordant here; the safer interpretation is that systemic therapy should be selected in the context of a downstaging strategy and multidisciplinary reassessment.
challenging_examples.rationale: Only the broad case-by-case selection principle remains clearly applicable. The specific recommendations about sequential versus upfront combination chemotherapy, small survival advantage, and hematologic toxicity are tied to unresectable metastatic disease treated palliatively and do not cleanly apply when the goal is potential conversion to surgery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with MDS
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation outside a clinical trial.
selected_rules.source_statement: Autologous stem cell transplantation is not recommended for patients with MDS.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with MDS
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend autologous SCT outside of a clinical trial.
selected_rules.source_statement: One systematic review comprising a total of 22 studies did not detect any benefit associated with autologous SCT (ASCT), and does not recommend it outside of a clinical trial (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT as the preferred post-remission strategy.
selected_rules.source_statement: ASCT is not recommended for patients with AML in first complete remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
selected_rules.source_statement: While associated with more favourable treatment-related mortality (TRM) rates, if longterm survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in first complete remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT as supported therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of ASCT for patients with AML in second or subsequent remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission and long-term survival is the primary outcome
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT for long-term survival benefit.
selected_rules.source_statement: If long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in second or subsequent remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1 and intermediate-risk or poor-risk disease
selected_rules.execute: Use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered.
selected_rules.source_statement: Significant benefits in favour of alloSCT for both intermediate and poor risk patients (p<0.01) were detected, but no difference was detected with good risk patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML who achieves a second or subsequent remission
selected_rules.execute: Recommend allogeneic transplantation.
selected_rules.source_statement: Allogeneic transplantation is the recommended option for patients with AML who achieve a second or subsequent remission.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with intermediate-risk acute myeloid leukemia was treated with induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission. His performance status is good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is considering post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet, and he is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control.
recommended_actions: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred post-remission strategy.
Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
If transplantation is being considered for this intermediate-risk AML in first complete remission, use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult patient with AML in first complete remission and intermediate-risk disease, triggering the rules against autologous SCT in CR1 and the recommendation favoring allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered. The patient’s concern about long-term disease control also aligns with the rule stating there is no evidence to support autologous SCT when long-term survival is the primary outcome.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with intermediate-risk acute myeloid leukemia received induction chemotherapy and is now being evaluated after count recovery. He has persistent minimal residual disease by flow cytometry despite morphologic remission, but the marrow still meets criteria for first complete remission. His performance status remains good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is discussing post-remission options, including transplant versus additional chemotherapy. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet, and he is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on intermediate-risk AML in apparent CR1 with a transplant decision, but the presence of persistent MRD changes the risk discussion and makes the original autologous-transplant-focused recommendation less directly applicable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred consolidation strategy.
Use autologous SCT to maximize long-term disease control.
Reassure him that transplant choice is straightforward because he remains in first complete remission.
recommended_actions: If transplantation is being considered for this intermediate-risk AML in first complete remission, use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains AML in first complete remission with intermediate-risk disease, so the rule favoring allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT or chemotherapy still applies. However, the persistent measurable residual disease makes the scenario higher risk and the question is no longer a clean fit for the original autologous-SCT discussion. The safer interpretation is to prioritize allogeneic transplantation if eligible and to avoid choosing autologous SCT as the main strategy.
challenging_examples.rationale: The modified vignette preserves CR1 and intermediate-risk AML, which still triggers the recommendation for allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT or chemotherapy. The added MRD positivity is a subtle risk modifier but does not create support for autologous SCT; it makes the case more concerning overall.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with intermediate-risk acute myeloid leukemia was treated with induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission after a second induction cycle. His performance status is good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is considering post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy. He had an HLA-matched sibling identified during workup, but the donor is currently unavailable because the sibling has an active infection and cannot proceed this month. He is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This still looks like a standard CR1 intermediate-risk AML transplant question, but the extra detail that remission was only achieved after a second induction cycle makes the course more complex and the donor delay may distract from the actual preferred strategy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Choose autologous stem cell transplantation because a matched sibling donor is temporarily unavailable.
Defer transplant planning and use chemotherapy alone since the donor cannot donate right now.
Treat autologous SCT as the best option to reduce relapse risk in first remission.
recommended_actions: If transplantation is being considered for this intermediate-risk AML in first complete remission, use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The patient is still in first complete remission with intermediate-risk AML, so the recommendation favoring allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT or chemotherapy remains the relevant guideline point. A temporary donor unavailability does not convert autologous SCT into the preferred option. The safer interpretation is to continue planning for allogeneic transplantation rather than substitute autologous SCT.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although the remission required two induction cycles and the donor is temporarily unavailable, the patient remains an adult with intermediate-risk AML in CR1. That continues to activate the rule recommending allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT or chemotherapy. No rule supports autologous SCT in this setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario
selected_rules.execute: do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Also, peptide receptor radionuclide therapy has not been approved for patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all adult patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: use the TNM staging system
selected_rules.source_statement: The TNM staging system should be used for all patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours undergoing surgery
selected_rules.execute: aim to achieve complete resection
selected_rules.source_statement: The aim of surgery in all cases is to achieve a complete resection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: discuss the patient at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, including higher-volume centres when possible
selected_rules.source_statement: Discussion of all patients at multidisciplinary cancer conference (MCC) is strongly recommended, not just at local MCC but also with higher-volume centres.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: consider presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board
selected_rules.source_statement: Presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board should be considered.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team plans formal staging and wants input from thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre because the tumour appears potentially resectable. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment.
recommended_actions: Use the TNM staging system for this patient.
