Discuss the case at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, and include a higher-volume centre when possible.
Do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment for this thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the general thymic epithelial tumour rules for all adult patients and the Ontario-specific restriction. The patient has a confirmed thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario, so TNM staging applies, multidisciplinary review is strongly recommended, and peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is not an approved treatment.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows a small anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour, but the pathology report notes the specimen is from a prior needle biopsy and the diagnosis remains pending expert review because lymphoma has not yet been fully excluded. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The oncology team is considering the next step before any treatment decision is made. The case is being reviewed with thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, and the patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used now instead of further workup or standard treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on an Ontario patient with a suspected thymic epithelial tumour and the same multidisciplinary workflow, but the diagnosis is not yet secure.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with TNM staging immediately and treat this as a confirmed thymic epithelial tumour.
Discuss the case at a multidisciplinary cancer conference and avoid peptide receptor radionuclide therapy.
Move directly to thymic cancer management based on the biopsy result.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the thymic epithelial tumour recommendations because the diagnosis is not yet confirmed; a competing diagnosis remains under consideration. The safer interpretation is to complete diagnostic clarification before applying thymic tumour-specific staging or treatment guidance.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source rules require a confirmed thymic epithelial tumour. Here, the pathology is still indeterminate and lymphoma has not been excluded, so the thymic tumour recommendations are not yet triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. Staging CT suggests the tumour is confined to the mediastinum but abuts the pericardium, and the thoracic surgeon feels complete resection may not be achievable without major morbidity. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team plans formal staging and wants input from thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette keeps the same Ontario setting, confirmed thymic epithelial tumour, and multidisciplinary review, but the tumour is likely unresectable rather than potentially resectable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use TNM staging and proceed with a plan for complete resection.
Discuss the case at multidisciplinary conference and refer for surgery as the main approach.
Avoid peptide receptor radionuclide therapy and treat the tumour as standard resectable disease.
recommended_actions: Consider presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case still warrants expert multidisciplinary review, but the surgical recommendation is no longer a fit because complete resection is not expected to be achievable. The safer interpretation is to pursue specialized discussion for locally advanced disease rather than assuming a resectable pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: The tumour is confirmed, so thymic tumour guidance still applies. However, the case no longer supports the resection-oriented framing in the source vignette, making the original combined recommendation set discordant; the remaining applicable item is expert tumour board review.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
selected_rules.execute: Use HDREB alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with previously untreated, symptomatic, endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) alone is more effective for palliation of respiratory symptoms than HDREB alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
selected_rules.execute: Use routine HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence does not provide conclusive results to suggest that routine use of HDREB and EBRT would provide improved symptom relief over EBRT alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction
selected_rules.execute: Use a surgical core out procedure before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB
selected_rules.source_statement: December 2022: the recommendation "For patients with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction, a surgical core out procedure may be needed before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB" is no longer endorsed because the updated evidence does not show a benefit of this procedure.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient previously treated by EBRT who is symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease
selected_rules.execute: Use HDREB if technically feasible
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients previously treated by EBRT who are symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease, HDREB is recommended, providing that endobronchial brachytherapy is technically feasible.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease where selected combined treatment is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Consider HDREB plus EBRT in selected patients
selected_rules.source_statement: June 2018: It is the opinion of the Lung Cancer Disease Site Group that HDREB and EBRT might be suitable in selected patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 3 weeks of worsening cough, intermittent hemoptysis, and dyspnea. CT chest shows a centrally located right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer causing partial endobronchial obstruction of the segmental bronchus with post-obstructive atelectasis, but not complete lung collapse. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable endobronchial lesion narrowing the airway. He is being evaluated for palliative treatment to relieve respiratory symptoms. The oncology team is considering whether to add endobronchial brachytherapy to external beam radiation, but there is no prior EBRT history and no clear reason to prefer a combined approach over radiation alone.
recommended_actions: Use external beam radiation therapy alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms rather than HDREB alone.
Do not routinely use HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone.
Consider HDREB plus EBRT only in selected patients, recognizing this is an opinion-based option rather than a routine recommendation.
source_case.rationale: The patient has previously untreated, symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer, which triggers the rules addressing initial palliation. EBRT alone is favored over HDREB alone, and routine combined HDREB plus EBRT is not supported by conclusive evidence. A combined approach may be considered only selectively.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 3 weeks of worsening cough, intermittent hemoptysis, and dyspnea. CT chest shows a centrally located right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer causing near-complete endobronchial obstruction of the segmental bronchus with a small area of post-obstructive atelectasis, but the distal lung remains partially aerated and there is no complete collapse. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable endobronchial lesion narrowing the airway. He is being evaluated for palliative treatment to relieve respiratory symptoms. The oncology team notes that because the obstruction is severe, they are considering endobronchial brachytherapy alone rather than external beam radiation, despite the absence of prior EBRT.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves untreated symptomatic endobronchial NSCLC with obstructive findings, but the lung is not completely collapsed and there is no prior EBRT history.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat with endobronchial brachytherapy alone to rapidly palliate the obstructing lesion.
Plan routine combined endobronchial brachytherapy plus external beam radiation for better symptom relief.
recommended_actions: Use external beam radiation therapy alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms rather than HDREB alone.
Do not routinely use HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone.
Consider HDREB plus EBRT only in selected patients, recognizing this is an opinion-based option rather than a routine recommendation.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette is not guideline-concordant for HDREB alone because EBRT alone remains favored for palliation in previously untreated symptomatic endobronchial NSCLC. The subtle near-miss is that the obstruction is severe but not a complete lung collapse, so the case does not justify changing to brachytherapy-first based on collapse-related concerns. A safer interpretation is to prioritize EBRT for symptom palliation and reserve combined treatment only for selected circumstances.
challenging_examples.rationale: Previously untreated symptomatic endobronchial NSCLC still triggers the palliative radiation guidance. EBRT alone is recommended over HDREB alone, and routine HDREB plus EBRT is not supported. The absence of complete collapse means the collapse-related procedural pathway does not apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 71-year-old woman with recurrent hemoptysis and progressive wheeze has known non-small cell lung cancer centered in the left mainstem bronchus. She previously completed thoracic EBRT 10 months ago and now has recurrent endobronchial obstruction on bronchoscopy. The lesion is technically reachable, but the interventional pulmonologist notes marked friability and bleeding risk, and the airway is narrowed to a slit with poor visualization beyond the lesion. The oncology team asks whether HDREB should be arranged for local palliation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has symptomatic endobronchial NSCLC with obstruction, but the key difference is prior EBRT and limited technical feasibility of brachytherapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with endobronchial brachytherapy for palliation of the recurrent obstructing lesion.
Add external beam radiation again or combine it with brachytherapy for better local symptom control.
recommended_actions: Use HDREB if technically feasible.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a case for routine primary palliation with EBRT or combined treatment because the patient has already received thoracic EBRT. The main guideline that applies is the recurrent-disease scenario, where HDREB is recommended only if technically feasible; here, feasibility is questionable because visualization and access are poor. The safer interpretation is to assess whether endobronchial brachytherapy can actually be delivered, and if not, consider other palliative measures.
challenging_examples.rationale: Prior EBRT with recurrent symptomatic endobronchial obstruction activates the recurrent-disease recommendation. However, the lesion is only technically borderline accessible, which narrows the practical applicability of HDREB. The previously untreated palliative recommendations do not apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Ipilimumab is not recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of interferon alpha (IFN-α) for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma outside of a clinical trial is no longer recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma
selected_rules.execute: do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, and isolated limb perfusion are not recommended for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma except as part of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and no BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Nivolumab or pembrolizumab is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma without BRAF V600E or V600K mutations with high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with BRAF V600E or V600K mutations and high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.execute: perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to guide treatment decisions
selected_rules.source_statement: Molecular testing of high-risk melanoma patients to characterize mutations should be conducted to help guide appropriate treatment decisions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with clinically positive lymph nodes before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: perform dissection of lymph nodes in the involved area before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: These trials and recommendations regarding axillary resection do not apply to patients with clinically positive lymph nodes (by palpation or radiologic investigation), and the standard of care is dissection of lymph nodes in that area (axillary, groin, or head and neck) prior to adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab, or targeted therapy if an actionable mutation is identified, as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) or targeted therapy (in patients with identified mutations) are recommended for adjuvant therapy of mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man underwent wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows complete resection with two involved regional lymph nodes, placing him at high risk of recurrence (stage III). He is recovering well and asks about “older immune shots” he read about online. He has no known BRAF V600E or V600K mutation status yet, and no clinical evidence of persistent nodal disease on exam or imaging. He is being seen to discuss adjuvant treatment options after surgery.
recommended_actions: Do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy.
Do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment outside a clinical trial.
Do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guérin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial.
Perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to help guide adjuvant treatment decisions.
source_case.rationale: The patient has completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high-risk features, which triggers the non-recommendations against ipilimumab, interferon alpha, and other older adjuvant approaches. Because he is a high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant therapy, molecular testing is also recommended to determine mutation status and guide treatment selection.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man underwent wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows complete resection with two involved regional lymph nodes, placing him at high risk of recurrence (stage III). He is recovering well and asks about adjuvant treatment options after surgery. He has a history of long-standing autoimmune colitis treated with chronic prednisone, and his oncology note states that immunotherapy would likely carry substantial risk of flare. BRAF V600E/V600K mutation status has not yet been reported, and there is no clinical evidence of persistent nodal disease on exam or imaging.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a completely resected, high-risk stage III melanoma patient after nodal surgery, but the added autoimmune colitis and chronic steroid use make standard adjuvant checkpoint immunotherapy a poor fit.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start adjuvant nivolumab or pembrolizumab now
Discuss older adjuvant immune therapy such as ipilimumab or interferon alpha
Consider a chemotherapy or vaccine-based adjuvant regimen
recommended_actions: Perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to guide treatment decisions.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a resected high-risk melanoma case, but the autoimmune colitis and chronic immunosuppression make routine adjuvant checkpoint therapy clinically problematic. The safer interpretation is to complete mutation testing and reassess adjuvant options in light of the patient’s autoimmune risk rather than reflexively starting standard immunotherapy.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient still meets the general context for molecular testing, but the presence of active autoimmune disease on chronic steroids makes the straightforward application of adjuvant immune checkpoint therapy nonconcordant. The older adjuvant options remain not recommended, but the case no longer cleanly supports the usual adjuvant nivolumab/pembrolizumab recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man underwent wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows the primary lesion is completely resected, but a postoperative PET/CT reveals a solitary 1.4-cm right supraclavicular node that is FDG-avid and clinically palpable. He is otherwise recovering well and asks about “older immune shots” he read about online. BRAF V600E/V600K mutation status is still pending, and the visit is being framed as a discussion of postoperative treatment options after surgery.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The story still centers on a post-excision melanoma patient with stage III-type language and adjuvant treatment questions, but the new palpable imaging-positive node means there is persistent clinically positive nodal disease rather than a fully resected adjuvant setting.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat this as routine adjuvant melanoma and start nivolumab or pembrolizumab
Avoid further nodal surgery and move directly to postoperative systemic therapy
Discuss older adjuvant options such as ipilimumab or interferon alpha
recommended_actions: Perform dissection of lymph nodes in the involved area before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer a straightforward adjuvant scenario because the postoperative PET/CT and exam show a clinically positive node, indicating residual regional nodal disease. The safer interpretation is to address the involved nodal basin surgically before considering adjuvant therapy or radiotherapy.
challenging_examples.rationale: The presence of palpable, radiologically confirmed nodal disease activates the recommendation for lymph node dissection in the involved area before adjuvant treatment. The usual adjuvant systemic recommendations for completely resected disease do not cleanly apply until the clinically positive node is managed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has undergone initial complete resection of GIST
selected_rules.execute: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, there is insufficient evidence to support the use of imatinib as an adjuvant therapy in patients who have undergone initial complete resection of disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering neoadjuvant treatment with imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Do not use neoadjuvant imatinib
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, the use of neoadjuvant imatinib is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
selected_rules.execute: Use imatinib as therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST, treatment with imatinib is a recommended therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: starting imatinib for KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST
selected_rules.execute: Prescribe imatinib 400 mg daily as the initial dose
selected_rules.source_statement: Until additional data become available, the initial dose of imatinib should be prescribed at a dose of 400 mg daily.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with stable disease on imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Continue imatinib until disease progression
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients with stable disease should be treated until disease progression.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient develops serious toxicity on imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Discontinue imatinib
selected_rules.source_statement: Treatment should be discontinued if serious toxicity develops.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient achieves complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Continue imatinib indefinitely until further data define optimal duration
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients who achieve a complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib, treatment should be continued indefinitely until further data is available regarding the optimum duration of therapy in such patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
selected_rules.execute: Apply these recommendations to this population
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent complete surgical resection with negative margins after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology confirmed a low-to-intermediate risk GIST, and postoperative recovery has been uncomplicated. At his oncology follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence. He has no evidence of residual or metastatic disease on postoperative imaging, and the tumor was not being treated as unresectable or metastatic disease. He feels well and is eager to do anything that might lower recurrence risk, but he has not started any systemic therapy yet.
recommended_actions: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy after initial complete resection of GIST.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches the rule for patients who have undergone initial complete resection of GIST. The guideline states there is insufficient evidence to support adjuvant imatinib in this setting, so imatinib should not be used postoperatively.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent laparoscopic wedge resection after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology showed a very low-risk GIST with negative margins, and postoperative recovery has been uncomplicated. At his oncology follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence. He has no evidence of residual or metastatic disease on postoperative imaging, and the tumor was completely removed. He feels well and is eager to do anything that might lower recurrence risk, but he has not started any systemic therapy yet.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This remains a post-resection gastric GIST follow-up with the same question about adjuvant imatinib, but the pathologic risk has been shifted to very low rather than low-to-intermediate.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start adjuvant imatinib to reduce recurrence risk after surgery.
Prescribe imatinib now given the diagnosis of gastric GIST.
Discuss long-term tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy postoperatively.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case still involves a completely resected GIST, but the modification does not create a guideline-supported indication for imatinib. With very low-risk disease and no residual or metastatic disease, the safer interpretation is observation rather than adjuvant treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation against adjuvant imatinib after complete resection still applies, and the lower-risk pathology makes the case even less supportive of postoperative imatinib.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with a large duodenal gastrointestinal stromal tumor is being evaluated by surgical oncology and medical oncology. Cross-sectional imaging shows a 9.5 cm mass abutting the pancreatic head with partial encasement of the superior mesenteric vessels, and the surgeon feels an upfront operation would likely require a pancreaticoduodenectomy with high morbidity. The tumor is KIT (CD117)-positive on biopsy, and she has no distant metastases. The team is considering imatinib before surgery to shrink the mass and make resection easier. She is otherwise healthy and has not received any systemic therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This preserves the same GIST treatment workflow and imatinib discussion, but the tumor is unresectable only in a technical sense because it is considered operable upfront rather than unresectable or metastatic disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start neoadjuvant imatinib to downstage the tumor before surgery.
Begin imatinib 400 mg daily preoperatively.
Use imatinib now to improve surgical resectability.
recommended_actions: Apply the imatinib recommendations to adult patients with unresectable or metastatic KIT-expressing GIST.
Use imatinib 400 mg daily when treating KIT-expressing unresectable or metastatic GIST.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a guideline-concordant neoadjuvant imatinib scenario because the tumor is potentially resectable with upfront surgery, even if the operation would be extensive. The safer interpretation is to prioritize surgical management and not assume a preoperative imatinib indication from this vignette alone.
challenging_examples.rationale: The no-neoadjuvant-imatinib rule is invalidated by the change in intent: the case is about a potentially resectable localized GIST rather than a true unresectable or metastatic setting. The remaining imatinib recommendations for unresectable or metastatic KIT-positive disease do not cleanly apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for allogeneic SCT
selected_rules.execute: Do not use allogeneic stem cell transplantation for AL amyloidosis
selected_rules.source_statement: There is no evidence supporting the use of allogeneic SCT for patients with AL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for treatment including bone marrow or stem cell transplantation
selected_rules.execute: Consider high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation as an option for selected patients, preferably in an investigative setting
selected_rules.source_statement: High-dose chemotherapy (CT) and autologous SCT is an option for selected patients with primary systemic amyloidosis, preferably within an investigative setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for autologous SCT
selected_rules.execute: Use autologous SCT only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals
selected_rules.source_statement: In consideration of the lack of curative treatment options and the limitations of the evidence reviewed, the Expert Panel believes that offering ASCT may be a reasonable option for some patients, depending on performance status, co-morbidities, patient preferences, and ultimate treatment goals.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: The patient selection process and the ultimate decision to perform an SCT should take into account not only disease-related characteristics, but also comorbidities and patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality
selected_rules.source_statement: Careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function is an emerging concept in amyloidosis that should be considered to reduce transplant-related mortality.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area and that first relapse transplantation may be considered as an emerging area pending new evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made on how newer agents affect the need for SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Newer agents are being investigated in the treatment of AL amyloidosis. At this time it is not known how they may impact the need for SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made on the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on transplantation outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with primary systemic AL amyloidosis presents after 5 months of bortezomib-based therapy with partial hematologic response. He has persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild peripheral edema, but no symptomatic heart failure. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild concentric thickening, and his creatinine is stable at 1.1 mg/dL. He remains active, works full-time, and wants the most aggressive treatment that offers the best chance of long-term disease control. His hematologist discusses stem cell transplantation as a next step. Because he has AL amyloidosis and is being evaluated for SCT, the team reviews his organ involvement, overall functional status, comorbidities, and treatment goals before deciding whether he is an appropriate transplant candidate.
recommended_actions: Use autologous stem cell transplantation only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals.
Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences.
Consider careful patient selection based on the degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT, which triggers the rules supporting selective use of autologous SCT and emphasizing individualized assessment. He is clinically stable and functionally well, so the vignette appropriately highlights evaluation of performance status, comorbidities, disease burden, organ function, and patient preferences before proceeding.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with primary systemic AL amyloidosis returns after 5 months of bortezomib-based therapy with a partial hematologic response. He still has nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild ankle edema, but no overt heart failure. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild concentric thickening, and his creatinine remains stable at 1.1 mg/dL. He is active, works full-time, and strongly wants the most aggressive treatment that offers the best chance of long-term disease control. His hematologist discusses stem cell transplantation as a next step. The transplant team confirms that the only available donor option would be a matched sibling donor, and they are asked whether to proceed with transplant evaluation on that basis.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on AL amyloidosis and stem cell transplantation, but the transplant being considered is allogeneic rather than autologous, which changes the guideline applicability.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with stem cell transplantation after routine organ and performance status review
Offer transplant based on his partial response and preserved cardiac and renal function
Base the decision mainly on disease burden, comorbidities, and patient preference
recommended_actions: Use autologous stem cell transplantation only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals
Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences
Consider careful patient selection based on the degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for autologous SCT decision-making because the vignette now involves an allogeneic donor option. The safer interpretation is that allogeneic transplantation is not supported for AL amyloidosis, so the team should not apply autologous SCT selection guidance to this scenario.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation applies to autologous SCT in selected AL amyloidosis patients. Changing the transplant type to allogeneic invalidates that recommendation. However, the general patient-selection principles for autologous SCT remain listed as triggered only if the case is interpreted as considering SCT broadly, but the clinically correct action is to avoid allogeneic SCT.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with primary systemic AL amyloidosis presents after 5 months of bortezomib-based therapy with a partial hematologic response. He has persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild peripheral edema, but no symptomatic heart failure. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild concentric thickening, and his creatinine is stable at 1.1 mg/dL. He remains active, works full-time, and wants the most aggressive treatment that offers the best chance of long-term disease control. His hematologist discusses stem cell transplantation as a next step. During pretransplant assessment, however, he reports recurrent syncope, and the workup reveals advanced cardiac involvement with markedly elevated biomarkers and restrictive physiology despite the preserved ejection fraction.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still appears like a functional AL amyloidosis patient being evaluated for transplant, but the newly identified severe cardiac involvement makes transplant candidacy much less favorable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Move forward with autologous stem cell transplantation because he is active and wants aggressive therapy
Recommend SCT after discussing comorbidities and organ function
Treat the case as a straightforward transplant candidate because kidney function is preserved
recommended_actions: Consider high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation as an option for selected patients, preferably within an investigative setting
Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette no longer fits routine favorable ASCT selection because the pretransplant assessment reveals advanced cardiac amyloid involvement with syncope and restrictive physiology. The safer interpretation is that transplant risk is substantially higher, so ASCT should only be considered, if at all, with extreme caution and specialized evaluation rather than assumed appropriate.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient still has AL amyloidosis and SCT consideration, which keeps the general conditional recommendation in play, but the newly discovered advanced cardiac disease undermines the specific support for selected ASCT candidacy. The case is discordant with the original favorable transplant pathway because organ involvement is now a major limiting factor.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and recurrence after prior radiation therapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use re-irradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: However, re-irradiation is not an option in cases of recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy as equally effective treatment options
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with early (T1) glottic cancer, recommended treatment options include the equally effective endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Choose between surgery and radiation based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition
selected_rules.source_statement: The choice between treatment modalities should be based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and anterior commissure involvement
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy over surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Anterior commissure involvement may be a factor that favours a recommendation of radiotherapy over surgery due to a common opinion that voice outcomes are particularly affected.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and tumor localized to the midportion of the vocal fold with uncompromised endoscopic accessibility
selected_rules.execute: Consider endolaryngeal surgery as an ideal candidate treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Tumours localized to the midportion of the vocal fold, and where endoscopic accessibility is uncompromised, may be considered ideal candidates for surgery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot tolerate a general anaesthetic
selected_rules.execute: Prefer radiation therapy over surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Other important practical considerations include the ability for patients to tolerate a general anaesthetic, which is required for surgery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot cooperate with daily treatment for four to six weeks
selected_rules.execute: Avoid radiation therapy and consider surgery if otherwise appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: In contrast, radiotherapy requires patient cooperation for daily treatment for four to six weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and local recurrence after surgery
selected_rules.execute: Consider partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery, is possible for local recurrence following surgery.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with persistent hoarseness 9 months after completing definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the left true vocal fold. Repeat laryngoscopy and biopsy confirm a small local recurrence confined to the glottis, still staged as early T1 disease. He remains otherwise well and asks whether another course of radiation could treat the recurrence. He is anxious to preserve voice but understands that the lesion is limited and potentially amenable to local salvage treatment.
recommended_actions: Do not use re-irradiation for this recurrence.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with early T1 glottic cancer and recurrence after prior radiation therapy, which triggers the rule that re-irradiation is not an option in cases of recurrence.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man presents with persistent hoarseness 10 months after completing definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the left true vocal fold. Repeat laryngoscopy shows an irregular lesion, and biopsy confirms recurrent disease, but imaging now demonstrates extension into the paraglottic space with subtle impaired vocal cord mobility, making this a small T2 local recurrence rather than a purely glottic T1 lesion. He is otherwise well and asks whether another course of radiation could treat the recurrence. He remains highly motivated to preserve voice and has not yet met with a surgeon.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on recurrent glottic cancer after prior radiation, but the recurrence is no longer limited to early T1 disease because it extends beyond the glottis.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend re-irradiation for the local recurrence
Offer another definitive course of radiation because the lesion is still small
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer the guideline scenario for recurrent early T1 glottic cancer after prior radiation. The new paraglottic extension and reduced mobility upstage the recurrence, so the original no-reirradiation rule for recurrent T1 disease does not directly apply. The safer interpretation is that this now needs multidisciplinary salvage planning rather than reflex reuse of the early-stage recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies only to adult patients with early T1 glottic cancer recurring after prior radiation. Here, the recurrence has progressed to small T2 disease with paraglottic extension, so the source rule is invalidated and no other supplied rule clearly triggers.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old man is newly diagnosed with a left true vocal fold squamous cell carcinoma after several months of hoarseness. Biopsy and staging show a T1 lesion confined to the glottis, with no prior treatment. The tumor is centered in the midportion of the vocal fold and endoscopic exposure is good. He is medically fit and can tolerate a general anesthetic, but he explains that his work schedule makes it impossible to attend daily treatment visits for the next five to six weeks because he will be traveling overseas immediately after diagnosis. He asks what the best initial treatment would be before leaving.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the classic previously untreated T1 glottic cancer and favorable anatomy, but the patient cannot reliably complete a multiweek radiation course.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer radiation therapy as a standard first-line option
Recommend treatment selection based only on voice outcomes, with radiation favored
recommended_actions: Consider endolaryngeal surgery as an ideal candidate treatment
Prefer surgery over radiation because the patient cannot cooperate with daily treatment for four to six weeks
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains an early untreated T1 glottic cancer, but the practical constraint is that he cannot commit to daily radiotherapy. The safer interpretation is that surgery is favored because radiation requires reliable daily attendance, and his midportion lesion with good endoscopic access also fits an ideal surgical candidate profile.
challenging_examples.rationale: R005 and R006 both still apply: the tumor is previously untreated, T1, midportion, and endoscopically accessible, and the patient cannot cooperate with the required daily radiotherapy schedule. Therefore surgery is guideline-concordant over radiation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving conventional fractionation radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not escalate dose beyond 66 Gy
selected_rules.source_statement: Dose escalation beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not use hyperfractionated radiotherapy regimens that do not accelerate the treatment course
selected_rules.source_statement: Hyperformated radiotherapy regimens that do not result in acceleration of the treatment course, even though the total nominal radiotherapy dose may be modestly increased, are not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with unresectable stage III NSCLC is being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: do not routinely give induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Routine use of induction chemotherapy prior to concurrent chemoradiotherapy is not recommended;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being considered for durvalumab after chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: do not exclude durvalumab solely because of EGFR or PD-L1 biomarker status
selected_rules.source_statement: At this time, it is inappropriate to exclude patients from therapy based solely on a biomarker such as epidermal growth factor receptor or programmed death-ligand 1 status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC (N2 or N3) and curative-intent treatment is planned
selected_rules.execute: use concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: Concurrent chemoradiation should be used for curative-intent treatment of patients with unresectable, lymph node-positive (N2 or N3) stage III NSCLC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving concurrent chemoradiation for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: A standard dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy given in fractions of 2 Gy once per day over six weeks is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage III NSCLC and has not progressed after chemoradiation and has no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor
selected_rules.execute: use durvalumab
selected_rules.source_statement: Durvalumab should be used in patients with stage III NSCLC who have not progressed postchemoradiation and have no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is having chemoradiotherapy and surgery for stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: schedule surgery three to five weeks after chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC who are having chemoradiotherapy and surgery, ensure that their surgery is scheduled for three to five weeks after the chemoradiotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with COPD and a 45-pack-year smoking history is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIA NSCLC with biopsy-proven N2 mediastinal lymph node involvement. He is being treated with curative intent and has started concurrent chemoradiation. The radiation oncology plan specifies 60 Gy in 30 once-daily fractions over six weeks with weekly on-treatment review. A medical oncologist asks whether the dose should be escalated to improve local control, but the team is otherwise proceeding with standard concurrent therapy. The patient has not yet completed chemoradiation and has no current contraindications to future immunotherapy.
recommended_actions: Use concurrent chemoradiation for curative-intent treatment of this unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC.
Use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks.
Do not escalate the radiation dose beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches unresectable, node-positive stage III NSCLC planned for curative treatment, which triggers concurrent chemoradiation. Because the patient is receiving concurrent chemoradiation, standard fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy once daily is recommended. The question of dose escalation is also addressed by the rules: conventional fractionation should not be escalated beyond 66 Gy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with COPD and a 45-pack-year smoking history is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIA NSCLC with biopsy-proven N2 mediastinal lymph node involvement. He is being treated with curative intent and has started concurrent chemoradiation. The radiation oncology plan specifies 70 Gy in 35 once-daily fractions over seven weeks because the team hopes to improve local control, with weekly on-treatment review. A medical oncologist asks whether the dose should be escalated further, but the team is otherwise proceeding with standard concurrent therapy. The patient has not yet completed chemoradiation and has no current contraindications to future immunotherapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves unresectable, node-positive stage IIIA NSCLC treated with curative-intent concurrent chemoradiation, but the radiation plan uses a conventional dose above the recommended upper limit.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with concurrent chemoradiation and continue the 70 Gy/35 fraction radiation plan.
Accept further dose escalation to improve local control.
Plan for durvalumab after chemoradiation if he completes treatment without progression.
recommended_actions: Use concurrent chemoradiation for curative-intent treatment of this unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC.
Avoid dose escalation beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation; adjust the plan to standard 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a curative-intent unresectable stage III, N2-positive NSCLC case, so concurrent chemoradiation is appropriate. However, the planned 70 Gy in 35 fractions exceeds the recommended conventional dose range. The safer interpretation is to keep standard fractionation within 60 to 66 Gy rather than escalating the dose.
challenging_examples.rationale: R005 still applies because the patient has unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC and curative-intent treatment is planned. R001 is invalidated because the proposed conventional dose is 70 Gy, which is beyond the recommended ceiling of 66 Gy. R006 does not trigger because the plan is no longer within the recommended standard range.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old woman with controlled asthma is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIA NSCLC involving biopsy-proven N2 disease. She completes concurrent chemoradiation with a standard 60 Gy course and has no radiographic progression afterward. Her tumor testing shows a sensitizing EGFR exon 19 deletion and low PD-L1 expression. At follow-up, the oncology team discusses consolidation treatment and asks whether biomarker results should influence the next step. She has recovered well from chemoradiation and has no autoimmune disease or other contraindication to immune checkpoint therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case closely resembles a routine post-chemoradiation stage III NSCLC follow-up with no progression, but the biomarker profile could tempt a clinician to withhold consolidation immunotherapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Defer durvalumab because the tumor is EGFR-mutated and PD-L1 is low.
Choose observation instead of consolidation immunotherapy based on biomarker status.
Limit post-treatment management to surveillance alone.
recommended_actions: Use durvalumab after chemoradiation because she has stage III NSCLC without progression and no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor.
Do not exclude durvalumab solely because of EGFR mutation or low PD-L1 expression.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This patient still meets the post-chemoradiation stage III NSCLC criterion for durvalumab because she has not progressed and has no contraindication to immune checkpoint therapy. Biomarker status alone should not be used to exclude her from consolidation treatment. The safer interpretation is to proceed with durvalumab if otherwise eligible.
challenging_examples.rationale: R007 applies because she has stage III NSCLC, has not progressed after chemoradiation, and has no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor. R004 is invalidated because the case tests withholding durvalumab solely due to EGFR mutation and low PD-L1, which is not appropriate. The concurrent chemoradiation and standard dose elements remain guideline-concordant but do not alter the post-treatment recommendation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering adding gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen is not recommended for adjuvant chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is older than 65 years and capecitabine is being considered instead of adjuvant AC or CMF
selected_rules.execute: do not use capecitabine as the adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of AC or CMF
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients older than 65 years, capecitabine is not recommended as an adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of adjuvant AC or CMF (oral cyclophosphamide).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen
selected_rules.execute: do not treat ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen as standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours (treated with or without chemotherapy) the addition of ovarian ablation or suppression to tamoxifen is not the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor
selected_rules.execute: do not use ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor as standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours, treated with or without chemotherapy, ovarian ablation or suppression plus five years of an AI is not the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient does not have HER2-positive breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab
selected_rules.source_statement: Only patients with HER2+ breast cancer (IHC 3+, ISH ratio ≥2, or 6+ HER2 gene copies per cell nucleus) should be offered adjuvant trastuzumab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving trastuzumab and has any of the following: LVEF under 55%, documented congestive heart failure, high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias, angina requiring medication, clinically significant valvular disease, transmural infarction on ECG, or poorly controlled hypertension
selected_rules.execute: do not offer trastuzumab
selected_rules.source_statement: trastuzumab should not be offered to patients with any of the following: a left ventricular ejection fraction LVEF of <55%; a history of documented congestive heart failure; high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias; angina pectoris requiring medication; clinically significant valvular disease; evidence of transmural infarction on electrocardiograph (ECG); poorly controlled hypertension.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering Ki-67 for routine risk stratification in early-stage breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter outside clinical trials
selected_rules.source_statement: It is premature to recommend its use as a standard parameter for patient risk stratification, although it may be evaluated in clinical trials.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a trial
selected_rules.execute: do not use intrinsic subtypes outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: At this point, the use of these subtypes in clinical decision making outside of a clinical trial is not recommended.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer has completed surgery and is being seen to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 1+. She is postmenopausal and has no cardiac history. The oncology team is discussing several options, including adding capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen and offering adjuvant trastuzumab because of her high-risk features. A colleague also suggests using Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype results to refine recurrence risk and guide treatment intensity. The patient asks for the most evidence-based plan and wants to avoid unnecessary treatment.
recommended_actions: Do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy.
Do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab because the cancer is not HER2-positive.
Do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter for routine risk stratification outside clinical trials.
Do not use intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a clinical trial.
source_case.rationale: The vignette is designed to trigger rules about adjuvant chemotherapy intensification, HER2-directed therapy, and biomarker use. Because the tumor is HER2-negative, trastuzumab is not recommended. The proposed addition of capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen is also not recommended. Ki-67 and intrinsic subtype testing are mentioned only as decision aids, but both are not recommended for routine clinical decision making outside trials.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer has completed surgery and is being seen to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 2+ with a negative reflex ISH assay. She is postmenopausal and has no cardiac history. The oncology team is discussing several options, including adding capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen and offering adjuvant trastuzumab because of her high-risk features. A colleague also suggests using Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype results to refine recurrence risk and guide treatment intensity. The patient asks for the most evidence-based plan and wants to avoid unnecessary treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still sounds high-risk and includes the same adjuvant decision points, but the HER2 workup is no longer positive because the reflex ISH is negative.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add capecitabine to the planned anthracycline-taxane adjuvant regimen.
Start adjuvant trastuzumab because the tumor appeared HER2-equivocal on IHC.
Use Ki-67 and intrinsic subtype results to select a more intensive adjuvant strategy.
recommended_actions: Do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy.
Do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter for routine risk stratification outside clinical trials.
Do not use intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a clinical trial.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for HER2-directed adjuvant therapy because the tumor is not HER2-positive; a 2+ IHC result with negative ISH does not qualify for trastuzumab. The safer interpretation is to proceed with standard non-HER2-directed adjuvant management while avoiding unvalidated biomarker-based escalation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the original context but changes HER2 status from clearly negative by IHC 1+ to equivocal IHC 2+ with negative ISH. That invalidates adjuvant trastuzumab. The other proposed actions remain non-recommended: adding capecitabine to anthracycline-taxane chemotherapy, and using Ki-67 or intrinsic subtype for routine decision making outside trials.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer has completed surgery and is being seen to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 1+. She is premenopausal, having resumed regular menses after surgery, and has no cardiac history. The oncology team is discussing several options, including adding capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen and offering adjuvant trastuzumab because of her high-risk features. A colleague also suggests using Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype results to refine recurrence risk and guide treatment intensity. The patient asks for the most evidence-based plan and wants to avoid unnecessary treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case remains very close to the source, but the menopausal status has been changed to premenopausal, which alters endocrine intensification considerations, while HER2 remains negative.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane adjuvant regimen.
Offer adjuvant trastuzumab because the disease is node-positive and high risk.
Escalate endocrine therapy with ovarian suppression plus tamoxifen or an aromatase inhibitor as standard treatment.
Use Ki-67 and intrinsic subtype testing to guide adjuvant treatment intensity.
recommended_actions: Do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy.
Do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter for routine risk stratification outside clinical trials.
Do not use intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a clinical trial.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case does not support trastuzumab because HER2 is negative, and it also does not support ovarian suppression plus tamoxifen or ovarian suppression plus an aromatase inhibitor as standard-of-care endocrine intensification in a premenopausal patient. The safer approach is standard adjuvant management without those non-recommended escalations, while avoiding routine use of Ki-67 or intrinsic subtype testing for treatment decisions.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source vignette is modified by changing menopausal status to premenopausal, which makes the endocrine intensification rules relevant but still not recommended as standard of care. HER2 remains negative, so trastuzumab is still inappropriate. Capecitabine addition and biomarker-based risk stratification remain non-recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
selected_rules.source_statement: The care of patients with head and neck cancer should be coordinated among members of an experienced Core Team, comprised of a group of physicians and allied healthcare providers who will be responsible for the assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care
selected_rules.source_statement: The Primary Care Provider will be responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient and will offer supportive care after treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: involve an extended team as needed to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
selected_rules.source_statement: The Extended Team will be called upon by the core team to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: members of the care team
selected_rules.execute: ensure team members have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers
selected_rules.source_statement: Members of the Teams must have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: recognize the primary care physician as not involved in day-to-day treatment but important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health
selected_rules.source_statement: The primary care physician is not involved in the day to day treatment of the head and neck cancer patient but plays an important role in post-treatment supportive care and is responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours
selected_rules.execute: use reconstruction expertise from a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Reconstruction expertise is required for the surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours and necessitates a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: reporting head and neck malignancies
selected_rules.execute: use CAP-CCO standards for pathology reporting
selected_rules.source_statement: Pathologists are advised to use the CAP-CCO standards for reporting head and neck malignancies (5).

selected_rules.activation_condition: reporting nodal dissections
selected_rules.execute: include levels and structures in the specimen, number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of nodes, and presence and location of extracapsular spread
selected_rules.source_statement: The reporting of nodal dissections should include a description of the levels and structures included in the specimen, including number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of these nodes, and the presence and location of extracapsular spread of tumour.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, head and neck surgeon, speech-language pathologist, dietitian, and social worker review his case together. The team discusses definitive treatment planning, swallowing rehabilitation, nutritional support, and survivorship needs. His family physician is informed of the diagnosis and remains responsible for his general medical care and supportive follow-up after cancer treatment. Because he may need surgery depending on response to therapy, the surgeon notes that reconstruction expertise would be required if an operation becomes necessary.
recommended_actions: Coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation.
Ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care.
Recognize that the primary care physician is not involved in day-to-day treatment but remains important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a patient with head and neck cancer, which triggers the general care-coordination recommendations. The multidisciplinary clinic and planned follow-up align with the need for an experienced core team and ongoing primary care involvement. No pathology-reporting or surgical-management-specific rule was selected as a triggered rule.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, head and neck surgeon, speech-language pathologist, dietitian, and social worker review his case together. The team discusses definitive treatment planning, swallowing rehabilitation, nutritional support, and survivorship needs. His family physician is informed of the diagnosis and remains responsible for his general medical care and supportive follow-up after cancer treatment. Because the patient is enrolled in a research protocol at another center, his local team plans to transfer primary treatment planning there, while continuing only supportive coordination and symptom management.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Same head and neck cancer diagnosis, same multidisciplinary clinic structure, and same family physician involvement, but the actual treatment planning is being transferred away from the local experienced core team.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with definitive treatment planning in the local multidisciplinary head and neck clinic
Treat the local team as the responsible core team for assessment, management, and survivorship coordination
Continue routine supportive care through the family physician while the local team directs cancer treatment
recommended_actions: Ensure the primary care provider remains responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care.
Recognize that the primary care physician is not involved in day-to-day treatment but remains important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still a head and neck cancer case, but the local clinic is no longer the team responsible for assessment and treatment planning because primary treatment is being transferred to another center. The safer interpretation is that only the primary care/supportive-care roles clearly remain concordant in this vignette.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original care-coordination recommendation depends on the local experienced core team being responsible for assessment and treatment planning. Here, that role is shifted externally, so the local clinic is not the guideline-concordant coordinating team for definitive management.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, head and neck surgeon, speech-language pathologist, dietitian, and social worker review his case together. The team discusses definitive treatment planning, swallowing rehabilitation, nutritional support, and survivorship needs. His family physician is informed of the diagnosis and remains responsible for his general medical care and supportive follow-up after cancer treatment. The surgeon notes that if an operation becomes necessary, the site’s usual reconstructive coverage is a general plastic surgeon without specific fellowship training in head and neck microvascular reconstruction.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a standard multidisciplinary head and neck cancer referral with possible surgery, but the reconstructive expertise described is not the specialized training required by the rule.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Rely on the available general plastic surgeon for any needed reconstruction
Proceed with surgical planning without arranging specialized head and neck reconstruction expertise
Assume the existing multidisciplinary team is sufficient for operative management
recommended_actions: Coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation.
Ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care.
Recognize that the primary care physician is not involved in day-to-day treatment but remains important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The patient still needs coordinated head and neck cancer care, but the surgical reconstruction statement is not guideline-concordant because the available reconstructive option lacks fellowship-trained microvascular head and neck expertise. If surgery is pursued, specialized reconstruction support should be arranged.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the same disease context and multidisciplinary workflow, but changes the reconstruction detail so it no longer meets the requirement for fellowship-trained microvascular reconstruction expertise in surgical management.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering radiotherapy alone given preoperatively or postoperatively
selected_rules.execute: Do not use radiotherapy alone as it showed no survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Randomized trials demonstrated no survival benefit for radiotherapy given alone, either preoperatively or postoperatively, compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering postoperative chemotherapy alone
selected_rules.execute: Do not use postoperative chemotherapy alone as it showed no survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Randomized trials demonstrated no survival benefit for postoperative chemotherapy given alone compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer and surgery is appropriate
selected_rules.execute: Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality
selected_rules.source_statement: Preoperative cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy is recommended as the preferred modality for the management of surgically resectable patients with esophageal cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer and surgery is appropriate
selected_rules.execute: Consider preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy alone as an alternative treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Preoperative cisplatin-based chemotherapy alone is an alternative choice for the management of surgically resectable patients with esophageal cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering preoperative therapy for resectable esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Prefer preoperative chemoradiotherapy over surgery alone when choosing preoperative treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the majority of the evidence available at this time, the GI DSG believes that preoperative chemoradiotherapy for resectable carcinoma of the esophagus is the preferred approach.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering preoperative therapy for resectable esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that either preoperative chemotherapy or preoperative chemoradiotherapy may provide survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: The GI DSG acknowledges there is evidence indicating survival benefits with either preoperative chemotherapy or chemoradiotherapy compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Before administering preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal
selected_rules.source_statement: Clinicians should recognize that the survival advantage of preoperative therapy may be minimal and a discussion with patients regarding potential adverse effects is required.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Deciding whether to give preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy
