selected_rules.source_statement: Decisions to administer preoperative therapy should be based on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is diagnosed with a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced but resectable tumor with preserved performance status. Thoracic surgery feels he is an appropriate candidate for esophagectomy after neoadjuvant treatment. He has well-controlled hypertension and no major cardiopulmonary disease. In clinic, he asks whether he can proceed directly to surgery or if treatment before surgery would improve his chances. The oncology team is considering preoperative therapy options and discussing expected toxicities and the fact that any survival benefit may be modest. The patient says he values the best chance of cure but wants to understand how treatment might affect swallowing, fatigue, and recovery before making a decision.
recommended_actions: Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality.
Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal.
Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult with resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer for whom surgery is appropriate, triggering the recommendation for preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy. Because preoperative therapy is being considered, the team should discuss adverse effects and recognize that the survival advantage may be minimal. The choice should also be individualized based on preferences, comorbidities, and fitness for trimodality therapy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is found to have a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced tumor. Thoracic surgery reviews the case and feels he could undergo esophagectomy after neoadjuvant treatment, but the patient has severe COPD with frequent exacerbations and a resting oxygen saturation of 88% on room air. He is otherwise motivated for curative treatment and asks whether preoperative therapy would improve his chances. The oncology team is weighing preoperative treatment options and discussing expected toxicities and the fact that any survival benefit may be modest. The patient says he values the best chance of cure but wants to understand how treatment might affect swallowing, fatigue, and recovery before making a decision.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The cancer type, nonmetastatic staging workup, and discussion of preoperative therapy are nearly unchanged, but his severe pulmonary disease makes him a poor candidate for trimodality therapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy followed by esophagectomy.
Recommend neoadjuvant treatment as the preferred curative approach without modifying the plan for comorbidities.
Focus only on expected toxicities and move toward trimodality therapy.
recommended_actions: Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case no longer cleanly fits the recommendation for preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy because the patient is not a good surgical candidate due to severe COPD and hypoxemia. The safer interpretation is that trimodality therapy may not be appropriate, so treatment selection should be individualized around operative fitness and patient goals.
challenging_examples.rationale: The key change is the new severe cardiopulmonary limitation, which undermines suitability for surgery after neoadjuvant treatment. That invalidates the recommendation to use preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy as the preferred modality in an otherwise operable patient. The remaining applicable guidance is to individualize decisions based on preferences, comorbidities, and trimodality suitability.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is diagnosed with a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced but resectable tumor with preserved performance status. Thoracic surgery feels he is an appropriate candidate for esophagectomy after neoadjuvant treatment. He has well-controlled hypertension and no major cardiopulmonary disease. In clinic, he asks whether he can proceed directly to surgery or if treatment before surgery would improve his chances. The oncology team is considering preoperative therapy options and discussing expected toxicities and the fact that any survival benefit may be modest. He has already started a different cancer workup at another institution and wants the least intensive option because he is worried about delaying surgery and is leaning strongly against radiation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still appears to be a standard resectable thoracic esophageal cancer case being considered for preoperative therapy, but the patient’s refusal of radiation and preference for minimal treatment changes the management question.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy as the preferred modality.
Advise that preoperative therapy should proceed despite his preference because surgery is planned.
Discuss toxicities only in the context of starting chemoradiation.
recommended_actions: Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The usual recommendation for preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy is not guideline-concordant here because the patient is not accepting the proposed trimodality approach. The safer interpretation is to individualize treatment around his preferences, with consideration of whether any preoperative therapy is acceptable at all.
challenging_examples.rationale: The clinical scenario remains resectable and potentially curable, but the patient’s firm preference against radiation changes the decision-making. This makes the default recommendation for preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy inappropriate as written. The remaining applicable guidance is to base treatment on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has bone pain from bone metastases and is receiving a BMA
selected_rules.execute: do not use the BMA alone as the only treatment for bone pain
selected_rules.source_statement: The analgesic effects of BMAs are modest, and they should not be used alone for bone pain.

selected_rules.activation_condition: monitoring BMA use with biochemical markers
selected_rules.execute: do not use biochemical markers to monitor BMA use routinely
selected_rules.source_statement: this ASCO guideline update does not alter the prior recommendation (No. 7) that the use of the biochemical markers to monitor BMA use is not recommended for routine care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has breast cancer with evidence of bone metastases
selected_rules.execute: treat with a bone-modifying agent (BMA)
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with breast cancer who have evidence of bone metastases should be treated with BMAs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with denosumab
selected_rules.execute: use denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: Options include denosumab, 120 mg subcutaneously, every 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with pamidronate
selected_rules.execute: use pamidronate 90 mg intravenously every 3 to 4 weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: Options include ... pamidronate, 90 mg intravenously, every 3 to 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with zoledronic acid
selected_rules.execute: use zoledronic acid 4 mg intravenously every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: Options include ... zoledronic acid, 4 mg intravenously every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with bone metastases is receiving zoledronic acid
selected_rules.execute: administer zoledronic acid over no less than 15 minutes
selected_rules.source_statement: If patients are treated with zoledronic acid, 4 mg intravenously administered over no less than 15 minutes, dosing options are every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has bone pain from bone metastases
selected_rules.execute: apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management
selected_rules.source_statement: The Update Committee recommends that the current standard of care for supportive care and pain management-analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management-be applied.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with ER-positive breast cancer presents with worsening deep aching pain in her pelvis and lower back. Imaging shows multiple lytic bone metastases without spinal cord compression. She is already receiving denosumab for skeletal-related event prevention. Despite the agent, her pain has progressed over the past month and is limiting sleep and walking. She has been using acetaminophen with only partial relief. Her oncology team is considering additional pain-directed treatment and referral for symptom control. She asks whether the bone-targeted therapy alone should be enough for the pain.
recommended_actions: Do not use the bone-modifying agent alone as the only treatment for her bone pain.
Apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management.
source_case.rationale: She has bone pain from bone metastases and is receiving a bone-modifying agent, which triggers the recommendation against using the BMA alone for pain control. Her pain from bone metastases also triggers the recommendation to use standard supportive care and pain management measures.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with ER-positive breast cancer reports worsening deep aching pain in her lower back and pelvis over the past several weeks. Recent imaging shows multiple sclerotic bone metastases without spinal cord compression or impending fracture. She has been receiving denosumab every 4 weeks for skeletal-related event prevention. The pain is now limiting sleep and walking, and acetaminophen has provided only partial relief. Her oncology team is discussing whether the current bone-targeted therapy is sufficient for symptom control and whether any additional pain-directed treatment is needed at this visit. She is otherwise tolerating treatment well and asks whether a change in the denosumab schedule would help the discomfort.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still features breast cancer, bone metastases, denosumab, and worsening pain, but the pain is not from metastatic bone disease progression and the imaging shows no active osseous lesion-related pain trigger; the main issue is chronic musculoskeletal pain rather than bone pain requiring the bone-agent recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Continue denosumab alone for pain control and adjust the schedule if needed.
Assume the bone-modifying agent should be the primary treatment for her current pain.
Delay other symptom-directed measures until the denosumab dose is changed.
recommended_actions: Apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for using a bone-modifying agent as the sole treatment for bone pain, because the pain description and imaging are more consistent with non-acute chronic pain without a clear metastatic pain driver. The safer interpretation is to treat it as a symptom-management problem and reassess for other causes rather than escalating denosumab for analgesia.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires bone pain from bone metastases while receiving a BMA. Here, the metastatic lesions are sclerotic and there is no clear progression or structural complication causing the pain, so the BMA-alone pain recommendation is not triggered. Supportive care for pain remains appropriate under R008.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with ER-positive breast cancer has progressive aching pain in her pelvis and lower back. Imaging demonstrates multiple lytic bone metastases, but there is no spinal cord compression and no impending fracture. She has been receiving denosumab for skeletal-related event prevention. Despite this, her pain has worsened over the past month and is limiting sleep and walking. She has used acetaminophen with only partial relief. Her oncology team is considering additional pain-directed treatment and referral for symptom control. She asks whether the bone-targeted therapy alone should be enough for the pain. A chart review also shows her recent labs are being followed with serial serum C-terminal telopeptide levels to monitor treatment response.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The core metastatic breast cancer pain scenario is unchanged, but the vignette adds routine biochemical marker monitoring, which can tempt a reader to focus on denosumab management rather than the specific guideline limitations.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the bone-modifying agent alone for her pain and continue serial biochemical marker monitoring of response.
Rely on CTX levels to guide denosumab effectiveness.
Treat the worsening pain by changing the bone-targeted agent rather than adding symptom-directed measures.
recommended_actions: Apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case still supports supportive care for metastatic bone pain, but it does not support using the bone-modifying agent alone as pain treatment. In addition, routine biochemical marker monitoring for BMA use is not recommended, so serial CTX testing should not be used for routine management decisions.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies only when a BMA is being used as the only treatment for bone pain from bone metastases; that remains inappropriate here. R002 is also triggered because the vignette includes routine biochemical marker monitoring of BMA use, which is not recommended. R008 still applies because she has bone pain from bone metastases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering changing management based on HPV test results outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing except in the context of a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Altering management decisions based on results from HPV testing is not recommended beyond the context of a clinical trial at this time.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using HPV PCR testing alone
selected_rules.execute: Avoid qualitative HPV PCR assay detection alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Qualitative HPV PCR assay detection alone should be avoided.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
selected_rules.execute: Routinely test the tumour for HPV status
selected_rules.source_statement: The tumours of all adult patients presenting with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinomas should be routinely tested for HPV status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has metastatic squamous cell carcinoma to neck nodes from an unknown head and neck primary
selected_rules.execute: Routinely test the neck nodal tissue for HPV status
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that the neck nodal tissue of patients with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma to neck nodes from an unknown head and neck primary be routinely tested for HPV status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV testing is being performed for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
selected_rules.execute: Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that HPV status in oropharyngeal SCC be initially determined using immunohistochemical (IHC) staining for p16.

selected_rules.activation_condition: p16 staining shows cytoplasmic and nuclear staining, is moderate to strong and diffuse, and is present in at least 70% of tumour cells
selected_rules.execute: Consider the p16 result positive
selected_rules.source_statement: IHC staining for p16 can be considered positive when the following three criteria are met: cytoplasmic and nuclear staining; staining is moderate to strong and diffuse; staining is present in at least 70% of tumour cells.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV testing is being done in the pathology laboratory
selected_rules.execute: Perform analysis on sections from paraffin blocks or unstained slides cut at 4 microns
selected_rules.source_statement: Analysis should be performed on sections from paraffin blocks or unstained slides cut at 4 microns.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV-status testing is being performed
selected_rules.execute: Carry out testing in licensed and accredited laboratories
selected_rules.source_statement: All testing should be carried out in licensed and accredited laboratories.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive sore throat, dysphagia to solids, and a painless left neck mass. Biopsy of the tonsillar lesion shows squamous cell carcinoma, and the pathology team plans HPV-status testing to help characterize the tumor. The specimen is being processed in a licensed, accredited laboratory using paraffin-embedded tissue sections cut at 4 microns. Initial p16 immunohistochemical staining shows diffuse, moderate-to-strong cytoplasmic and nuclear staining in about 80% of tumour cells. The oncologist asks whether the HPV result should change the treatment plan immediately.
recommended_actions: Routinely test the tumour for HPV status because this is an adult with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status.
Consider the p16 result positive because the staining is cytoplasmic and nuclear, moderate to strong and diffuse, and present in at least 70% of tumour cells.
Perform the testing on paraffin-block sections or unstained slides cut at 4 microns in a licensed and accredited laboratory.
Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing outside the context of a clinical trial.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches adult oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, so routine HPV testing is indicated. Because HPV testing is being performed for oropharyngeal SCC, p16 IHC is the recommended initial test. The described staining pattern meets the positivity criteria. The laboratory and specimen handling also match the testing recommendations. Although the HPV result is available, management should not be changed on the basis of HPV testing outside a clinical trial.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive sore throat, dysphagia to solids, and a painless left neck mass. Biopsy of the tonsillar lesion shows squamous cell carcinoma, and the pathology team plans HPV-status testing to help characterize the tumor. The specimen is being processed in a licensed, accredited laboratory using paraffin-embedded tissue sections cut at 4 microns. Initial p16 immunohistochemical staining shows diffuse, moderate-to-strong cytoplasmic and nuclear staining in about 62% of tumour cells. The oncologist asks whether the HPV result should change the treatment plan immediately.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a typical oropharyngeal SCC with appropriate HPV testing workflow, but the p16 staining falls just below the positivity threshold.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat the p16 result as positive and proceed with HPV-associated tumor assumptions.
Use the HPV result to adjust treatment intensity right away.
Document the tumor as HPV-positive based on the diffuse staining pattern.
recommended_actions: Routinely test the tumour for HPV status because this is an adult with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status.
Perform the testing on paraffin-block sections or unstained slides cut at 4 microns in a licensed and accredited laboratory.
Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing outside the context of a clinical trial.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains an adult oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, so HPV testing is appropriate, but the p16 stain is not positive because it is present in only 62% of tumor cells, below the 70% cutoff. The safer interpretation is that the result is indeterminate or negative by this criterion and should not be overcalled as HPV-positive.
challenging_examples.rationale: Adult oropharyngeal SCC still triggers routine HPV testing, and p16 IHC is still the initial test in this setting. However, the modified staining proportion fails the positivity threshold, so the original positive interpretation no longer applies. Testing conditions remain appropriate, and management should not be changed based on HPV testing outside a trial.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive sore throat, dysphagia to solids, and a painless left neck mass. Biopsy of the tonsillar lesion shows squamous cell carcinoma, and the pathology team plans HPV-status testing to help characterize the tumor. The specimen is being processed in a licensed, accredited laboratory using a cytology smear from a fine-needle aspirate rather than paraffin-embedded tissue or unstained 4-micron sections. Initial p16 immunohistochemical staining shows diffuse, moderate-to-strong cytoplasmic and nuclear staining in about 80% of tumour cells. The oncologist asks whether the HPV result should change the treatment plan immediately.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The tumor type, site, and p16 pattern remain the same, but the specimen type is not the recommended histologic preparation for this testing workflow.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Accept the HPV workup as complete using the current smear specimen.
Call the p16 result positive and use it to guide treatment decisions now.
Proceed without further tissue-based testing because the staining pattern is convincing.
recommended_actions: Routinely test the tumour for HPV status because this is an adult with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status.
Carry out testing in licensed and accredited laboratories.
Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing outside the context of a clinical trial.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still an adult oropharyngeal SCC, so HPV testing is appropriate, and the p16 pattern meets positivity criteria. The discordant detail is the specimen preparation: a cytology smear is not the recommended paraffin-block section or 4-micron unstained slide. The safer interpretation is that the testing workflow is not guideline-concordant and should be repeated on appropriate tissue if HPV status is needed.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case continues to meet the indication for routine HPV testing in adult oropharyngeal SCC, and p16 IHC remains the initial test. The p16 staining is positive by pattern and proportion. However, the specimen is not one of the recommended preparations, so the pathology workflow is not guideline-concordant. Management should still not be altered based on HPV testing outside a trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering PET-CT during follow-up
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET-CT for routine follow-up surveillance.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Positron emission tomography (PET) with computed tomography (PET-CT),

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering other imaging tests in an asymptomatic patient
selected_rules.execute: Do not use other imaging tests routinely in asymptomatic patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Other imaging or biomarker tests in asymptomatic patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering biomarker tests in an asymptomatic patient
selected_rules.execute: Do not use biomarker tests routinely in asymptomatic patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Other imaging or biomarker tests in asymptomatic patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient was treated with radiotherapy and is in follow-up beyond five years
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform cytological follow-up.
selected_rules.source_statement: Cytological follow-up is not recommended for patients who have been treated with radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is clinically disease-free and asymptomatic after potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use this follow-up guideline for surveillance after primary treatment; do not apply it to women treated for cervical precancer.
selected_rules.source_statement: This practice guideline applies to women who are clinically disease free and asymptomatic after receiving potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer. This guideline does not apply to the follow-up of women who have been treated for cervical precancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: providing follow-up care after primary treatment for cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
selected_rules.source_statement: Follow-up care after primary treatment should be conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in the surveillance of patients with cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: during follow-up care after primary treatment
selected_rules.execute: Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
selected_rules.source_statement: Continuity of care and dialogue between the healthcare professional and patient about symptoms of recurrence may enhance and facilitate early cancer recurrence detection because the majority of women who develop a recurrence have symptoms and signs that occur outside scheduled follow-up visits.

selected_rules.activation_condition: follow-up within the first 5 years after treatment
selected_rules.execute: Schedule visits every 3 to 4 months during years 1 to 2, then every 6 to 12 months during years 3 to 5.
selected_rules.source_statement: A reasonable follow-up strategy involves visits at the following intervals: o every three to four months within the first two years, o every six to 12 months from years 3 to 5.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman returns for surveillance 18 months after completing chemoradiation for stage IIB cervical cancer. She is clinically disease-free, reports no vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, weight loss, or bowel/bladder changes, and her exam is unremarkable. Her oncologist is discussing whether to order a PET-CT because she feels anxious about recurrence, but the patient has no symptoms suggestive of relapse. The team reviews her follow-up plan and notes that she is still within the first two years after treatment. She asks how often she should be seen and what warning signs should prompt an earlier visit.
recommended_actions: Do not use PET-CT for routine follow-up surveillance.
Use this follow-up guideline for surveillance after primary treatment; do not apply it to women treated for cervical precancer.
Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
Schedule visits every 3 to 4 months during years 1 to 2, then every 6 to 12 months during years 3 to 5.
source_case.rationale: She is an asymptomatic, clinically disease-free patient after potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer, so the surveillance guideline applies. Because she is considering PET-CT during routine follow-up, that test is not recommended. Follow-up should be coordinated by a clinician experienced in cancer surveillance, with ongoing communication about recurrence symptoms. Since she is within the first two years after treatment, visits every 3 to 4 months are appropriate.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman returns for surveillance 18 months after completing chemoradiation for stage IIB cervical cancer. She is clinically disease-free and has no vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, weight loss, or bowel/bladder changes. Her pelvic exam is unremarkable. At the visit, her oncologist notes that she is asking for a PET-CT because of anxiety about recurrence, and the team is considering whether imaging should be added since her last scan showed stable post-radiation changes. She is still within the first two years after treatment and asks how often she should be seen going forward.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Nearly identical surveillance visit after curative treatment for cervical cancer, still asymptomatic and within the first two years, but the follow-up discussion now includes a prior scan and the patient is requesting imaging rather than simply routine surveillance.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a PET-CT to reassure the patient and evaluate for occult recurrence.
Continue standard cervical cancer surveillance visits every 3 to 4 months.
Review recurrence symptoms and maintain coordinated oncology follow-up.
recommended_actions: Use this follow-up guideline for surveillance after primary treatment; do not apply it to women treated for cervical precancer.
Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
Schedule visits every 3 to 4 months during years 1 to 2, then every 6 to 12 months during years 3 to 5.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains an asymptomatic post-treatment surveillance case for cervical cancer, so the follow-up guideline still applies, but the PET-CT is not guideline-concordant because routine PET-CT surveillance is not recommended in this setting. The safer interpretation is continued clinical follow-up without routine imaging.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient is clinically disease-free after potentially curative treatment and is within the first 2 years, so routine surveillance intervals and symptom review apply. The added PET-CT consideration is the discordant element because routine PET-CT is not advocated for surveillance.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 44-year-old woman presents 7 months after definitive surgery and adjuvant radiotherapy for microinvasive cervical adenocarcinoma found on a cone biopsy, with final pathology showing no residual invasive disease. She is asymptomatic, has a normal pelvic exam, and asks whether she should get a PET-CT because she is worried about recurrence. Her new gynecology provider is reviewing a follow-up plan and notes that she has been doing well since treatment. The visit is being coordinated in a general community clinic, and she asks how frequently she needs follow-up over the next few years.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like routine post-treatment surveillance for cervical cancer with a patient requesting PET-CT, but the final diagnosis was microinvasive disease found on cone biopsy rather than potentially curative treatment for invasive cervical cancer.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order PET-CT for routine surveillance because she is worried about recurrence.
Follow the standard cervical cancer surveillance schedule every 3 to 4 months.
Reassure her using the invasive cervical cancer follow-up guideline.
recommended_actions: Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette no longer cleanly fits the surveillance guideline for clinically disease-free survivors of potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer because her original disease was microinvasive and managed from a cone biopsy context. Routine PET-CT still is not appropriate, but the specific follow-up schedule from the invasive cancer guideline should not be automatically applied; the safer approach is individualized follow-up based on her actual pathology and treatment history.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient is asymptomatic, so routine PET-CT remains unsupported. However, the source guideline is intended for women after potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer, not precancer or very limited microinvasive disease managed differently. Therefore the standard surveillance schedule should not be assumed to apply unchanged.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient at elevated risk of clinically significant prostate cancer and a candidate for curative management with suspected clinically localized prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: Perform multiparametric MRI prior to biopsy
selected_rules.source_statement: MPMRI is recommended prior to biopsy in patients who are candidates for curative management with suspected clinically localized prostate cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient has a positive multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.execute: Perform multiparametric MRI-targeted biopsy together with transrectal ultrasound systematic biopsy
selected_rules.source_statement: If the MPMRI is positive, MPMRI-TB and TRUS-SB should be performed together to maximize detection of CSPCa.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient had a prior negative transrectal ultrasound systematic biopsy and is at high risk of clinically significant prostate cancer with curative management being considered
selected_rules.execute: Perform multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients who had a prior negative TRUS-SB and demonstrate a high risk of having CSPCa in whom curative management is being considered: MPMRI should be performed,

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient had a prior negative transrectal ultrasound systematic biopsy and multiparametric MRI is positive
selected_rules.execute: Perform targeted biopsy
selected_rules.source_statement: If the MPMRI is positive, targeted biopsy should be performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient has a negative multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.execute: Consider forgoing biopsy after discussing risks and benefits with the patient and arranging ongoing follow-up
selected_rules.source_statement: If the MPMRI is negative, consider forgoing any biopsy after discussion of the risks and benefits with the patient as part of shared decision making and ongoing followup.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Biopsy-naive patient has a negative multiparametric MRI
selected_rules.execute: Inform the patient that a negative multiparametric MRI may miss clinically significant prostate cancer
selected_rules.source_statement: Between 8% and 24% of patients with CSPCa may be missed by a negative MPMRI.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering multiparametric MRI for a biopsy-naive patient
selected_rules.execute: Use multiparametric MRI only if high-quality interpretation is available and operators have experience performing targeted biopsies
selected_rules.source_statement: MPMRI should only be performed if there is availability of high-quality MPMRI interpretation and operators with experience performing targeted biopsies (see Recommendation 3).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering multiparametric MRI for a biopsy-naive patient
selected_rules.execute: Use multiparametric MRI only when there is intent for curative management if biopsy is positive
selected_rules.source_statement: Due to the limited availability, MPMRI is recommended only for patients where there is intent of curative management should the biopsy be positive for CSPCa.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with a PSA of 11.8 ng/mL and an abnormal digital rectal exam showing mild left-sided induration. He has never had a prostate biopsy. He is otherwise healthy, has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years, and says he would want curative treatment if clinically significant prostate cancer is found. His urologist considers him at elevated risk for clinically significant, clinically localized prostate cancer and is planning diagnostic workup before biopsy.
recommended_actions: Perform multiparametric MRI prior to biopsy.
source_case.rationale: This biopsy-naive patient has elevated risk of clinically significant prostate cancer and is a candidate for curative management with suspected clinically localized disease, which matches the indication for pre-biopsy multiparametric MRI. No additional rule is triggered because MRI results are not yet known.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with a PSA of 11.8 ng/mL and an abnormal digital rectal exam showing mild left-sided induration. He has never had a prostate biopsy. He is otherwise healthy, has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years, and says he would want curative treatment if clinically significant prostate cancer is found. His urologist considers him at elevated risk for clinically significant, clinically localized prostate cancer and is planning diagnostic workup before biopsy. The practice site has a prostate MRI service, but the images are routinely interpreted by general radiology staff with limited prostate-specific experience, and the center does not have an established MRI-targeted biopsy program.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like a biopsy-naive, high-risk patient being evaluated for localized disease with curative intent, but the local MRI workflow lacks the quality and operator expertise needed to support the recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a multiparametric prostate MRI before biopsy to guide the workup.
Proceed with MRI-first evaluation and then decide on biopsy based on the imaging findings.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for pre-biopsy multiparametric MRI because the recommendation depends on access to high-quality MRI interpretation and experienced operators for targeted biopsy. The safer interpretation is to avoid assuming MRI is appropriate at this site and to pursue an alternative diagnostic pathway or referral to an experienced center.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient still meets the clinical risk and curative-intent features, but the local system fails the prerequisite of high-quality interpretation and operator experience. That invalidates the MRI-before-biopsy recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 71-year-old man is referred for evaluation of a rising PSA of 9.6 ng/mL and a suspicious left apical nodule on digital rectal exam. He has never had a prostate biopsy. He is active and otherwise healthy, but he says that if cancer is diagnosed he would prefer observation and would not pursue surgery or radiation because of concerns about treatment side effects. His urologist notes that he is at elevated risk for clinically significant prostate cancer and is considering imaging before biopsy. The patient is willing to undergo testing only if it would directly change management, but he is not interested in curative therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still features a biopsy-naive man with a suspicious PSA and exam, so it is easy to assume pre-biopsy MRI is indicated, but his treatment preferences remove the curative-intent condition.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Obtain a multiparametric MRI before biopsy.
Use MRI to refine biopsy planning in this elevated-risk patient.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is discordant with the pre-biopsy MRI recommendation because multiparametric MRI is intended for patients in whom there is intent for curative management if clinically significant cancer is found. Since he would not pursue curative treatment, the safer interpretation is that MRI is not clearly indicated for this purpose and the workup should be aligned with his goals of care.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although the patient is biopsy-naive and at elevated risk, he lacks intent for curative management, which invalidates the MRI-before-biopsy indication. No other listed recommendation applies.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult or paediatric patient after allogeneic transplant with steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory acute GVHD
selected_rules.execute: Consider extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: ECP is an acceptable therapy for the treatment of steroid-dependent / refractory acute GVHD in adult and paediatric patients

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult or paediatric patient after allogeneic transplant with steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory chronic GVHD
selected_rules.execute: Use extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: ECP is an effective therapy for the treatment of steroid-dependent / refractory chronic GVHD in adult and paediatric patients

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has steroid-refractory acute GVHD with primarily refractory skin involvement
selected_rules.execute: Consider extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with primarily refractory skin GVHD should be considered for ECP treatment

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has steroid-refractory GVHD and meets Ontario eligibility criteria
selected_rules.execute: Note that ECP is a covered therapy in Ontario
selected_rules.source_statement: ECP is currently a covered therapy in Ontario for patients with steroid refractory GVHD who meet certain eligibility criteria

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old woman is 74 days after matched unrelated donor allogeneic stem cell transplant for acute myeloid leukemia. She developed diffuse erythematous rash, watery diarrhea, and mild transaminitis consistent with acute GVHD. Despite 10 days of high-dose prednisone, her diarrhea improved only slightly and the rash persists, so the transplant team considers her acute GVHD steroid-refractory. She remains hemodynamically stable and has no active infection. Because the GVHD is not responding adequately to steroids, the team is discussing next-line therapy.
recommended_actions: Consider extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as therapy for steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory acute GVHD.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult after allogeneic transplant with steroid-refractory acute GVHD, which directly matches R001. No additional rules were triggered because chronic GVHD is not present, skin-predominant refractory disease is not the primary focus, and Ontario coverage criteria were not specified.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old woman is 74 days after matched unrelated donor allogeneic stem cell transplant for acute myeloid leukemia. She developed diffuse erythematous rash, watery diarrhea, and mild transaminitis after transplant, but a recent endoscopic biopsy of the colon instead showed CMV colitis with only minimal apoptotic change and no convincing acute GVHD. She has been on high-dose prednisone for 10 days with only modest improvement in symptoms, and the transplant team is debating additional therapy. She remains hemodynamically stable, but her diarrhea is now better explained by the biopsy-proven viral colitis, which is being treated with ganciclovir.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The timing after allogeneic transplant, ongoing diarrhea, rash, and incomplete response to steroids closely resemble steroid-refractory acute GVHD, but the dominant process is biopsy-confirmed CMV colitis rather than active acute GVHD.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with extracorporeal photopheresis for presumed steroid-refractory acute GVHD
Escalate GVHD-directed therapy because the symptoms are not fully resolving on prednisone
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for ECP in steroid-refractory acute GVHD because the current symptoms are better explained by CMV colitis on biopsy rather than confirmed active GVHD. The safer interpretation is to treat the infectious colitis and reassess for GVHD only if compatible features persist.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory acute GVHD. Here, the apparent GVHD flare is undermined by biopsy evidence of CMV colitis and lack of convincing acute GVHD, so the trigger is not met.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old woman is 74 days after matched unrelated donor allogeneic stem cell transplant for acute myeloid leukemia. She developed a diffuse erythematous rash, watery diarrhea, and mild transaminitis consistent with acute GVHD. After 10 days of high-dose prednisone, the diarrhea has improved and the rash is fading, but she has developed severe steroid-induced hyperglycemia and proximal myopathy. Because of these toxicities, the team is tapering prednisone and considering whether to add another agent before the GVHD worsens. She remains afebrile, with no active infection and no new organ involvement.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The post-transplant setting, acute GVHD symptoms, and steroid complications make the case look like steroid-refractory disease, but the GVHD itself is improving rather than failing to respond to steroids.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start extracorporeal photopheresis for steroid-refractory acute GVHD
Escalate to second-line GVHD therapy because prednisone toxicity is limiting treatment
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for ECP as treatment of steroid-refractory acute GVHD because the patient is responding to prednisone; the issue is steroid toxicity, not refractory GVHD. A safer interpretation is to continue guided tapering and monitor closely rather than labeling the disease steroid-refractory.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies to steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory acute GVHD. Improvement in diarrhea and rash means the disease is not refractory, so the recommendation is not triggered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: tumour pathology creates doubt
selected_rules.execute: Have the pathology reviewed by an expert
selected_rules.source_statement: A tumour pathology causing doubt should be reviewed by an expert.

selected_rules.activation_condition: restaging of a sub-optimally staged patient reveals more advanced disease
selected_rules.execute: Use chemotherapy as the preferred treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: If the restaging of a sub-optimally staged patient reveals a more advanced disease, chemotherapy is the preferred treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: lymph node sampling was not conducted as part of staging surgery
selected_rules.execute: Favor adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: A subgroup analysis of one randomized controlled trial demonstrated that if lymph node sampling is not conducted as part of the staging surgery then adjuvant chemotherapy is favoured in terms of overall survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has undergone optimal surgical staging including pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling and has stage I disease
selected_rules.execute: Consider adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have undergone optimal surgical staging, including pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling, and have stage I disease may or may not benefit from adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has not undergone optimal surgical staging
selected_rules.execute: Offer re-operation to optimally define tumour stage and then give adjuvant therapy based on findings
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have not undergone optimal surgical staging can be offered two options. The first option is that they undergo re-operation to optimally define the tumour stage and then be offered adjuvant therapy based on the findings.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has not undergone optimal surgical staging
selected_rules.execute: Offer platinum-based chemotherapy to decrease recurrence risk and improve survival
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have not undergone optimal surgical staging can be offered two options. The other option is that they be offered platinum-based chemotherapy to decrease the risk of recurrence and improve survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: reoperation confirms stage I disease after suboptimal staging
selected_rules.execute: Base the adjuvant chemotherapy decision on discussion with the patient about potential benefits and risks
selected_rules.source_statement: The treatment decision must be based on a discussion with the patient about potential benefits and risks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering adjuvant pelvic radiation
selected_rules.execute: Do not make a recommendation because evidence is insufficient
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to make a recommendation on the role of adjuvant pelvic radiation, whole abdominal-pelvic radiotherapy, or intraperitoneal radioactive chromic phosphate.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman underwent surgery for a 4.2 cm epithelial ovarian tumor found incidentally during evaluation for bloating. The operative report notes hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, but no pelvic or para-aortic lymph node sampling was performed, so the staging was suboptimal. Final pathology suggests stage I disease, but the report also states that the tumor morphology is somewhat unusual and the pathologist is not fully confident about the exact histologic subtype. She is otherwise recovering well and asks whether she needs more treatment now or whether additional surgery would help clarify the stage.
recommended_actions: Have the pathology reviewed by an expert.
Offer re-operation to optimally define the tumour stage and then give adjuvant therapy based on findings.
Offer platinum-based chemotherapy to decrease recurrence risk and improve survival.
source_case.rationale: The pathology report explicitly creates doubt about the tumour diagnosis, which triggers expert review. The patient has not undergone optimal surgical staging because lymph node sampling was omitted, which triggers the options of re-operation for definitive staging or platinum-based chemotherapy. These recommendations are directly supported by the selected rules.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman recovered from surgery for a 4.2 cm epithelial ovarian tumor found incidentally during workup for bloating. The operative note documents hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with no pelvic or para-aortic lymph node sampling, so the staging was incomplete. Final pathology is read as stage I disease, and the report says the histologic subtype is a serous ovarian tumor, but the pathologist notes some atypical features and asks whether a second opinion is needed. She is clinically well and asks whether more treatment is necessary now or whether a second operation would help clarify the extent of disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still has incomplete staging and the same postoperative decision point, but the histology is now fairly well characterized and only the staging problem remains.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Send the pathology for expert review because the subtype is uncertain.
Offer re-operation to complete staging and then decide on adjuvant treatment.
Start platinum-based chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk.
recommended_actions: Offer re-operation to optimally define the tumour stage and then give adjuvant therapy based on findings.
Offer platinum-based chemotherapy to decrease recurrence risk and improve survival.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still a suboptimally staged epithelial ovarian tumor, so re-operation or platinum-based chemotherapy remain appropriate options. However, the pathology itself no longer clearly creates diagnostic doubt; the pathologist is asking only about atypical features, not an uncertain tumor diagnosis. The safer interpretation is that expert pathology review is not specifically triggered by the available details.
challenging_examples.rationale: Incomplete staging persists because lymph node sampling was not performed, which supports re-operation or chemotherapy. The original pathology-doubt trigger is weakened because the subtype is already assigned and the concern is not enough to make the diagnosis uncertain.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman underwent surgery for a 4.2 cm epithelial ovarian tumor discovered during evaluation for bloating. The operative report notes hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling, and omentectomy, and the surgeon documented optimal staging. Final pathology shows stage IA disease with no surface involvement. The pathologist mentions that the morphology is somewhat unusual and asks for expert review to confirm the exact histologic subtype. She has recovered well and wants to know whether she needs more treatment or whether another surgery would help clarify anything further.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The tumor size, surgery type, and pathology uncertainty are very similar to the source, but the staging is now complete and clearly stage I.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer re-operation to complete surgical staging.
Start platinum-based chemotherapy because the staging was incomplete.
Ignore the pathology uncertainty and proceed directly to surveillance.
recommended_actions: Have the pathology reviewed by an expert.
Consider adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The patient has undergone optimal surgical staging, so the suboptimal-staging pathways no longer apply. The remaining guideline-concordant issues are expert review of the uncertain pathology and consideration of adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy in a fully staged stage I epithelial ovarian cancer, depending on risk discussion.
challenging_examples.rationale: Expert pathology review is still triggered by diagnostic uncertainty. Because pelvic and para-aortic nodal sampling were done and staging is optimal, the re-operation and ‘chemotherapy because staging was incomplete’ recommendations are not applicable; only the stage I adjuvant chemotherapy consideration remains.

selected_rules.activation_condition: primary care or community setting and need to improve breast or cervical cancer screening access
selected_rules.execute: Use NPs in alternate provider roles to improve access to breast and cervical cancer screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: In primary care and community-based settings, NPs working in alternate provider roles can be utilized to improve access to breast and cervical cancer screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: need for Pap smear screening
selected_rules.execute: Allow NPs to perform Pap smears as alternate providers to physicians.
selected_rules.source_statement: Two studies demonstrated that the quality of Papanicolaou (Pap) smears conducted by NPs was not detectably different and, in some cases, was superior to physicians (23,24).

selected_rules.activation_condition: need for esophagoscopy, flexible sigmoidoscopy, or colonoscopy for cancer screening
selected_rules.execute: Use NPs as alternate providers to physicians for esophagoscopy, flexible sigmoidoscopy, and colonoscopy.
selected_rules.source_statement: As alternate providers to physicians, NPs can provide safe and effective care in performing esophagoscopy, flexible sigmoidoscopy, and colonoscopy for cancer screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman with cervical dysplasia needing colposcopy-guided biopsy to diagnose cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use NPs as alternate providers to physicians for colposcopy-guided biopsies.
selected_rules.source_statement: For women with cervical dysplasia, NPs are an appropriate alternate provider to physicians in performing colposcopy-guided biopsies to diagnose cervical cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed cancer patient receiving surgery or radiation therapy and CNS-led alternate model is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Use CNS-led care as an appropriate alternate model to physician care.
selected_rules.source_statement: Clinical nurse specialist (CNS)-led care is an appropriate alternate model to care provided by physicians, particularly for newly diagnosed patients undergoing surgery or radiation therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: new diagnosis of cancer and post cancer surgery or receiving chemotherapy or radiation treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider adding complementary CNS care to usual care to improve psychological and mental well-being and possibly survival.
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of complementary CNS care to usual care may improve psychological and mental well-being and survival for patients with a new diagnosis of cancer who are post cancer surgery or receiving chemotherapy or radiation treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast or colorectal cancer follow-up and telephone follow-up is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Consider CNS- or NP-delivered telephone follow-up as a safe and acceptable alternate model to outpatient physician follow-up.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with breast and colorectal cancer, CNS-or NP-delivered telephone follow-up may provide a safe and acceptable alternate model to outpatient clinic follow-up care provided mostly by physicians.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman following breast cancer treatment with menopausal symptoms
selected_rules.execute: Consider a complementary NP-led comprehensive assessment and intervention program to reduce menopausal symptoms.
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of a complementary and comprehensive assessment and intervention program provided by a NP may be effective for reducing menopausal symptoms in women following treatment for breast cancer.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old woman presents to a community health clinic for an overdue cervical cancer screening. She recently moved to the area, does not have an established primary care physician, and says the clinic’s schedule is full for the next two months. She is sexually active, has no prior abnormal Pap results, and is due for routine screening. She asks whether the visit can be completed today because taking time off work is difficult and she is concerned about delaying care. The clinic has an experienced nurse practitioner available in an alternate provider role and routinely serves patients with limited access to screening services.
recommended_actions: Use NPs in alternate provider roles to improve access to breast and cervical cancer screening.
Allow NPs to perform Pap smears as alternate providers to physicians.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a primary care/community-based setting with limited access to cervical cancer screening, triggering the access-focused recommendation for NP alternate provider roles. It also specifically involves need for Pap smear screening, which supports NP performance of Pap smears as an alternate provider model.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old woman comes to a community health clinic for an overdue cervical cancer screening. She recently moved to the area, does not have an established primary care physician, and says the clinic’s schedule is full for the next two months. She is sexually active, has no prior abnormal Pap results, and is due for routine screening. She asks whether the visit can be completed today because taking time off work is difficult and she is worried about delaying care. The clinic has an experienced nurse practitioner available in an alternate provider role and routinely serves patients with limited access to screening services. However, today the clinic is only able to offer HPV-based self-collection, and the patient specifically declines a pelvic exam because of severe vaginismus.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting still looks like a routine access-limited cervical screening visit in a community clinic with NP availability, but the actual screening method available is not a Pap smear and the patient is declining pelvic examination.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with an NP-performed Pap smear during this visit.
Use the available NP alternate-provider clinic visit to complete cervical screening today.
Document the screening as completed with routine Pap testing.
recommended_actions: Use NPs in alternate provider roles to improve access to breast and cervical cancer screening.
Arrange an appropriate cervical cancer screening strategy that does not require a Pap smear at this visit, with follow-up for a clinician-performed exam if needed.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains an access-to-screening scenario, so the NP alternate-provider access recommendation still applies. But the specific Pap-smear recommendation is not triggered because the patient is not undergoing a Pap smear today and declines a pelvic exam. The safer interpretation is to address access and select a screening approach consistent with what the patient will actually accept.
challenging_examples.rationale: The community/primary care access context supports NP alternate-provider involvement, but the modified visit no longer includes need for a Pap smear. The patient’s refusal of pelvic examination and the use of self-collection invalidate the Pap-smear-specific recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old woman presents to a community health clinic for an overdue cervical cancer screening. She recently moved to the area, does not have an established primary care physician, and says the clinic’s schedule is full for the next two months. She is sexually active, has no prior abnormal Pap results, and is due for routine screening. She asks whether the visit can be completed today because taking time off work is difficult and she is concerned about delaying care. The clinic has an experienced nurse practitioner available in an alternate provider role and routinely serves patients with limited access to screening services. During triage, however, the patient reports a positive home pregnancy test from last week and has not yet had prenatal care.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on an overdue cervical screening in a community clinic with an NP available, but pregnancy changes the immediate workflow and makes the routine Pap-smear pathway less straightforward.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Have the NP perform the Pap smear during the same visit.
Complete routine cervical screening immediately to avoid further delay.
Treat this as a standard overdue screening appointment.
recommended_actions: Use NPs in alternate provider roles to improve access to breast and cervical cancer screening.

Defer routine Pap testing until the appropriate obstetric/gynecologic evaluation pathway is established and confirm whether screening is indicated now based on prenatal status and prior screening history.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The access-focused NP recommendation still fits, but the Pap-smear-specific recommendation is not automatically guideline-concordant here because the patient is newly pregnant and needs pregnancy-aware screening triage rather than an unqualified routine screening workflow. The safer approach is to confirm timing and appropriateness of cervical screening in prenatal care.
challenging_examples.rationale: The clinic access problem still supports alternate-provider use, but the subtle change to newly confirmed pregnancy means the vignette no longer cleanly satisfies the routine Pap-smear scenario from the source case. Screening should be triaged within prenatal/gynecologic care rather than assumed to proceed unchanged.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman has DCIS and is a candidate for breast-conserving surgery
selected_rules.execute: offer choice of breast-conserving surgery or total mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with DCIS of the breast who are candidates for breast-conserving surgery should be offered the choice of breast-conserving surgery or total mastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast-conserving surgery is performed for DCIS
selected_rules.execute: remove all mammographically suspicious calcifications and ensure microscopically clear margins of DCIS
selected_rules.source_statement: When breast-conserving surgery is performed, all mammographically suspicious calcifications should be removed and margins should be microscopically clear of DCIS.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DCIS is large enough that breast-conserving surgery would leave an unacceptable cosmetic result
selected_rules.execute: recommend mastectomy with option of reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Mastectomy, with the option of reconstruction, is recommended for those women who have an area of DCIS large enough that breast-conserving surgery would leave them with an unacceptable cosmetic result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DCIS treated with breast-conserving surgery and whole-breast irradiation
selected_rules.execute: aim for at least 2 mm margin to reduce local recurrence risk
selected_rules.source_statement: A 2016 joint guideline from the Society of Surgical Oncology (SSO), American Society of Radiation Oncology (ASTRO), and American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) recommends margins of at least 2 mm to reduce the risk of local recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DCIS treated with breast-conserving surgery and whole-breast irradiation and margin is positive (ink on DCIS)
selected_rules.execute: recognize positive margin as associated with significant risk of ipsilateral breast tumour recurrence
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with DCIS treated with breast-conserving surgery and whole-breast irradiation, a positive margin (ink on DCIS) is associated with a significant risk of ipsilateral breast tumour recurrence (IBTR).

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman has DCIS and has undergone breast-conserving surgery
selected_rules.execute: offer adjuvant breast irradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with DCIS who have undergone breast-conserving surgery should be offered adjuvant breast irradiation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DCIS treated with breast-conserving surgery
selected_rules.execute: consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces invasive and non-invasive ipsilateral recurrence
selected_rules.source_statement: In each, the risk of invasive and non-invasive ipsilateral recurrence was reduced with adjuvant radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman prefers to maximize local control
selected_rules.execute: consider mastectomy with option for reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Mastectomy with the option for reconstruction remains an acceptable choice for women preferring to maximize local control.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman is diagnosed with ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) after a screening mammogram showed clustered microcalcifications in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. Core biopsy confirms intermediate-grade DCIS. Breast imaging shows a 2.1 cm localized area without multifocal disease, and the breast surgeon feels she is a good candidate for breast-conserving surgery. The patient is anxious about recurrence but strongly prefers to preserve her breast if oncologically appropriate. She asks about treatment options and whether radiation would still be needed after surgery.
recommended_actions: Offer her the choice of breast-conserving surgery or total mastectomy.
After breast-conserving surgery, offer adjuvant breast irradiation.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces invasive and non-invasive ipsilateral recurrence.
source_case.rationale: She has DCIS and is a candidate for breast-conserving surgery, which triggers the recommendation to offer a choice between breast-conserving surgery and total mastectomy. Because she undergoes breast-conserving surgery, she should be offered adjuvant breast irradiation, and adjuvant radiotherapy should be considered since it reduces both invasive and non-invasive ipsilateral recurrence.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman is diagnosed with ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) after a screening mammogram showed clustered microcalcifications in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. Core biopsy confirms intermediate-grade DCIS. Breast imaging shows a 2.1 cm localized area without multifocal disease, and the breast surgeon feels she is a good candidate for breast-conserving surgery. She is anxious about recurrence but strongly prefers to preserve her breast if oncologically appropriate. She asks about treatment options and whether radiation would still be needed after surgery. On preoperative review, she has no history of prior breast irradiation and no contraindication to surgery.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still centers on newly diagnosed DCIS with breast-conserving surgery under consideration, but the key change is that she has not yet undergone surgery, so post-lumpectomy radiation recommendations do not yet apply.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer breast-conserving surgery as the preferred option and plan adjuvant whole-breast irradiation after surgery.
Tell her that adjuvant radiotherapy should be recommended now because it reduces ipsilateral recurrence.
Discuss postoperative radiation as the next standard step after breast-conserving surgery.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case remains an initial DCIS diagnosis in a candidate for breast-conserving surgery, but she has not yet had surgery. The post-operative radiation recommendations are therefore not yet triggered. The safer interpretation is to counsel on local treatment options and defer adjuvant-irradiation planning until after definitive surgery and pathology.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation applies after breast-conserving surgery has been performed. In this modified vignette, she is still preoperative, so neither the post-surgical radiation offer nor the evidence-based consideration of adjuvant radiotherapy is guideline-concordant at this point. The case still supports discussion of surgical options, but no supplied rule is directly triggered yet.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old woman is found to have ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) after a screening mammogram revealed grouped microcalcifications in the right breast. Core biopsy shows low-intermediate grade DCIS. Imaging demonstrates a 4.8 cm segment of calcifications extending close to the nipple-areolar complex, and the breast surgeon notes that breast-conserving surgery would likely leave a poor cosmetic result with uncertain margin clearance. The patient hopes to preserve her breast if possible and asks whether lumpectomy plus radiation would be reasonable. She has no palpable mass, and there is no evidence of invasive cancer on biopsy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The clinical picture still appears to be early DCIS diagnosed on screening, but the lesion is larger and positioned such that breast-conserving surgery would likely be cosmetically unacceptable, changing the preferred local treatment approach.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer breast-conserving surgery followed by adjuvant whole-breast irradiation.
Reassure her that lumpectomy remains the standard breast-preserving option.
Plan postoperative breast irradiation after breast-conserving surgery.
recommended_actions: Recommend mastectomy with the option of reconstruction.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still DCIS, but the lesion is large enough that breast-conserving surgery is unlikely to provide an acceptable cosmetic result or reliable local control. The guideline-concordant recommendation shifts away from lumpectomy-based management toward mastectomy, with reconstruction discussed if desired.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source case triggered recommendations for breast-conserving surgery eligibility and post-lumpectomy irradiation. Here, the extent of DCIS makes breast-conserving surgery potentially unsuitable, so the relevant rule is the mastectomy recommendation for large DCIS. Because surgery has not been performed and lumpectomy is not the preferred option, the adjuvant breast irradiation rules do not apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: clinical stage I-III colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use routine PET for diagnosis or staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET is not recommended for the diagnosis or staging of clinical Stage I-III colorectal cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: locally advanced rectal cancer before and after preoperative chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely use PET to measure treatment response.
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET is not recommended for the measurement of treatment response in locally advanced rectal cancer before and after preoperative chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: short-term response assessment after chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET as a substitute for CT.
selected_rules.source_statement: The study suggested that PET should not be used as a substitute for CT for short-term response and should not be used as a surrogate for long-term clinical endpoints.

selected_rules.activation_condition: long-term clinical endpoint assessment such as time to progression or overall survival
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET as a surrogate for long-term clinical endpoints.
selected_rules.source_statement: The study suggested that PET should not be used as a substitute for CT for short-term response and should not be used as a surrogate for long-term clinical endpoints.

selected_rules.activation_condition: one-time PET after preoperative therapy to predict complete pathologic response
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on a single post-therapy PET for predicting complete pathologic response.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, one-time PET after preoperative therapy poorly predicted complete pathologic response after preoperative therapy in Capirci et al (13) and Kalff et al (14) and poorly predicted posttherapy staging in Capirci et al (12).

selected_rules.activation_condition: after chemotherapy when deciding on liver resection
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET in decision making for liver resection.
selected_rules.source_statement: The study showed that ultrasonography also guides surgical decision during intraoperative assessment and suggested PET after chemotherapy should not be used in decision making for liver resection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: single PET/CT after preoperative therapy to predict complete pathologic response
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on a single PET/CT after therapy for predicting complete pathologic response.
selected_rules.source_statement: Kristiansen et al ( 17) suggested a single PET/CT after preoperative therapy poorly predicted complete pathologic response.

selected_rules.activation_condition: routine surveillance after curative surgery in patients at high risk for recurrence
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET routinely for surveillance.
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for routine surveillance in patients with colorectal cancer treated with curative surgery at high risk for recurrence.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with newly diagnosed adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon undergoes colonoscopy after several months of intermittent hematochezia and iron-deficiency anemia. Biopsy confirms colorectal cancer, and contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows a localized cT3N1 lesion without definite distant metastases. His oncology team discusses staging before surgery, and the patient asks whether a PET scan would better define the extent of disease. He has no prior treatment and no symptoms suggesting recurrence or metastatic complications. The team plans standard surgical and oncologic management based on the available anatomic imaging and pathology.
recommended_actions: Do not use routine PET for diagnosis or staging of this clinical stage I-III colorectal cancer.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a patient with clinical stage I-III colorectal cancer undergoing initial staging. Rule R001 is triggered because routine PET is not recommended for diagnosis or staging in this setting. No other rule applies because the patient is not being assessed after chemotherapy, after preoperative therapy, or in surveillance after curative surgery.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon presents after several months of intermittent hematochezia and iron-deficiency anemia. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows a cT3N1 lesion with no definite distant metastases.
