The surgical oncologist is planning standard resection, but the patient asks whether a PET scan should be added to better define the extent of disease before surgery. He has not yet undergone any treatment, and there are no symptoms or laboratory abnormalities suggesting metastatic complications. A recent MRI performed for an unrelated liver cyst also shows no suspicious hepatic lesions. The team is reviewing staging studies based on the available anatomic imaging and pathology.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like newly diagnosed stage III colon cancer with preoperative staging questions, but the CT already shows a T3N1 lesion and an incidental liver MRI has been added to make the workup seem more extensive.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a PET scan for routine staging before surgery.
Use PET to look for occult metastatic disease despite the negative CT.
Add PET to complete the initial colon cancer staging workup.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a newly diagnosed clinical stage I-III colorectal cancer scenario, but routine PET still is not guideline-concordant for initial diagnosis or staging. The safer interpretation is to proceed with standard anatomic staging and surgical planning without adding PET routinely.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 applies to routine PET for diagnosis or staging in clinical stage I-III colorectal cancer. The added liver MRI does not create a new indication for PET, and no other listed rule is triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old woman with locally advanced rectal adenocarcinoma completed neoadjuvant chemoradiation six weeks ago and is being evaluated for response before total mesorectal excision. Her baseline pelvic MRI showed a T3bN2 tumor, and the post-treatment MRI demonstrates marked tumor shrinkage but still a small residual low-signal thickening at the site of the primary lesion. A CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows no distant metastases. Because the endoscopic exam is limited by post-radiation mucosal edema, the team wonders whether a PET/CT could clarify whether she has achieved a complete response and might avoid surgery. She is clinically well, with improving bowel symptoms and no new pain or weight loss.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This closely resembles a post-preoperative-therapy response-assessment case, but the imaging still shows residual rectal wall thickening rather than a clear complete response, making the PET question seem tempting without meeting the cited rule conditions.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Obtain a PET/CT now to determine whether she has a complete pathologic response.
Use a single post-treatment PET/CT to decide if surgery can be omitted.
Rely on PET to confirm complete response before proceeding.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is a post-neoadjuvant rectal cancer response-assessment scenario, but the current findings do not establish a complete response, and a single PET/CT should not be used to predict complete pathologic response. The safer interpretation is to continue standard restaging and surgical decision-making based on clinical exam, MRI, and endoscopy rather than PET.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case no longer fits a one-time PET after preoperative therapy to predict complete pathologic response because residual abnormality remains on MRI, and the guideline discourages relying on a single PET/CT for that purpose. No other supplied rule clearly applies.

selected_rules.activation_condition: clinically suspicious groin nodes are present
selected_rules.execute: do not perform SLNB
selected_rules.source_statement: SLNB is not recommended when there are clinically suspicious groin nodes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering blue dye alone for SLNB mapping
selected_rules.execute: do not use blue dye alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Blue dye alone should be discouraged because of its low detection rate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering use of blue dye alone because of low detection rate
selected_rules.execute: avoid blue dye alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Blue dye alone should be discouraged because of its low detection rate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman with unifocal vulvar squamous cell tumour smaller than 4 cm and clinically nonsuspicious groin nodes
selected_rules.execute: perform sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB)
selected_rules.source_statement: SLNB is recommended for women with unifocal tumours <4 cm in size and clinically nonsuspicious nodes in the groin.

selected_rules.activation_condition: performing SLNB for early-stage vulvar cancer
selected_rules.execute: ensure appropriate surgical training with supervised SLNB experience, complete IFLD follow-up without false negatives, and ongoing annual experience
selected_rules.source_statement: Appropriate surgical training (i.e., supervised experiences with SLNB procedures followed by complete IFLD without any false negatives and ongoing annual experience with cases to maintain competence) is recommended to optimize patient outcomes and safety.

selected_rules.activation_condition: performing SLNB for early-stage vulvar cancer
selected_rules.execute: have the procedure performed by gynecologic oncologists in Gynecologic Oncology Centres
selected_rules.source_statement: This procedure should be performed by gynecologic oncologists in Gynecologic Oncology Centres.

selected_rules.activation_condition: centre performing SLNB for vulvar cancer
selected_rules.execute: ensure at least 10 successful SLNB followed by IFLD cases per centre
selected_rules.source_statement: Although volume has not been explicitly studied, the Working Group agrees that successful experience with SLNB followed by IFLD in at least 10 patients per centre is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: performing SLNB mapping
selected_rules.execute: use radiocolloid tracers alone or with blue dye
selected_rules.source_statement: Radiocolloid tracers should be used alone or with blue dye.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old woman presents with a 3.2 cm unifocal squamous cell carcinoma of the left vulva. On exam, the groin nodes are enlarged, firm, and mildly tender bilaterally, making them clinically suspicious for metastasis. She has no prior groin surgery and is otherwise fit for treatment. The gynecologic oncology team is discussing the next step in nodal staging, and the patient asks whether a sentinel lymph node biopsy could be done to avoid a larger dissection.
recommended_actions: Do not perform sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) because clinically suspicious groin nodes are present.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because the patient has clinically suspicious groin nodes on examination. Under this rule, SLNB is not recommended in that setting. No other rule is triggered because the vignette does not present clinically nonsuspicious nodes, nor does it specifically involve SLNB mapping technique or center/training details.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old woman presents with a 3.2 cm unifocal squamous cell carcinoma of the left vulva. On exam, the groin nodes are not enlarged or fixed, and ultrasound of both groins shows only small, morphologically benign nodes. She has no prior groin surgery and is otherwise fit for treatment. The gynecologic oncology team is discussing the next step in nodal staging, and the patient asks whether a sentinel lymph node biopsy could be done to avoid a larger dissection.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The tumor size, unifocal histology, and surgical decision-making are unchanged, but the groin nodes are now clinically nonsuspicious rather than suspicious.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with sentinel lymph node biopsy for nodal staging
Arrange SLNB with standard radiocolloid mapping, with or without blue dye
recommended_actions: Perform sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB)
Use radiocolloid tracers alone or with blue dye
Ensure the procedure is performed by a gynecologic oncologist in a Gynecologic Oncology Centre with appropriate SLNB experience
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case no longer meets the contraindication for SLNB because the groin nodes are clinically and sonographically nonsuspicious. The safer interpretation is an early vulvar cancer patient who is eligible for standard SLNB-based nodal staging rather than upfront dissection.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is not triggered because the nodes are not clinically suspicious. The remaining criteria fit R004, and the general procedural recommendations for SLNB also apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman has a 2.7 cm unifocal squamous cell carcinoma of the right vulva. Her groin examination is normal, and she has no prior groin surgery. Because she wants the least morbid staging approach, the team plans SLNB using technetium radiocolloid. The hospital notes that the case would be done by a fellowship-trained gynecologic oncologist, but this is the center’s first vulvar SLNB case and it has not yet built a local SLNB follow-up program.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The tumor characteristics and node exam still suggest an SLNB candidate, but the center lacks the minimum accumulated SLNB experience referenced in the guidance.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with sentinel lymph node biopsy at this hospital
Use radiocolloid mapping for SLNB and move forward with nodal staging
recommended_actions: Perform sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) only in a Gynecologic Oncology Centre with appropriate SLNB experience
Ensure the centre has successful experience with SLNB followed by inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy in at least 10 patients before offering the procedure
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The patient remains an anatomic candidate for SLNB, but this specific center does not satisfy the experience threshold in the guidance. The safer interpretation is referral to an experienced gynecologic oncology center for nodal staging rather than proceeding at a first-time site.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is not triggered because the nodes are not clinically suspicious. R004 applies to the patient’s tumor and nodal status, but the center-level requirement in R007 is not met, making local performance of SLNB discordant with the guidance.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Before preoperative therapy is planned
selected_rules.execute: Perform adequate preoperative staging investigations including transrectal ultrasound and/or MRI for T category, MRI for N category, digital rectal exam, CT or MRI to assess mesorectal margin, CT or MRI of the abdomen for metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.
selected_rules.source_statement: Recommendations for preoperative therapy presuppose adequate preoperative staging investigations, including transrectal ultrasound and/or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with surface or endorectal coil to assess the T category, MRI with surface or endorectal coil to assess the N category, a good digital rectal exam, computerized axial tomography (CAT) scan or MRI to assess the mesorectal margin, CAT scan or MRI of the abdomen to assess for potential metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When preoperative staging may be inaccurate
selected_rules.execute: Discuss potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumor staging with the patient to support informed decision-making.
selected_rules.source_statement: Potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumour staging should be discussed with patients to allow them to make informed decisions (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: When planning rectal surgery after preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use total mesorectal excision (TME) principles for the eventual rectal surgery.
selected_rules.source_statement: The eventual rectal surgery is expected to include total mesorectal excision (TME) principles.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering preoperative treatment benefits
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that the quality of surgery strongly influences the potential benefits of preoperative treatments.
selected_rules.source_statement: The quality of surgery greatly influences the potential benefits of preoperative treatments.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When evaluating preoperative treatment evidence
selected_rules.execute: Account for the fact that many trials did not use currently recommended surgical standards, including TME.
selected_rules.source_statement: A substantial number of trials included in the evidentiary base did not use currently recommended standards of surgery, including TME.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When preoperative therapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Ensure preoperative therapy recommendations are applied only after adequate staging investigations have been completed.
selected_rules.source_statement: Recommendations for preoperative therapy presuppose adequate preoperative staging investigations...

selected_rules.activation_condition: When discussing preoperative therapy with the patient
selected_rules.execute: Discuss the potential inaccuracies of staging tests so the patient can make an informed choice.
selected_rules.source_statement: Potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumour staging should be discussed with patients to allow them to make informed decisions (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: When planning surgery after preoperative RT
selected_rules.execute: Plan surgery with TME principles in mind.
selected_rules.source_statement: The eventual rectal surgery is expected to include total mesorectal excision (TME) principles.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a recently diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma is being evaluated for possible neoadjuvant treatment. He has mild intermittent rectal bleeding but no bowel obstruction. On digital rectal exam, the tumor is palpable on the posterior rectal wall, about 7 cm from the anal verge, with reduced mobility. The oncology team is considering preoperative chemoradiation because the lesion may be locally advanced, but the staging workup is not yet complete. He has had no dedicated pelvic MRI, no endorectal ultrasound, and no CT of the abdomen or chest imaging. The patient asks whether the tumor is definitely stage III, and the team explains that preoperative staging can be imperfect and the final extent may differ from initial tests.
recommended_actions: Perform adequate preoperative staging investigations, including transrectal ultrasound and/or MRI for T category, MRI for N category, digital rectal exam, CT or MRI to assess the mesorectal margin, CT or MRI of the abdomen for metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.
Ensure preoperative therapy recommendations are applied only after adequate staging investigations have been completed.
Discuss the potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumor staging with the patient to support informed decision-making.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes consideration of preoperative therapy before staging is complete, which triggers the requirement for adequate staging first. It also includes the need to counsel the patient that staging tests can be inaccurate. No other rules are needed to support the scenario.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a recently diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma is being evaluated for possible neoadjuvant treatment. He has mild intermittent rectal bleeding but no bowel obstruction. On digital rectal exam, the tumor is palpable on the posterior rectal wall, about 7 cm from the anal verge, with reduced mobility. Pelvic MRI has already been completed and shows a cT3N1 lesion with threatened mesorectal fascia; a chest x-ray is normal and CT of the abdomen shows no distant disease. The oncology team is considering preoperative chemoradiation because the lesion appears locally advanced. The patient asks whether the tumor is definitely stage III, and the team explains that preoperative staging can be imperfect and the final extent may differ from initial tests.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a locally advanced mid-rectal cancer being considered for neoadjuvant therapy, but the staging workup is now essentially complete, removing the need to defer treatment on the basis of missing preoperative studies.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed directly with preoperative chemoradiation without further staging tests.
Counsel the patient that rectal staging can still be imperfect despite the completed imaging.
recommended_actions: Discuss the potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumor staging with the patient to support informed decision-making.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the staging-driven recommendation because adequate preoperative staging has already been performed. The safer interpretation is that neoadjuvant treatment may now be considered based on the completed workup, while still counseling the patient about limitations of staging accuracy.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original trigger for mandatory staging before preoperative therapy is no longer present because MRI, chest x-ray, and abdominal CT have already been done. However, the patient-facing counseling recommendation about staging inaccuracies still applies.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed low-rectal adenocarcinoma is referred to radiation oncology for possible neoadjuvant treatment. She has occasional rectal bleeding and tenesmus but no obstruction. Digital rectal exam reveals a firm posterior tumor about 5 cm from the anal verge with limited mobility. She underwent pelvic MRI and endorectal ultrasound last week, both suggesting a cT2N0 lesion without threatened mesorectal margin. CT of the chest and abdomen are negative for metastases. Because the lesion is close to the sphincter, the team is discussing whether preoperative chemoradiation would improve resectability. The patient asks how confident the team is about the stage, and the clinician notes that imaging can sometimes over- or understage rectal tumors.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The workflow still centers on deciding on neoadjuvant therapy for rectal cancer, but the available staging now suggests an early-stage tumor rather than a clearly locally advanced one, so the original preoperative-treatment presumption no longer fits.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start neoadjuvant chemoradiation because the tumor is low in the rectum and may threaten sphincter preservation.
recommended_actions: Discuss the potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumor staging with the patient to support informed decision-making.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette does not support the original staging-based recommendation for preoperative therapy because the completed MRI, ultrasound, and CT show a cT2N0 tumor without threatened margin or metastatic disease. A safer interpretation is that the patient should be managed according to the confirmed stage rather than escalated solely based on location.
challenging_examples.rationale: Adequate preoperative staging has been completed, so the requirement to obtain staging studies before recommending preoperative therapy is not the active issue. The communication recommendation about staging uncertainty still applies.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, locally advanced squamous cell or undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (stage III or IV)
selected_rules.execute: Use chemoradiotherapy-based management with consideration of adding neoadjuvant, concurrent, or adjuvant chemotherapy as addressed in the guideline
selected_rules.source_statement: The objectives of the guideline are to make recommendations regarding the addition of neoadjuvant/concurrent/adjuvant chemotherapy to chemoradiotherapy in the management of locally advanced squamous cell or undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (NPC).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Administer induction chemotherapy every 3 weeks for 3 cycles; at minimum give 2 cycles
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, the regimens should be administered every three weeks for a total of three cycles, or at the minimum two cycles.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Start chemoradiotherapy within 21 to 28 days from the first day of the last induction chemotherapy cycle
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, chemoradiotherapy should be commenced within 21-28 days from the first day of the last cycle of induction chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer PF every 4 weeks for a total of 3 cycles
selected_rules.source_statement: For all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy, PF ... administered every four weeks for a total of three cycles should be offered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with locally advanced NPC treated in Ontario context
selected_rules.execute: Apply the endorsed CSCO/ASCO recommendations as modified by Ontario Health endorsement
selected_rules.source_statement: The Chemotherapy and Radiotherapy for Nasopharyngeal Cancer Development Group (GDG) of Ontario Health (Cancer Care Ontario) endorses the Chinese Society of Clinical Oncology (CSCO)/American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) recommendations ... modified by the endorsement process described in this document.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a 3-week cycle interval for induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, the regimens should be administered every three weeks for a total of three cycles, or at the minimum two cycles.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a 4-week cycle interval for PF adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy, PF ... administered every four weeks for a total of three cycles should be offered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not delay chemoradiotherapy beyond 28 days after the first day of the last induction cycle
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, chemoradiotherapy should be commenced within 21-28 days from the first day of the last cycle of induction chemotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show a stage IVB locally advanced primary tumor with bilateral cervical nodal involvement but no distant metastases. He has good performance status, normal renal and hepatic function, and is considered chemotherapy-eligible. The oncology team plans induction chemotherapy followed by definitive chemoradiotherapy. He understands the treatment will be coordinated closely because the timing of the transition to radiation is important after induction therapy.
recommended_actions: Use a chemoradiotherapy-based management approach for this newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible stage IV locally advanced nasopharyngeal cancer.
If induction chemotherapy is given, administer it every 3 weeks for a total of 3 cycles; at minimum, 2 cycles are acceptable.
Start chemoradiotherapy within 21 to 28 days from the first day of the last induction chemotherapy cycle.
source_case.rationale: The vignette matches newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, locally advanced undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (stage IV), triggering the recommendation for chemoradiotherapy-based management. It also specifies induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiotherapy, which triggers the induction schedule and timing rules: q3-week induction for 3 cycles minimum 2, and initiation of chemoradiotherapy within 21–28 days from the first day of the last induction cycle.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show a stage II locally advanced primary tumor with bilateral cervical nodal involvement and no distant metastases. He has good performance status, normal renal and hepatic function, and is considered chemotherapy-eligible. The oncology team is discussing whether to proceed with induction chemotherapy before radiation. He understands the treatment will be coordinated closely because timing matters if induction is chosen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a typical newly diagnosed Ontario nasopharyngeal cancer patient with nodal disease and planned multidisciplinary treatment, but the primary stage is only II rather than III/IV.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use a chemoradiotherapy-based management approach with added induction chemotherapy.
Give induction chemotherapy every 3 weeks for 3 cycles before chemoradiotherapy.
Schedule chemoradiotherapy to start within 21 to 28 days after the first day of the last induction cycle.
recommended_actions: Apply the endorsed CSCO/ASCO recommendations as modified by Ontario Health endorsement.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the locally advanced NPC chemotherapy recommendations because the tumor is stage II, not stage III or IV. The induction-chemotherapy and post-induction timing recommendations do not apply without the qualifying locally advanced stage and treatment sequence. A safer interpretation is that management should be individualized for stage II disease rather than automatically following the locally advanced induction pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, locally advanced stage III or IV NPC. That condition is not met. Because induction therapy is only being discussed hypothetically and not actually selected within a qualifying stage III/IV pathway, R002, R003, R006, and R008 are not triggered. Ontario endorsement context remains relevant.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show a stage IVB locally advanced primary tumor with bilateral cervical nodal involvement and no distant metastases. He has good performance status and normal renal and hepatic function, but his calculated creatinine clearance is borderline and the team plans to avoid platinum-based induction chemotherapy. He is being set up for definitive chemoradiotherapy, with no induction phase scheduled.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation matches a classic stage IVB, chemotherapy-eligible nasopharyngeal carcinoma case in Ontario, but the treatment plan explicitly omits induction chemotherapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start induction chemotherapy every 3 weeks for 3 cycles.
Book chemoradiotherapy to begin 21 to 28 days after the first day of the last induction cycle.
Use the standard post-induction transition timeline.
recommended_actions: Use a chemoradiotherapy-based management approach for this newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible stage IV locally advanced nasopharyngeal cancer.
Apply the endorsed CSCO/ASCO recommendations as modified by Ontario Health endorsement.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case still meets the broad locally advanced NPC criterion, so chemoradiotherapy-based management remains appropriate. However, the specific induction-chemotherapy recommendations are not triggered because induction is not being given. Without an induction cycle, the 21–28 day transition rule also does not apply. The safer interpretation is definitive chemoradiotherapy without assuming an induction sequence.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies because this is newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, stage IV undifferentiated NPC. R002, R003, R006, and R008 require that the patient be receiving induction chemotherapy, which is not the case here. Ontario endorsement remains applicable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the document is used as a guideline reference
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted from the provided text.
selected_rules.source_statement: The provided text contains only document front matter, section headings, and copyright/contact information, with no clinical recommendations.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is seen in clinic for a new guideline review after his primary care physician asked for oncology guidance on a recently diagnosed condition. The document available for reference contains only the front matter, section headings, and copyright/contact information, without any disease-specific management content or treatment recommendations. The patient’s history, exam, and test results are not being used to derive any intervention from this source because no clinical guidance is present in the document.
recommended_actions: No clinical action can be extracted from the provided text.
source_case.rationale: Rule R001 is triggered because the document is being used as a guideline reference, but the source contains only non-clinical front matter and no actionable recommendations. Therefore, no management action can be derived from this text.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is seen in clinic for a new guideline review after his primary care physician asked for oncology guidance on a recently diagnosed condition. The only document available for reference is a scanned appendix page that includes copyright text, contact information, and a table of contents, but the actual management section is missing because the page was clipped during upload. The patient is clinically stable, and no disease-specific staging, biomarker results, or treatment recommendations are available from the source text. The team is asking whether any action can be justified from the provided document.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still centers on a guideline review for oncology, but the accessible source is incomplete and contains no actionable management content beyond front matter and a table of contents.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with the oncology treatment pathway described in the missing management section.
Start the standard first-line therapy based on the presumed guideline topic.
Arrange disease-specific staging and treatment discussions using the unavailable recommendation set.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the accessible source still lacks any disease-specific management recommendations. A safer interpretation is that no clinical action can be extracted from the provided text and the full guideline must be obtained before making recommendations.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 is not satisfied in a clinically actionable way because the available document does not contain recommendations, only non-clinical or incomplete front matter content. No management action can be derived from the text provided.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is seen in clinic for a new guideline review after his primary care physician asked for oncology guidance on a recently diagnosed condition. The reference packet includes the guideline cover page, section headings, and copyright/contact information, but the body text is redacted because the file was exported in a restricted format. The patient’s current evaluation is otherwise unremarkable, and the clinician is specifically trying to determine whether the source document supports any treatment recommendation or diagnostic pathway.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a straightforward request for guideline-based oncology advice, but the only accessible material is document metadata and headings rather than usable clinical content.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the guideline to select an initial treatment plan.
Follow the presumed management recommendations from the title and section headings.
Initiate disease-specific workup and therapy based on the cited reference packet.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains discordant with the rule because the source contains no readable recommendation text. The safer interpretation is that the document cannot be used to derive any clinical action until the full guideline content is available.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 applies only when the document can be used as a guideline reference, but here the accessible material is limited to non-clinical front matter and headings with no actionable recommendations. Therefore, no clinical action can be extracted.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not routinely give adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The 2007 joint CCO/ASCO guideline recommended chemotherapy for stage II and IIIA disease but not stage IA.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC with sensitizing EGFR mutation
selected_rules.execute: do not use EGFR-TKI routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: These data were considered insufficient to justify the routine use of EGFR-TKIs in patients with tumors with sensitizing EGFR mutations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC without EGFR mutation selection
selected_rules.execute: do not use erlotinib routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: No significant differences in DFS or OS were detected in the overall unselected study population.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC without EGFR mutation selection
selected_rules.execute: do not use gefitinib routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: There was no benefit for either of the subgroups with EGFR wild-type tumors or the 15 tumors (4% of the total sample) with EGFR sensitizing mutations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage I to IIIA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not use MAGE-A3 immunotherapy routinely as adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: MAGRIT... found no significant difference in the primary outcome DFS... Because there was no difference between groups, the study was unable to identify a biomarker that would enable selection of patients for this treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage I to IIIA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not use bevacizumab added to chemotherapy routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: a phase III RCT... found that the addition of angiogenesis inhibitor bevacizumab to chemotherapy failed to improve DFS or OS for individuals with surgically resected earlystage NSCLCs compared with chemotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage I or II NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not give postoperative radiotherapy (PORT) routinely
selected_rules.source_statement: there has been no new evidence that would alter the recommendation against PORT in patients with stage I or II disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage II or IIIA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: offer adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Cisplatin-based adjuvant chemotherapy was recommended for routine use in patients with stage II or IIIA disease extent

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with a 35-pack-year smoking history underwent right upper lobectomy for a 2.1 cm lung adenocarcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected stage IA (pT1bN0) non-small cell lung cancer with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated, and she is now seen in oncology clinic 4 weeks later asking whether she should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. Her performance status is good, and she has no evidence of residual disease on imaging.
recommended_actions: Do not routinely give adjuvant chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches completely resected stage IA NSCLC, which triggers the recommendation against routine adjuvant chemotherapy. No other provided rule is needed because the tumor is stage IA and there is no EGFR-directed or radiotherapy indication in the rule set for this case.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with a 35-pack-year smoking history underwent right upper lobectomy for a 2.1 cm lung adenocarcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected stage IB (pT2aN0) non-small cell lung cancer with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated, and she is now seen in oncology clinic 4 weeks later asking whether she should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. Her performance status is good, and there is no evidence of residual disease on imaging. Tumor testing later returns an activating EGFR exon 19 deletion.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like an uncomplicated post-lobectomy follow-up for early-stage NSCLC, but the pathologic stage is IB rather than IA, and the tumor carries a sensitizing EGFR mutation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Do not routinely give adjuvant chemotherapy.
Start an adjuvant EGFR-targeted TKI because the tumor has an EGFR exon 19 deletion.
Consider routine adjuvant bevacizumab or MAGE-A3 immunotherapy to reduce recurrence risk.
recommended_actions: Do not use EGFR-TKI routinely as adjuvant therapy.
Do not use erlotinib routinely as adjuvant therapy.
Do not use gefitinib routinely as adjuvant therapy.
Do not use MAGE-A3 immunotherapy routinely as adjuvant treatment.
Do not use bevacizumab added to chemotherapy routinely as adjuvant therapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer stage IA disease, so the specific recommendation against adjuvant chemotherapy for stage IA NSCLC does not apply. The safer interpretation is that the patient has completely resected stage IB NSCLC, and routine EGFR-TKI, erlotinib, gefitinib, MAGE-A3 immunotherapy, and bevacizumab are still not guideline-concordant.
challenging_examples.rationale: Changing the tumor from stage IA to stage IB invalidates R001. Because the case now remains completely resected stage IB NSCLC and includes sensitizing EGFR mutation testing, the anti-routine recommendations for EGFR-TKI, erlotinib, gefitinib, MAGE-A3 immunotherapy, and bevacizumab still apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 71-year-old man with severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease underwent left lower lobectomy for a 3.8 cm squamous cell carcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected stage IIA (pT2bN0) non-small cell lung cancer with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling. He recovered without complication and returns 5 weeks later to discuss postoperative treatment. Imaging shows no residual disease, but he remains frail and his renal function is borderline with creatinine clearance of 42 mL/min. The pathology report also notes that the tumor is EGFR wild-type. He asks whether there is any role for radiation instead of chemotherapy because he wants to avoid systemic treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The workflow still centers on a post-lobectomy oncology visit for a resected lung cancer, but the stage is IIA rather than IA and the question has shifted toward radiation in a patient with no PORT indication.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Do not routinely give adjuvant chemotherapy.
Offer postoperative radiotherapy to improve local control.
Start erlotinib or gefitinib routinely because the tumor is EGFR wild-type and resected.
recommended_actions: Offer adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy.
Do not use erlotinib routinely as adjuvant therapy.
Do not use gefitinib routinely as adjuvant therapy.
Do not use MAGE-A3 immunotherapy routinely as adjuvant treatment.
Do not use bevacizumab added to chemotherapy routinely as adjuvant therapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not stage IA disease, so the recommendation against adjuvant chemotherapy for stage IA NSCLC no longer applies. The patient has completely resected stage IIA disease, for which adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy is recommended; PORT is not routinely indicated for completely resected stage I or II NSCLC. Routine EGFR-directed therapy, MAGE-A3 immunotherapy, and bevacizumab remain unsupported.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is invalidated by upstaging to stage IIA, and R007 does not apply because postoperative radiotherapy is not routinely recommended for completely resected stage I or II disease. R008 is triggered because stage II NSCLC should be offered adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy. Additional routine adjuvant biologic therapies remain nonconcordant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
selected_rules.execute: Do not expect a survival benefit from CLND based on MSLT-II.
selected_rules.source_statement: The non-significant difference in MSS and increase in adverse events of the CLND group indicates that CLND may not be optimal for patients and does not offer a survival benefit.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
selected_rules.execute: Do not expect benefit from CLND for any subgroup based on MSLT-II.
selected_rules.source_statement: Although the majority of patients had low volume tumor metastases, sub set analysis did not demonstrate a benefit for any groups of patient receiving CLND.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
selected_rules.execute: Do not assume CLND improves distant metastasis-free survival, overall survival, or recurrence-free survival based on DeCOG-SLT.
selected_rules.source_statement: The DeCOG-SLT trial found no difference in distant metastasis-free survival, overall survival, or recurrence-free survival when SLN positive patients who received CLND were compared to patients who were observed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and considering CLND versus observation
selected_rules.execute: Avoid routine CLND when observation with ultrasonography is feasible because CLND increases adverse events.
selected_rules.source_statement: Adverse events occurred with more frequency among the CLND patients than the observation group with lymphedema being the most common (24.1% of patients vs. 6.3% at last follow-up, p<0.001).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is managed with nodal observation after positive sentinel node
selected_rules.execute: Monitor the affected nodal basin with ultrasonography and clinical exam every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months from years 3 to 5.
selected_rules.source_statement: Monitoring with ultrasonography of the affected nodal basin and clinical exam will be required, at minimum, every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years and every 6 months from 3-5 years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: ultrasound shows any two suspicious nodal features
selected_rules.execute: Confirm suspected nodal recurrence with biopsy of the basin.
selected_rules.source_statement: Suspicions of nodal recurrence via ultrasound should be confirmed with a biopsy of the basin.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has sentinel node metastasis
selected_rules.execute: Consider nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than completion lymph node dissection.
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients with sentinel node metastasis should be considered for nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than CLND.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive sentinel node metastasis and CLND is the only option for local control
selected_rules.execute: Consider CLND as the better option than nodal observation with ultrasonography.
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with extensive sentinel node metastasis in which CLND would be the only option for local control

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a history of cutaneous melanoma on the left calf underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single positive sentinel node with a small metastatic deposit, and staging scans show no distant disease. He is otherwise well and asks whether he should proceed with completion lymph node dissection (CLND) or be observed. The surgical team discusses that he is eligible for nodal observation with ultrasound rather than routine CLND, since the sentinel node metastasis is limited and ultrasound follow-up is feasible. He is comfortable with close surveillance and prefers to avoid the added morbidity of more extensive surgery.
recommended_actions: Do not pursue routine CLND when observation with ultrasonography is feasible, because CLND increases adverse events.
Consider nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than completion lymph node dissection for sentinel node metastasis.
Monitor the affected nodal basin with ultrasonography and clinical examination every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months from years 3 to 5.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches a patient with positive sentinel node metastasis in whom CLND is being compared with observation. The selected rules support observation over routine CLND because survival benefit has not been shown and adverse events are higher with CLND. Since the patient is managed with nodal observation, scheduled ultrasound and clinical follow-up are recommended.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a history of cutaneous melanoma on the left calf underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single positive sentinel node, but the metastatic focus measures 2.1 mm with focal extranodal extension. Staging scans show no distant disease. He is otherwise well and asks whether he should proceed with completion lymph node dissection (CLND) or be observed. The surgical team notes that ultrasound follow-up would be difficult because the involved nodal basin is not reliably accessible on exam and high-quality surveillance imaging is not available locally. He is comfortable with surgery and wants the most definitive local control.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves a single positive sentinel node after melanoma surgery with no distant metastases, but the deposit is larger and ultrasound surveillance is not feasible.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend nodal observation with serial ultrasound instead of CLND.
Defer further surgery because sentinel node positivity alone can be followed.
Schedule routine nodal surveillance every 4 to 6 months without additional intervention.
recommended_actions: Consider CLND as the better option than nodal observation with ultrasonography when CLND is the only option for local control.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for routine observation because the sentinel node metastasis is no longer low-volume and the basin cannot be followed reliably with ultrasound. The safer interpretation is that completion lymph node dissection may be appropriate when local control cannot be maintained with observation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation favors observation only when ultrasound surveillance is feasible and the nodal metastasis is limited. Here, the metastatic deposit is larger with extranodal extension, and the basin is not adequately accessible for serial ultrasound follow-up, shifting the balance toward CLND.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a history of cutaneous melanoma on the left calf underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single positive sentinel node with a small metastatic deposit, and staging scans show no distant disease. He is otherwise well and asks whether he should proceed with completion lymph node dissection (CLND) or be observed. The surgical team discusses nodal observation, but on postoperative review the patient has already moved out of the region and cannot reliably return for the recommended serial ultrasound examinations. He prefers to avoid additional surgery if possible, but follow-up access is uncertain.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The melanoma stage and nodal finding closely mirror the source case, but the key difference is that ongoing ultrasound surveillance is not feasible because the patient cannot attend follow-up.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Choose nodal observation with ultrasound because the sentinel node metastasis is limited.
Avoid CLND and arrange routine clinic follow-up only.
Plan standard ultrasound surveillance of the nodal basin every few months.
recommended_actions: Consider CLND as the better option than nodal observation with ultrasonography when CLND is the only option for local control.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Observation is not guideline-concordant when the patient cannot complete the required ultrasound-based surveillance. The safer interpretation is that local control may require CLND if nodal observation is impractical or unavailable.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation depends on feasible nodal observation with ultrasound. In this modification, the patient cannot reliably return for serial imaging, so the observation pathway no longer fits the guideline conditions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Do not use sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy as standard practice
selected_rules.source_statement: Sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy should not be recommended as standard practice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Avoid sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy because no survival or local control benefit was shown and toxicity was increased
selected_rules.source_statement: There was no evidence to suggest any survival or local control benefit using sequential RTCT compared with RT alone. Given these results and the increased adverse effects associated with sequential RTCT, this approach should not be recommended in standard practice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with localized esophageal carcinoma, good performance status, considering non-surgical curative treatment, and combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated
selected_rules.execute: Use concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is recommended over radiotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with localized esophageal carcinoma considering a non-surgical approach and combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated
selected_rules.execute: Consider concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with favourable performance status and who have a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT, this approach is a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has favourable performance status and a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT
selected_rules.execute: Offer concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as a curative-intent non-surgical approach
selected_rules.source_statement: The current review illustrates a benefit in local control and survival with the use of concomitant RTCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with localized (T1-3, small volume N1, M0) esophageal carcinoma and good performance status considering non-surgical treatment
selected_rules.execute: Apply the guideline recommendations to this patient group
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with localized (T1-3, small volume N1, M0) carcinoma of the esophagus and good performance status who are considering a non-surgical approach and for whom combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated in the judgment of the treating oncologist.

selected_rules.activation_condition: when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being used
selected_rules.execute: Prefer a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen is a reasonable chemotherapy regimen to use when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is used.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being considered for concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Inform the patient about increased acute toxicity with this approach
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients should be made aware of the increased acute toxicity associated with this approach.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with dysphagia and a newly diagnosed localized mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma is evaluated by oncology. Staging shows T2N1 with a small-volume nodal burden and no distant metastases. He has an ECOG performance status of 1, maintains oral intake with soft foods, and has no major cardiac or renal comorbidities. After discussion of treatment options, he declines surgery and asks about curative non-surgical therapy. The team considers combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy, which he is felt likely to tolerate. Because he is otherwise fit, the oncologist discusses a concomitant approach rather than radiotherapy alone. The patient also asks about side effects and which chemotherapy backbone is usually used with radiation.
recommended_actions: Use concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone.
Offer concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as a curative-intent non-surgical approach.
Prefer a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is used.
Inform the patient about increased acute toxicity associated with concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with localized esophageal carcinoma, good performance status, and a reasonable chance of completing concomitant chemoradiotherapy in a non-surgical curative setting. The rules support concomitant RTCT over radiotherapy alone, indicate it is a reasonable curative-intent option, suggest cisplatin-based chemotherapy as a reasonable regimen, and advise counseling about increased acute toxicity.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with dysphagia and newly diagnosed localized mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma is evaluated by oncology. Staging shows T2N1 with a small-volume nodal burden and no distant metastases. He has an ECOG performance status of 1, maintains oral intake with soft foods, and has no major cardiac or renal comorbidities. After discussion of treatment options, he declines surgery and asks about curative non-surgical therapy. The team reviews radiotherapy with chemotherapy, but he has a recent hospitalization for aspiration pneumonia and remains oxygen-dependent with borderline functional reserve. He would still prefer to avoid surgery and asks whether a combined approach is possible. The oncologist is considering whether treatment intensity should be reduced because he is unlikely to complete a full concurrent course. The patient also asks about side effects and which chemotherapy backbone is usually used with radiation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Very similar localized esophageal cancer case with refusal of surgery and discussion of chemoradiation, but the new oxygen dependence and poor reserve make full concomitant therapy unlikely to be tolerable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as curative non-surgical treatment
Choose a cisplatin-based concurrent regimen
Counsel only briefly about toxicity and move ahead with standard chemoradiation
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case no longer fits the subgroup for which concomitant RT and chemotherapy is recommended, because the patient’s recent aspiration pneumonia, oxygen dependence, and borderline reserve make completion of a concurrent curative regimen doubtful. The safer interpretation is that he needs reassessment of fitness and individualized discussion of less intensive or supportive options rather than automatic definitive chemoradiation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation applies to localized esophageal cancer patients with favorable performance status and a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT. Here, the added pulmonary complication and limited reserve invalidate that assumption, so the guideline-supported recommendation cannot be applied confidently.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with dysphagia and a newly diagnosed mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma is evaluated by oncology. Staging shows a bulky T3 primary with multiple enlarged mediastinal nodes and a separate suspicious liver lesion on CT; PET confirms hepatic uptake consistent with metastatic disease. He has an ECOG performance status of 1 and is still eating soft foods. He declines surgery and asks about curative non-surgical therapy. The team discusses treatment options, including radiotherapy with chemotherapy, but notes that the extent of disease is no longer localized. The patient asks whether a cisplatin-based regimen should be used with radiation and whether the acute side effects are worth it.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still includes esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, good performance status, and a discussion of non-surgical therapy, but the new liver metastasis changes the intent from localized curative treatment to metastatic disease management.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer curative concurrent chemoradiation
Prefer cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiation
Frame treatment as a standard non-surgical curative approach
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer the localized T1-3, small-volume N1, M0 setting addressed by the recommendation. Because metastatic liver disease is present, the guideline-concordant curative non-surgical concurrent chemoradiation pathway does not apply; management should be redirected toward systemic and palliative planning based on metastatic disease.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rules are contingent on localized, non-metastatic esophageal carcinoma with good performance status. The new distant metastasis invalidates those criteria, so the prior recommendation is not appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has pathologic T3 or margin-positive prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: In the only RCT for which long-term follow-up data are available, adjuvant RT following radical prostatectomy in patients with pathologic T3 or margin-positive prostate cancer has been shown to improve OS and reduce distant metastases compared to observation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has positive surgical margins
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has extracapsular extension
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has seminal vesicle invasion
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has any of positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion
selected_rules.execute: Use adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: On the basis of the overall trial findings as well as these subgroup analyses, it can be concluded that men found at radical prostatectomy to have any of positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion benefit from adjuvant RT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy and the comparison is observation with salvage therapy reserved
selected_rules.execute: Prefer adjuvant radiotherapy over observation with salvage therapy reserved
selected_rules.source_statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.execute: Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it improves overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.execute: Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces distant metastases
selected_rules.source_statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT3a disease with extracapsular extension and a focally positive posterior surgical margin. His postoperative PSA is currently undetectable, and he is recovering well with improving urinary continence. At follow-up, he asks whether he should wait for a PSA rise before doing anything further. The oncology team reviews the pathology and discusses postoperative management options, noting that his adverse pathologic features place him at higher risk of recurrence despite the undetectable PSA.
recommended_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Use adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it improves overall survival.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces distant metastases.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers postoperative adjuvant radiotherapy rules because the patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has pathologic T3 disease with extracapsular extension plus a positive surgical margin. Those findings meet the activation conditions for recommending adjuvant radiotherapy and for favoring adjuvant treatment after prostatectomy. The cited rules also support considering adjuvant radiotherapy due to overall survival and distant metastasis benefits.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT2c disease confined to the gland, with no extracapsular extension, no seminal vesicle invasion, and negative surgical margins. His postoperative PSA is currently undetectable, and he is recovering well with improving urinary continence. At follow-up, he asks whether he should wait for a PSA rise before doing anything further. The oncology team reviews the pathology and discusses postoperative management options, noting that his current pathology does not show the adverse features that would usually prompt immediate adjuvant treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still follows the same postoperative prostatectomy workflow with an undetectable PSA and a discussion of adjuvant therapy, but the final pathology is organ-confined and margin-negative.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Recommend immediate postoperative radiotherapy because of the prostate cancer history.
Advise treatment now rather than observation with PSA surveillance.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for adjuvant radiotherapy because the pathology lacks the qualifying adverse features: there is no pT3 disease, no positive margin, no extracapsular extension, and no seminal vesicle invasion. The safer interpretation is observation with routine PSA surveillance rather than immediate postoperative radiation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The supplied rules require pathologic T3 disease and/or positive margins, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion after radical prostatectomy. None are present here, so the adjuvant radiotherapy recommendation is not triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT3a disease with focal extracapsular extension, but the surgical margins are negative and there is no seminal vesicle invasion. His postoperative PSA is currently detectable at 0.3 ng/mL on the first check, and he is recovering well with improving urinary continence. At follow-up, he asks whether he should wait for a further PSA rise before doing anything further. The oncology team reviews the pathology and discusses postoperative management options, noting that his adverse pathologic feature still raises concern for recurrence, but the current PSA result suggests residual disease has already declared itself.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The pathology still includes extracapsular extension and the visit still centers on postoperative management, but the PSA is no longer undetectable, shifting the situation away from adjuvant treatment after complete resection.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Use adjuvant radiotherapy because of extracapsular extension.
Recommend immediate postoperative radiation based on the adverse pathology alone.
recommended_actions: Consider postoperative radiotherapy in the setting of detectable PSA after prostatectomy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette is not a clean adjuvant-radiotherapy scenario because the PSA is already detectable after surgery, which indicates biochemical persistence rather than the undetectable-PSA setting assumed by the adjuvant recommendation. The safer interpretation is to manage this as early salvage/postoperative radiotherapy consideration rather than routine adjuvant treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: Extracapsular extension would ordinarily trigger the adjuvant RT rule, but the detectable postoperative PSA changes the clinical context. The original recommendation about adjuvant RT after prostatectomy with undetectable PSA is no longer the best fit, so the correct management shifts to salvage-oriented postoperative radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy specimen is being handled in the operating room
selected_rules.execute: do not perform frozen section analysis for margin status
selected_rules.source_statement: Frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen (RPS) for margin status is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: processing the apex of radical prostatectomy specimen
selected_rules.execute: do not use an en face shave technique at the apex
selected_rules.source_statement: An en face (shave) technique is to be discouraged at the apex, as this approach can result in false-positive margin interpretation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is low-risk and surgery is the preferred option
selected_rules.execute: offer radical prostatectomy after full discussion and consideration of patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: RP should be offered to low-risk and intermediate-risk patients for whom surgery is the preferred option after full discussion with patient and taking into account patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is intermediate-risk and surgery is the preferred option
selected_rules.execute: offer radical prostatectomy after full discussion and consideration of patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: RP should be offered to low-risk and intermediate-risk patients for whom surgery is the preferred option after full discussion with patient and taking into account patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is high-risk
selected_rules.execute: offer referral for radiation consultation or review at a Multidisciplinary Cancer Conference (MCC)
selected_rules.source_statement: High-risk patients should be offered a referral for radiation consultation or review at a Multidisciplinary Cancer Conference (MCC).

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins
selected_rules.source_statement: The main goals of RP are (a) complete eradication of the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins, (b) preservation of urinary function, and (c) preservation of erectile function, where appropriate, but, in some cases, it is not possible to achieve all three.

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: balance margin rates with functional outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: Radical Prostatectomy should aim at achieving a negative margin, while ensuring a balance between margin rates and functional outcomes

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: consider sparing the neurovascular bundles as the standard approach except in high-risk patients
selected_rules.source_statement: Sparing of the neurovascular bundles should be considered the "standard approach" except for high-risk patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. The team discusses the operative goals, including complete removal of the cancer-containing organ and achieving negative margins while balancing functional outcomes. Because the patient is not considered high-risk, nerve-sparing is being considered as part of the standard approach. The specimen is then handed off for routine pathologic processing.
recommended_actions: Aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins.
Balance margin rates with functional outcomes.
Consider sparing the neurovascular bundles as the standard approach, since the patient is not high-risk.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a patient undergoing radical prostatectomy, which triggers operative goals related to negative margins and functional preservation. The patient is not high-risk, so neurovascular bundle sparing is appropriately considered. No frozen section or apex-specific processing details are included, and no radiation referral is indicated because the patient is intermediate-risk and already selected surgery.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. The team discusses the operative goals, including complete removal of the cancer-containing organ and achieving negative margins while balancing functional outcomes. However, a rapidly rising PSA and new pelvic lymph node enlargement on preoperative imaging have shifted concern for more extensive disease, and the surgeon is considering wider excision rather than nerve sparing. The specimen is then handed off for routine pathologic processing.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on radical prostatectomy for prostate cancer with preserved baseline function, but subtle preoperative progression to suspected nodal disease changes the appropriateness of routine nerve-sparing.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Preserve the neurovascular bundles as the standard approach
Proceed with routine nerve-sparing prostatectomy because the patient started as intermediate-risk
Focus only on margin status and functional preservation without adjusting the dissection plan
recommended_actions: Aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins.
Balance margin rates with functional outcomes.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer guideline-concordant for standard nerve-sparing because the new pelvic lymph node enlargement and rising PSA raise concern for more extensive disease. The safer interpretation is that oncologic completeness should take priority, and nerve sparing is not the default choice in this setting.
challenging_examples.rationale: Radical prostatectomy still triggers the general operative goals of negative margins and balancing function. The subtle new nodal concern invalidates the routine standard-approach nerve-sparing recommendation, since the patient is no longer a straightforward non-high-risk case.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 74-year-old man with localized prostate adenocarcinoma chose surgery after discussion of treatment options. He has intermediate-risk disease and is otherwise fit, with acceptable urinary control. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and notes that the specimen will require careful apical handling. The team reviews the plan to maximize cancer clearance while limiting functional harm. Because the anatomy is tight at the apex, the assistant suggests an en face shave to simplify processing and help identify a possible positive margin. The specimen is then sent to pathology.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette still describes routine radical prostatectomy in an intermediate-risk patient, but the only change is the proposed apical processing method.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use an en face shave technique at the apex to assess the margin
Process the apical specimen with a shave section because it is technically easier
Send the apex for en face margin assessment to reduce the chance of missing disease
recommended_actions: Aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins.
Balance margin rates with functional outcomes.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case does not support the apex-specific recommendation because en face shave processing at the apex is discouraged. The safer interpretation is to avoid that technique due to false-positive margin interpretation risk, while still pursuing standard radical prostatectomy goals.
challenging_examples.rationale: Radical prostatectomy still supports the general goals of negative margins and functional preservation. The modified detail specifically changes the apical processing step, making the discouraged en face shave recommendation inapplicable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has porphyria or known allergy to porphyrins
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PDT
selected_rules.source_statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include porphyria or known allergies to porphyrins.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Tumor erodes into a major blood vessel
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PDT
selected_rules.source_statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include ... tumours that erode into a major blood vessel.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Existing tracheoesophageal fistula
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PDT
selected_rules.source_statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include ... existing tracheoesophageal fistulas.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is pregnant, nursing, or a child
selected_rules.execute: Do not use Photofrin because its use has not been established
selected_rules.source_statement: Its use in pregnant women, nursing women, and children has not been established, and, therefore, Photofrin® is not recommended for use in those cases (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives endobronchial PDT
selected_rules.execute: Perform toilet bronchoscopy after treatment and repeat as indicated
selected_rules.source_statement: Toilet bronchoscopies should always be completed following endobronchial PDT to minimize the risk of bronchial obstruction, and the risk of hemoptysis should be considered prior to therapy and monitored post therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives PDT
selected_rules.execute: Monitor closely after the procedure for airway obstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Since tumour necrosis can result in post-treatment airway obstruction, patients should be closely monitored after undergoing the procedure and toilet bronchoscopies repeated as indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives Photofrin-based PDT
selected_rules.execute: Advise avoidance of direct sunlight and brightly focused indoor light for at least the photosensitivity period
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients must avoid exposure of eyes and skin to direct sunlight or brightly focused indoor light.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary NSCLC and medically inoperable early-stage disease accessible by bronchoscopy
selected_rules.execute: Consider photodynamic therapy as an option
