selected_rules.source_statement: Photodynamic therapy could be considered as an option for the treatment of early stage lung cancer in patients with medically inoperable disease that is accessible by bronchoscopy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 72-year-old man with severe COPD and poor pulmonary reserve has a newly diagnosed 1.8 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe bronchus. He is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy shows the lesion is accessible endobronchially without evidence of invasion into a major vessel or a tracheoesophageal fistula. He has no history of porphyria or porphyrin allergy. Because the tumor is early stage and bronchoscopically accessible, photodynamic therapy is being considered. The team plans treatment with Photofrin-based PDT and discusses the need for post-procedure airway surveillance. The patient also reports he works outdoors and will need clear instructions about light precautions after therapy.
recommended_actions: Consider photodynamic therapy as an option for this medically inoperable early-stage NSCLC that is accessible by bronchoscopy.
After PDT, closely monitor the patient for airway obstruction due to tumour necrosis.
Perform toilet bronchoscopy after endobronchial PDT and repeat as indicated to reduce the risk of bronchial obstruction.
If Photofrin-based PDT is used, advise avoidance of direct sunlight and brightly focused indoor light for at least the photosensitivity period.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches the rule for medically inoperable early-stage primary NSCLC accessible by bronchoscopy, supporting consideration of PDT. It also triggers the endobronchial PDT follow-up recommendations: toilet bronchoscopy and close monitoring for airway obstruction. Because Photofrin-based PDT is used, photosensitivity precautions are also indicated. No contraindication rules are triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 72-year-old man with severe COPD and poor pulmonary reserve has a newly diagnosed 1.8 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe bronchus. He is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy shows the lesion is visible but extends beyond the bronchial lumen with subtle narrowing of the adjacent mediastinal interface. There is no porphyria or porphyrin allergy, and no tracheoesophageal fistula. The team is discussing Photofrin-based photodynamic therapy because the tumor is still small and endobronchial, but they note that the lesion appears to be abutting a major vessel on contrast CT. The patient works outdoors and asks about light precautions if treatment proceeds.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case keeps the same age, COPD, histology, size, and endobronchial location, but the imaging now suggests major-vessel abutment, which changes the safety profile for PDT.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with photodynamic therapy for this medically inoperable early-stage lesion.
Plan Photofrin-based PDT with standard post-procedure airway surveillance and photosensitivity counseling.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for PDT consideration because the tumor now appears to abut a major vessel, making PDT unsafe under the supplied criteria. The safer interpretation is to avoid PDT and consider other non-PDT local or palliative options appropriate for a medically inoperable patient.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation depended on a medically inoperable early-stage NSCLC accessible by bronchoscopy without major-vessel involvement. The new detail of major-vessel abutment invalidates that condition and also raises a specific contraindication.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 69-year-old woman with severe emphysema and poor lung reserve has a newly diagnosed 1.5 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the left main bronchus. She is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy confirms the lesion is accessible endobronchially with no invasion into a major vessel and no tracheoesophageal fistula. She has no history of porphyria or porphyrin allergy. Because the tumor is early stage and bronchoscopically accessible, the team plans Photofrin-based photodynamic therapy. After treatment, they arrange close airway surveillance and a toilet bronchoscopy to clear necrotic debris. She mentions she is currently breastfeeding her 3-month-old infant and is worried about the medication used during PDT and whether she can continue nursing.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The disease stage, accessibility, and PDT workflow remain unchanged, but the patient is breastfeeding, which affects Photofrin use.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with Photofrin-based photodynamic therapy and give routine light-avoidance instructions.
Arrange toilet bronchoscopy and close monitoring after endobronchial PDT.
Continue breastfeeding without changing the treatment plan.
recommended_actions: Consider photodynamic therapy as an option for this medically inoperable early-stage NSCLC that is accessible by bronchoscopy.
After PDT, closely monitor the patient for airway obstruction due to tumour necrosis.
Perform toilet bronchoscopy after endobronchial PDT and repeat as indicated to reduce the risk of bronchial obstruction.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case still supports PDT consideration for an accessible early-stage, medically inoperable NSCLC and still triggers endobronchial PDT follow-up measures. However, Photofrin use is not established in nursing women, so the specific Photofrin-based recommendation is not guideline-concordant. A safer interpretation is to avoid Photofrin while considering alternative approaches consistent with her lactation status.
challenging_examples.rationale: The breastfeeding status invalidates the Photofrin-specific recommendation while leaving the broader PDT consideration and endobronchial post-treatment surveillance recommendations intact.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma and proposed risk-adapted surveillance based on tumour size or rete testis invasion
selected_rules.execute: Do not use a risk-adapted approach as the basis for management
selected_rules.source_statement: Thus, a risk-adapted approach cannot be recommended at this time.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma after orchidectomy
selected_rules.execute: Use surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy
selected_rules.source_statement: The DSG recommends surveillance as the preferred option, because adjuvant therapy is associated with important short and long-term toxicities and second malignancy risks with no evidence of improved survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma on primary surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Follow a minimum surveillance program with physical examination every 3 to 4 months, chest X-ray every 6 to 12 months, and CT abdomen/pelvis every 3 to 4 months for the first 3 years, then less often thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: The minimum surveillance program should be a physical examination every three to four months, chest X-ray every six to twelve months, and computerised tomography (CT) of the abdomen and pelvis every three to four months in the first three years and then less often thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma on primary surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Include follow-up investigations of sites at risk of relapse
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, follow-up should include appropriate investigations of sites at risk of relapse.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma who prefers immediate treatment or is unsuitable for primary surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients who prefer immediate treatment, or who are unsuitable for primary surveillance, adjuvant RT is the recommended option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma receiving adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a radiotherapy dose of at least 20 Gy and no more than 30 Gy
selected_rules.source_statement: When adjuvant RT is the preferred option, a radiation dose of at least 20 Gy and no more than 30 Gy is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma treated with adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Continue post-treatment monitoring for relapse; except after extended-field radiotherapy, follow-up should be as thorough as surveillance
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients treated with adjuvant therapy, post treatment monitoring for disease relapse is still necessary. Except in the specific case of extended-field radiotherapy, the follow-up after adjuvant therapy should be as thorough as the surveillance conducted in the absence of adjuvant therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma and planned adjuvant therapy with fertility concerns
selected_rules.execute: Offer sperm banking; if radiotherapy is used, offer scrotal shielding
selected_rules.source_statement: If adjuvant therapy is planned, sperm banking (and scrotal shielding with RT) should be offered if future fertility is of concern to the patient.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man underwent right radical orchidectomy for a 2.1 cm pure seminoma confined to the testis, with negative margins and no metastatic disease on staging scans. He asks whether the size of the tumour and the absence of rete testis invasion could justify a less intensive follow-up plan. He is otherwise healthy and is considering options for post-orchidectomy management. After discussion, he is told that surveillance is the preferred approach after orchidectomy, and that management should not be based on a risk-adapted strategy using tumour size or rete testis invasion. He agrees to proceed with surveillance and understands the need for regular follow-up visits and imaging.
recommended_actions: Use surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy.
Do not use a risk-adapted approach as the basis for management.
source_case.rationale: This case matches adult stage I testicular seminoma after orchidectomy, triggering the recommendation for surveillance. The vignette also explicitly raises tumour size and rete testis invasion as the basis for tailoring follow-up, which triggers the rule against using a risk-adapted approach.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man underwent right radical orchidectomy for a 2.1 cm pure seminoma confined to the testis, with negative margins and no metastatic disease on staging scans. He asks whether the size of the tumour and the absence of rete testis invasion could justify a less intensive follow-up plan. He is otherwise healthy but says he has major difficulty travelling for repeated imaging and would prefer a treatment that minimizes follow-up burden. After discussion, he is told that because he is not a good candidate for regular surveillance visits, adjuvant radiotherapy is an appropriate post-orchidectomy option. He is also counselled that if radiotherapy is chosen, fertility preservation should be addressed first and scrotal shielding should be used.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves stage I pure seminoma after orchidectomy and mentions tumour size and rete testis invasion, but the key change is that surveillance is no longer suitable because of follow-up barriers.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Choose surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy approach and avoid risk-adapted follow-up.
Base follow-up intensity on tumour size and rete testis invasion.
Defer adjuvant treatment and schedule routine surveillance imaging instead.
recommended_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy.
Use a radiotherapy dose of at least 20 Gy and no more than 30 Gy if adjuvant RT is selected.
Continue post-treatment monitoring for relapse with follow-up as thorough as surveillance.
Offer sperm banking and scrotal shielding if fertility is a concern and radiotherapy is planned.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still stage I seminoma after orchidectomy, but the patient is unsuitable for primary surveillance because regular follow-up is not feasible. That shifts management away from surveillance and makes adjuvant radiotherapy guideline-concordant, while still requiring standard relapse monitoring and fertility counseling.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 and R001 are invalidated because surveillance is no longer the preferred strategy when the patient is unsuitable for it, and the vignette no longer supports using a risk-adapted surveillance rationale. R005 applies because adjuvant RT is recommended for patients unsuitable for primary surveillance. R006, R007, and R008 also apply if adjuvant RT is chosen.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old man had a left radical orchidectomy for a 1.8 cm pure seminoma. Pathology shows invasion into the rete testis, but staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows no metastatic disease. He is anxious about relapse and asks whether the rete testis finding means he should receive less frequent follow-up because his risk is low. The oncology team explains that he has stage I seminoma and recommends surveillance after orchidectomy. They also document that management should not be changed on the basis of tumour size or rete testis invasion alone, and that routine follow-up imaging and examinations will be arranged.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case closely mirrors stage I seminoma after orchidectomy and still references tumour size and rete testis invasion, but the subtle change is that rete testis invasion is present rather than absent.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use rete testis invasion to justify a risk-adapted follow-up schedule.
Recommend adjuvant treatment because the tumour has a higher-risk feature.
Intensify or de-intensify surveillance based on tumour size and rete testis invasion.
recommended_actions: Use surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy.

Follow a minimum surveillance program with physical examination every 3 to 4 months, chest X-ray every 6 to 12 months, and CT abdomen/pelvis every 3 to 4 months for the first 3 years, then less often thereafter.
Include appropriate investigations of sites at risk of relapse.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains stage I seminoma after orchidectomy with no metastatic disease, so surveillance is still the preferred management. The presence of rete testis invasion does not make a risk-adapted approach guideline-concordant, because management should not be based on that factor.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 applies because the patient has adult stage I testicular seminoma after orchidectomy. R003 and R004 apply because he is on primary surveillance. R001 is invalidated because the vignette no longer presents a guideline-supported basis for risk-adapted management, even though the tempting clue of rete testis invasion remains.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering routine allogeneic transplantation for multiple myeloma
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend allogeneic transplantation as routine therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to recommend allogeneic transplantation as routine therapy for multiple myeloma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient may be eligible for transplantation and stem cells have not yet been collected
selected_rules.execute: Avoid extensive exposure to alkylating agents such as melphalan before stem cell collection
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who are potentially eligible for transplantation should be referred for transplant assessment early after diagnosis and should not be given extensive exposure to alkylating agents such as melphalan prior to the collection of stem cells.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering tandem transplantation outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: Do not use two transplants performed in succession outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to recommend a treatment plan that includes two transplants performed in succession (tandem transplantation) outside of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed patient is being considered for thalidomide
selected_rules.execute: Use thalidomide only in the context of a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Thalidomide should only be used in newly diagnosed patients in the context of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering planned double (tandem) autologous stem cell transplantation
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend planned tandem ASCT on current evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: Planned double (tandem) ASCT cannot be recommended on the current evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering purging or selection of harvested stem cells
selected_rules.execute: Do not use currently available purging methods routinely
selected_rules.source_statement: Currently available methods of purging have not demonstrated clinical benefit and are not, therefore, recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with advanced-stage multiple myeloma and good performance status
selected_rules.execute: Recommend autologous transplantation
selected_rules.source_statement: Autologous transplantation is recommended for patients with advanced-stage myeloma and good performance status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is under 65 years old, has no significant renal dysfunction, and has had hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Proceed with autologous transplantation; evidence is strongest in this group
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence is strongest for patients under 65 years of age without significant renal dysfunction following hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is newly diagnosed with IgG multiple myeloma after presenting with fatigue, anemia, lytic bone lesions, and mild hypercalcemia. He has ECOG performance status 1, normal creatinine, and no major comorbidities. After hydration and initial remission-induction chemotherapy, his disease burden has improved. He asks whether he should undergo stem cell transplant and whether he should receive a planned second transplant because a friend told him that “more is better.” His oncologist is also considering adding thalidomide to the upfront regimen and wonders if stem cells should be purged before collection. Stem cells have not yet been collected.
recommended_actions: Proceed with autologous transplantation; evidence is strongest in patients under 65 years old without significant renal dysfunction after hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy.
Refer for transplant assessment early and avoid extensive exposure to alkylating agents such as melphalan before stem cell collection.
Do not recommend planned tandem transplantation outside a clinical trial.
Use thalidomide only in the context of a clinical trial.
Do not use currently available stem cell purging methods routinely.
source_case.rationale: This patient matches the strongest evidence group for autologous transplantation: age under 65, preserved renal function, and completion of hydration plus induction therapy. Because stem cells have not yet been collected and he may be transplant-eligible, exposure to alkylating agents should be limited before collection. The vignette also raises two practices that are not routinely recommended on current evidence: planned tandem transplantation outside a trial and routine purging of harvested stem cells. Thalidomide should only be used in newly diagnosed patients within a clinical trial.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with IgG multiple myeloma presents after 2 cycles of induction therapy for fatigue, anemia, lytic bone lesions, and mild hypercalcemia. He remains ECOG 1 and has normal creatinine, but a pre-treatment evaluation showed an ejection fraction of 28% with intermittent decompensated heart failure, which has limited further therapy. Because he has not yet had stem cells collected, his oncologist asks whether he should still be referred for autologous transplant and whether melphalan should be held back until collection. The patient also asks about a planned second transplant because a relative said it improves remission depth, and the team wonders whether thalidomide should be added upfront or whether harvested cells should be purged before storage.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case keeps the same age, myeloma subtype, induction setting, and transplant-related questions, but adds a major cardiac contraindication that makes the strongest autologous transplant recommendation no longer fit.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with autologous transplantation now that induction has been given
Refer urgently for transplant collection and avoid melphalan before harvest
Plan tandem transplantation to maximize depth of response
Add thalidomide to the upfront regimen
Use a stem cell purging method before cryopreservation
recommended_actions: Do not recommend autologous transplantation without careful reassessment of transplant fitness; address the severe cardiomyopathy first.
Do not plan tandem transplantation outside a clinical trial.
Use thalidomide only in the context of a clinical trial.
Do not use currently available stem cell purging methods routinely.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette no longer matches the strong autologous transplant recommendation because the patient has significant cardiac dysfunction with heart failure, making him a poor transplant candidate despite his age and renal function. The safer interpretation is that transplant eligibility is uncertain and needs cardiology/hematology reassessment rather than routine proceeding with ASCT.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation depended on being under 65 with preserved renal function after induction and hydration. The added severe systolic dysfunction and decompensated heart failure invalidate that transplant-eligibility assumption. The other non-recommended practices still remain in the case: tandem transplant outside a trial, thalidomide outside a trial, and routine purging of collected stem cells.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old woman with newly diagnosed IgG multiple myeloma has completed hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy for anemia, lytic lesions, and mild hypercalcemia. She is ECOG 0, creatinine is normal, and she is being evaluated for autologous stem cell transplant. However, because she received several months of oral melphalan from her local clinic before referral, stem cell collection has already become difficult and the transplant team notes poor mobilization. She asks whether a planned second transplant would improve cure rates. Her oncologist is also considering thalidomide as part of a newly diagnosed regimen, and the collection lab asks whether stem cells should be purged before freezing.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This remains a transplant-eligible myeloma patient after induction with the same questions about transplant strategy, but prior extensive melphalan exposure has already undermined the collection step that the original recommendation assumes is still pending.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed directly with autologous transplantation based on age and renal function
Continue with routine stem cell collection planning without concern for prior therapy
Recommend tandem transplantation because she is fit
Start thalidomide in the upfront regimen
Purge the harvested stem cells before storage
recommended_actions: Recognize that extensive prior melphalan exposure has compromised stem cell collection; transplant planning should be individualized with reassessment of mobilization options.
Do not plan tandem transplantation outside a clinical trial.
Use thalidomide only in the context of a clinical trial.
Do not use currently available stem cell purging methods routinely.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although she otherwise resembles a standard transplant candidate, the prior prolonged melphalan exposure makes the early-collection recommendation no longer applicable in the intended way because stem cell mobilization is already impaired. The safer interpretation is to reassess transplant feasibility and collection strategy rather than simply proceeding under the usual pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation about limiting alkylator exposure before stem cell collection is violated here because the exposure has already occurred. The case still supports the anti-tandem, anti-plain-thalidomide, and anti-routine-purging recommendations, but not the straightforward transplant pathway implied by the original vignette.

selected_rules.activation_condition: accessory labeled single use or disposable
selected_rules.execute: do not reprocess the accessory
selected_rules.source_statement: Accessories labeled as either single use or disposable should not be reprocessed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: withdrawal time as a quality metric
selected_rules.execute: do not require a specific withdrawal time target or audit withdrawal time for quality assurance
selected_rules.source_statement: It is not necessary to achieve a specific withdrawal time target or to audit this indicator for quality assurance purposes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: gastroenterologist seeking credentialing
selected_rules.execute: complete a formal two-year subspecialty training program, with an option for a third year, before entering full-time practice
selected_rules.source_statement: To be considered for credentialing, gastroenterologists must complete a formal two-year subspecialty training program, with the option of a third year of subspecialty training, before entering full-time practice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: other physician including surgical resident seeking qualification for colonoscopy
selected_rules.execute: acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over at least six months
selected_rules.source_statement: Prior to being qualified, other physicians, including surgical residents, must acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over a period of at least six months.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee being assessed for competence
selected_rules.execute: ensure average independent cecal intubation rate is at least 85% for all colonoscopies
selected_rules.source_statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee completing colonoscopy training
selected_rules.execute: ensure at least 300 colonoscopies are performed during training
selected_rules.source_statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee completing 300 colonoscopies during training
selected_rules.execute: ensure at least 50 polypectomies are anticipated during training
selected_rules.source_statement: If 300 colonoscopies are performed during training, it is anticipated that at least 50 polypectomies would have been performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee completing training
selected_rules.execute: assess cognitive competence including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition to proficiency in the technical aspects of colonoscopy, proficiency in cognitive aspects of the procedure is essential, including knowledge of appropriate contraindications and indications for colonoscopy, application of appropriate screening and surveillance intervals (4), histologic classification of polyps and their significance, and knowledge of how to deal with findings encountered at the time of colonoscopy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old surgical resident is rotating through gastroenterology and asks to be signed off for independent colonoscopy practice before starting a community job next month. He has completed a short endoscopy elective and has assisted on several procedures, but his supervising physician notes that he has not yet completed a structured colonoscopy training period. During review, the resident says he is comfortable with basic scope handling but has not independently performed enough procedures to demonstrate consistent performance, and he is still learning indications, contraindications, and surveillance intervals.
recommended_actions: Acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over a period of at least six months before being qualified.

Assess cognitive competence, including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings encountered at colonoscopy.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes an other physician/surgical resident seeking qualification for colonoscopy, which triggers the recommendation for at least six months of specific knowledge and technical training. It also describes a trainee completing training with incomplete cognitive knowledge, triggering assessment of the cognitive competencies required for colonoscopy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 31-year-old surgical resident is finishing a gastroenterology elective and asks to be signed off for independent colonoscopy practice before starting a community job in two months. He has assisted on many procedures and has independently completed a focused colonoscopy rotation, but the supervising physician notes that the formal training period has been just under six months. He reports he is comfortable with basic scope handling and has independently completed 290 colonoscopies during training, including 48 polypectomies. On review, he can identify common indications and contraindications, but he is still uncertain about the correct surveillance interval after several polyp histologies and needs prompting when discussing management of findings encountered during colonoscopy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still features a surgical resident seeking colonoscopy credentialing with substantial hands-on experience and partial cognitive familiarity, but the formal training duration and procedure counts fall just short of the competence thresholds.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Sign off the resident for independent colonoscopy practice now
Accept his training as sufficient because he has nearly 300 cases and has already performed polypectomies
Focus only on technical scope handling and ignore the remaining cognitive gaps
recommended_actions: Assess cognitive competence, including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings encountered at colonoscopy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for qualification because the resident has not yet completed at least six months of specific colonoscopy training and has not reached the expected training volume. The safer interpretation is that he remains in training and should not be signed off for independent practice yet, although cognitive competence should still be formally assessed.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the same trainee-credentialing context, but the training period is just under six months and the procedural experience is below 300 colonoscopies with fewer than 50 polypectomies. That invalidates the qualification recommendations tied to R004, R006, and R007. The remaining applicable guidance is R008 because he is still being evaluated for colonoscopy competence.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 28-year-old surgical resident asks for independent colonoscopy privileges after completing an endoscopy elective and assisting on multiple procedures during residency. He has been involved in a structured training program for more than six months and has completed 320 colonoscopies, with 52 polypectomies logged under supervision. The program director notes that his cecal intubation rate is 86% when assisted cases are excluded, but when all colonoscopies are counted, including several markedly incomplete exams early in training, his average independent cecal intubation rate falls to 82%. He has a solid grasp of indications and contraindications, but still occasionally hesitates when asked to choose surveillance intervals after removal of serrated lesions.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The resident appears to have exceeded the usual training time and case volume, with a cecal intubation rate that sounds acceptable on first glance, but the overall average across all colonoscopies remains below the competence threshold.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Grant independent colonoscopy privileges based on his case volume and supervised performance
Document him as competent because his assisted cecal intubation rate is above 85%
Move directly to credentialing without further review of his surveillance knowledge
recommended_actions: Acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over at least six months before being qualified.
Ensure at least 300 colonoscopies are performed during training.
Ensure average independent cecal intubation rate is at least 85% for all colonoscopies.

Assess cognitive competence, including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings encountered at colonoscopy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not concordant with the competence criterion because the relevant cecal intubation rate is 82% when all colonoscopies are counted, which is below the 85% threshold. The safer interpretation is that he has adequate exposure but is not yet fully competent and still needs further training and cognitive review.
challenging_examples.rationale: The scenario closely resembles a trainee nearing credentialing, but the key performance metric is below the required average independent cecal intubation rate. Because he has more than six months of training, over 300 colonoscopies, and more than 50 polypectomies, the training-volume rules still apply, along with cognitive assessment. Only R005 is invalidated by the subthreshold overall CIR.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has not progressed on first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
selected_rules.source_statement: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at a dose of 25 Gy in 10 fractions to people with limited-stage disease SCLC and World Health Organization (WHO) performance status 0 to 2, if their disease has not progressed on first-line treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites
selected_rules.execute: Consider thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: Consider thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation for people with extensive-stage disease SCLC who have had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has responded to first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
selected_rules.source_statement: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at a dose of 25 Gy in 10 fractions for people with extensive-stage disease SCLC and WHO performance status 0 to 2, if their disease has responded to first-line treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has responded to treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider PCI because the recommendation strength is not strong
selected_rules.source_statement: The committee agreed that "consider" is the appropriate strength for the recommendation on PCI.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has responded to treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider PCI when benefits are judged to probably outweigh harms, acknowledging uncertainty
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group members agreed that the benefits are equal to or probably outweigh the harms, but they had some uncertainty about this. Therefore, they agreed with the NICE committee that the recommendation should not be strong and using the word "consider" was appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has SCLC without brain metastases and the question is whether to use prophylactic cranial irradiation instead of routine MRI follow-up
selected_rules.execute: Support further research comparing prophylactic cranial irradiation with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up
selected_rules.source_statement: What is the effectiveness and cost effectiveness of prophylactic cranial irradiation compared with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up in people with SCLC without brain metastases?

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Do not add extra recommendations beyond the endorsed NICE recommendations
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group members decided to endorse all of the recommendations from NICE without any modifications.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Use the endorsed recommendations as written because the Working Group endorsed them without modifications
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group members decided to endorse all of the recommendations from NICE without any modifications.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with limited-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of platinum-etoposide chemotherapy followed by concurrent thoracic radiotherapy. He has WHO performance status 1, no neurologic symptoms, and post-treatment CT shows no progression of disease. Brain MRI shows no metastases. He is recovering well, with mild fatigue but otherwise stable weight and appetite. At follow-up, the oncology team discusses next steps to reduce the risk of future brain metastases. The patient asks whether any additional treatment is recommended now that his cancer has not progressed on first-line therapy.
recommended_actions: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and no progression after first-line treatment, which directly triggers the recommendation for prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with limited-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of platinum-etoposide chemotherapy followed by concurrent thoracic radiotherapy. He has WHO performance status 1, no neurologic symptoms, and post-treatment CT shows stable residual hilar fullness without new lesions. Brain MRI shows no metastases. He is recovering well, with mild fatigue but otherwise stable weight and appetite. At follow-up, the oncology team discusses next steps to reduce the risk of future brain metastases. The patient asks whether any additional treatment is recommended now that his cancer has not grown on first-line therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall setting is still post-treatment small cell lung cancer with no brain metastases and a discussion about preventing CNS relapse, but the thoracic imaging shows residual stable disease rather than no progression after first-line treatment.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.
Proceed directly with brain-directed prophylaxis now that treatment is complete.
recommended_actions: Support further research comparing prophylactic cranial irradiation with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up in people with SCLC without brain metastases.
Use the endorsed recommendations as written because the Working Group endorsed them without modifications.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the limited-stage PCI recommendation because the vignette no longer clearly states disease has not progressed; it describes stable residual disease, which is not the same activation condition. A safer interpretation is that routine MRI surveillance and individualized oncology follow-up remain appropriate unless the broader clinical context confirms non-progression.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires limited-stage SCLC, WHO PS 0 to 2, and no progression after first-line treatment. The modified case weakens the response criterion by describing residual stable disease rather than clearly no progression, so the specific PCI recommendation is no longer triggered. The general research/endorsement statements remain applicable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 71-year-old woman with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer received carboplatin-etoposide with an excellent systemic response. She has WHO performance status 2, no neurologic symptoms, and her chest CT shows near-complete response in the thorax. However, PET-CT after treatment reveals a new 1.4 cm adrenal lesion that is metabolically active, while brain MRI remains negative for metastases. She is asking about further treatment to reduce the risk of brain relapse now that chemotherapy is finished.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like post-chemotherapy small cell lung cancer with good response and no brain metastases, but there is a new metastatic adrenal lesion, so the disease has not responded at all sites.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.
Consider adding thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation.
Move ahead with PCI because she responded well in the chest.
recommended_actions: Support further research comparing prophylactic cranial irradiation with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up in people with SCLC without brain metastases.
Use the endorsed recommendations as written because the Working Group endorsed them without modifications.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This does not meet the extensive-stage PCI criteria because the disease has not had a partial or complete response at distant sites; the new adrenal metastasis indicates incomplete response/progression outside the thorax. The safer approach is to recognize that the PCI recommendations are not triggered and to manage according to active metastatic disease and surveillance needs.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 requires extensive-stage SCLC with partial or complete response within the thorax and at distant sites. R003-R005 require extensive-stage disease that has responded to first-line treatment. The new adrenal lesion means there is no complete or partial response at distant sites, so these PCI-related recommendations are not activated. The non-recommendation research/endorsement entries still apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with high-risk localized prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with high-risk localized prostate cancer, bisphosphonates are not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC), denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage is not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: creatinine clearance is below 30 mL/min or serum creatinine is above 265 µmol/L in a man considered for zoledronic acid
selected_rules.execute: do not give zoledronic acid
selected_rules.source_statement: ZA is not recommended below a CrCl of 30 mL/min (or SCr >265 µmol/L).

selected_rules.activation_condition: a patient is receiving denosumab and zoledronic acid together
selected_rules.execute: do not give denosumab and zoledronic acid in combination
selected_rules.source_statement: Denosumab and ZA should not be given in combination.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use zoledronic acid at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with symptomatic mCRPC, Ra-223 is recommended to extend overall survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer and predominantly bony metastases with no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases
selected_rules.execute: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: This recommendation applies to men with predominantly bony metastases and no evidence of visceral metastases or large nodal metastases.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with prostate adenocarcinoma is seen after staging workup for a rising PSA and abnormal MRI. Biopsy shows Gleason 9 disease with extracapsular extension, but CT and bone scan show no distant metastases. He has no bone pain, maintains a good performance status, and has not started androgen deprivation therapy yet. His creatinine clearance is 78 mL/min. He asks whether he should start a bone-strengthening medication now to prevent cancer spread to the bones. He is otherwise healthy and takes only tamsulosin for urinary symptoms.
recommended_actions: Do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis in this patient with high-risk localized prostate cancer.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a man with high-risk localized prostate cancer without metastatic disease, which matches the activation condition for R001. The rule specifically states bisphosphonates are not recommended for reducing the risk of first bone metastasis in this setting.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 71-year-old man with prostate adenocarcinoma returns after restaging for rising PSA despite castrate testosterone on leuprolide. He has diffuse bone pain requiring occasional oxycodone, and imaging shows multiple blastic bone metastases with no liver or lung lesions. However, the CT also shows a 3.2 cm retroperitoneal lymph node conglomerate. He asks whether radium-223 could help him live longer and improve his pain. His ECOG performance status is 1, and he has not previously received a bone-targeted agent.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with bone-only emphasis, but the presence of a large nodal mass removes the clean eligibility for radium-223 under the bone-predominant/no-large-node criterion.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start radium-223 for symptomatic mCRPC to improve survival and pain
Proceed with a bone-targeted agent at the metastasis-indicated dose without further consideration
recommended_actions: Use zoledronic acid or denosumab at a bone metastasis-indicated dosage to help prevent or delay skeletal-related events in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer.
Do not select radium-223 as the guideline-concordant option here because of the large nodal disease burden.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still symptomatic mCRPC with bone metastases, so the bone-modifying agent recommendations apply. The vignette no longer cleanly fits the radium-223 subgroup because there is a 3.2 cm retroperitoneal nodal conglomerate, which is a large nodal metastasis. A safer interpretation is to manage skeletal risk with a bone-targeted agent rather than assuming radium-223 is indicated.
challenging_examples.rationale: R008 requires symptomatic mCRPC with predominantly bony metastases and no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases. The added 3.2 cm nodal conglomerate violates that condition. R005 and R006 still apply because he has mCRPC with bone metastases.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with high-risk prostate adenocarcinoma is evaluated after biopsy and staging. His Gleason score is 9 with extracapsular extension, and he is planning definitive external beam radiation plus long-term androgen deprivation therapy. Bone scan and CT do not show distant metastases. He has no bone pain and remains physically active. At today’s visit he asks whether he should start a medication to strengthen his bones and reduce the chance of the cancer spreading to bone. His creatinine clearance is 92 mL/min. He has not yet started treatment, but his PSA is expected to be monitored closely.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The cancer appears high-risk and nonmetastatic like the source case, but the intended treatment is definitive local therapy for high-risk disease rather than a scenario warranting preventive bisphosphonate use.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start a bisphosphonate now to prevent first bone metastasis
Add zoledronic acid prophylactically during initial management of his localized disease
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains nonmetastatic high-risk prostate cancer, but there is no guideline-supported role for bisphosphonates to prevent first bone metastasis in this setting. The safer interpretation is to proceed with standard definitive local and hormonal therapy and reserve bone-directed agents for other indications.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is the relevant rule for high-risk localized prostate cancer, and it specifically advises against bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis. No other supplied rule is triggered because he is not metastatic, not castration-resistant, and not receiving a bone agent with renal contraindication or combination therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 5 and is age 25
selected_rules.execute: do not start screening from age 25
selected_rules.source_statement: This (Ontario) Working Group, however, did not find justification for screening from age 25 in this group (or any of the other high risk groups).

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in target population risk category 1, 2, 3, or 5
selected_rules.execute: perform MRI in addition to mammography
selected_rules.source_statement: MRI in addition to mammography is recommended for women in target population risk categories 1, 2, 3, and 5 above.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
selected_rules.execute: screen annually
selected_rules.source_statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the appropriate screening frequency, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually, as this was the frequency typical of the identified studies on which the recommendation for screening is based.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
selected_rules.execute: screen from age 30 to 69 years
selected_rules.source_statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the ages of patients who should be screened, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually from 30 to 69 years of age.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 5 and received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30
selected_rules.execute: use MRI in addition to mammography because benefits outweigh harms
selected_rules.source_statement: It is the opinion of the Working Group that the benefits of MRI in terms of increased sensitivity outweigh the potential harms of higher recall rates and biopsy rates for all women in risk category 5 who received ≥20 Gy radiation before the age of 30.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 4 with high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer plus breast density at least 50% and family history of breast cancer but not enough to be category 3
selected_rules.execute: consider adding MRI to mammography
selected_rules.source_statement: With respect to risk category 4, there are preliminary data that a subgroup of patients in risk category 4 might benefit from the addition of MRI to mammography (e.g., women who in addition to a high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer, also have breast density ≥50% and a family history of breast cancer though insufficient to put them in category 3).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is in risk category 4
selected_rules.execute: do not make a recommendation for MRI screening based on current evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence is insufficient to recommend MRI screening for patients in risk category 4.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman presents to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 30 Gy, and her current risk assessment places her in risk category 5. She has no breast symptoms and is anxious because a relative was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. She asks whether she should begin screening now, since she has heard that high-risk women need early imaging. Her clinician reviews her history and confirms that she falls into a high-risk group, but also notes that she is only 25 years old. The discussion focuses on what screening, if any, should be started at this age and whether MRI should be added to mammography later on.
recommended_actions: Do not start screening from age 25.
If/when screening is undertaken in risk category 5 patients who received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30, use MRI in addition to mammography because the benefits outweigh the potential harms.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because the patient is a woman in risk category 5 and is age 25, and the rule explicitly says not to start screening from age 25. R005 is also triggered because she is in risk category 5 and received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30; for this group, the Working Group considers MRI in addition to mammography to have benefits that outweigh harms.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman presents to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 18 Gy, and her current risk assessment places her in risk category 5. She has no breast symptoms and is anxious because a relative was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. She asks whether she should begin screening now, since she has heard that high-risk women need early imaging. Her clinician reviews her history and confirms that she falls into a high-risk group, but also notes that she is only 25 years old. The discussion focuses on what screening, if any, should be started at this age and whether MRI should be added to mammography later on.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still sounds like a very high-risk young survivor with prior chest radiation, but the radiation dose is just below the threshold needed for the MRI-plus-mammography recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start breast screening now because she is high risk and already 25.
Add breast MRI to mammography based on her prior mantle radiation and risk category 5 status.
recommended_actions: Do not start screening from age 25.
Plan annual screening beginning at age 30 to 69 years if she remains in a high-risk screening program.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the age-25 start and not concordant for the radiation-based MRI recommendation because she received only 18 Gy, not at least 20 Gy before age 30. The safer interpretation is that she remains high risk but does not meet the specific threshold for the MRI-plus-mammography recommendation tied to prior radiation exposure.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 no longer applies because the scenario is no longer the exact high-risk group specified by the rule, and R005 is invalidated because the radiation dose is below 20 Gy. The remaining applicable guidance is the general high-risk screening framework that favors annual screening from age 30 to 69.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman presents to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 30 Gy, and her current risk assessment places her in risk category 4 because her family history is limited to a second-degree relative with breast cancer. She has no breast symptoms and is anxious because a relative was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. She asks whether she should begin screening now, since she has heard that high-risk women need early imaging. Her clinician reviews her history and confirms prior chest radiation, but notes that her risk category is not 5. The discussion focuses on what screening, if any, should be started at this age and whether MRI should be added to mammography later on.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The history of mantle radiation and the age are unchanged, but the risk category is downgraded, which removes the specific category-5 radiation rule.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Begin breast screening now because she had mantle radiation and is worried about breast cancer.
Use MRI in addition to mammography because of her prior chest radiation exposure.
recommended_actions: Do not start screening from age 25.
Do not make a recommendation for MRI screening based on current evidence.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case does not meet the category-5 radiation criterion needed for the MRI-plus-mammography recommendation, and the age-25 start rule also does not apply in the same way once she is not in that specified group. The safer interpretation is that she is risk category 4, for which evidence is insufficient to recommend MRI screening.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is invalidated because the vignette no longer matches the exact high-risk category described in the rule, and R005 is invalidated because the patient is not in risk category 5. R007 applies because the patient is in risk category 4, for which the evidence is insufficient to recommend MRI screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A lung cancer survivor develops symptoms suggestive of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: Evaluate according to the symptoms
selected_rules.source_statement: Survivors who develop symptoms suggestive of recurrence, should be evaluated according to those symptoms.

selected_rules.activation_condition: After curative-intent treatment for NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: After curative-intent treatment for SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for SCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has late side effects of curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide support to help manage symptoms and improve quality of life
selected_rules.source_statement: Health-related quality of life (QoL) is very important for long-term survivors suffering from late side effects of their curative-intent therapy (including surgery, chemotherapy and radiation therapy). The following is a summary of issues reported by survivors. Health care professionals need to aid lung cancer survivors in handling these symptoms to improve QoL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide surveillance follow-up care
selected_rules.source_statement: For lung cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required and may be provided by specialists, family physicians or hospital-based nurses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy for NSCLC or SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Provide smoking cessation counselling
selected_rules.source_statement: Smoking cessation counselling is recommended for patients who have completed curativeintent therapy for NSCLC and SCLC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Providing smoking cessation advice to a lung cancer survivor
selected_rules.execute: Use behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice when possible
selected_rules.source_statement: Although verbal cessation advice from a health care professional is of benefit, interventions that involve behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy and ongoing surveillance is being planned
selected_rules.execute: Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has developed a persistent dry cough, new left-sided chest discomfort, and unintentional weight loss of 4 kg. He also reports increasing fatigue but no fever or infectious symptoms. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. Given the new symptoms in a lung cancer survivor, recurrence must be considered, while he also remains due for ongoing surveillance after curative-intent treatment.
recommended_actions: Evaluate the new cough, chest discomfort, weight loss, and fatigue according to symptoms suggestive of recurrence.
Provide surveillance follow-up care after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging.
Provide smoking cessation counselling.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because this lung cancer survivor has new symptoms suggestive of recurrence. R005 applies because he has completed curative-intent therapy, so surveillance follow-up care is required. R008 applies because ongoing surveillance should include medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging. R006 applies because smoking cessation counselling is recommended after curative-intent therapy for NSCLC.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has developed a persistent dry cough and increasing fatigue, but he denies chest pain, fever, hemoptysis, or unintentional weight loss. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. A chest CT obtained 2 weeks ago at his scheduled surveillance visit showed only stable postoperative scarring and no suspicious lesions. The visit today is to review symptoms and ongoing follow-up after curative-intent treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves a lung cancer survivor after curative-intent therapy with new respiratory complaints and ongoing surveillance needs, but the symptoms are less specific and recent imaging is reassuring.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Escalate immediately for presumed recurrence based on the new cough and fatigue
Order an urgent metastatic workup despite a recent negative surveillance CT
Treat this as symptomatic relapse without further clinical correlation
recommended_actions: Provide surveillance follow-up care.
Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging.
Provide smoking cessation counselling.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the recurrence-symptom pathway because the current complaints are nonspecific and a recent chest CT showed no suspicious disease. The safer interpretation is continued surveillance with symptom review, exam, and appropriate follow-up rather than presuming recurrence.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is not triggered because the symptoms are not sufficiently suggestive of recurrence in the context of a recent negative surveillance CT and no alarm features. R005, R006, and R008 still apply because he completed curative-intent therapy for NSCLC and remains in surveillance, with smoking cessation counselling indicated.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old woman is 14 months after chemoradiation for limited-stage small cell lung cancer. She completed curative-intent treatment and has been followed in clinic. She now reports 4 weeks of mild exertional dyspnea and intermittent chest tightness, but no cough, weight loss, neurologic symptoms, or focal bone pain. She recently recovered from a viral upper respiratory infection, and her home pulse oximetry has been 97% to 98% on room air. On exam, breath sounds are clear with only mild wheeze during forced expiration. She is a former smoker and quit shortly before treatment. Her last surveillance chest imaging 6 weeks ago showed no evidence of recurrence, and today’s visit is for routine follow-up after completion of therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient remains a post-treatment lung cancer survivor in routine follow-up, but the new breathing symptoms are mild, have an alternative explanation, and were preceded by a normal recent surveillance scan.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Assume recurrent small cell lung cancer and launch an urgent recurrence evaluation
Skip routine surveillance because the symptoms appear cancer-related
Focus only on symptom workup without continuing scheduled follow-up care
recommended_actions: Provide surveillance follow-up care.
Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter.
Provide smoking cessation counselling.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette does not clearly meet the symptom-suggestive-of-recurrence trigger because the dyspnea is mild, nonspecific, and plausibly post-viral, with a recent negative surveillance scan. The appropriate approach is continued scheduled surveillance and supportive evaluation of symptoms rather than presuming relapse.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is invalidated because the symptoms are not clearly suggestive of recurrence. R003 applies because she completed curative-intent therapy for SCLC, and R005, R008, and R006 also remain applicable as ongoing surveillance and smoking cessation counselling are indicated after curative-intent treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance reaches age 80.
selected_rules.execute: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
selected_rules.source_statement: Serial biopsy should not continue past the age of 80.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has low-risk localized prostate cancer (Gleason score ≤6).
selected_rules.execute: Use active surveillance as the preferred disease management strategy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with low-risk (Gleason score ≤6) localized prostate cancer, AS is the preferred disease management strategy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Include PSA testing every 3 to 6 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -PSA test every 3 to 6 months.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Include digital rectal examination every year.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -DRE every year.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Perform a 12- to 14-core confirmatory TRUS biopsy including anterior directed cores within 6 to 12 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance after confirmatory biopsy.
selected_rules.execute: Repeat serial biopsy at least every 3 to 5 years.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance has rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, or very low PSA free/total ratio.
selected_rules.execute: Investigate further with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Discordant findings between a patient's clinical course and pathologic findings can include rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, and very low PSA free/total ratio. Presence of these findings requires further investigation with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance has discordant clinical findings and pathologic findings.
selected_rules.execute: Use mpMRI at entry or during follow-up when indicated.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, being cognizant of both the high cost of mpMRI and its promise, it is recommended that when a patient's clinical findings are discordant with the pathologic findings, a mpMRI is indicated. When indicated, it may be considered at entry or during follow-up.

source_case.patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) has been managed with active surveillance. His PSA has been stable around 5.1 ng/mL, his digital rectal exams have remained normal, and his confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago showed no upgrading. He feels well and asks whether he still needs scheduled biopsies now that he is older. He has no new urinary symptoms and no concerning changes in PSA kinetics or exam findings.
recommended_actions: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule that serial biopsy should not continue once a patient on active surveillance reaches age 80. No additional discordant findings are present to trigger imaging or earlier repeat biopsy recommendations.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) has been managed with active surveillance. His PSA had been stable around 5.1 ng/mL, but over the last 8 months it has risen to 7.4 ng/mL, and the PSA density is now 0.23 based on a recent prostate volume estimate. His digital rectal exam remains normal, and his confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago showed no upgrading. He feels well and asks whether he still needs scheduled biopsies now that he is older. He has no new urinary symptoms and is otherwise unchanged.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on an 80-year-old man on active surveillance asking about ongoing biopsy needs, but the PSA kinetics and PSA density have become discordant with prior pathology.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Stop serial biopsies because he has reached age 80
Continue routine surveillance without further workup
Defer any additional imaging until the next scheduled visit
recommended_actions: Investigate further with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.
Use mpMRI at entry or during follow-up when indicated.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for stopping biopsies outright because the patient has new discordant findings: a rising PSA and PSA density above 0.2. The safer interpretation is that he needs further evaluation with mpMRI and/or an earlier repeat biopsy rather than simply discontinuing surveillance.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although age 80 would ordinarily argue against continuing serial biopsy, the new PSA rise and elevated PSA density activate the discordance recommendation for further investigation. The original age-based stopping rule is therefore not the only relevant guidance.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) has been managed with active surveillance. His PSA has stayed near 5 ng/mL, his PSA density remains 0.11, and his digital rectal exams have remained normal. He had a confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago showing no upgrading, and he is now asking whether he should have another scheduled biopsy. A recent office note mentions that he is planning to move out of state and is worried about the burden of ongoing testing. He has no new urinary symptoms or other concerning changes.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This looks like a routine aging active-surveillance follow-up question, but the clinical data remain stable and do not create a new indication for urgent re-biopsy or imaging.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Stop serial biopsies because he is 80 years old
Order an urgent mpMRI because he is on active surveillance
Repeat biopsy now because he is due for more intensive surveillance
recommended_actions: Include PSA testing every 3 to 6 months.
Include digital rectal examination every year.
Repeat serial biopsy at least every 3 to 5 years.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette does not support the age-based recommendation to stop serial biopsy because the modified scenario does not clearly establish that surveillance has crossed the threshold for discontinuation in a way that eliminates all other follow-up needs. The safer interpretation is to continue standard active-surveillance monitoring, including PSA, annual DRE, and periodic serial biopsy if surveillance is still being pursued.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves low-risk disease and stable findings without discordant features. The temptation is to stop all biopsy because of age, but the vignette does not add a new contraindication to standard surveillance intervals beyond the age-based rule being made less certain by the follow-up context.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document contains only publication/contact and copyright information, with no clinical recommendations.
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.
selected_rules.source_statement: For information about this document, please contact Dr. Eric Winquist, the lead author, through the PEBC via: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document states that the evidence review has been published as a supplement.
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence review has been published as a Supplement

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document states copyright restrictions on reproduction of the report and illustrations.
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.
selected_rules.source_statement: This report is copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario; the report and the illustrations herein may not be reproduced without the express written permission of Cancer Care Ontario.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. At the end of the note, the document includes only publication details and contact information for the lead author, Dr. Eric Winquist, through the PEBC, along with a statement that the evidence review has been published as a supplement.
