It also notes that the report and illustrations are copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario and may not be reproduced without written permission. No diagnostic algorithm, treatment recommendation, or follow-up plan is provided in the document excerpt.
recommended_actions: No clinical action can be extracted from this document excerpt.
source_case.rationale: The excerpt contains only publication/contact information, a statement that the evidence review was published as a supplement, and copyright restrictions, with no clinical recommendations. Therefore the triggered rules all map to no actionable clinical guidance.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man is seen in pulmonary clinic for an enlarging right upper lobe nodule discovered during workup of a chronic cough. The radiology note and oncology referral mention that the full evidence review is available as a Supplement and include the PEBC contact details for the lead author, Dr. Eric Winquist, if clarification is needed. The report footer also states that the document and its illustrations are copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario and may not be reproduced without written permission. Unlike the version circulated at the tumor board, this copy includes a brief appendix with staging suggestions, but the appendix is marked “draft” and does not include any treatment algorithm or actionable recommendation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The note still looks like a document excerpt dominated by publication details, supplement language, and copyright text, but it now also contains a non-actionable draft appendix rather than being only contact and copyright information.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with a lung cancer staging and treatment workup based on the appendix
Treat the appendix as an actionable guideline and start planning therapy
recommended_actions: No clinical action can be extracted from this document excerpt.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This excerpt is still non-actionable overall. Although it now includes a draft appendix, there is no finalized diagnostic algorithm, treatment recommendation, or follow-up plan to follow; the safer interpretation is to treat it as a publication excerpt rather than a clinical directive.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves the supplement notice and copyright restriction, but the addition of a draft appendix means it is no longer purely publication/contact information. However, there remains no usable clinical recommendation, so the only appropriate action is still no clinical action.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. The clinic intake packet contains publication details for the evidence review, including contact information for Dr. Eric Winquist through the PEBC and a statement that the report was published as a Supplement. The footer also says the report and illustrations are copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario and may not be reproduced without written permission. In this version, however, the radiology report is attached separately and describes the nodule as a stable 3 mm perifissural lymph node unchanged for 18 months, with no suspicious features and no recommendation for oncologic follow-up.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The packet still appears to be the same oncology referral with publication and copyright language, but the attached imaging summary shows a benign stable finding rather than a lesion requiring guideline-driven oncologic action.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Initiate a cancer staging evaluation for the lung nodule
Refer urgently for biopsy or thoracic oncology assessment
recommended_actions: No clinical action can be extracted from this document excerpt.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The document excerpt remains non-actionable, and the attached radiology detail actually suggests a benign, stable perifissural lymph node rather than a suspicious lung nodule. The safer interpretation is observation per the imaging report, not oncologic intervention based on the publication footer.
challenging_examples.rationale: The publication/contact and copyright elements still satisfy the non-recommendation rules, but the added imaging detail makes the clinical situation even less supportive of oncologic action. No guideline recommendation can be extracted from the excerpt.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as routine primary therapy with chemotherapy in this population
selected_rules.execute: Do not use G-CSF routinely as primary therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as primary therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering a fractionated weekly CHOP schedule instead of standard CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Use standard CHOP rather than fractionated weekly CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: No benefits from fractionating the treatment schedule have been detected; the potential for outcomes to be inferior is suggested.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide in a compressed 14-day schedule
selected_rules.execute: Avoid CHOEP-14 because of excessive toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: patients receiving CHOEP every 14 days experienced excessive toxicity

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide versus CHOP alone in older patients
selected_rules.execute: Do not add etoposide routinely to CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: No differences were detected in response rate, TTF, or OS, and the authors commented that the addition of etoposide was associated with significant toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering more intensive regimens than CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Avoid more intensive regimens when they add toxicity without survival benefit
selected_rules.source_statement: Two studies testing chemotherapy regimens that are more intensive than CHOP both detected an increase in toxicity with no survival benefit detected.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering second- or third-generation regimens instead of CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Prefer CHOP because it is at least as effective or less toxic
selected_rules.source_statement: Subset analyses comparing CHOP with "second and third generation" regimens show that CHOP is at least as effective or less toxic than these other regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is older than 60 with newly diagnosed advanced-stage aggressive histology non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ECOG less than 4, and no significant comorbid illness
selected_rules.execute: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy with cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine and prednisone (CHOP) is recommended for patients with no apparent cardiac disease or significant comorbidity. Dose and schedule should be the same as that used in younger patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and meets the guideline target population
selected_rules.execute: Add rituximab to CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of rituximab to CHOP is recommended for patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with bulky abdominal nodes, night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has well-controlled hypertension but no known cardiac disease, heart failure, or other major comorbid illness. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH; renal and liver function are acceptable. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure.
recommended_actions: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients.
Add rituximab to CHOP.
source_case.rationale: This patient fits the guideline target population: older than 60, newly diagnosed advanced-stage aggressive histology non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ECOG less than 4, and no significant comorbidity. For this group, CHOP is recommended at the same dose and schedule as in younger patients. Because he has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, rituximab should be added to CHOP.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man is newly diagnosed with stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma after biopsy of bulky abdominal nodes. He reports night sweats and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has well-controlled hypertension and denies any history of heart failure, ischemic heart disease, or other major comorbidity. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH, with acceptable renal and liver function. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure, and the team also notes that his pathology report shows weak CD20 expression rather than clear positivity.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like an older, fit patient with advanced DLBCL being considered for standard curative-intent therapy, but the CD20 finding is not convincingly positive.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start CHOP with rituximab
Proceed with standard-dose CHOP-based therapy and add rituximab routinely
Escalate to a more intensive regimen because he is fit and has bulky stage IV disease
recommended_actions: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case does not cleanly meet the rituximab recommendation because the lymphoma is not clearly CD20-positive; the guideline support for adding rituximab depends on diffuse large B-cell lymphoma that is appropriate for anti-CD20 therapy. The safer interpretation is to treat as an older patient with aggressive lymphoma using standard CHOP dosing, while clarifying the immunophenotype before deciding on rituximab.
challenging_examples.rationale: The patient still fits the older, advanced-stage aggressive lymphoma population for standard CHOP. However, the subtle change to weak CD20 expression invalidates the automatic rituximab recommendation. The curative-intent CHOP recommendation remains applicable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 71-year-old woman presents with newly diagnosed stage III diffuse large B-cell lymphoma involving cervical, mediastinal, and retroperitoneal nodes. She has drenching night sweats, weight loss, and an ECOG performance status of 1. Her only chronic illness is mild, well-controlled hypertension; she has no known cardiac disease, heart failure, or other significant comorbidity. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH with preserved renal and hepatic function. Her oncologist is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of her age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or compress the schedule to every 14 days with growth factor support. The pathology report confirms diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with CD20 positivity, but the team notes that the patient has a documented prior severe anaphylactic reaction to rituximab during an earlier course of therapy for a different lymphoma.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall setting still suggests an older, fit patient with newly diagnosed advanced DLBCL, but the prior rituximab anaphylaxis makes routine addition of rituximab unsafe.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add rituximab to CHOP
Give standard CHOP with rituximab despite the prior reaction
Use an intensified immunochemotherapy approach because the disease is advanced
recommended_actions: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The patient still meets criteria for standard CHOP in an older, fit individual with advanced aggressive lymphoma. However, rituximab is not guideline-concordant here because she has a documented severe prior rituximab hypersensitivity, so anti-CD20 therapy should be avoided unless a specialist desensitization strategy is explicitly chosen for a separate indication.
challenging_examples.rationale: Age, stage, histology, ECOG status, and lack of major comorbidity still support standard CHOP. The added detail of prior severe rituximab anaphylaxis invalidates the routine rituximab recommendation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HIV-related lymphoma and CD4 count below 50/mm3
selected_rules.execute: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of rituximab to chemotherapy in patients with CD4 <50/mm 3 is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated HIV-related lymphoma, CD4 count at least 50/mm3, and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with HIV-related lymphoma with a CD4 count ≥50/mm 3 who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and candidate for chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL), who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy, should receive their chemotherapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, no prior rituximab, and candidate for chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who have not previously received rituximab and who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy should receive this chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, prior rituximab exposure, response lasted at least one year, and candidate for therapy
selected_rules.execute: Give therapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who have previously received rituximab ... and who have achieved a response of at least one year's duration from the last rituximab administration and who are appropriate candidates for therapy should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and response to combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab
selected_rules.execute: Follow treatment with maintenance rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding SLL, who respond to treatment with combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab, this treatment should be followed by the use of maintenance rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Any patient planned for rituximab treatment
selected_rules.execute: Screen for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B core antibody before rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: The Hematology Disease Site Group recommends that all patients be screened for surface antigen for hepatitis B (HBsAg) and for hepatitis B core antibody (HBcAb) prior to treatment with rituximab.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 34/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease but no CNS involvement. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team considers him a candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function. He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
recommended_actions: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers R001 because the patient has HIV-related lymphoma and a CD4 count below 50/mm3. Under this rule, rituximab should not be added to chemotherapy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 68/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease without CNS involvement. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team considers him a candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function. He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like HIV-associated aggressive lymphoma in a curative-intent setting, but the CD4 count is just above the threshold that changes the rituximab recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add rituximab to the planned combination chemotherapy.
Proceed with standard rituximab-containing curative-intent chemoimmunotherapy without further infectious screening.
recommended_actions: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab.
Screen for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B core antibody before rituximab.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer a CD4 <50/mm3 case, so the prohibition against rituximab does not apply. Because he has previously untreated HIV-related lymphoma, CD4 count at least 50/mm3, and is a candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy, rituximab is appropriate. HBV screening remains required before starting rituximab.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation is invalidated by raising the CD4 count above 50/mm3. The modified case now satisfies the HIV-related lymphoma rituximab recommendation and the general rituximab HBV screening recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with newly diagnosed follicular lymphoma is seen for first-line treatment planning. Biopsy confirms indolent CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma, and she has no prior lymphoma therapy. She has bulky abdominal adenopathy causing early satiety, is symptomatic, and the oncology team believes she is an appropriate candidate for chemotherapy with palliative intent. Her ECOG performance status is 1, and renal and hepatic function are adequate. The team is considering rituximab as part of treatment and asks whether it should be paired with chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette keeps the same indolent CD20-positive lymphoma and treatment-planning workflow, but it is no longer treatment-naive in the strict sense because she already received rituximab during a recent prior course.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start chemotherapy in combination with rituximab.
Use rituximab-containing therapy as the default first-line regimen for her follicular lymphoma.
Order the regimen without additional pretreatment evaluation beyond routine labs.
recommended_actions: Screen for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B core antibody before rituximab.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This does not meet the previously untreated indolent lymphoma recommendation because she already completed bendamustine-rituximab 8 months ago and now has symptomatic relapse. The appropriate interpretation is relapsed indolent CD20-positive lymphoma after prior rituximab exposure with a response lasting less than one year, so the previously untreated rituximab recommendation is not applicable. Before any rituximab is given, HBV screening is still indicated.
challenging_examples.rationale: The prior rituximab exposure and short remission invalidate the first-line indolent lymphoma rule. The only clearly triggered rule left is HBV screening before any planned rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with relapsed SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Consider second-line chemotherapy only after weighing treatment-free interval, response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity, and performance status.
selected_rules.source_statement: The selection of patients for treatment with second-line therapy should be dependent on the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and the performance status of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
selected_rules.source_statement: patients who relapse three or more months following the completion of first-line chemotherapy may benefit from retreatment with the same regimen that induced their initial response. This would generally mean retreatment with etoposide-cisplatin.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider alternative regimens such as CAV or carboplatin plus etoposide.
selected_rules.source_statement: Alternative regimens may include cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and vincristine (CAV) or carboplatin and etoposide.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient initially responded to chemotherapy and response duration was 45 days or longer
selected_rules.execute: Consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral topotecan is a possible alternative for patients who initially responded to chemotherapy and had a response duration of 45 days or longer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is not suitable for intravenous therapy and topotecan is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Consider oral topotecan because it is more convenient and may be an option when IV therapy is unsuitable.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is more convenient and may be a treatment option for patients not suitable for intravenous therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: topotecan is given orally
selected_rules.execute: Monitor for grade 3/4 diarrhea.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

selected_rules.activation_condition: topotecan is given intravenously
selected_rules.execute: Monitor for grade 3/4 neutropenia.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient fails to respond to first-line therapy or relapses shortly after first-line therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider enrollment in a clinical trial to help determine the optimal treatment regimen.
selected_rules.source_statement: Clinical trials are needed to determine the optimal treatment regimen.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin 5 months ago and had a partial response with marked symptom improvement. He now returns with worsening cough, weight loss, and new right hilar enlargement on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. His treatment-free interval is just over 5 months. He has mild residual peripheral neuropathy from prior cisplatin but remains ambulatory, independent in activities of daily living, and has an ECOG performance status of 1. He is interested in further treatment and asks about options. Because he previously responded and the relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of chemotherapy, retreatment with the same regimen is being considered, along with other second-line options. Oral topotecan is also discussed as an alternative, since he responded initially and his response lasted longer than 45 days.
recommended_actions: Consider second-line chemotherapy after weighing the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and performance status.
Because relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of first-line chemotherapy, consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
Because he initially responded and the response duration was longer than 45 days, consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
source_case.rationale: This vignette meets the activation conditions for relapsed SCLC, relapse more than 3 months after first-line chemotherapy, and prior response lasting at least 45 days. The recommendations are limited to those specified by the triggered rules and reflect the need to balance treatment-free interval, prior response, residual toxicity, and performance status.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin 5 months ago and had a partial response with marked symptom improvement. He now returns with worsening cough, weight loss, and new right hilar enlargement on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. His treatment-free interval is just over 5 months. He has mild residual peripheral neuropathy from prior cisplatin but remains ambulatory, independent in activities of daily living, and has an ECOG performance status of 1. He is interested in further treatment and asks about options. The team is considering retreatment with the same regimen because of his prior response, and oral topotecan is also being discussed as an alternative despite his recent return of disease only a little over 5 months after therapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Nearly identical relapse history, prior response, and performance status, but the prior response lasted less than 45 days because the original course ended 5 months ago and the current relapse is treated as having an inadequate response duration for topotecan eligibility.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Restart etoposide-cisplatin based on the prior partial response and long treatment-free interval
Offer oral topotecan as a standard second-line option
Proceed with second-line chemotherapy without further reassessment
recommended_actions: Consider second-line chemotherapy after weighing the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and performance status.
Because relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of first-line chemotherapy, consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
Consider alternative regimens such as CAV or carboplatin plus etoposide.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still relapsed extensive-stage SCLC, and the long treatment-free interval supports consideration of second-line therapy and retreatment with the original regimen. However, the vignette no longer supports oral topotecan under the specified rule because the response-duration criterion is not clearly met in the modified case. Safer interpretation is to favor the relapse-sensitive regimens that remain guideline-concordant and avoid assuming topotecan eligibility.
challenging_examples.rationale: Relapse remains more than 3 months after first-line chemotherapy, so the rules for second-line treatment and same-regimen retreatment still apply. The modification removes the clean trigger for oral topotecan by undermining the required response-duration condition.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin 2 months ago and had a partial response with marked symptom improvement. He now returns with worsening cough, weight loss, and new right hilar enlargement on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. His treatment-free interval is just under 2 months. He has mild residual peripheral neuropathy from prior cisplatin but remains ambulatory, independent in activities of daily living, and has an ECOG performance status of 1. He is interested in further treatment and asks about options. Because he previously responded, the team is considering repeating etoposide-cisplatin, and oral topotecan is mentioned as another second-line choice.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The clinical picture, prior response, and functional status are preserved, but the relapse occurred before the 3-month threshold, which weakens the case for repeating the original regimen and also removes the response-duration basis for oral topotecan.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Repeat etoposide-cisplatin because he responded before
Use oral topotecan as a routine second-line option
Treat the relapse with the same approach used for sensitive relapse
recommended_actions: Consider second-line chemotherapy after weighing the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and performance status.
Consider enrollment in a clinical trial to help determine the optimal treatment regimen.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is relapsed extensive-stage SCLC, but the relapse is now within 3 months of first-line chemotherapy, so the guideline support for retreatment with the same regimen is not met. The short interval also makes this a less favorable setting for standard sensitive-relapse options; a clinical trial is the safer guideline-concordant consideration.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case still meets the general relapsed SCLC assessment, but the treatment-free interval is now too short for the same-regimen retreatment recommendation. The change also removes the basis for topotecan as a favored alternative under the supplied rule set.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with inoperable, advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making
selected_rules.source_statement: Intermittent systemic treatment strategies should be part of an informed discussion of treatment options for this group of patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer wants a break from treatment
selected_rules.execute: Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who want a break from treatment can be reassured that intermittent strategies of administering first-line therapy are a reasonable alternative to continuous administration.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Meta-analysis demonstrates no clinically significant survival difference between the continuous and intermittent chemotherapy strategies (HR, 1.02; 95%CI, 0.95-1.10, p=0.62).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Intermittent strategies of administering first-line systemic therapies to patients with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC) do not result in a statistically significant reduction in overall survival and either improve or maintain quality of life compared to continuous administration of therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is receiving intermittent systemic therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval
selected_rules.execute: Continue best supportive care
selected_rules.source_statement: During maintenance therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval, best supportive care should be continued for patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment as a reasonable guideline to consider
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Monitor with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Disease progresses during the intermittent phase
selected_rules.execute: Reintroduce the induction chemotherapy at disease progression
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with inoperable stage IV colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has liver and lung metastases, good performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. After reading about treatment side effects, he says he would prefer a plan that includes treatment breaks if possible, because he wants to spend more time at home and maintain his energy for family activities. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and explains that intermittent first-line systemic treatment is also a reasonable option to consider. The discussion includes the possibility that intermittent therapy may preserve quality of life without a statistically significant survival disadvantage compared with continuous therapy. The patient agrees that an intermittent approach fits his goals if disease monitoring is built in.
recommended_actions: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making.
Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy.
Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy.
Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with inoperable advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy, and he also explicitly wants a break from treatment. The selected rules support discussing and reassuring about intermittent therapy, noting no statistically significant overall survival reduction and potential quality-of-life benefit versus continuous therapy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with unresectable stage III colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy after colon resection was deemed not feasible because of local invasion. He has no distant metastases on staging CT, good performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. After reading about treatment side effects, he says he would prefer a plan that includes treatment breaks if possible, because he wants to spend more time at home and maintain his energy for family activities. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and explains that intermittent first-line treatment is also a reasonable option to consider. The discussion includes the possibility that intermittent therapy may preserve quality of life without a statistically significant survival disadvantage compared with continuous therapy. The patient agrees that an intermittent approach fits his goals if disease monitoring is built in.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still sounds like advanced colorectal cancer with a desire for treatment breaks, but the disease is locally advanced stage III rather than unresectable metastatic stage IV.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Discuss intermittent first-line systemic therapy as a reasonable alternative to continuous treatment.
Reassure the patient that taking treatment breaks is a standard option here.
Recommend intermittent therapy because survival is similar to continuous therapy and quality of life may be better.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This patient does not have unresectable metastatic stage IV colorectal cancer, so the intermittent first-line metastatic colorectal cancer recommendation is not guideline-concordant. The safer interpretation is to manage him according to stage III locally advanced disease pathways, where the evidence quoted for intermittent metastatic therapy does not apply.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation is limited to adults with inoperable, advanced stage IV or unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer considering first-line systemic therapy. Stage III localized/regional disease falls outside that activation condition.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with inoperable stage IV colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has liver and lung metastases, good performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. After reading about treatment side effects, he says he would prefer a plan that includes treatment breaks if possible, because he wants to spend more time at home and maintain his energy for family activities. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and explains that intermittent first-line treatment is also a reasonable option to consider. The discussion includes the possibility that intermittent therapy may preserve quality of life without a statistically significant survival disadvantage compared with continuous therapy. The patient agrees that an intermittent approach fits his goals if disease monitoring is built in, but the oncology team notes that his tumor is microsatellite instability-high and he is expected to start immunotherapy rather than cytotoxic chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The cancer stage, metastatic burden, and preference for treatment breaks all match the original vignette, but the planned first-line regimen is immunotherapy rather than the intermittent versus continuous chemotherapy strategy addressed by the rule.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Discuss intermittent chemotherapy as a reasonable first-line option.
Reassure the patient that intermittent therapy is an evidence-based alternative to continuous treatment.
Plan an induction-and-break chemotherapy strategy with surveillance imaging.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although he has unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer, the intended first-line treatment is immunotherapy for MSI-high disease, not the intermittent chemotherapy approach addressed by the source recommendation. The safer interpretation is to follow the biomarker-directed immunotherapy pathway rather than applying the intermittent-versus-continuous chemotherapy guidance.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule set is about intermittent strategies for first-line systemic therapy in unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer, specifically in the context of continuous versus intermittent chemotherapy. A biomarker-driven immunotherapy plan means the cited recommendation does not cleanly apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman has never been sexually active
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform cervical screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is younger than 21 years old
selected_rules.execute: Do not screen routinely.
selected_rules.source_statement: the authors of this guideline have concluded that the harms of screening women under 21 years of age significantly outweigh the benefits.

selected_rules.activation_condition: organized screening program with adequate database infrastructure
selected_rules.execute: Use HPV DNA testing of cervical cells for primary cervical screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: HPV DNA testing of cells collected from the cervix is recommended for primary cervical screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive HPV DNA test result
selected_rules.execute: Perform cytology screening as triage rather than using cytology as the primary screen.
selected_rules.source_statement: Cytology screening, which was recommended for primary screening in the previous version of this guideline, is now recommended only in the event of a positive HPV DNA test result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is 30 years of age or older
selected_rules.execute: Use HPV-based primary screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: the primary recommendations presented in Part 1 of this guideline are for HPVbased testing for women 30 years of age and over.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is 30 to 65 years old and initial HPV test is negative
selected_rules.execute: Repeat HPV DNA testing at five-year intervals.
selected_rules.source_statement: For women aged 30-65, HPV DNA testing is to occur at five-year intervals after an initial negative result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV test is positive
selected_rules.execute: Assess the result with cytology testing and do not refer directly to colposcopy.
selected_rules.source_statement: HPV-positive tests should be assessed with cytology testing and not referred directly to colposcopy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV positive and cytology negative
selected_rules.execute: Repeat HPV testing after one year.
selected_rules.source_statement: Repeat HPV testing for results of HPV positive/cytology negative should be conducted after one year.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a Pap test. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior cervical screening. She is otherwise healthy and has no immunocompromising conditions. After discussing her preventive care questions, she specifically asks whether cervical cancer screening is needed now.
recommended_actions: Do not perform cervical screening.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule that women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening. The recommended action is therefore to avoid cervical screening in this patient.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a Pap test. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior cervical screening. She is otherwise healthy and has no immunocompromising conditions. She notes that she is also asking because a new partner is likely in the near future, but she has not yet had any sexual contact. After discussing her preventive care questions, she specifically asks whether cervical cancer screening is needed now.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient remains a young, healthy woman seeking routine cervical screening advice, but the sexual history is changed from never sexually active to not yet sexually active with anticipated future activity.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Defer cervical cancer screening because she has not been sexually active yet.
Reassure her that no Pap test is needed at this visit.
Schedule routine cervical screening only after she becomes sexually active.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for the 'never sexually active' exemption because she has a planned future sexual relationship and the vignette no longer clearly establishes lifelong abstinence. The safer interpretation is that routine screening decisions should follow age-based cervical screening guidance rather than the never-sexually-active exception.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies only when the woman has never been sexually active. The modified vignette weakens that condition by suggesting impending sexual activity and therefore does not cleanly satisfy the exemption.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 20-year-old woman comes to primary care for a school physical and asks whether she should have cervical cancer screening. She has never been sexually active, has no history of genital symptoms or abnormal bleeding, and is otherwise healthy without immunocompromising conditions. She mentions that her mother was diagnosed with cervical cancer in her late 40s, which has made her anxious about screening. She asks whether she needs a Pap test now.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still appears to be a low-risk young woman asking about screening, but the age is now under 21, which changes the relevant recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Perform a Pap test now because of her family history and anxiety.
Start routine cervical screening at this visit.
Order cervical cancer screening despite her age because she has never been screened before.
recommended_actions: Do not screen routinely.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The never-sexually-active exemption is still present, but the more specific age-based recommendation applies here: she is younger than 21, so routine cervical screening is not recommended. Her family history does not change this screening rule in the vignette.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule R001 remains true, but R002 also triggers because she is under 21. The correct guideline-concordant action is not to screen routinely at this age.

selected_rules.activation_condition: inflammatory breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
selected_rules.source_statement: BCS is not advised in inflammatory breast cancer because the extent of tumour involvement cannot be reliably ascertained.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sentinel lymph node biopsy is being considered both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform SLNB both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: This suggests that SLNB should not be performed both before and after NACT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: cases being discussed for treatment planning
selected_rules.execute: Discuss cases in a multidisciplinary case conference.
selected_rules.source_statement: Communication between oncologists, surgeons, radiologists, and pathologists is essential. A multidisciplinary case conference is the recommended forum for discussion of cases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: specimen submitted after neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Indicate any prior neoadjuvant therapy on the pathology specimen submission.
selected_rules.source_statement: Any prior use of neoadjuvant therapy should be indicated when specimens are submitted for pathologic examination.

selected_rules.activation_condition: before starting neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Insert surgical clips marking the original pretreatment tumour location.
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that surgical clips marking the original (pretreatment) tumour location be inserted before administration of neoadjuvant therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer and surgery is being planned after response to neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: For most patients with LABC, mastectomy should be considered to be the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient strongly desires breast-conserving surgery
selected_rules.execute: Perform wide excision of the remaining tumour in the original tumour bed plus radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Wide excision of the remaining tumour in the region of the original pre-neoadjuvant treatment tumour bed plus RT is recommended for patients with LABC who strongly desire BCS.

selected_rules.activation_condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer after mastectomy
selected_rules.execute: Give postoperative radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Radiotherapy following mastectomy is recommended for patients with LABC.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman presents with a 7 cm left breast mass, peau d’orange, and erythema involving more than one-third of the breast, consistent with inflammatory breast cancer. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma. She is being evaluated at diagnosis for local treatment options, and she asks whether she can have lumpectomy because she hopes to avoid mastectomy. The team is also planning neoadjuvant therapy, with surgery to be reconsidered afterward depending on response. No surgery has been performed yet.
recommended_actions: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients with locally advanced breast cancer after response to neoadjuvant therapy.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes inflammatory breast cancer, which triggers the recommendation against breast-conserving surgery because tumour extent cannot be reliably ascertained. It also includes locally advanced breast cancer being managed with neoadjuvant therapy and later surgery planning, which supports considering mastectomy as standard of care for most such patients.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman presents with a 7 cm left breast mass, peau d’orange, and erythema involving more than one-third of the breast, consistent with inflammatory breast cancer. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma. She is being evaluated at diagnosis for local treatment options and asks whether she can have lumpectomy because she hopes to avoid mastectomy. The team is planning neoadjuvant therapy, but an immediate surgery discussion is also being scheduled if the tumor shrinks. No surgery has been performed yet.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still sounds like inflammatory, locally advanced breast cancer at diagnosis with neoadjuvant treatment planning and a question about breast conservation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Advise breast-conserving surgery because the patient wants to avoid mastectomy.
Proceed with lumpectomy if the mass responds to neoadjuvant therapy.
Treat this as a standard candidate for breast conservation after systemic therapy.
recommended_actions: Discuss the case in a multidisciplinary case conference.
Insert surgical clips marking the original pretreatment tumour location before neoadjuvant therapy.
Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients with locally advanced breast cancer after response to neoadjuvant therapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer guideline-concordant for breast-conserving surgery because the key inflammatory breast cancer feature is missing: the erythema and peau d’orange now involve less than one-third of the breast. The safer interpretation is that this remains locally advanced breast cancer, so breast conservation is not automatically excluded, but treatment planning should be individualized in a multidisciplinary setting with clip placement before neoadjuvant therapy and mastectomy still considered standard for most patients.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original non-recommendation against BCS depends on inflammatory breast cancer. By reducing the skin involvement below the inflammatory threshold, that specific rule no longer applies. The case still fits locally advanced breast cancer management and pre-neoadjuvant workflow, so multidisciplinary discussion, clip placement, and consideration of mastectomy remain applicable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 56-year-old woman has a 6.5 cm right breast mass with peau d’orange and diffuse erythema over the breast. Core biopsy shows invasive ductal carcinoma. She is being staged as locally advanced breast cancer and is scheduled for neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Her surgeon asks radiology to place clips in the primary tumor bed before treatment, and the team is discussing surgery afterward depending on response. The patient asks whether she can keep the breast if the tumor shrinks enough.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation closely resembles inflammatory, locally advanced disease being managed with neoadjuvant therapy and later surgery planning.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend lumpectomy as a reasonable option if there is a good response to neoadjuvant therapy.
Tell the team to avoid mastectomy because breast conservation can be pursued after shrinkage.
Plan standard breast-conserving surgery with radiation after chemotherapy.
recommended_actions: Discuss the case in a multidisciplinary case conference.
Indicate prior neoadjuvant therapy on the pathology specimen submission when tissue is examined later.
Insert surgical clips marking the original pretreatment tumour location before neoadjuvant therapy.
Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients with locally advanced breast cancer after response to neoadjuvant therapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This does not meet the inflammatory breast cancer criterion because the skin findings are diffuse but limited and do not clearly involve more than one-third of the breast. The safer interpretation is locally advanced breast cancer without a firm inflammatory designation, so breast-conserving surgery is not automatically prohibited, but the standard approach for most patients remains mastectomy after neoadjuvant therapy, with proper multidisciplinary planning and specimen documentation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The near-miss removes the specific threshold needed for inflammatory breast cancer while preserving the rest of the management context. Because the patient still has locally advanced breast cancer planned for neoadjuvant therapy, the applicable recommendations are multidisciplinary review, clip placement, pathology notation, and consideration of mastectomy rather than routine breast conservation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in CR2 or greater, including refractory or relapsed disease
selected_rules.execute: Perform allo-SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Allo-SCT is recommended in CR2 or greater (refractory or relapsed).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in first complete remission (CR1) and eligible for allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) as a treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) is an option for adult patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in first complete remission (CR1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is deemed unsuitable for standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC)
selected_rules.execute: Use reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) for SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) is an option for patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in remission when they are deemed unsuitable for the standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC) regimen.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is ineligible for MAC allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider RIC as an effective therapeutic option
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the evidence, the members of the Working Group have determined that RIC could be an effective therapeutic option for patients with ALL who are ineligible for MAC allo-SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient with high risk of treatment-related mortality from MAC
selected_rules.execute: Consider RIC instead of MAC
selected_rules.source_statement: The systematic review (4) stated that RIC may be a potential therapeutic option in patients with high risk of treatment-related mortality (TRM) associated with MAC regimens, as there was a lack of overall survival benefit of MAC over RIC regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) after allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Use a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) post-transplant
selected_rules.source_statement: Post-transplant use of a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) in patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) is a reasonable option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with Ph+ ALL undergoing allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider TKI therapy pre- and/or post-transplant
selected_rules.source_statement: The consensus is that TKI therapy is useful pre and/or posttransplant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in CR1 or later who lacks a suitable related or unrelated donor
selected_rules.execute: Consider haploidentical hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (haplo-SCT)
selected_rules.source_statement: Haploidentical hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation (haplo-SCT) for patients with ALL in CR1 or later who lack a suitable related or unrelated donor is a reasonable option.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a documented second complete remission (CR2) with no detectable blasts on marrow exam. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, and he has an HLA-matched sibling donor available. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. Because he is an adult patient with ALL in CR2 after relapse, the case meets criteria for transplant in the recurrence setting.
recommended_actions: Perform allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT).
source_case.rationale: The vignette matches adult ALL in CR2 after relapse, which directly triggers the recommendation for allo-SCT. No additional rules are activated because the patient is not described as Ph+ ALL, unsuitable for myeloablative conditioning, or lacking a suitable donor.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a documented second complete remission (CR2) with no detectable blasts on marrow exam. He remains functionally independent, but his pretransplant evaluation shows an ejection fraction of 32% from prior anthracycline exposure. He has no significant renal or pulmonary disease, and an HLA-matched sibling donor is available. The transplant team is reviewing whether to proceed with definitive post-remission therapy in the setting of relapse. The main question is whether the patient can safely undergo the planned transplant approach given his remission status and cardiac reserve.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still has adult relapsed ALL in CR2 with a matched sibling donor, but severe LV dysfunction changes transplant feasibility and conditioning intensity.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed directly with allogeneic stem cell transplantation using standard myeloablative conditioning.
List the patient for allo-SCT without modifying the conditioning plan.
Treat CR2 relapsed ALL as a straightforward transplant candidate despite the low ejection fraction.
recommended_actions: Consider reduced-intensity conditioning for stem cell transplantation rather than standard myeloablative conditioning.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains relapsed adult ALL in CR2, but the newly described severe cardiomyopathy makes standard myeloablative conditioning unsafe. The guideline-concordant interpretation is that the patient is in remission but unsuitable for MAC, so reduced-intensity conditioning is the safer transplant approach rather than routine allo-SCT with full-intensity conditioning.
challenging_examples.rationale: CR2 after relapse still fits the recurrence setting, but the added ejection fraction of 32% makes him ineligible for standard myeloablative conditioning. That invalidates the direct recommendation for allo-SCT as originally stated and activates the conditional guidance for RIC in patients unsuitable for MAC.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 39-year-old woman with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia completed induction and consolidation and is currently in her first complete remission. She has minimal residual disease negativity on marrow testing and is being evaluated for consolidation strategy. Her course has been uncomplicated, but she has no HLA-matched sibling or matched unrelated donor available. Her only potential donor is a haploidentical adult child, and the transplant service is discussing whether to move forward with a donor-dependent curative approach. She remains otherwise fit, with normal cardiac, renal, and pulmonary function. The decision point is how to manage CR1 disease in the setting of limited donor availability.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient is still an adult with ALL in remission and transplant planning, but she is in CR1 rather than CR2 and lacks a suitable matched donor.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend allogeneic stem cell transplantation because she is an adult with ALL in remission.
Proceed with matched-donor allo-SCT planning despite the absence of a suitable donor.
Use standard transplant consolidation without addressing donor availability.
recommended_actions: Consider haploidentical hematopoietic stem cell transplantation because she is in CR1 and lacks a suitable related or unrelated donor.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the CR2 relapse scenario that directly triggers routine allo-SCT. She is in CR1 and has no suitable related or unrelated donor, so the relevant guideline-concordant option is haploidentical transplantation rather than assuming standard matched-donor allo-SCT applies.
challenging_examples.rationale: The key change is remission status and donor availability. CR1 does not meet the recurrent-disease criterion for mandatory allo-SCT, while lack of a suitable related or unrelated donor activates the haplo-SCT option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient intends postoperative radiation and has lymph node positive early breast cancer, inflammatory breast cancer, or locally advanced breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: early breast cancer who are lymph node positive, inflammatory breast cancer or locally advanced breast cancer who will require postoperative RT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Invasive tumor involves the skin or nipple-areolar complex clinically
selected_rules.execute: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: any clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex (NAC) involvement by invasive tumour

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has Paget disease of the breast or a retroareolar tumor
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform nipple-sparing or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: NSM and ASM are not recommended for women with Paget disease of the breast or women with a retroareolar tumour.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Postmastectomy breast reconstruction has been performed
selected_rules.execute: Do not use surveillance mammography routinely in the reconstructed breast
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of postmastectomy surveillance mammography in the reconstructed breast.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At the time mastectomy is offered for therapeutic mastectomy
selected_rules.execute: Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: The discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction should be initiated at the time that mastectomy is offered by the general surgeon.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for DCIS
selected_rules.execute: Perform preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon should be performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for early stage breast cancer and adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy may be needed
selected_rules.execute: Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in preoperative evaluation, either by formal consultation or multidisciplinary cancer conference
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for early stage breast cancer who will potentially require adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy (RT), a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist should be included in preoperative evaluation, either through a formal consultation or by a multidisciplinary cancer conference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate reconstruction is being planned
selected_rules.execute: Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate reconstruction, there should be adequate preoperative imaging of the breasts, aligning with existing guideline recommendations.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma presents to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement. The breast surgeon recommends mastectomy, and the patient is interested in immediate reconstruction if feasible. Because she is node-positive and is expected to receive postoperative radiation, the surgical team reviews reconstruction options carefully. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has already had diagnostic mammography and breast MRI for staging. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical, reconstructive, and radiation planning before finalizing the operative approach.
recommended_actions: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy because she intends to receive postoperative radiation and has lymph node-positive early breast cancer.
Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction at the time mastectomy is offered.
Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in the preoperative evaluation through formal consultation or a multidisciplinary cancer conference.
Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because the patient has lymph node-positive early breast cancer and will receive postoperative radiation, making skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, and areola-sparing mastectomy not recommended. R005 applies because mastectomy is being offered, so reconstruction should be discussed. R007 applies because immediate reconstruction is being considered and adjuvant radiotherapy is likely, so oncology input should be included preoperatively. R008 applies because immediate reconstruction is being planned and adequate preoperative breast imaging is required.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma returns to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement. The breast surgeon recommends mastectomy, and the patient is interested in immediate reconstruction if feasible. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has already had diagnostic mammography and breast MRI for staging. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical and reconstructive planning, but the radiation oncologist notes that postoperative radiation is unlikely because her sentinel node biopsy is negative and the axillary biopsy was thought to sample a benign node.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like node-positive early breast cancer planning mastectomy with immediate reconstruction and preoperative imaging, but the nodal disease is no longer confirmed.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Avoid skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, and areola-sparing mastectomy because postoperative radiation is expected.
Proceed with immediate reconstruction without further multidisciplinary input.
Assume the breast MRI and mammogram already satisfy all preoperative planning needs without additional review.
recommended_actions: Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction at the time mastectomy is offered.
Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in the preoperative evaluation through formal consultation or a multidisciplinary cancer conference.
Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the no-skin-sparing/nipple-sparing restriction because biopsy-proven nodal involvement is no longer established and postoperative radiation is not currently expected. The safer interpretation is a patient being evaluated for mastectomy with reconstruction planning, where reconstruction discussion, oncology input, and adequate imaging still apply.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires intended postoperative radiation plus confirmed lymph node-positive early breast cancer or other listed advanced settings. Here, that key condition is no longer met. R005, R007, and R008 still apply because mastectomy and immediate reconstruction are being considered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma presents to finalize surgical planning. The primary tumor measures 4.2 cm, and axillary biopsy has shown metastatic involvement. She is leaning toward mastectomy and asks whether immediate reconstruction is possible. Clinical exam shows no obvious skin invasion, but the tumor is described on MRI as abutting the nipple-areolar complex without definite involvement. She has already completed diagnostic mammography and breast MRI. The team schedules a preoperative conference with surgery, plastic surgery, and oncology to coordinate the plan. Because her disease is considered node-positive and she will likely need postoperative radiation, the surgeon wants to discuss whether nipple preservation could still be offered.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall scenario remains node-positive breast cancer with mastectomy and reconstruction planning, but the nipple is only abutted on MRI rather than clinically involved.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer nipple-sparing or areola-sparing mastectomy because there is no overt skin or nipple involvement on exam.
Proceed directly to immediate reconstruction without a preoperative oncology discussion.
Rely on the existing MRI and mammogram without confirming whether the retroareolar region is suitable for preservation.
recommended_actions: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy because she intends to receive postoperative radiation and has lymph node-positive early breast cancer.
Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction at the time mastectomy is offered.
Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in the preoperative evaluation through formal consultation or a multidisciplinary cancer conference.
Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case does not meet the prohibition based on clinical nipple-areolar complex involvement, because there is no definite clinical involvement; the nipple is only adjacent on imaging. However, the patient still meets the separate criterion for avoiding skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, and areola-sparing mastectomy because she has node-positive early breast cancer and is expected to receive postoperative radiation. Reconstruction discussion, oncology input, and imaging remain appropriate.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 is invalidated because there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex invasion. R001 still applies because she remains node-positive with anticipated postoperative RT. R005, R007, and R008 also still apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering limited ACP implementation
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
selected_rules.source_statement: Addressing only one, or a few, of these elements creates the risks of leaving critical gaps that could prevent positive impacts on quality patient care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When using education alone to improve ACP care
selected_rules.execute: Do not expect education alone to improve care or patient experience without supporting system changes.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, high-quality education, although essential, on its own is unlikely to improve care or the patient experience in the absence of supporting systemic changes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering whether pediatric ACP causes distress
selected_rules.execute: Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
selected_rules.source_statement: Concerns that ACP discussions will cause distress in children and parents are not supported by the evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering ACP or GoCD tools for practice
selected_rules.execute: Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
selected_rules.source_statement: Outcome evidence for ACP suggests the greatest impact is likely to be made by a system-wide approach to integrating ACP into practice, one that maximizes clinical skills of all interprofessional team members.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When developing ACP tools or processes
selected_rules.execute: Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
selected_rules.source_statement: Considering these emerging themes from the evidence suggests that tool development should focus on enabling healthcare providers to facilitate values-based discussions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When evaluating ACP tools
selected_rules.execute: Use values-based outcome measures to assess impact on patient care.
selected_rules.source_statement: Future studies with corresponding valuesbased outcome measures are needed to evaluate the impact on patient care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When implementing ACP programs
selected_rules.execute: Include community engagement, professional education, standard practices, quality improvement monitoring, supporting policies, and technology infrastructure.
selected_rules.source_statement: A comprehensive approach to ACP includes community engagement, professional education, development of standard practices, monitoring of these practices through Quality Improvement initiatives, supporting policies and practices, and a technology infrastructures.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When planning ACP implementation across care settings
selected_rules.execute: Apply ACP across public health and healthcare sectors with system-level coordination and planning.
selected_rules.source_statement: This approach must be applied across public health and healthcare sectors to support the exchange of clinical information and will involve system-level coordination and planning.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team notes that advance care planning has been discussed inconsistently across visits: one clinician focused mainly on code status, while another gave a pamphlet about end-of-life decisions.
