The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but he has not had a structured conversation about these values. His daughter asks whether these discussions might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team is considering a new outpatient ACP workflow and realizes that a few isolated steps have not changed care quality in the past.
recommended_actions: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes limited, fragmented ACP implementation, which triggers the warning against relying on only a few ACP elements. It also includes concern that ACP discussions may distress the patient and family, matching the rule that evidence does not support assuming increased distress. The team is planning broader ACP workflow changes, which supports a system-wide approach, and the patient’s stated priorities point to values-based rather than treatment-only discussions.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team notes that advance care planning has been discussed inconsistently across visits: one clinician focused mainly on code status, while another gave a pamphlet about end-of-life decisions. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but he has not had a structured conversation about these values. His daughter asks whether these discussions might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team is planning a brief clinician-education session on ACP, but there is no broader workflow redesign, standardized documentation, or interprofessional process in place.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on metastatic lung cancer, fragmented ACP, and concerns about distress, but the key change is that the team is only planning education without systemic implementation support.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Rely on the upcoming ACP teaching session to improve care quality and patient experience.
Proceed with a limited ACP intervention focused on code status and a pamphlet since the patient has already expressed some preferences.
Reassure the daughter that ACP discussions are likely to be distressing and should be minimized.
recommended_actions: Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette no longer supports the recommendation to use education alone; a brief teaching session without workflow, documentation, or team-based changes is unlikely to improve ACP care. The safer interpretation is that any ACP improvement plan should be system-level and values-based rather than dependent on isolated education or treatment-focused materials.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source case supported broad ACP implementation, but here the only proposed change is clinician education, which does not satisfy the system-wide requirement. The patient’s values still justify values-based discussion tools, but the vignette no longer supports relying on fragmented ACP elements or assuming distress from discussion.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 9-year-old girl with relapsed leukemia is seen in clinic with her parents after a recent hospitalization. The team is considering whether to introduce advance care planning because the family has asked for help thinking through future decisions. The parents worry that talking about prognosis will upset their daughter, who has become tearful during prior hospital discussions. The child herself says she wants to know what is happening and wants to spend more time at home if treatment stops helping. The oncology service already uses a standardized ACP pathway with social work, nursing, and physician involvement, and the family has been offered a structured conversation next week. However, the team notes that the discussion would be limited to treatment escalation orders and resuscitation preferences, with no plan to explore the child’s goals or daily priorities.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting remains pediatric oncology with family concern about emotional distress and an ACP workflow, but the planned conversation is narrowly treatment-focused rather than values-based.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Avoid the ACP discussion because it may increase distress in the child and parents.
Document resuscitation status and treatment escalation preferences as the main ACP intervention.
Treat the existing pathway as sufficient and skip a broader goals-of-care conversation.
recommended_actions: Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case does not justify the belief that ACP discussions will increase distress, and the family’s concerns should not automatically prevent the conversation. The key limitation is that the planned ACP is treatment-focused and narrow, so the safer interpretation is to proceed with a team-based process that supports a broader, values-based discussion rather than only code status or escalation orders.
challenging_examples.rationale: The modified vignette keeps pediatric ACP and distress concerns, but the actual plan is a narrow treatment-oriented discussion. That invalidates the values-based/tool-development recommendation while still supporting a system-wide ACP approach through the existing interdisciplinary pathway.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer and not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria
selected_rules.execute: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: Ineligible patients include those who have received >2 prior anticancer regimens or who had refractory disease, patients with a history of bowel obstruction (including subocclusive disease) related to underlying disease, a history of abdominal fistula, gastrointestinal perforation, or intra-abdominal abscess, or evidence of rectosigmoid involvement by pelvic examination, bowel involvement on computed tomography, or clinical symptoms of bowel obstruction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Assess each patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference; recognize systemic therapy is not curative.
selected_rules.source_statement: Systemic therapy for recurrent ovarian cancer is not curative. As such, it is recognized that, to determine the optimal therapy, each patient needs to be assessed individually in terms of recurrence, sensitivity to platinum, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer participation in clinical trials if appropriate.
selected_rules.source_statement: All patients should be offered the opportunity to participate in clinical trials, if appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer and a single platinum agent is not being considered because of toxicity or allergy
selected_rules.execute: Use monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin.
selected_rules.source_statement: If a single platinum agent is not being considered (e.g., because of toxicity or allergy), then monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin is a reasonable treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer with known BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation, somatic or germline, after platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease
selected_rules.execute: Use PARP inhibitor maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: PARP inhibitors are recommended for patients with known BRCA 1 or 2 mutation (somatic and germline) as maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Set treatment goals to improving quality of life by extending symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or prolonging life if possible.
selected_rules.source_statement: Lower levels of response to treatment are expected for this group; therefore, the goals of treatment should be to improve patient's quality of life by extending the symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or if possible, prolonging life.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
selected_rules.source_statement: Single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, and gemcitabine have demonstrated activity in this patient population and are reasonable treatment options.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer chemotherapy with biologics after discussing the safety profile.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer should be offered chemotherapy with biologics after a discussion concerning the safety profile

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with symptomatic recurrence 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has received three prior anticancer regimens and now has increasing abdominal discomfort, early satiety, and a CT scan showing peritoneal progression without bowel obstruction. Her disease is therefore platinum-resistant and she is not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria because she has had more than two prior anticancer regimens. She asks about the next treatment steps and whether the goal is cure.
recommended_actions: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
Set treatment goals around improving quality of life, symptom control, and prolonging progression-free interval or life if possible.
Consider single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
Assess the patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference, recognizing that systemic therapy is not curative.
Offer participation in a clinical trial if appropriate.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer and specifically excludes AURELIA eligibility because the patient has had more than two prior anticancer regimens. That triggers the recommendation not to use the bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy option for AURELIA-eligible patients. Platinum-resistant disease also supports setting palliative goals and using one of the listed single-agent chemotherapy options. Because she has recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer, individualized treatment assessment and trial consideration are also recommended.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with abdominal bloating and early satiety 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has received only two prior anticancer regimens, but a current CT scan shows peritoneal progression with a small partial small-bowel obstruction and mild proximal dilation. She asks about the next treatment steps and whether adding bevacizumab would be reasonable. Her disease is still described as platinum-resistant because the recurrence occurred within 6 months of platinum.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case keeps the same platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer context and the same short interval after platinum, but the bowel findings make the anti-VEGF option unsafe.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start bevacizumab with chemotherapy for recurrent disease
Choose a standard single-agent palliative regimen and proceed without reconsidering the bowel findings
Frame treatment as noncurative and discuss symptom-focused goals
recommended_actions: Set treatment goals around improving quality of life, symptom control, and prolonging progression-free interval or life if possible.
Consider single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
Assess the patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference, recognizing that systemic therapy is not curative.
Offer participation in a clinical trial if appropriate.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains platinum-resistant recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer, but the partial bowel obstruction makes her ineligible for the bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy approach. A safer interpretation is to avoid anti-VEGF therapy and focus on palliative systemic options and goals of care.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation against the AURELIA bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy option depends on specific ineligibility factors. Here, the subtle change is bowel obstruction, which independently excludes use of that regimen. The recurrent platinum-resistant context still triggers palliative goal setting, individualized assessment, and single-agent chemotherapy options.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with increasing abdominal discomfort and early satiety 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has had three prior anticancer regimens and a recent CT scan shows peritoneal progression without bowel obstruction or fistula. She asks whether bevacizumab plus chemotherapy is an option and whether the disease can still be cured. Her oncologist confirms the recurrence is platinum-resistant.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation, timing, histology, and platinum-resistant status are unchanged, but the added prior treatment makes her miss the trial eligibility cutoff by one regimen.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use bevacizumab with chemotherapy because the scan shows no obstruction
Treat with a curative intent discussion
Default to a platinum-based combination despite the short platinum-free interval
recommended_actions: Set treatment goals around improving quality of life, symptom control, and prolonging progression-free interval or life if possible.
Consider single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
Assess the patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference, recognizing that systemic therapy is not curative.
Offer participation in a clinical trial if appropriate.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: She remains platinum-resistant with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer, but having more than two prior anticancer regimens makes her ineligible for the bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients. The safer interpretation is palliative, individualized treatment rather than that specific combination.
challenging_examples.rationale: The modified case preserves the original disease setting but changes the number of prior regimens from two or fewer to three, which invalidates the AURELIA eligibility-based recommendation. The remaining recurrent ovarian cancer guidance still applies, including palliative goals, individualized assessment, and single-agent treatment choices.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced stage or recurrent endometrial cancer excluding sarcomas and squamous cell carcinomas
selected_rules.execute: use combination chemotherapy rather than single-agent chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy is favoured over single agent chemotherapy because of higher response rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin, recognizing improved response rate and median survival but increased toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: Paclitaxel in combination with cisplatin/doxorubicin chemotherapy improves both response rate and median survival; however, the use of this three-drug combination is associated with increased toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer and minimal symptoms or non-life threatening disease
selected_rules.execute: consider hormonal therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Hormonal therapy may be a therapeutic option for those patients with minimal symptoms or non-life threatening advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer when choosing between cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel and doxorubicin/cisplatin
selected_rules.execute: weigh greater toxicity against about a three-month median survival increase before using the three-drug regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The decision to use the three-drug combination, consisting of cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel, should be made with consideration of both the greater toxicity and the three-month increase in median survival time in comparison with the two-drug doxorubicin/cisplatin regimen.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: note that recent data suggest no recurrence-free survival benefit for the three-drug combination and increased toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: However, recent data suggest no benefit to the three-drug combination in terms of recurrence-free survival and was associated with increased toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider doxorubicin/cisplatin as a comparator regimen with reported response rates of 28-45%
selected_rules.source_statement: For double-agent chemotherapy, randomized trials of doxorubicin/cisplatin reported response rates ranging from 28-45%.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider doxorubicin/paclitaxel as a double-agent option with reported response rate of 43%
selected_rules.source_statement: For double-agent chemotherapy, randomized trials of doxorubicin/cisplatin reported response rates ranging from 28-45%, other agents in combination with doxorubicin reported response rates of 30% (cyclophosphamide) and 43% (paclitaxel).

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider single-agent doxorubicin or platinum agents only with awareness of lower reported response rates
selected_rules.source_statement: Single-agent chemotherapy has reported response rates as follows: doxorubicin 17-27% and platinum agents 21%.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a history of stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma presents with progressive vaginal bleeding, pelvic discomfort, and new pulmonary nodules on surveillance CT, consistent with recurrent advanced disease. She remains ambulatory and has good renal function and performance status, but she is seeking treatment that offers the best chance of tumor shrinkage. Her oncology team discusses systemic therapy options, including single-agent and combination chemotherapy. Because her disease is advanced/recurrent and she is fit for treatment, the team considers multi-agent regimens rather than single-agent therapy. They also review the potential benefits and increased toxicity of a paclitaxel-containing three-drug regimen versus a two-drug comparator.
recommended_actions: Use combination chemotherapy rather than single-agent chemotherapy.
Consider paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin, recognizing improved response rate and median survival but increased toxicity.
Weigh the greater toxicity against about a three-month median survival increase before using the three-drug cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel regimen.
Note that recent data suggest no recurrence-free survival benefit for the three-drug combination and increased toxicity.
Consider doxorubicin/cisplatin as a comparator regimen with reported response rates of 28-45%.
Consider doxorubicin/paclitaxel as a double-agent option with reported response rate of 43%.
Consider single-agent doxorubicin or platinum agents only with awareness of lower reported response rates.
source_case.rationale: This vignette fits advanced/recurrent endometrial cancer in an adult and triggers the chemotherapy selection rules. The patient is symptomatic and fit for systemic treatment, so combination chemotherapy is favored over single-agent therapy. The vignette also supports discussion of paclitaxel-containing combinations, the toxicity-survival tradeoff of the three-drug regimen, and comparator response-rate data for alternative regimens.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a history of stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma presents with progressive vaginal bleeding, pelvic discomfort, and enlarging pulmonary nodules on surveillance CT, consistent with recurrent advanced disease. She remains ambulatory with good renal function, but her ECOG performance status is now 3 because of severe cancer-related fatigue and dyspnea with minimal exertion. Her oncology team discusses systemic therapy options, including single-agent and combination chemotherapy, because the family is hoping for meaningful tumor shrinkage. The team reviews standard multi-agent regimens but notes concern about tolerability given her limited functional status. They also mention the toxicity of paclitaxel-containing triplet therapy versus a two-drug comparator.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The cancer remains advanced/recurrent and the visit still centers on selecting chemotherapy for tumor shrinkage, but her markedly poor performance status changes the guideline fit.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with combination chemotherapy because she has recurrent metastatic endometrial cancer.
Discuss paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin as an appropriate next regimen.
Compare the three-drug regimen with doxorubicin/cisplatin and consider the higher-toxicity option for maximal response.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still advanced recurrent endometrial cancer, but the patient’s ECOG 3 status makes the original recommendation for combination chemotherapy no longer guideline-concordant. The safer interpretation is that aggressive multi-agent chemotherapy is unlikely to be well tolerated, so treatment should be individualized and alternatives such as symptom-directed care or less intensive approaches should be considered.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation assumes a fit patient. Changing only the performance status to poor invalidates the combination-chemotherapy trigger while keeping the overall disease context similar.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with recurrent endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma returns with vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, and new pulmonary nodules on CT. She is ambulatory, has preserved renal function, and wants therapy with the best chance of tumor shrinkage. Her oncologist reviews systemic options for advanced disease and notes that paclitaxel-containing combinations can be considered. After discussion, however, the patient says she wants to avoid treatment-related toxicity and prefers the least intensive option available, even if response rates are lower. The team therefore revisits whether a multi-agent regimen is truly aligned with her goals.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation and staging remain almost identical to the source case, but the treatment goal has shifted away from maximal tumor shrinkage toward minimizing toxicity.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start combination chemotherapy because her disease is recurrent and metastatic.
Offer paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin for the best response rate.
Recommend the three-drug cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel regimen despite toxicity concerns.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although she has advanced recurrent disease, the patient’s expressed preference is to avoid toxicity and choose the least intensive option, so the original recommendation to favor combination chemotherapy is not guideline-concordant in this modified scenario. The safer interpretation is to revisit goals of care and consider lower-intensity or supportive approaches rather than automatically escalating to multi-agent treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case remains clinically similar, but the treatment intent is no longer maximal response. That subtle change makes the aggressive chemotherapy recommendation nonconcordant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Metastatic gastric or GEJ carcinoma that does not overexpress HER2 and first-line chemotherapy is being given
selected_rules.execute: Do not add a biological agent to the first-line chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with metastatic gastric cancer or GEJ carcinoma not overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), medical oncologists should not prescribe a biological agent in addition to a first-line chemotherapy regimen

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and HER2 is not overexpressed
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe a biological agent with first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with metastatic gastric cancer or GEJ carcinoma not overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), medical oncologists should not prescribe a biological agent in addition to a first-line chemotherapy regimen

selected_rules.activation_condition: Advanced gastric or GEJ carcinoma and later-line therapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor in addition to standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with gastric or GEJ carcinoma undergoing later lines of therapy, medical oncologists should not prescribe ICI in addition to standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult with advanced, non-resectable gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Prescribe a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical oncologists should prescribe either a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen in the first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Recurrent or metastatic gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Add an immune checkpoint inhibitor to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with recurrent or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) in addition to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HER2 overexpressing gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Add trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with HER2 overexpressing gastric or GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe the addition of trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and first-line treatment is being selected
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen with an immune checkpoint inhibitor
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with recurrent or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) in addition to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and first-line treatment is being selected
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical oncologists should prescribe either a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen in the first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with metastatic gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and testing shows HER2 is not overexpressed. He has not received prior systemic therapy and is being evaluated for first-line treatment. His performance status is fair, and he is interested in starting treatment promptly. The oncology team considers adding a targeted biologic agent to chemotherapy because of the metastatic disease, but the tumor is HER2-negative and this is his initial systemic regimen.
recommended_actions: Prescribe a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen for first-line treatment.
Do not add a biological agent to the first-line chemotherapy regimen because the tumor does not overexpress HER2.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes metastatic gastric/GEJ carcinoma starting first-line therapy with HER2 not overexpressed, which triggers the recommendation against adding a biological agent to first-line chemotherapy. It also meets the general first-line advanced gastric/GEJ treatment rule recommending a fluoropyrimidine-based doublet regimen.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and testing shows HER2 is not overexpressed. PD-L1 testing is pending, and he has not received prior systemic therapy. He is being evaluated for first-line treatment and wants to start promptly. His performance status is fair. The oncology team is considering a targeted biologic agent with chemotherapy because of the metastatic disease, but the tumor is HER2-negative and this is his initial systemic regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like metastatic first-line GEJ adenocarcinoma with HER2-negative disease, but PD-L1 is not yet known and the wording removes any clear basis for adding an immune checkpoint inhibitor.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add a biologic agent to first-line chemotherapy for metastatic disease
Start a fluoropyrimidine-platinum regimen with an immune checkpoint inhibitor
recommended_actions: Prescribe a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen for first-line treatment.
Do not add a biological agent to the first-line chemotherapy regimen because the tumor does not overexpress HER2.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains metastatic, HER2-negative gastroesophageal junction cancer starting first-line therapy, so the anti-HER2 biologic recommendation is not guideline-concordant. With PD-L1 status unavailable, the safer interpretation is to use standard fluoropyrimidine-based doublet chemotherapy and avoid a HER2-targeted agent.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case still meets the general first-line advanced gastric/GEJ chemotherapy rule and the rule against adding a biologic agent in HER2-negative disease. It no longer supports any HER2-directed addition, and PD-L1 is not provided to justify an ICI-based recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with metastatic gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and testing shows HER2 is overexpressed. He has not received prior systemic therapy and is being evaluated for first-line treatment. His performance status is fair, and he is interested in starting treatment promptly. The oncology team is considering adding a targeted biologic agent to chemotherapy because of the metastatic disease, but the tumor is HER2-positive and this is his initial systemic regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This is nearly identical to the source metastatic first-line GEJ case, but the HER2 result is flipped to overexpressed, changing the biologic treatment implication.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Omit targeted therapy and use chemotherapy alone
Avoid adding trastuzumab because the disease is metastatic
recommended_actions: Prescribe a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen for first-line treatment.
Add trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is no longer one in which a biologic agent should be withheld for HER2-negative disease; it is now HER2-overexpressing metastatic GEJ adenocarcinoma starting first-line therapy. The safer interpretation is to include trastuzumab with a fluoropyrimidine-based doublet rather than treating as HER2-negative disease.
challenging_examples.rationale: HER2 overexpression invalidates the original no-biologic recommendation. The case still fits first-line advanced gastric/GEJ treatment, and the HER2-positive status triggers trastuzumab addition.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using a 37-mm plastic cassette for acrylamide sampling
selected_rules.execute: Do not use plastic cassettes because recoveries are poor
selected_rules.source_statement: Plastic cassettes (37-mm) yielded poor recoveries of acrylamide and are therefore unsuitable

selected_rules.activation_condition: Monitoring airborne acrylamide exposure
selected_rules.execute: Use a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube
selected_rules.source_statement: A worker's exposure to airborne acrylamide should be determined by using a personal sampling train consisting of a glass-fiber filter in a Swinnex cassette (13-mm) followed by a silica gel tube

selected_rules.activation_condition: Collecting airborne acrylamide samples
selected_rules.execute: Collect samples at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for at least 2 hours and no more than 120 liters of air
selected_rules.source_statement: Samples should be collected at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for a minimum of 2 hr; the maximum air volume should be 120 liters

selected_rules.activation_condition: After collecting airborne acrylamide samples
selected_rules.execute: Extract acrylamide from the silica gel tube with methanol
selected_rules.source_statement: The silica gel tube should then be treated with methanol to extract the acrylamide

selected_rules.activation_condition: After sampling airborne acrylamide on glass-fiber filters
selected_rules.execute: Transfer filters immediately to glass vials containing 1 ml methanol to avoid evaporation losses
selected_rules.source_statement: An important step in this method is the transfer of the glass-fiber filters to glass vials containing 1 ml of methanol immediately after sampling to avoid losses of acrylamide from the filter by evaporation

selected_rules.activation_condition: Analyzing airborne acrylamide samples
selected_rules.execute: Use gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector
selected_rules.source_statement: Analysis should be conducted by gas chromatography using a nitrogen/phosphorus detector

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using animal evidence to guide acrylamide exposure limits
selected_rules.execute: Base occupational exposure limits on animal studies showing exposure-response relationships
selected_rules.source_statement: However, many studies demonstrate a relationship between exposure and adverse health effects in animals. These studies are the bases for occupational exposure limits recommended by many organizations and government agencies

selected_rules.activation_condition: Evaluating acrylamide carcinogenicity in animals
selected_rules.execute: Treat acrylamide as an animal carcinogen based on rat and mouse studies
selected_rules.source_statement: Studies in rats and mice indicate that acrylamide is an animal carcinogen

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant comes to occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. They initially considered a 37-mm plastic cassette because it is commonly used for other dusts, but the sampling plan is being revised after a review of the acrylamide method. The hygienist also wants to ensure the sample can be analyzed reliably after collection and that losses during transport are minimized.
recommended_actions: Do not use a 37-mm plastic cassette for acrylamide sampling because recoveries are poor.
Use a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube.
Collect samples at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for at least 2 hours and no more than 120 liters of air.
After sampling, transfer the glass-fiber filters immediately to glass vials containing 1 ml methanol to avoid evaporation losses.
Extract acrylamide from the silica gel tube with methanol.
Analyze the samples by gas chromatography using a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes workplace monitoring for airborne acrylamide and explicitly includes use of a 37-mm plastic cassette, which triggers the recommendation against that device due to poor recoveries. The scenario also supports the standard acrylamide monitoring method: a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube, sampled at no more than 1 L/min for at least 2 hours and with a maximum air volume of 120 L. Post-collection handling and analysis steps are also triggered: immediate transfer of filters to methanol-containing glass vials, methanol extraction of the silica gel tube, and gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant comes to occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. They are considering a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube, but the proposed plan will start with a 2.5 L/min pump setting to improve capture during the short shift. The hygienist also wants to ensure the sample can be analyzed reliably after collection and that losses during transport are minimized.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves routine occupational acrylamide monitoring with the same cassette/filter/tube setup, but the sampling flowrate is subtly above the method limit.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with the personal acrylamide sampling train using the higher 2.5 L/min flow to maximize collection efficiency.
Keep the glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette and analyze the sample by gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.
Transfer the filter immediately to a glass vial with methanol and extract the silica gel tube with methanol after sampling.
recommended_actions: Do not use a 37-mm plastic cassette for acrylamide sampling because recoveries are poor.
Use a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube.
Collect samples at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for at least 2 hours and no more than 120 liters of air.
After sampling, transfer the glass-fiber filters immediately to glass vials containing 1 ml methanol to avoid evaporation losses.
Extract acrylamide from the silica gel tube with methanol.
Analyze the samples by gas chromatography using a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still an acrylamide personal monitoring scenario, but the proposed flowrate of 2.5 L/min exceeds the recommended maximum of 1 L/min. The safer interpretation is to keep the standard sampling train and handling steps, but adjust the pump to the validated flowrate and volume limits.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source workflow remains the same, so the acrylamide method is still relevant. The subtle mismatch is only in the sampling rate, which invalidates the specific collection recommendation while leaving the rest of the method applicable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant comes to occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. They initially considered a 37-mm plastic cassette because it is commonly used for other dusts, but the sampling plan is being revised after a review of the acrylamide method. The hygienist also wants to ensure the sample can be analyzed reliably after collection and that losses during transport are minimized. The only change is that the post-collection workflow would place the glass-fiber filter into a polypropylene vial rather than a glass vial because the glass containers are temporarily unavailable.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same acrylamide monitoring setup and even keeps the filter transfer step, but the container material is subtly changed from glass to polypropylene.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the same personal sampling train and transfer the filter into the available polypropylene vial with methanol.
Extract the silica gel tube with methanol and analyze by gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.
Continue with the 13-mm Swinnex cassette and silica gel tube because the rest of the method is unchanged.
recommended_actions: Do not use a 37-mm plastic cassette for acrylamide sampling because recoveries are poor.
Use a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube.
Collect samples at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for at least 2 hours and no more than 120 liters of air.
Extract acrylamide from the silica gel tube with methanol.
Analyze the samples by gas chromatography using a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The modified case no longer satisfies the filter-handling recommendation because the validated method specifies immediate transfer into glass vials containing methanol. A polypropylene vial introduces an unvalidated container choice and risks analyte loss or contamination, so the safer interpretation is to follow the standard glass-vial handling or defer to a validated alternative method.
challenging_examples.rationale: The scenario still calls for acrylamide personal monitoring, but the container material change breaks the specific post-sampling preservation step. Other steps remain concordant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering any antiretroviral medication other than TDF/FTC or TDF for PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Do not use other antiretroviral medications for PrEP
selected_rules.source_statement: The use of other antiretroviral medications for PrEP, either in place of or in addition to TDF/FTC (or TDF) is not recommended. (IIIA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering noncontinuous or coitally timed oral PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe oral PrEP for intermittent, episodic, or other noncontinuous daily use
selected_rules.source_statement: The prescription of oral PrEP for coitally-timed or other noncontinuous daily use is not recommended. (IIIA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult MSM at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
selected_rules.execute: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
selected_rules.source_statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for sexually-active adult MSM (men who have sex with men) at substantial risk of HIV acquisition (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: heterosexually active adult men or women at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
selected_rules.execute: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
selected_rules.source_statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for adult heterosexually active men and women who are at substantial risk of HIV acquisition. (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult injection drug users at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
selected_rules.execute: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
selected_rules.source_statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for adult injection drug users (IDU) at substantial risk of HIV acquisition. (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: before prescribing PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Exclude acute and chronic HIV infection by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before prescribing
selected_rules.source_statement: Acute and chronic HIV infection must be excluded by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before PrEP is prescribed. (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: prescribing PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Use daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) as the approved regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The only medication regimen approved by the Food and Drug Administration and recommended for PrEP with all the populations specified in this guideline is daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is taking PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Assess HIV infection at least every 3 months
selected_rules.source_statement: HIV infection should be assessed at least every 3 months while patients are taking PrEP so that those with incident infection do not continue taking it. The 2-drug regimen of TDF/FTC is inadequate therapy for established HIV infection, and its use may engender resistance to either or both drugs. (IA)

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man who has sex with men presents for HIV prevention counseling. He reports multiple male partners over the past 6 months, inconsistent condom use, and one recent bacterial STI. He has no fever, rash, sore throat, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection, and he had a negative HIV test 2 weeks ago. He asks about starting PrEP and wonders whether he can take it only on days he expects sex, or whether he should add another antiretroviral medication for extra protection. He is otherwise healthy and has normal renal function. After discussing options, he is interested in daily PrEP and agrees to follow-up testing.
recommended_actions: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option.
Exclude acute and chronic HIV infection by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before prescribing PrEP.
Use daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) as the approved regimen.
Assess HIV infection at least every 3 months while the patient is taking PrEP.
Do not use other antiretroviral medications for PrEP.
Do not prescribe oral PrEP for intermittent, episodic, or other noncontinuous daily use.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the recommendation for daily oral PrEP in an adult MSM at substantial risk of HIV acquisition, along with the requirement to exclude HIV infection before prescribing and to use the approved TDF/FTC regimen. It also triggers follow-up HIV assessment every 3 months while on PrEP. The patient’s questions about adding another antiretroviral or using PrEP only on sex days activate the not-recommended rules against other antiretrovirals and noncontinuous oral PrEP.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man who has sex with men presents for HIV prevention counseling. He reports multiple male partners over the past 6 months, inconsistent condom use, and one recent bacterial STI. He has no fever, rash, sore throat, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection, and he had a negative HIV test 2 weeks ago. He asks about starting PrEP and says he prefers to take it only around weekends when he expects to have sex. He is otherwise healthy and has normal renal function. He is interested in daily PrEP but wants the option to stop and restart it based on anticipated exposure. The visit focuses on selecting a prevention strategy and arranging follow-up.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient remains a high-risk MSM requesting PrEP with a recent negative HIV test and no symptoms, but the key change is his preference for intermittent, sex-timed use rather than continuous daily dosing.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start oral PrEP and instruct him to take the tablets only on days he expects sex.
Offer daily TDF/FTC but reassure him that weekend-only dosing is an acceptable PrEP strategy.
Provide PrEP and plan to tailor the antiretroviral schedule around anticipated exposures.
recommended_actions: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option.
Exclude acute and chronic HIV infection by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before prescribing PrEP.
Use daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) as the approved regimen.
Assess HIV infection at least every 3 months while the patient is taking PrEP.
Do not use other antiretroviral medications for PrEP.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a candidate for daily PrEP because he is an adult MSM at substantial risk, but the coitally timed or intermittent dosing request is not guideline-concordant. The safer interpretation is to counsel him toward continuous daily TDF/FTC rather than event-driven oral PrEP.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the indication for PrEP, HIV exclusion, and approved regimen, but the patient’s intended intermittent use directly conflicts with the recommendation against noncontinuous oral PrEP.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old heterosexual woman presents for HIV prevention counseling. She has a partner with ongoing injection drug use, inconsistent condom use, and a recent bacterial STI. She has no fever, rash, sore throat, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection, and she had a negative HIV test last month. She asks about starting PrEP and wonders whether she should take an extra antiretroviral pill for better protection if her partner continues to use drugs. Her renal function is normal. She is interested in prevention options and agrees to follow-up testing after counseling.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has substantial HIV exposure risk and is asking about PrEP, but the subtle difference is her request to add another antiretroviral medication rather than use the standard single approved regimen.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add a second antiretroviral agent to enhance PrEP efficacy.
Prescribe a combination PrEP strategy with more than TDF/FTC because her exposure risk is high.
Use an intensified antiretroviral regimen for prevention.
recommended_actions: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option.
Exclude acute and chronic HIV infection by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before prescribing PrEP.
Use daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) as the approved regimen.
Assess HIV infection at least every 3 months while the patient is taking PrEP.
Do not prescribe oral PrEP for intermittent, episodic, or other noncontinuous daily use.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: She still meets criteria for PrEP consideration as a heterosexually active woman at substantial risk, but adding another antiretroviral is not recommended. The safer interpretation is to use the standard daily TDF/FTC regimen only, after confirming HIV-negative status immediately before starting.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case continues to support daily PrEP in a high-risk heterosexual woman and routine HIV testing, but it invalidates the recommendation against additional antiretroviral medications by introducing a request for combination therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Bird has signs of avian chlamydiosis
selected_rules.execute: Do not buy or sell the bird.
selected_rules.source_statement: Avoid purchasing or selling birds that have signs of AC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Birds are being treated for avian chlamydiosis
selected_rules.execute: Do not expose treated birds to untreated birds or other potential sources of infection.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because treated birds can be reinfected, they should not be exposed to untreated birds or other potential sources of infection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Humans with psittacosis
selected_rules.execute: Use tetracyclines as the drugs of choice.
selected_rules.source_statement: Tetracyclines are the drugs of choice (3 ).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Most patients with psittacosis
selected_rules.execute: Treat with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily or tetracycline hydrochloride 500 mg four times daily.
selected_rules.source_statement: Most patients respond to oral therapy (100 mg of doxycycline administered twice a day or 500 mg of tetracycline hydrochloride administered four times a day).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Severely ill patient with psittacosis needing initial treatment
selected_rules.execute: Give intravenous doxycycline hyclate 4.4 mg/kg/day divided into two infusions, up to 100 mg per dose.
selected_rules.source_statement: For initial treatment of severely ill patients, doxycycline hyclate can be administered intravenously at a dosage of 4.4 mg/kg (2 mg/lb) body weight per day divided into two infusions per day (up to 100 mg per dose).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Psittacosis treatment
selected_rules.execute: Continue treatment for at least 10-14 days after fever abates.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, relapse can occur, and treatment must continue for at least 10-14 days after fever abates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with psittacosis
selected_rules.execute: Use standard infection-control precautions; do not use special isolation procedures such as private room, negative pressure airflow, or masks.
selected_rules.source_statement: Standard infection-control precautions are sufficient for patients with psittacosis, and specific isolation procedures (e.g., private room, negative pressure air flow, and masks) are not indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Person in contact with infected birds
selected_rules.execute: Inform them about the disease and have them wear protective clothing, gloves, a disposable surgical cap, and an N95 or higher-efficiency respirator when cleaning cages or handling infected birds.
selected_rules.source_statement: Inform all persons in contact with infected birds about the nature of the disease. Instruct them to wear protective clothing, gloves, a disposable surgical cap, and a respirator with an N95 rating or a higher-efficiency respirator when cleaning cages or handling infected birds.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue. He reports cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week and says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he still helped move them for sale. Today he is febrile to 39.1°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. He asks whether he should keep working around the birds while he recovers.
recommended_actions: Do not buy or sell the birds that have signs of avian chlamydiosis.
Treat the patient with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily or tetracycline hydrochloride 500 mg four times daily.
Use standard infection-control precautions; special isolation procedures such as a private room, negative pressure airflow, or masks are not indicated.
source_case.rationale: The patient has psittacosis consistent with exposure to ill birds, triggering the recommendation for tetracycline-class treatment and standard infection-control precautions. The vignette also includes birds with signs of avian chlamydiosis, which triggers the recommendation not to buy or sell those birds.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue. He reports cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week and says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he still helped move them for sale. Today he is febrile to 39.1°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. He asks whether he should keep working around the birds while he recovers. The store owner adds that the birds with respiratory signs were already separated from the rest of the flock and are being monitored by an avian veterinarian.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The exposure history and clinical picture still strongly suggest psittacosis, but the birds with signs are no longer being bought or sold.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Advise against buying or selling the visibly ill birds
Start oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily
Use only standard infection-control precautions without special isolation
recommended_actions: Treat the patient with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily or tetracycline hydrochloride 500 mg four times daily.
Use standard infection-control precautions; special isolation procedures such as a private room, negative pressure airflow, or masks are not indicated.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a likely psittacosis case in the patient, so tetracycline-class therapy and routine infection control are appropriate. However, the bird-related recommendation to avoid buying or selling ill birds is not triggered because the affected birds have already been separated and are not being sold.
challenging_examples.rationale: Human psittacosis still triggers antibiotic treatment and standard precautions. The bird-sale recommendation only applies when birds with signs of avian chlamydiosis are being bought or sold; that condition is removed here.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue after cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week. He says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he continued handling the birds because the shop was short-staffed. Today he is febrile to 39.0°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. A chest radiograph obtained in urgent care shows only mild bibasilar atelectatic change without focal infiltrate or severe respiratory compromise. He asks whether he needs to be admitted for isolation before returning to work.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like psittacosis, but the patient is not severely ill and does not need initial IV doxycycline.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Admit him for airborne isolation
Start intravenous doxycycline as initial therapy
Place him in a private negative-pressure room
recommended_actions: Treat the patient with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily or tetracycline hydrochloride 500 mg four times daily.
Use standard infection-control precautions; special isolation procedures such as a private room, negative pressure airflow, or masks are not indicated.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The patient still fits psittacosis and should receive oral tetracycline-class therapy with routine precautions. The IV doxycycline recommendation is reserved for severely ill patients needing initial treatment, which is not supported by his stable condition and imaging.
challenging_examples.rationale: This presentation supports psittacosis, but the severity threshold for IV doxycycline is not met. Standard precautions remain sufficient, and oral therapy is guideline-concordant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is used primarily as a softener in plastics, especially PVC; occupational exposure can occur during production, addition to plastics, or processing of softened plastics
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action specified
selected_rules.source_statement: Occupational exposure occurs during the production of DEHP, during the addition of DEHP to plastics, and in the processing of softened plastics.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP exposure is being assessed in an occupational setting
selected_rules.execute: Consider that reported air concentrations in workplaces have ranged from very low to high levels depending on industry
selected_rules.source_statement: Air concentrations between 0.01 and 2.0 mg/m3 were recorded... Concentrations between 1.7 and 66 mg/m3 were recorded... DEHP air concentrations between 0.09 and 0.16 mg/m3 were reported.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is administered orally in rats
selected_rules.execute: Expect substantial gut hydrolysis to MEHP before absorption
selected_rules.source_statement: DEHP is hydrolyzed in the gut.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is given at high oral doses in rats
selected_rules.execute: Expect unmetabolized DEHP to appear in liver only after high doses
selected_rules.source_statement: In the liver unhydrolyzed DEHP can only be found after administration of high doses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: MEHP is administered orally
selected_rules.execute: Expect MEHP to be well absorbed
selected_rules.source_statement: Experiments with p.o. administration of MEHP indicated that this metabolite was well absorbed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is administered intravenously in rats
selected_rules.execute: Expect rapid elimination from blood with short half-lives
selected_rules.source_statement: Intravenously administered DEHP is rapidly eliminated from blood.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP exposure is being evaluated for distribution in the body
selected_rules.execute: Expect rapid distribution to liver, carcass, lung, and spleen after intravenous dosing
selected_rules.source_statement: Radioactivity from DEHP was rapidly distributed to liver, carcass, lung and spleen when administered i.v.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is administered orally
selected_rules.execute: Expect distribution mainly as MEHP
selected_rules.source_statement: Perorally administered DEHP is mainly distributed as MEHP.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 41-year-old man works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. He reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing and processing, and he asks about a chemical used in the materials. No acute symptoms are reported, but he is concerned after learning the compound is common in flexible plastics. The occupational health nurse documents that his exposure is related to production and processing tasks rather than a one-time spill. The workplace also has variable ventilation, and measured air levels in similar facilities are known to range widely depending on industry and task.
recommended_actions: Recognize that the patient’s occupational exposure is plausibly related to DEHP use in PVC/plastic production and processing.
Consider that workplace air concentrations can vary widely, from very low to high levels depending on the industry and task.
source_case.rationale: The vignette matches DEHP exposure in an occupational setting during production and processing of softened plastics, triggering R001 and R002. No clinical intervention is specified by these rules beyond recognizing the exposure context and noting that workplace air concentrations may vary widely.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 41-year-old man works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. He reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing and processing, and he asks about a chemical used in the materials. No acute symptoms are reported, but he is concerned after learning the compound is common in flexible plastics. The occupational health nurse documents that his exposure is related to maintenance work around the storage area rather than production or processing tasks, and he usually remains outside the area where the softened PVC is cut. The workplace has variable ventilation, and measured air levels in similar facilities are known to range widely depending on industry and task.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting remains a PVC flooring plant with concern about the same plasticizer and similar industrial hygiene context, but the exposure is shifted from direct production/processing to peripheral maintenance around storage.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Attribute the exposure to DEHP from PVC production and processing
Note that workplace air concentrations for this type of exposure can vary widely
recommended_actions: Consider that reported air concentrations in workplaces have ranged from very low to high levels depending on industry
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case no longer clearly fits the production, addition, or processing exposure condition for DEHP because the worker’s duties are limited to maintenance near storage and not the softened-plastics process itself. The safer interpretation is that DEHP exposure is possible in the workplace, but the specific rule about direct occupational exposure during production/processing is not triggered.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires direct exposure during production, addition to plastics, or processing of softened plastics. The vignette shifts the worker to indirect maintenance around storage, so that condition is not met. R002 still applies because DEHP exposure is being assessed occupationally, and workplace air concentrations can vary widely.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 41-year-old man works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. He reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing and processing, and he asks about a chemical used in the materials. No acute symptoms are reported, but he is concerned after learning the compound is common in flexible plastics. The occupational health nurse documents that his exposure is related to production and processing tasks rather than a one-time spill. The workplace has fixed, well-functioning local exhaust ventilation and measured air levels in his facility have consistently been low on repeated industrial hygiene sampling, unlike the wide ranges seen in some other plants.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The plant type, task description, and concern about the same plasticizer are unchanged, but the exposure monitoring detail is narrowed to a single facility with consistently low measurements instead of a variable workplace range.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recognize an occupational DEHP exposure and emphasize that workplace air concentrations may vary widely
Document the exposure as representative of the broader range reported in similar facilities
recommended_actions: Recognize that the patient’s occupational exposure is plausibly related to DEHP use in PVC/plastic production and processing
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette still fits the occupational production/processing exposure condition for DEHP, but it does not support the broader statement about workplace air concentrations ranging widely in this specific case because the facility’s monitoring is consistently low and controlled. The safer interpretation is direct occupational DEHP exposure from PVC processing with no basis to generalize high variability from this single workplace.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 remains triggered because the man works in PVC production and processing with plasticizers. R002 is not well supported here because the vignette specifies consistently low measured air levels at this facility, weakening the intended generalization about widely varying workplace concentrations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: child aged 2 through 4 years with asthma, recurrent wheezing, or wheezing/asthma in the past 12 months
selected_rules.execute: Do not give FluMist.
selected_rules.source_statement: Children whose parents or caregivers answer 'yes' to this question and children who have asthma or who had a wheezing episode noted in the medical record within the past 12 months should not receive FluMist.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person caring for severely immunosuppressed persons who require a protective environment
selected_rules.execute: Do not give FluMist / LAIV.
selected_rules.source_statement: Persons who care for severely immunosuppressed persons who require a protective environment should not receive FluMist given the theoretical risk for transmission of the live-attenuated vaccine virus.

selected_rules.activation_condition: child aged under 9 years
selected_rules.execute: Do not use Afluria.
selected_rules.source_statement: ACIP does not recommend the U.S.-licensed CSL Biotherapies' TIV, Afluria, for children aged <9 years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person with a previous severe allergic reaction to influenza vaccine
selected_rules.execute: Do not give future influenza vaccine.
selected_rules.source_statement: A previous severe allergic reaction to influenza vaccine, regardless of the component suspected to be responsible for the reaction, is a contraindication to future receipt of the vaccine.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all persons aged 6 months or older
selected_rules.execute: Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
selected_rules.source_statement: Routine annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all persons aged ≥6 months.

selected_rules.activation_condition: vaccination is being planned and influenza activity has not yet started in the community
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate as soon as vaccine is available, before influenza activity begins if possible.
selected_rules.source_statement: Vaccination optimally should occur before onset of influenza activity in the community. Therefore, vaccination providers should offer vaccination as soon as vaccine is available.

selected_rules.activation_condition: influenza viruses are circulating in the community
selected_rules.execute: Continue offering vaccination throughout the influenza season.
selected_rules.source_statement: Vaccination should be offered throughout the influenza season (i.e., as long as influenza viruses are circulating in the community).

selected_rules.activation_condition: child aged 6 months through 8 years receiving influenza vaccine for the first season
selected_rules.execute: Give 2 doses of influenza vaccine at least 4 weeks apart.
selected_rules.source_statement: Children aged 6 months through 8 years require 2 doses of influenza vaccine (administered a minimum of 4 weeks apart) during their first season of vaccination to optimize immune response.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He has never received an influenza vaccine before. The community has not yet reported influenza activity this season.
recommended_actions: Do not give FluMist.
Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
Vaccinate as soon as vaccine is available, before influenza activity begins if possible.
Give 2 doses of influenza vaccine at least 4 weeks apart for his first season of vaccination.
source_case.rationale: R001 applies because he is age 2 through 4 years with asthma and wheezing in the past 12 months, so FluMist should not be used. R005 applies because routine annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all persons aged 6 months or older. R006 applies because vaccination is being planned before influenza activity has started in the community. R008 applies because he is 6 months through 8 years old and is receiving influenza vaccine for the first season, so he needs 2 doses at least 4 weeks apart.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of mild asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He has never received an influenza vaccine before. The community has already reported several weeks of influenza activity this season.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The age, asthma history, first-time vaccination status, and routine preventive visit are unchanged; the only meaningful shift is that influenza is already circulating locally.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Do not give FluMist.
Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
Give 2 doses of influenza vaccine at least 4 weeks apart.
Vaccinate immediately before more flu spreads in the community.
recommended_actions: Do not give FluMist.
Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
Give 2 doses of influenza vaccine at least 4 weeks apart.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This child still meets criteria to avoid FluMist because he is 2 to 4 years old with asthma and a wheezing episode within the past 12 months. He also still needs routine influenza vaccination and, because this is his first season, 2 doses at least 4 weeks apart. However, the specific recommendation to vaccinate before influenza activity begins is no longer applicable because influenza is already circulating in the community; vaccination should still be offered during the season, but that pre-season timing rule is invalidated.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies because he is age 2 through 4 years with asthma and wheezing in the past 12 months, so FluMist should not be used. R005 applies because routine annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all persons aged 6 months or older. R008 applies because he is 6 months through 8 years old and is receiving influenza vaccine for the first season, so he needs 2 doses at least 4 weeks apart.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He received one influenza vaccine dose last year during infancy, but the chart does not show a second dose that season. The community has not yet reported influenza activity this season.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The child looks almost identical to a first-season vaccine candidate, but the record shows prior influenza immunization, changing the dose requirement.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Do not give FluMist.
Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
Vaccinate as soon as vaccine is available, before influenza activity begins if possible.
Give 2 doses of influenza vaccine at least 4 weeks apart for his first season of vaccination.
recommended_actions: Do not give FluMist.
Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
Vaccinate as soon as vaccine is available, before influenza activity begins if possible.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: He still should not receive FluMist because of asthma with wheezing in the past year, and he should receive annual influenza vaccination before community activity begins if possible. The subtle mismatch is that he is not a true first-season influenza vaccine recipient: he already received one prior dose last year. That means the automatic 2-dose first-season recommendation does not cleanly apply on the basis of this vignette alone, so the safer interpretation is to verify prior influenza vaccine history before deciding on any additional dose schedule.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies because he is age 2 through 4 years with asthma and wheezing in the past 12 months, so FluMist should not be used. R005 applies because routine annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all persons aged 6 months or older. R006 applies because vaccination is being planned and influenza activity has not yet started in the community, so vaccination should occur as soon as vaccine is available. R008 is not triggered because the child is not clearly receiving influenza vaccine for the first season; he had a prior influenza dose last year.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Control employee exposure by adhering to the standard sections in this document.
selected_rules.source_statement: recommends that employee exposure to inorganic lead in the workplace be controlled by adherence to the following sections.
