selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Apply the standard as a lifetime 8-hour day, 40-hour week protection target.
selected_rules.source_statement: The standard is designed to protect the health and safety of workers for an 8-hour day, 40-hour week over a working lifetime; compliance with the standard should therefore prevent adverse effects of lead on the health and safety of workers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques for compliance determinations.
selected_rules.source_statement: The standard is measurable by techniques that are valid, reproducible, and available to industry and government agencies.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that sufficient technology exists to comply with the recommended standard.
selected_rules.source_statement: Sufficient technology exists to permit compliance with the recommended standard.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace air exposure to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Keep the 8-hour time-weighted average airborne lead concentration at or below 0.15 mg Pb/m^3.
selected_rules.source_statement: workers shall not be exposed to inorganic lead at a concentration greater that 0.15 mg Pb/m^ determined as a time-weighted average (TWA) exposure for an 8-hour workday.

selected_rules.activation_condition: environmental samples are collected for lead monitoring
selected_rules.execute: Collect environmental samples using Appendix I or an equivalent method, and analyze them using Appendix II or an equivalent method with comparable precision and accuracy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Procedures for collection of environmental samples shall be as provided in Appendix I, or by an equivalent method. Analysis of samples shall be as provided in Appendix II, or by any method shown to be equivalent in precision and accuracy to the method specified in Appendix II.

selected_rules.activation_condition: worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Make biologic monitoring available to the worker.
selected_rules.source_statement: Biologic monitoring shall be made available to all workers subject to "exposure to inorganic lead."

selected_rules.activation_condition: worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Use whole blood lead testing, or urine lead testing as an alternative, to assess lead absorption.
selected_rules.source_statement: It consists of sampling and analysis of whole blood, or alternatively, of urine for lead content.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years, works full time, and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations where inorganic lead exposure is possible.
recommended_actions: Control employee exposure to inorganic lead by adhering to the standard sections in this document.
Use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques for compliance determinations.
Make biologic monitoring available to the worker.
Use whole blood lead testing, or urine lead testing as an alternative, to assess lead absorption.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a worker with occupational exposure to inorganic lead, which triggers the general control and monitoring recommendations. Because he is subject to exposure, biologic monitoring should be made available, and lead absorption should be assessed with whole blood lead testing or urine lead testing as an alternative. The workplace is also performing air sampling, so compliance determinations should use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years, works full time, and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations where inorganic lead exposure is possible. However, he was recently reassigned to administrative duties in a separate office, with no direct contact with battery dismantling, smelting, or cleanup tasks for the past 9 months.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The workplace and lead-recycling setting are unchanged, but the worker’s current assignment is office-based with no ongoing inorganic lead exposure.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Continue lead-exposure medical surveillance and biologic monitoring.
Obtain a whole blood lead level at this visit.
Treat him as an active lead-exposed worker for compliance monitoring.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This worker’s current duties no longer place him subject to exposure to inorganic lead, so the lead-specific workplace surveillance and biologic monitoring recommendations are not triggered. A safer interpretation is to verify the reassignment and assess whether any residual exposure history requires separate follow-up, but not to apply the inorganic lead monitoring standard to his present office role.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule requires ongoing occupational exposure to inorganic lead. Here, that exposure has stopped because he has been reassigned away from lead-handling tasks, so the lead surveillance and testing recommendations do not apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years, works full time, and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations where inorganic lead exposure is possible. The current air-monitoring report shows 8-hour time-weighted average lead concentrations of 0.08 mg/m^3, with no excursions above the action level during the last quarter.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The exposure setting, job tasks, and monitoring workflow are the same, but the measured airborne lead concentration is below the occupational limit.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend reducing airborne lead exposure below the standard limit and continuing compliance monitoring.
Use the air-monitoring result as evidence that the worker still needs lead surveillance.
Obtain a whole blood lead level and continue biologic monitoring because he works around lead.
recommended_actions: Control employee exposure to inorganic lead by adhering to the standard sections in this document.
Use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques for compliance determinations.
Collect environmental samples using Appendix I or an equivalent method, and analyze them using Appendix II or an equivalent method with comparable precision and accuracy.
Make biologic monitoring available to the worker.
Use whole blood lead testing, or urine lead testing as an alternative, to assess lead absorption.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case still involves an inorganic lead-exposed worker, so the general exposure-control, environmental sampling, and biologic monitoring recommendations remain applicable. The subtle change is that the measured 8-hour TWA airborne lead concentration is below the stated limit of 0.15 mg/m^3, so the specific recommendation to keep exposure at or below a higher threshold is not the issue here. The safer interpretation is that current airborne levels are already within the cited limit, while surveillance and control procedures should still be maintained.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves occupational lead exposure, which continues to trigger control, valid measurement, environmental sampling, and biologic monitoring recommendations. It invalidates only the notion that exposure exceeds the numeric airborne limit, because the measured TWA is below 0.15 mg/m^3.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the public may be worried or outraged
selected_rules.execute: Do not dismiss outrage or tell people they should not be afraid.
selected_rules.source_statement: Don't Dismiss Outrage. ... When people are afraid and worried, the worst thing to do is pretend that they're not. The second worst thing is to tell them they shouldn't be.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When giving reassurance during an early crisis
selected_rules.execute: Do not over-reassure or minimize the risk.
selected_rules.source_statement: Don't over-reassure. ... Reassurance can backfire.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the public or media ask about cause, blame, or liability early in the event
selected_rules.execute: Do not discuss liability, blame, or cause before the situation is contained.
selected_rules.source_statement: Avoid discussing liability: Questions of cause, blame, responsibility, and liability often follow a crisis. It is not appropriate to discuss them at the early stages.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the event involves a possible bioterrorism claim or rumor
selected_rules.execute: Do not speculate about bioterrorism; refer questions to law enforcement and coordinate statements.
selected_rules.source_statement: CDC will not comment to the media about the possibility of an outbreak being a bioterrorist event; nor will it comment on the FBI's participation in an investigation unless first confirmed by the FBI to the media.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When communicating during a crisis or emergency
selected_rules.execute: Use crisis and emergency risk communication principles to help people make the best possible decisions for their well-being.
selected_rules.source_statement: CERC is the attempt by public health professionals to provide information that allows individuals, stakeholders, and entire communities to make the best possible decisions for their well-being during a crisis or emergency.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When communicating during a crisis or emergency
selected_rules.execute: Be first, be right, be credible, express empathy, promote action, and show respect.
selected_rules.source_statement: Throughout this book, six principles of effective crisis and risk communication are emphasized: 1. Be First... 2. Be Right... 3. Be Credible... 4. Express Empathy... 5. Promote Action... 6. Show Respect.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the public is affected by a crisis
selected_rules.execute: Provide simple, credible, accurate, consistent information on time.
selected_rules.source_statement: When communicating in the initial phase of an emergency, it is important to present information that is simple, credible, accurate, consistent, and delivered on time.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When people need to know what to do during a crisis
selected_rules.execute: Give people meaningful actions they can take and offer a range of responses.
selected_rules.source_statement: Giving people meaningful things to do calms anxiety, helps restore order, and promotes a restored sense of control.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea within the same day. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic has not yet received confirmed public health information, and local officials are still investigating the source and scope of the illness.
recommended_actions: Use crisis and emergency risk communication principles to guide the response.
Be first, be right, be credible, express empathy, promote action, and show respect.
Provide simple, credible, accurate, consistent information on time.
Give people meaningful actions they can take and offer a range of responses.
Do not dismiss the anxiety or tell people they should not be afraid.
Do not discuss blame, responsibility, liability, or cause before the situation is contained; avoid speculating about a deliberate cause.
source_case.rationale: The vignette involves an unfolding community illness event with public anxiety, early uncertainty, and questions about cause and blame. That triggers the crisis communication rules: communicate with CERC principles, provide timely accurate information, offer actionable steps, and respond with empathy and respect. Because the patient and community are worried, the response should not minimize fear or dismiss outrage. Because the source is not yet confirmed, discussions of blame or a deliberate cause should be avoided.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea within the same day. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic received a preliminary notice from local public health that the source appears to be a known norovirus exposure, but investigation is still ongoing and the number of affected students is increasing.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting, anxiety, and outbreak concern are nearly unchanged, but a preliminary source has already been identified, making early speculation about cause less problematic than in the original case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Reassure her that the outbreak is probably minor and should not worry her family.
Explain that the school cafeteria is the likely culprit and discuss possible responsibility.
Tell her the illness is definitely norovirus and that the source has been confirmed.
Give general advice without addressing her concerns or offering concrete next steps.
recommended_actions: Use crisis and emergency risk communication principles to guide the response.
Be first, be right, be credible, express empathy, promote action, and show respect.
Provide simple, credible, accurate, consistent information on time.
Give people meaningful actions they can take and offer a range of responses.
Do not dismiss the anxiety or tell people they should not be afraid.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a crisis communication scenario, so empathetic, accurate, timely messaging and practical actions are still indicated. However, the case no longer fits the rule against discussing cause because public health has already issued a preliminary exposure source. The safer interpretation is to avoid overstatement and present only confirmed or clearly labeled preliminary information.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette still involves an active outbreak and public anxiety, which triggers crisis communication principles and the need for empathy, accuracy, and actionable guidance. The subtle change is that local public health has already provided a preliminary source. That weakens the prohibition on discussing cause, so the original no-cause recommendation is no longer fully applicable. The response should stay careful, avoid certainty, and focus on confirmed facts and next steps.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea within the same day. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic has received a call from the county health department stating that the illness cluster is limited to one classroom, all affected children recovered quickly, and no further cases are expected; the clinician is asked only to advise routine hand hygiene and return precautions.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient’s emotional presentation and the outbreak theme are preserved, but the event is now described as contained and low-risk, so the full crisis messaging framework is less clearly indicated.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Launch a full crisis-response script with urgent public messaging and broad emergency precautions.
Emphasize that the situation is still unfolding and may be more serious than it appears.
Encourage the patient to alert coworkers that a major outbreak is underway.
Focus on detailed outbreak communication rather than the specific hand hygiene advice already provided.
recommended_actions: Do not dismiss the anxiety or tell people they should not be afraid.
Do not discuss blame, responsibility, liability, or cause before the situation is contained; avoid speculating about a deliberate cause.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The event has been described by public health as limited and expected to resolve, so the broad crisis communication recommendation is less clearly triggered. The key remaining issue is to respond respectfully without speculation about deliberate cause or blame. The safer interpretation is routine reassurance paired with the already provided public health advice.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although the encounter still contains anxiety and questions about an outbreak, the cluster is now reported as limited, recovered, and not expected to spread. That makes the full emergency communication framework less applicable than in the source case. What remains clearly applicable is avoiding blame or speculation about a deliberate cause while not dismissing the patient’s concern. The modified vignette is a near-miss because it looks like the same outbreak communication scenario, but the public health context has changed materially.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection
selected_rules.execute: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection and is HIV-negative
selected_rules.execute: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI for either HIV-negative or HIV-infected persons.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection and is HIV-infected
selected_rules.execute: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI for either HIV-negative or HIV-infected persons.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection is taking other liver-injury medications
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 1) are concurrently taking other medications associated with liver injury

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection drinks excessive alcohol
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 2) drink excessive amounts of alcohol, even if alcohol use is discontinued during treatment

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection has underlying liver disease
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 3) have underlying liver disease

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection has a history of isoniazid-associated liver injury
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 4) have a history of INH-associated liver injury.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient taking rifampin plus pyrazinamide has AST greater than five times upper limit of normal without symptoms
selected_rules.execute: discontinue rifampin plus pyrazinamide immediately and do not resume
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of RZ should be discontinued immediately and not resumed for any of the following findings: 1) AT greater than five times the upper limit of normal range in an asymptomatic person

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He has no cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats, and chest radiograph is normal, consistent with latent tuberculosis infection. He reports drinking 4–6 beers nightly for years, though he recently cut down to 2 beers on weekends. He asks whether he can take the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because a coworker completed it. He has no known HIV infection, but he does take atorvastatin and has mildly elevated baseline liver enzymes from suspected fatty liver disease.
recommended_actions: Do not generally offer the rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen for latent tuberculosis infection.
Do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because the patient drinks excessive amounts of alcohol, even if alcohol use is reduced during treatment.
Do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because he has underlying liver disease.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers latent tuberculosis infection with HIV-negative status, excessive alcohol use, and underlying liver disease. Under the supplied rules, rifampin plus pyrazinamide should generally not be offered for LTBI, and it should never be offered in patients with excessive alcohol use or underlying liver disease.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He has no cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats, and his chest radiograph is normal, consistent with latent tuberculosis infection. He reports drinking 4–6 beers nightly for years, though he has now stopped completely since the screening result. He asks whether he can take the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because a coworker completed it. He has no known HIV infection, but he does take atorvastatin and his baseline liver enzymes are normal with no known history of liver disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on LTBI with a request for rifampin plus pyrazinamide, but the heavy alcohol use and liver disease from the source have been removed while another liver-injury medication remains.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start the rifampin plus pyrazinamide latent TB regimen since he has stopped drinking.
Offer the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide option because his chest x-ray is normal and he is HIV-negative.
Proceed with rifampin plus pyrazinamide without additional concern.
recommended_actions: Do not generally offer the rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen for latent tuberculosis infection.
Do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because he is concurrently taking another medication associated with liver injury.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains latent tuberculosis infection, so the regimen is still not generally recommended. The original alcohol-use and underlying liver disease contraindications are no longer present, but concurrent atorvastatin keeps the case discordant with use of rifampin plus pyrazinamide under the supplied rules.
challenging_examples.rationale: LTBI and HIV-negative status still activate the general non-recommendation. The modified case removes excessive alcohol use and underlying liver disease, so those specific contraindications no longer apply. However, atorvastatin is still a concurrent medication associated with liver injury, so the regimen remains inappropriate.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He has no cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats, and his chest radiograph is normal. He reports drinking 4–6 beers nightly for years, though he recently cut down to 2 beers on weekends. He asks whether he can take the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because a coworker completed it. He was diagnosed with latent tuberculosis infection years ago, but the chart also notes a prior episode of isoniazid-associated hepatitis during an earlier treatment attempt. He has no known HIV infection and no known chronic liver disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall presentation is nearly unchanged from the source, including LTBI, alcohol use, and a request for rifampin plus pyrazinamide, but the liver disease detail has been swapped for prior INH-associated liver injury.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer the rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen since he does not have active tuberculosis.
Recommend the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide because the prior problem was with isoniazid, not rifampin.
Proceed with rifampin plus pyrazinamide after counseling about alcohol reduction.
recommended_actions: Do not generally offer the rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen for latent tuberculosis infection.
Do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because the patient drinks excessive amounts of alcohol, even if alcohol use is reduced during treatment.
Do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because he has a history of isoniazid-associated liver injury.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The patient still has latent tuberculosis infection, and the regimen remains not generally recommended. Although he does not have underlying liver disease, his ongoing excessive alcohol use and prior isoniazid-associated liver injury make rifampin plus pyrazinamide inappropriate under the supplied rules.
challenging_examples.rationale: LTBI and HIV-negative status trigger the general non-recommendation. Excess alcohol use still applies, and the new history of isoniazid-associated liver injury introduces another explicit contraindication. Removing underlying liver disease invalidates R006, but the case remains discordant for other reasons.

selected_rules.activation_condition: heat exposure is prolonged or severe enough to cause body temperature above 38°C
selected_rules.execute: do not continue further exposures during the same day
selected_rules.source_statement: There is abundant evidence that the physiological strain of an exposure which raises body temperature above 38°C is such as to contraindicate further exposures during the same day; it may take hours for complete recovery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: employee exposure to heat in the workplace
selected_rules.execute: control heat exposure by requiring compliance with the work practice standard
selected_rules.source_statement: NIOSH recommends that employee exposure to heat in the workplace be controlled by requiring compliance with the work practice standard set forth in the following sections.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hot environmental conditions exceed WBGT 79°F
selected_rules.execute: apply the standard to all places of employment indoors and outdoors and to all employees except those required to wear impermeable protective clothing
selected_rules.source_statement: The provisions of this standard are applicable to all places of employment, indoors and outdoors, and to all employees except those who are required to wear impermeable protective clothing.

selected_rules.activation_condition: continuous exposure for one hour or intermittent exposure for two hours and WBGT exceeds 79°F for men or 76°F for women
selected_rules.execute: initiate one or a combination of the listed protective practices to keep body core temperature from exceeding 100.4°F
selected_rules.source_statement: When exposure of an employee is continuous for one hour or intermittent for a period of two hours and the time-weighted average WBGT exceeds 79°F for men or 76°F for women, then any one or combination of the following practices shall be initiated to insure that the employee's body care temperature does not exceed 100.4°F:

selected_rules.activation_condition: employee is unacclimatized
selected_rules.execute: acclimatize over 6 days starting at 50 percent of anticipated total work load and time exposure on day 1, then increase by 10 percent daily to 100 percent on day 6
selected_rules.source_statement: Unacclimatized employees shall be acclimatized over a period of 6 days. The acclimatization schedule shall begin with 50 percent of the anticipated total work load and time exposure on the first day, followed by daily 10 percent increments building up 100 percent total exposure on the sixth day.

selected_rules.activation_condition: regular acclimatized employee returns after nine or more consecutive calendar days of leave
selected_rules.execute: use a 4-day reacclimatization schedule starting at 50 percent on day 1 and increasing by 20 percent daily to 100 percent on day 4
selected_rules.source_statement: Regular acclimatized employees who return from nine or more consecutive calendar days of leave, shall undergo a four day acclimatization period. The acclimatization schedule shall begin with 50 percent of the anticipated total exposure on the first day, followed by daily 20 percent increments building up to 100 percent total exposure on the fourth day.

selected_rules.activation_condition: regular acclimatized employee returns after four consecutive days of illness
selected_rules.execute: obtain medical permission to return to work and use a 4-day reacclimatization schedule as above
selected_rules.source_statement: Regular acclimatized employees who return from four consecutive days of illness should have medical permission to return to the job, and should undergo a four day re-acclimatization period as defined in (2) above.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workers are exposed to hot jobs
selected_rules.execute: implement a work and rest regimen to reduce peaks of physiological strain and improve recovery during rest periods
selected_rules.source_statement: A work and rest regimen shall be implemented to reduce the peaks of physiological strain and to improve recovery during rest periods.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a non-air-conditioned loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 38.4°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot all day, and several coworkers were also complaining of the heat.
recommended_actions: Do not continue further heat exposures during the same day.
Control employee heat exposure by requiring compliance with the work practice standard.
Implement a work and rest regimen to reduce peaks of physiological strain and improve recovery during rest periods.
source_case.rationale: The worker had prolonged workplace heat exposure with a measured body temperature above 38°C, which triggers the recommendation to stop further exposures for the rest of the day. Because this is employee heat exposure in the workplace, heat exposure should be controlled through compliance with the work practice standard. Since workers are exposed to hot jobs, a work-and-rest regimen is recommended to reduce physiological strain and support recovery.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a non-air-conditioned loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 37.8°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot all day, and several coworkers were also complaining of the heat.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting, symptoms, and hot-work exposure are nearly identical to the source case, but the measured temperature stays below the threshold that would prohibit further same-day exposure.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Send him back to the loading dock once he feels better.
Treat this as simple heat stress and continue the shift with more water breaks only.
Keep the same work pace but remind the crew to take occasional rest breaks.
recommended_actions: Control employee heat exposure by requiring compliance with the work practice standard.
Implement a work and rest regimen to reduce peaks of physiological strain and improve recovery during rest periods.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still a workplace heat-exposure case, so heat controls and a work-rest regimen remain appropriate. However, the core temperature does not exceed 38°C, so the specific recommendation to stop all further exposures for the rest of the day is not triggered. The safer interpretation is symptomatic heat strain requiring ongoing heat-exposure controls, not mandatory same-day cessation based on temperature threshold.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 requires body temperature above 38°C; this vignette has 37.8°C. The workplace heat-exposure context still supports R002 and R008.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a non-air-conditioned loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 38.5°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot all day, but today he is using a sealed impermeable chemical-protective suit because of a spill cleanup assignment, and the hot work area has already been shut down pending ventilation repairs.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The worker still looks like a straightforward hot-environment occupational heat case with fatigue, sweating, and a temperature above 38°C, but the job context has shifted to a protected area and impermeable clothing is in use.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Keep him off the dock for the rest of the day because of the elevated temperature.
Continue applying the heat work practice standard to the spill cleanup crew.
Start a work-rest regimen for the hot loading-dock tasks.
recommended_actions: Do not continue further heat exposures during the same day.
Control employee heat exposure by requiring compliance with the work practice standard.
Apply the standard to all places of employment indoors and outdoors and to all employees except those required to wear impermeable protective clothing.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The elevated temperature still triggers stopping further heat exposure for the day, and the general workplace heat-exposure standard applies. However, the specific work-and-rest regimen recommendation tied to workers exposed to hot jobs does not cleanly apply here because the crew is working in impermeable protective clothing and the hot loading-dock job is no longer the actual current exposure setting. The safer interpretation is to focus on removal from heat exposure and the applicable workplace standard for this protected-clothing scenario.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 and R002 remain triggered by the febrile heat-exposure presentation. R008 is invalidated because the modified setting centers on impermeable protective clothing, which changes the applicability of the hot-jobs work-rest regimen recommendation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: occupational exposure to HIV with negligible risk
selected_rules.execute: do not give postexposure prophylaxis
selected_rules.source_statement: For exposures with negligible risk, PEP is not justified (Table 1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: exposure is not occupational exposure to HIV
selected_rules.execute: do not offer PEP
selected_rules.source_statement: Not offer-PEP should not be offered because these are not occupational exposures to HIV (1 ).

selected_rules.activation_condition: skin exposure to HIV without increased risk
selected_rules.execute: do not give PEP because drug toxicity outweighs benefit
selected_rules.source_statement: For skin exposures without increased risk, the risk for drug toxicity outweighs the benefit of PEP.

selected_rules.activation_condition: suspected heroin overdose with coma or severe respiratory distress
selected_rules.execute: give naloxone
selected_rules.source_statement: Naloxone remains the treatment of choice for coma and severe respiratory distress associated with possible drug overdose.

selected_rules.activation_condition: new case of scopolamine-containing heroin overdose
selected_rules.execute: report promptly to local poison-control center and health department
selected_rules.source_statement: new cases should be reported promptly to the local poison-control center and health department.

selected_rules.activation_condition: occupational exposure to HIV with highest risk for transmission
selected_rules.execute: recommend chemoprophylaxis
selected_rules.source_statement: Chemoprophylaxis should be recommended to exposed workers after occupational exposures associated with the highest risk for HIV transmission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering any HIV postexposure prophylaxis regimen
selected_rules.execute: include zidovudine (ZDV)
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, ZDV should be considered for all PEP regimens because ZDV is the only agent for which data support the efficacy of PEP in the clinical setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HIV postexposure prophylaxis regimen is being selected
selected_rules.execute: usually add lamivudine (3TC) to ZDV
selected_rules.source_statement: 3TC should usually be added to ZDV for increased antiretroviral activity and activity against many ZDV-resistant strains.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with known untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and penetrated deeply into her finger before she was able to wash the area. She presents within 45 minutes of the exposure. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. This is an occupational exposure with a substantial risk of HIV transmission, rather than a negligible-risk contact or a nonoccupational exposure.
recommended_actions: Recommend HIV chemoprophylaxis after this high-risk occupational exposure.
Include zidovudine (ZDV) in the postexposure prophylaxis regimen.
Usually add lamivudine (3TC) to ZDV when selecting the regimen.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a high-risk occupational exposure to HIV, which triggers the recommendation for chemoprophylaxis. The selected rules also support including zidovudine in all PEP regimens and usually adding lamivudine to zidovudine to increase antiretroviral activity.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with known untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and penetrated deeply into her finger before she was able to wash the area. She presents within 45 minutes of the exposure. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. The only added detail is that the source patient’s chart documents the nurse as the patient’s close relative, and the exposure occurred during a family visit rather than during her scheduled hospital shift. The injury itself is substantial, but the setting is not a formal workplace exposure.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The exposure is still a deep, blood-filled needlestick from a patient with untreated HIV and high viral load, making it easy to focus on transmission risk and overlook that it occurred outside an occupational context.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start HIV postexposure prophylaxis immediately.
Include zidovudine in the regimen and add lamivudine.
Treat this as a high-risk occupational HIV exposure.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for occupational HIV PEP because the exposure did not occur in the course of employment; it was a nonoccupational exposure despite the similar mechanism. The safer interpretation is to evaluate it using nonoccupational exposure guidance rather than automatically applying the occupational PEP recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R006 requires occupational exposure to HIV with highest risk for transmission. The modified vignette preserves the biologic risk but changes the setting to a family visit, so the occupational criterion is no longer met.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with known untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and penetrated deeply into her finger before she was able to wash the area. She presents within 45 minutes of the exposure. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. Occupational health confirms that the needle had been used only to access a superficial venipuncture, the injury was a brief scratch without actual skin penetration, and the nurse immediately rinsed intact skin after contact with a small amount of blood. The source patient remains high risk, but the contact itself appears limited to intact skin.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The source patient, timing, and healthcare setting are unchanged, so the case still sounds like a classic HIV exposure, but the actual injury is only intact-skin contact without a true percutaneous exposure.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Begin HIV chemoprophylaxis now.
Use a zidovudine-based regimen with lamivudine.
Manage this as a high-risk occupational needlestick exposure.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This does not meet criteria for high-risk occupational HIV exposure because there was no true percutaneous injury or blood inoculation; the final assessment is a negligible-risk skin contact. The safer interpretation is observation and routine exposure documentation rather than HIV PEP.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation depends on an occupational exposure associated with highest transmission risk. Here, the modification changes the event to intact-skin contact after a superficial scratch, which invalidates the exposure severity needed for R006 and therefore also the regimen-related rules R007 and R008.

selected_rules.activation_condition: routine prevention of hepatitis A in the United States
selected_rules.execute: Implement routine hepatitis A vaccination for children nationwide.
selected_rules.source_statement: All children should receive hepatitis A vaccine at age 1 year (i.e., 12-23 months).

selected_rules.activation_condition: state, county, or community already has a hepatitis A vaccination program for children aged 2-18 years
selected_rules.execute: Maintain the existing childhood hepatitis A vaccination program.
selected_rules.source_statement: States, counties, and communities with existing hepatitis A vaccination programs for children aged 2-18 years are encouraged to maintain these programs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person is traveling to or working in a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity and is susceptible
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate before departure or give immune globulin before departure; vaccine is preferred.
selected_rules.source_statement: All susceptible persons traveling to or working in countries that have high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity (Figure 4) should be vaccinated or receive IG before departure (Tables 1-4). Hepatitis A vaccination at the age-appropriate dose is preferred (Tables 2-4).

selected_rules.activation_condition: travel is planned and first vaccine dose can be given now
selected_rules.execute: Give the first hepatitis A vaccine dose as soon as travel is considered.
selected_rules.source_statement: The first dose of hepatitis A vaccine should be administered as soon as travel is considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: traveler departs in less than 4 weeks and cannot or will not receive immune globulin
selected_rules.execute: Still give hepatitis A vaccine and inform the traveler protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks.
selected_rules.source_statement: Travelers departing in <4 weeks who do not or cannot receive IG should nonetheless receive hepatitis A vaccine and be informed that they might not be optimally protected from acquiring hepatitis A in the immediate future (i.e., subsequent 2-4 weeks).

selected_rules.activation_condition: traveler is allergic to a vaccine component or declines vaccine
selected_rules.execute: Give a single dose of immune globulin 0.02 mL/kg; if travel lasts more than 2 months, use 0.06 mL/kg and repeat if travel lasts more than 5 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: Travelers who are allergic to a vaccine component or who elect not to receive vaccine should receive a single dose of IG (0.02 mL/kg), which provides effective protection against hepatitis A for up to 3 months (Table 1). Travelers whose travel period is >2 months should be administered IG at 0.06 mL/kg; administration must be repeated if the travel period is >5 months (Table 1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: person is an adolescent or adult man who has sex with men
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate against hepatitis A.
selected_rules.source_statement: MSM (both adolescents and adults) should be vaccinated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person uses injection or noninjection illicit drugs
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate against hepatitis A.
selected_rules.source_statement: Vaccination is recommended for users of injection and noninjection illicit drugs.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to rural Peru. He has never received hepatitis A vaccine and does not recall prior infection. He says he is healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no vaccine allergies. He asks whether he can still do anything to reduce his risk before leaving. Because departure is soon, he would like protection as quickly as possible and is willing to be vaccinated today.
recommended_actions: Vaccinate before departure because he is a susceptible traveler going to a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity.
Give the first hepatitis A vaccine dose as soon as travel is considered.
Because departure is in less than 4 weeks, administer hepatitis A vaccine now and inform him that protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks.
source_case.rationale: This traveler is susceptible and is going to a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity, so pre-departure hepatitis A vaccination is recommended. Since travel is already planned, the first dose should be given as soon as travel is considered. Because he is leaving in less than 4 weeks, he should still receive vaccine now and be told that immediate protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to rural Peru. He has documentation of completing a full hepatitis A vaccine series in childhood but does not remember the details. He says he is healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no vaccine allergies. He asks whether he can still do anything to reduce his risk before leaving. Because departure is soon, he would like protection as quickly as possible and is willing to be vaccinated today.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The travel destination and timing are unchanged, but he is no longer susceptible because he already completed hepatitis A vaccination.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Give an additional hepatitis A vaccine dose before departure because the trip is imminent.
Administer hepatitis A vaccine today and counsel that protection may still be incomplete for the next few weeks.
Offer immune globulin because he is leaving in less than 4 weeks.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This no longer meets the travel-related hepatitis A vaccination recommendation because he is already documented as having completed the vaccine series, so he is not a susceptible traveler. The safer interpretation is that no additional hepatitis A pre-travel prophylaxis is indicated solely for this trip, assuming the childhood series is valid.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule applies to susceptible travelers to countries with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity. Prior complete vaccination removes susceptibility, so the pre-departure recommendation is no longer triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to rural Peru. He has never received hepatitis A vaccine and does not recall prior infection. He says he is healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no vaccine allergies. He asks whether he can still do anything to reduce his risk before leaving. He notes that he will only be staying at a resort in Lima and on the Pacific coast, with no plans to visit rural areas, eat street food, or travel outside the capital region. Because departure is soon, he would like protection as quickly as possible and is willing to be vaccinated today.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient is still unvaccinated and the trip is still to Peru, but the itinerary is now limited to low-risk urban and resort settings rather than the higher-risk rural exposure described in the source case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Administer hepatitis A vaccine today because he is traveling to Peru.
Tell him to get vaccinated before departure and note that immediate protection may still be incomplete.
Recommend immune globulin because the trip is less than 4 weeks away.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette does not clearly satisfy the travel-endemicity trigger as written, because the itinerary is restricted to a resort and the urban coast rather than the rural setting that makes the exposure risk more compelling. A safer interpretation is that the pre-travel hepatitis A recommendation is not supported by the modified details alone, so other travel-risk factors would need to be assessed before acting.
challenging_examples.rationale: The guideline recommendation is for susceptible persons traveling to countries with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity. The modified itinerary subtly lowers the exposure context enough that the original assumption of higher-risk travel is no longer secure from the vignette alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults aged 65 years or older
selected_rules.execute: implement fall prevention efforts to reduce injury, disability, and death
selected_rules.source_statement: Falls are the leading cause of injury deaths and disabilities among persons aged ≥65 years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults at risk of falling
selected_rules.execute: use multifaceted fall prevention programs that combine exercise, environmental modifications, education, medication review, and risk factor reduction
selected_rules.source_statement: Research has established that effective fall intervention programs employ a multifaceted approach and incorporate both behavioral and environmental elements: exercises to improve strength and balance, environmental modifications, education about fall prevention, medication review and assessment to minimize side effects, and risk factor reduction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: community-dwelling older adults with fall risk factors
selected_rules.execute: provide behavioral instructions, targeted training, exercise to increase strength, and medication adjustments
selected_rules.source_statement: A total of 153 persons participated in an intervention that consisted of behavioral instructions and training to reduce specific risk factors ... exercise programs to increase strength, and medication adjustments.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults living in the community
selected_rules.execute: increase physical activity that improves strength, balance, and coordination
selected_rules.source_statement: Activities that improve strength, balance, and coordination can reduce the risk for falls and fall-related injuries among healthy and frail persons.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults living at home
selected_rules.execute: address home hazards and use home-visiting programs to identify and correct fall risks
selected_rules.source_statement: Because approximately 50%-60% of all falls among older adults occur at home, fall prevention programs should address home hazards that can contribute to falls.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults with chronic diseases or hip fracture risk factors
selected_rules.execute: maximize control of concomitant chronic diseases and counsel against inappropriate weight loss
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical approaches might include reducing fall risk factors (e.g., maximizing control of concomitant chronic diseases) and reducing hip fracture risk factors (e.g., counseling women aged ≥65 years against inappropriate weight loss).

selected_rules.activation_condition: nursing-home residents at high risk of falls
selected_rules.execute: implement intensive multifaceted interventions including environmental modifications, medication review, and attention to individual needs
selected_rules.source_statement: A randomized trial ... evaluated an intensive, multifaceted intervention of extensive environmental modifications ... medication review, and increased attention to individual resident needs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women starting an exercise program
selected_rules.execute: start slowly at a level matched to current physical fitness and set realistic goals
selected_rules.source_statement: Women starting exercise programs should be realistic about their goals and start slowly at frequency, duration, and intensity levels commensurate with their current physical fitness condition.

source_case.patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who lives independently in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had two near-falls while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom. Mrs. L wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks.
recommended_actions: Implement fall prevention efforts to reduce injury, disability, and death.
Use a multifaceted fall prevention program that combines exercise, environmental modifications, education, medication review, and risk factor reduction.
Address home hazards and consider a home-visiting program to identify and correct fall risks.
Maximize control of concomitant chronic diseases and counsel against inappropriate weight loss if relevant.
source_case.rationale: She is an older adult aged 65 years or older, which triggers general fall prevention. She is also at risk of falling due to prior falls, unsteadiness, home hazards, and medications, supporting a multifaceted approach. Because she lives at home, home hazard assessment and correction are indicated. Her chronic disease burden supports attention to optimizing chronic disease control.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who lives independently in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had one near-fall while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom. She wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks. On review, her blood pressure is well controlled, she exercises regularly, and she has no history of prior fractures or other chronic disease-related complications.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still sounds like a classic community-dwelling older adult with falls, home hazards, and fall-risk medications, but the chronic disease/hip fracture risk element has been removed.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Implement a multifaceted fall prevention plan with medication review and home hazard correction.
Arrange a home visit to identify and remove fall hazards.
Counsel her about chronic disease optimization and avoiding inappropriate weight loss.
recommended_actions: Implement fall prevention efforts to reduce injury, disability, and death.
Use a multifaceted fall prevention program that combines exercise, environmental modifications, education, medication review, and risk factor reduction.
Address home hazards and consider a home-visiting program to identify and correct fall risks.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: She still meets general fall-prevention criteria because she is 65 or older, has had falls, and has home and medication-related fall risks. However, the added chronic disease/hip fracture-risk component is not supported here because her chronic conditions are well controlled and there is no relevant hip fracture risk or inappropriate weight loss issue.
challenging_examples.rationale: Age and fall history still trigger general fall-prevention recommendations, including a multifaceted approach and home hazard assessment. The modification removes the chronic disease/hip fracture-risk indication, so the weight-loss/chronic-disease counseling element is no longer guideline-concordant.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who lives independently in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had two near-falls while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom. Mrs. L wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks. She asks whether her daughter should install grab bars and remove rugs. It turns out she has been living in an assisted-living apartment for the last year, with meals, housekeeping, and staff on site, and she does not perform her own household maintenance.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The story preserves the same fall history, medications, and home-safety concerns, but the living arrangement is no longer clearly a personal home where a home-hazard visit is the right fit.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Set up a home-visiting program to assess and correct the hazards in her apartment.
Prioritize environmental modifications in the living space.
Use a multifaceted fall prevention program including medication review and exercise.
recommended_actions: Implement fall prevention efforts to reduce injury, disability, and death.
Use a multifaceted fall prevention program that combines exercise, environmental modifications, education, medication review, and risk factor reduction.
Maximize control of concomitant chronic diseases and counsel against inappropriate weight loss if relevant.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: She still warrants fall-prevention measures because she is an older adult with prior falls and multiple risk factors. But the specific home-visit recommendation is not well matched to this vignette because she is in assisted living rather than managing a private home environment; environmental review should be directed to the facility-based setting instead.
challenging_examples.rationale: General fall-prevention and multifaceted risk reduction remain appropriate, and chronic disease optimization can still be considered. The home-hazard/home-visiting component is invalidated by the change from living at home to assisted living, which shifts the appropriate setting for environmental assessment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: planning school food environment
selected_rules.execute: Discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, schools should discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars (e.g., candy, fried chips, and soda) on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.

selected_rules.activation_condition: teachers use food in classroom management
selected_rules.execute: Discourage teachers from using food to reward or discipline students.
selected_rules.source_statement: Schools should discourage teachers from using food for disciplining or rewarding students.

selected_rules.activation_condition: school-based nutrition education is being planned or implemented
selected_rules.execute: Use a comprehensive school health program to promote healthy eating.
selected_rules.source_statement: School-based programs can play an important role in promoting lifelong healthy eating.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.
selected_rules.source_statement: Policy: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Include input from students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.
selected_rules.source_statement: A school nutrition policy should be a brief document that incorporates input from all relevant constituents of the school community: students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Adapt the policy to local needs and the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.
selected_rules.source_statement: The policy should meet local needs and be adapted to the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Include follow-up input and a process for revising the policy as needed.
selected_rules.source_statement: The policy plan should include means of obtaining follow-up input from all parties and means of revising the plan as needed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Use a nutrition advisory committee or nutrition subcommittee with student members to develop and promulgate the policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: A nutrition advisory committee or a nutrition subcommittee of the school health advisory council having student members can develop and promulgate a coordinated school nutrition policy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a school health planning meeting after the district notices inconsistent food practices across campuses. The middle school is drafting a new nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse reports that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester but also updated after feedback from families, staff, and students. The team is looking for a practical plan that supports healthy eating without ignoring local food traditions or the needs of different student groups.
recommended_actions: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.
Include input from students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.
Adapt the policy to local needs and the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.
Include follow-up input and a process for revising the policy as needed.
Use a nutrition advisory committee or nutrition subcommittee with student members to develop and promulgate the policy.
Discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.
Discourage teachers from using food to reward or discipline students.
source_case.rationale: The vignette centers on developing a school nutrition policy, which triggers the policy-related recommendations. It also includes planning a school food environment and teacher use of food for classroom management, triggering the corresponding discouragement recommendations. No unsupported recommendations were added.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a district wellness committee meeting after the district notices inconsistent food practices across campuses. The middle school is drafting a new nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse reports that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester, but the draft will be finalized by the superintendent without additional family or student review. The team is looking for a practical plan that supports healthy eating without ignoring local food traditions or the needs of different student groups.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This still presents a school nutrition policy with vending, fundraisers, classroom rewards, and concern for local food preferences, but the policy process omits follow-up revision and student-involved advisory development.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Adopt the coordinated school nutrition policy immediately and have the superintendent finalize it without further input.
Set up the school nutrition policy to include a student-member advisory committee and a process for later revision.
Discourage the sale of soda, chips, and other high-fat, high-sodium, or high-sugar foods on campus and at fundraisers.
Discourage teachers from using candy to reward students.
recommended_actions: Discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.
Discourage teachers from using food to reward or discipline students.
Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.
Include input from students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.
Adapt the policy to local needs and the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case still supports school food-environment recommendations, but it is no longer guideline-concordant for the policy-development process because the draft is being finalized without the recommended follow-up input and without a nutrition advisory committee or subcommittee with student members. A safer interpretation is to address campus food practices while building a broader, revisable policy process.
challenging_examples.rationale: The modified vignette preserves the school nutrition policy context and the food-reward/fundraising issues, which still trigger the non-recommended discouragements and core policy content. However, it explicitly removes follow-up revision and student-member advisory development, invalidating R007 and R008.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a school health planning meeting after the district notices inconsistent food practices across campuses. The middle school is drafting a new nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse reports that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester and updated after feedback from families, staff, and students. The team is considering a nutrition advisory committee, but the draft policy will be written by a small administrative task force that includes only the principal, cafeteria manager, and district accountant.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The school policy discussion, stakeholder language, and plans for revision remain intact, but the committee structure lacks student members and broader advisory development.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the nutrition advisory committee draft to promulgate the school nutrition policy.
Finalize a coordinated policy with input from the school task force alone.
Discourage soda and chip fundraisers and stop teachers from using candy rewards.
Build in a revision process after family and student feedback.
recommended_actions: Discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.
Discourage teachers from using food to reward or discipline students.
Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.
Include input from students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.
Adapt the policy to local needs and the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.
Include follow-up input and a process for revising the policy as needed.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette still fits school nutrition policy guidance, including stakeholder input and revision, but it does not meet the committee recommendation because the policy is being developed by a small administrative task force rather than a nutrition advisory committee or subcommittee with student members. The safer interpretation is to ensure broader stakeholder participation before promulgation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case continues to activate the school nutrition policy and school food environment recommendations. The only invalidated rule is R008 because the described development group lacks the recommended nutrition advisory committee or student-member subcommittee structure.
